text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with depression and anxiety, history of suicide attempts, presents with suicidal ideation and dizziness. # Suidical ideation - Due to depression and PTSD. Psychiatry concluded that patient would benefit greatly from transfer to inpatient psych when instability is better managed. Pt was placed on ___ and with 1:1. We continued home sertraline 150 daily and alprazolam bid prn anxiety. We held prazosin as it may be contributing to her unsteadiness. Resuming prazosin will be in the discrection of our psychiatry colleagues or pt's outpatient psychiatrist. # headache - Patient has history of migraine but has been using analgesics (oxycodone, compazine, toradol, reglan, fioricet (daily use). Thus, this is chronic daily headache d/t medication overuse with underlying migraine vs post-LP low CSF pressure headaches. We discontinued NSAIDS, narcotics and fioricet. We managed headaches with tylenol and anti-emetics. We started amitriptyline 10mg QHS for prophylaxis. We did a trial of propranolol given risk of overdose with TCA's in a suicical pt but pt's HR was in the ___ and we discontinued it. Neurology was consulted and recommended MRI to r/o low CSF pressure/leak but MRI was unremarkable. However, given orthostatic nature of headaches, neurology recommended pain anesthesia consult for blood patch. Pain anesthesia concluded that blood patch is not urgently indicated and addressing underlying psychiatric issues has priority as her symptoms may very likely be functional/somatic. Thus, pt was transferred to psychiatric unit for further management. We recommend that neurology continues to follow up to assess and make further evaluations for headaches/unsteadiness. Headaches d/t low CSF pressure and medication overuse self resolve with time. We managed pt conservatively with aggressive hydration and caffeine intake. # Instability - The patient complains of lightheadedness, "rocking boat", on standing with gait instability. No nystagmus on exam. Onset of these symptoms appear to coincide with her recent hospitalization for viral illness and may be a consequence of post-LP headache (see discussion above). Presentation and exam indicate functional etiology vs. peripheral neurititis given history of viral illness about a month ago vs. LP complication. Pt was evaluated by ___ who cleared pt and recommended using a walker for ambulation. All possible etiologies mentioned above self resolve with time. We discontinued prazosin as it may be contributing to her orthostatic symptoms. Orthostatics are negative for orthostatic hypotension; however, pt is symptomatically orthostatic with ambulation. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [] prazosin was held throughout hospital course on medical floor as it may be contributing/causing her unsteadiness. Resuming it will be in the discretion of our psychiatric colleagues for nightmares. [] neurology follow up while admitted on inpatient psych for headache/dizziness [] pt on amitriptyline 10mg QHS for headache prophylaxis. Given overdose potential in patients with SI, we recommend re-evaluation about dosing and administration
summary: You presented to us with suicidal ideation as well as headache and unsteadiness. We consulted our psychiatry colleagues who recommended inpatient admission to monitor you closely and ensure your safety. We also consulted our neurology colleagues for your headaches/unsteadiness. We believe your headaches are most likely due to pain medication overuse and/or low cerebral spinal fluid pressure. We performed an MRI and it was negative for any evidence of low CSF pressure. We stopped your analgesics and started you on a medication called amitriptyline for prophylaxis. With medication overuse headaches and low CSF pressure headaches, it will self resolve with time. We consulted physical therapy to evaluate your unsteadiness. We consulted pain anesthesia regarding repeating blood patch. They concluded that blood patch is not urgently indicated as MRI was negative and such headaches self resolve with time. You were further admitted to inpatient psychiatric floor.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with a history of dementia, depression with prior SI attempts, and DM who presents to the ED after a mechanical fall and complaining of suicidal ideation, concerning for orthostasis. #Falls. #Orthostatic hypotension Patient presented after a fall at her nursing home and was complaining of back pain and a headache. Imaging with CT Head and CT C and L spine only demonstrated chronic old anterior osteophyte fractures in L spine and no acute findings. She endorses feeling lightheaded for a while but was unable to specify how long. She was kept on telemetry for 24 hours after admission without any events. TTE demonstrated normal biventricular cavity sizes with preserved regional and global biventricular systolic function, mildly dilated aortic arch, and increased PCWP. She was found to have orthostatic hypotension during her work up for fall. AM cortisol was low at 4.3. She underwent cosyntropin stimulation test with appropriate response to stimulation, ruling out primary adrenal insufficiency. At time of discharge, ACTH and aldosterone levels pending. Patient was found to have large volumes of urine output and could possibly have nephrogenic diabetes insipidus in context of long history of lithium use; however, she was never hypernatremic or volume depleted. Other contributing factors could be psychiatric medications or autonomic dysfunction from diabetes. She received IV fluids and was encouraged to have better po intake. She was no longer orthostatic at time of discharge. #Depression with suicidal ideation Patient has a history of prior suicidal ideation with resultant inpatient psychiatric hospitalizations. She endorsed suicidal ideation upon presentation and endorsed auditory hallucinations instructing her to take over dose on her medications. ___ was implemented in the ED. Psychiatry evaluated patient after admission who agreed with plan to section. She had a 1:1 sitter during hospitalization. Her SI remained present throughout She was kept on home fluoxetine. She was discharged to inpatient psychiatric unit after medically cleared. #Orthopnea Patient presented with complaint of orthopnea, concerning for new onset heart failure. CXR demonstrated mild pulmonary congestion. proBNP was wnl at presentation. Initial EKG demonstrated T wave inversion in V2 and V3; repeat EKG after presentation showed resolution of this. TTE demonstrated LVEF 55% and normal biventricular cavity sizes with preserved regional and global biventricular systolic function, mildly dilated aortic arch, and increased PCWP. Patient did not have O2 desaturation with ambulation or while supine. No diuresis was initiated and symptoms improved. #DM Patient is on metformin and sitagliptin at home. These were held while in the hospital and she was placed on ISS. She required minimal #Seizure disorder. She was continued on home lamictal and lacosamide. #Anxiety #Mood disorder She was continued on home risperidone. Her home trazodone was discontinued as it could be contributing to orthostatic hypotension. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Discharge labs: Na 139 Discontinued medications: trazodone 100mg QHS - medication could be contributing to orthostatic hypotension [] pending results: ACTH, aldosterone [] follow up with endocrinology for ongoing evaluation of orthostatic hypotension and low AM cortisol
summary: You were admitted because you had a fall at your nursing home. You were evaluated for your fall. You were found to have low blood pressure when you stand. You were given fluids through your IV and encouraged to drink more fluids. Your blood pressure improved with this. You were complaining of difficulty breathing while lying down. Your lungs and heart were evaluated with imaging and appeared to be normal. You expressed feelings of wanting to hurt yourself by taking all of your medications. The psychiatry team evaluated you and felt that you would benefit from going to an inpatient psychiatry unit. Please continue to drink plenty of fluids to prevent you from feeling lightheaded.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old year old male with past medication history significant for HBV/HCV cirrhosis s/p liver transplant in ___, CKD with proteinuria, medication (tacrolimus vs interferon) induced polymyositis in ___ and Acute kidney injury on Chronic kidney disease stage 3 thought be due to tacrolimus toxicity admitted for initiation of dialysis. HD was tolerated well however course was complicated by GI bleed and STEMI while on HD. #. ACUTE ON CHRONIC RENAL FAILURE leading to End Stage Renal Disease: Likely progression of his underlying chronic kidney disease. Switched off tacrolimus to Cellcept last admission, although restarted tacrolimus and Cellcept dose reduced due to elevation in liver enzymes . Tunneled line catheter was placed with subsequent dialysis three days weekly. He tolerated HD well aside from one episode of orthostasis (resolved with temporarily holding his BP meds) and a STEMI (see below). He will need to have care established with a renal/dialysis physcian when he leaves the rehabilitation facility, preferably near his home location. He previously saw Dr. ___ (___) at ___, however Dr. ___ not manage outpatient dialysis patients. . # GI BLEED: On ___ he had a large melenatic stool. He was started on IV pantoprazole, made NPO, and transfused 1u pRBCs given slightly altered mental status. On ___ he had an EGD which showed a duodenal ulcer (clipped and injected) as well as ___ esophagitis. He was last transfused ___, but has maintained a stable Hct >30 since then, without melena, and remains hemodynamically stable. He was transitioned to PPI PO BID which should be continued. Nystatin swish and swallow was started for his esophagitis (note fluconazole not used due to risk of hepatotoxicity and patient did not endorse dysphagia). # STEMI: On ___ during dialysis, he developed tachycardia HR 150bpm but was completely asymptomatic. EKG revealed ST elevations V3 and V4. CODE STEMI was called and the patient was taken to the catheterization lab where a 90% LAD lesion was found and a BMS was placed successfully. He was started on aspirin, plavix, atorvastatin and restarted on his labetolol. Note his aspirin dose was 81mg not ___ due to his ongoing GI bleed. He was not started on an ACE-I because his EF>50%. He does not smoke. His cardiac enzymes peaked and downtrended. He did not have any further chest pain. #. HISTORY OF LIVER TRANSPLANT in ___ due to alcohol/hepatitis B & C: Tacrolimus restarted at low dose 0.5mg BID, Cellcept decreased to 500 mg po BID and he is now on prednisone ___ every other day for polymyositis. He should continue on Bactrim SS daily while on prednisone. His liver enzymes improved while on tacrolimus. . #. Polymyositis: Continued on prednisone 30 mg / 40 mg every other day (as per neurology recommendation two weeks ago) for his polymyositis which is clinically controlled per EMG. He will follow up with Dr. ___ at which point his prednisone should be tapered. . #. Seizure disorder/Epilepsy. Continued on oxcarbazepine at 150 mg BID . #. Hypertension: Well controlled on labetalol 200 mg po BID. #. Depression: He initially expressed suicidal ideation to housestaff and nursing staff. Psychiatry was consulted and venlafaxine was increased to goal 150mg daily. Ritalin was also added and titrated to goal 5mg qam and 5mg qnoon with improvement in his mood. . # OSTEOPOROSIS: His alendronate will be restarted on discharge. . He was FULL CODE for this admission.
summary: You were admitted for initiation of dialysis. A tunneled catheter line was placed with help of intervention radiology. You tolerated dialysis well. During your hospital stay you developed an ulcer in your small intestines requiring several blood transfusions. You had an endoscopy and the ulcer was clipped. You stopped bleeding and your anemia improved. During a hemodialysis session, your heart rate increased and you had a heart attack. You were taken to the catheterization lab immediately and a bare metal stent was placed in an artery in your heart. You were started on Plavix and Aspirin. You MUST continue to take your plavix to prevent a future heart attack. Please do not stop this medication unless told to do so by your cardiologist.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o M w/ h/o DM, HTN, Hyperlipidemia, and depression presents with 65lb weight loss, and pain with eating. . #. Weight Loss/Anorexia: Most likely patient is suffering from depression leading to poor appetitie. Patient's review of systems is consistent with depression. He endorses depressed mood, lack of energy and interest in activities, decreased appetite, insomnia, guilt, psychomotor agitation. He denies suicidal ideation. The patient reports a history of severe depression leading to weight loss in the past. It is uncertain how recent this weight loss is. Since ___, in OMR we have a documented weight loss of 45lbs. The PCP's office records show a 6 lb weight loss in the past month. The patient reports symptoms of fullness and early satiety. He denied abdominal pain associated with eating or at any time. The patinet did not have any significant lab abnormalities. In any elderly patient with weight loss, early satiety and decreased appetite must consider malignancy. The patient denied B-symptoms. The patient and his wife both reported that the patient had undergone a colonoscopy in the last ___ years and that they were told it was normal. I recommend that if the patient has not had a colonoscopy, that he have one done. The patient's prostate exam was slightly enlarged consistent with BPH with a mildly elevated PSA. We recommend that the patient compare this PSA with prior prostate cancer screening and follow up as appropriate. A CT scan of the torso was negative for obvious masses or malignancy. The patient had a gastric emptying study to evaluate for gastroparesis which was negative. The patient was not malnourished by exam or by laboratory analysis. The patient was tolerating POs and was discharged with close PCP ___. The PCP had referred the patient to a mental health provider and we encouraged the patient to follow up with that provider. . #. Depression: We continued the patient on his home dose of sertraline and trazadone. He may benefit from the addition of remeron to help promote sleep and improve appetite. . #. Constipation: It was unclear what role this plays in patients poor PO intake. The patient was given a bowel regimen with fiber, colace and senna with resolution of his constipation. . #. Renal Failure, acute likely: The patient had a previously normal Cr several years ago. His Creatinine improved with IVF. Unimpressive Ua would argue for either pre-renal or post-renal etiology. The patient's U/A did show Proteinuria; we recommend that he continue ACE inhibitor for diabetic proteinuria . # Hypertension: On admission the patient had a hypertensive urgency requiring IV anti-hypertensive to control his BP. BP medications were confirmed by reviewing his medication bottles which his wife brought in. However, once his home medications/doppler. The patient was only mildly symptomatica and the were restarted the patient had a drop in his blood pressure to 70/doppler. This resolved with holding the patient's BP medications and IV fluids. The patient did not have any evidence of infection or other cause for his hypotension. The patient's terazosin dose was decreased, his lisinopril dose was decreased and his diltiazem was discontinued. The patient will require close blood pressure monitoring . #. Atrial Fibrillation: The patient was in sinus rhythm on admission. The patient states that he is s/p cardioversion and has not had to take coumadin since his cardioversion. We recommended that he discuss the utility of further anticoagulation with his PCP. . #. Diabetes: Diet controlled. His fingersticks ranged between 100-___ontrolled. . #. Hyperlipidemia: Continued patient's statin. . #. Gout: chronic. renally dose allopurinol & indomethacin PRN . #. BPH: continue home medications. . EMERGENCY CONTACT: Wife: ___: ___
summary: You were admitted for concern over a 50lb weight loss over the past couple of months. You were evaluated with lab tests, a gastric emptying study and a CT scans of your torso. The final report of the gastric emptying study is still pending but the preliminary report is negative for poor stomach motility (gastroparesis). We did not find any medical cause for your weight loss. You were not malnourished. You were tolerating food and fluid prior to discharge. We feel that your loss of appetite was most likely related to depression. We recommend that you see a psychiatrist as an outpatient. During this admission, you had an episode of low blood pressure so we held you blood pressure medications. We have restarted these medications at a lower dose.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ man with a history of HIV/AIDS who initially presented with active suicidal ideation, confusion, and 2 months of cachexia and 37-lb unintended weight loss. # Suicidal ideation and depression. Upon admission, he denied any past or current SI and denied any desire or plan to hurt himself. He was evaluated by Psychiatry team. He did not meet criteria for ___. 1:1 sitter was discontinued. His outpatient psychiatrist is Dr. ___ at ___. Continued on home citalopram, mirtazapine, and clonazepam. # HIV neurocognitive disorder. Patient was alert and oriented, but had poor insight into his cognitive deficits and overall functional decline. His PCP, ___, and ___ all reported waxing and waning periods of delirium, confusion, and decreased cognition from baseline. Ddx includes HIV neurocognitive disorder (most likely), CNS lymphoma, and delirium from malnutrition. Less likely dx include neurosyphilis, viral encephalitis (CMV titers low), PML, and CJD. Vitamin B12 and TSH were normal on last admission. He did have an LP with only 1 WBC. CSF labs including HSV, VZV, CMV, EBV, VDRL, and ___ virus are negative. Patient lacks insight into both his medical illness and home troubles. He has illogical thinking and is not able to rationalize or understand the consequences of his decision. He claims he gained 20 lbs from drinking Boost supplements in the past several weeks, but his weight does not reflect this. Discharge weight on ___ was 52.8 kg (116 lbs). 147 lbs on ___, 124 lbs on ___, and 110 lbs on ___. # HIV/AIDS. Atripla was stopped on ___ due to concern for resistance but genotype does not suggest NRTI/NNRTI resistance. Etiology of elevated HIV viral load is from medication noncompliance. Patient has had severe apathy (sacral ulcer, ignoring bills, fecal incontinence) and difficulties attending outpatient appointments even with a very high level of coordination of care. ___ HIV CD4 was ___k. ___ CD4 ___k. Patient was started on Truvada 1 tab daily, darunavir 800mg daily, ritonavir 100mg daily on ___. He was continued on Bactrim DS daily for PCP and ___ prophylaxis given his CD4<100. # Cachexia. He has lost 37 lbs over the past 3 months. No infection or malignancy found. Etiology may be related to progressive HIV disease, depression, and malnutrition. CT abdomen identified left ischiorectal fossa with left rectal wall thickening suggestive of abscess/fistula. He has not had anal sex in ___ yrs, and recent colonoscopy in ___ was normal (including rectum). Colorectal Surgery consulted. Since he was asymptomatic and this was not a new or concerning finding, they recommended no further intervention. Patient was continued on nutritional supplements. # Pancytopenia. Largely resolved since prior admission. DDx includes nutritional deficiencies versus viral bone marrow suppression. This was stable. No evidence of malignancy on exam or CT torso. # Sacral ulcer, stage 2. Wound care per Nursing protocol. # Code: Do resuscitate, do NOT intubate # CONTACT: ___ (___, ___, ___, pt states it is okay to discuss matters with him, ___ ### TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ### 1) Low phosphate intermittently throughout hospitalization. Discharged on Neutra Phos 2 packets QID. Likely due to malnutrition and refeeding syndrome. Will need close electrolyte monitoring to ensure within normal limits. Check daily for the next week. 2) Continue to work toward HIV housing or rest home 3) F/u with ID, PCP, and neuro as scheduled.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of confusion, failure to thrive, and expressing thoughts of harming yourself. You were stabilized with IV fluids. You received a workup for your continued weight loss and mental status changes. We have not been able to find an infection or malignancy to explain the malnutrition. You were found to have a rectal abscess or fistula and were evaluated by the Colorectal Surgery team. They recommended no intervention as it was not concerning and you were asymptomatic. You were started on new HIV medications. It is very important that you remain compliant with taking these 3 medications daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #. Delirium due to suicidal Ingestion of tranquilizers due to Suicidal Ideation: - Pt took a total of approximately 20mg of Xanax in a suicide attempt. Tox screen was positive for benzos and tricyclics. ___ was sommolent on arrival and was briefly hypotensive which responded to painful stimuli and IVF. Toxicolgy was consulted and did not reccomend further intervention. The patient was monitored in the MICU and remained stable. She was transfered to regular medical floor where VS remained stable, EKG was stable, no events on telemetry, Chem 7, LFTs, CBC normal. Currently medically stable s/p overdose; stable for transfer to psych bed. - Clonazepam for CIWA was continued given possible withdrawal per psych. - Patient continues to be actively suicidal, threatening to kill herself if allowed to leave. Per psychiatry consultation, needed inpatient psychiatry admission. - We continued a 1:1 sitter until transfer. - Further intensive treatment of depression is needed. # Schizoaffective, Histrionic Borderline Personality: - diagnoses as per inpatient pysch evaluation. - Further treatment defered in medical hospital setting. - She has a history of following with psychiatry in ___ and ___ and has been on Respiridal, Effexor, tegretol, xanax, trazadone, and prozac in the recent past. Medication regimen should be clearified at inpatient psych facility. She also appears to be getting medications from multiple sources. # Abnormal EKG: - Serial EKGs show NSR with isolated q wave in III, no TWI or ST changes. Qtc within normal limits. Poor R wave progression. - Changes are not accompanied with cardiac symptoms, VS stable, no events on telemtry. - Medically stable for pyschiatry transfer. - Follow up EKG by PCP in future is warrented. # Hypothyroidism: - Patient on levothyroxine as outpatient. TSH wnl in hosptial. #. Hyperglycemia: - Patient with no stated history of diabetes per daughter, however, has filled prescription for metformin in past. She says she has not needed metformin since her wt loss procedure in ___. HgbA1c within normal limits, fingersticks normal, no need for Insulin or metformin #. Code - Full code confirmed with daughter on admission #. ___ - daughter - ___ ___ ___ on Admission: alprazolam 0.5mg 1 tab BID - 60 tabs filled ___ fluoxetine 20mg 1 tab daily - 30 tabs filled ___ levothyroxine 137mcg 1 tab daily - 30 tabs filled ___ trazodone 50mg 1 tab daily - 30 tabs filled ___ metformin 750mg PO BID - 60 tabs filled ___ Effexor- from ___ Risperdone- from ___ Tegretol-from ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you attempted to hurt yourself by taking an large quantity of xanax. This was not safe and you were found to be sleep. You were admitted to the intensive care unit for close monitoring. You were evaluate by the medical service and felt to be medically cleared from injury. You were seen by psychiatry who felt that you were a threat to yourself and required additional treatment in an inpatient psychiatric facility. You are being transferred to the care of a psychiatry unit. You will no longer need to take Metformin. This is a medicine for diabetes and it was felt you do not have diabetes. Your Synthroid was continued for your hypothyroidism. You were given a nicotine patch to help alleviate your desire to smoke cigarettes. All of your other medications were stopped and you should discuss with your psychiatrist what medications to continue before going home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is an ___ year old Farsi-speaking female past medical history CAD, diastolic CHF, seronegative rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate and sulfasalazine, recent antibiotic treatment for pneumonia, admitted ___ with cough and reported fevers, imaging without signs of pneumonia but consistent with small airway disease with nebulizers and cough suppressants, clinically improving with treatment for acute post-infectious reactive airway disease, returning to baseline, breathing comfortably, able to be discharged to rehab # Hypoxemia / Acute Reactive Airway Disease - Patient with recent pneumonia treated at OSH, presenting with cough and intermittent hypoxia with exertion; workup included CXR without infiltrate, but exam with mild wheezing; CTA showed no evidence of pulmonary embolism, but did show small airway disease and chronic lung disease; this was felt to represent post-pneumonia acute reactive airway disease. Patient started on nebulizers without improvement to baseline. She was discharged on albuterol nebulizers, with recommendation for pulmonary followup. #Depression: Prior to admission, patient had been seen by outpatient psychiatrist, at which time patient was reported passive suicidal ideation; case discussed with outpatient psychiatrist, and inpatient psychiatry consult was requested. On their evaluation, they determined that she had no active suicidal ideation and did not pose an immediate threat to herself or others. They recommended close follow up with her outpatient psychiatrist. She was continued on her home mirtazapine. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # Chronic Diastolic CHF - Patient appeared euvolemic on exam, iwth no evidence of pleural effusions, pulmonary edema, or lower extremity edema, and with a stable BNP. She was continued on home lasix and metoprolol. #Rheumatoid Arthritis: Patient was continued on home sulfasalazine. She takes methotrexate once a week (on ___, did not require this medication during the hospital course. #Diabetes Type 2: She was placed on an insulin sliding scale. Her home gabapentin was continued. #Hypertension: She was continued on home amlodipine. #GERD: She was continued on home omeprazole. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== -Patient started on prn albuterol, tessalon perles, guaifenisin - On discharge from rehab patient would benefit from scheduled pulmonary clinic appointment at ___
summary: Ms. ___, You were admitted to ___ for worsening shortness of breath and low oxygen levels. During your stay you were started on oxygen therapy due to your low oxygen levels and received antibiotics due to concerns that your shortness of breath may be due to pneumonia. You underwent chest imaging including an xray and a ct scan, which showed no evidence of pneumonia, and you were taken off of antibiotics. Your cough and shortness of breath may be due to some airway irritation that can happen after a pneumonia. Your oxygen levels also improved with nebulizer treatments, and you were able to come off of oxygen therapy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Key Information for Outside Providers: Ms ___ is a ___ year old homeless female with past medical history of latent TB, HCV, asthma, and depression who was admitted ___ with alcohol intoxication, suicidal ideation, reported sexual assault, status post treatment for alcohol withdrawal, treatment for pneumonia, who was medically cleared and transferred for psychiatric inpatient stay. # Latent TB / Acute bacterial pneumonia - patient presented with cough and pulmonary infiltrates ___ the setting of noncompliance with TB eradication therapy x ___ weeks. She was ruled out for TB given her noncompliance, using 3 concentrated AFB smears. She improved on course of Cefpodoxime and Zithro, was without symptoms at time of discharge. INH restarted. # Alcohol Hepatitis / HCV - admitted with abnormal LFTs (ALT 52, AST 113, Alk Phos 116, normal Tbili). Thought to be secondary to mild alcoholic hepatitis, with potential contribution from underlying HCV. Resolved to ALT 36, AST 65, AlkPhos 85 by time of discharge. Given that she is on INH, would recommend rechecking LFTs monthly to ensure continued normalization. # Sexual Assault - Patient reported a sexual assault prior to admission. Patient was seen by social work. She received post-assault antibiotics and was placed on HIV post exposure prophylaxis (28 days duration). # Suicidal Ideation - Patient was seen by Psychiatry, was placed under ___ and transferred to inpatient Pysch facility once medically cleared. # Asthma - continued home inhalers TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== -Please assess suicidal ideations and need for alteration of psychiatric medication. Pt's current Psych meds, per inpatient Psych, include: ** Gabapentin 800 mg PO Q6H ** BuPROPion (Sustained Release) 150 mg PO BID) ** ChlorproMAZINE 50 mg po QAM, 100 mg po QHS ** Buprenorphine-Naloxone (8mg-2mg) 1 TAB SL BID (Confirmed with pharmacy) -Pt's home Straterra (Atomoxetine) was DC'd per Psych -Please continue Cefpodoxime 200mg BID to finish seven day course, last day ___ -please continue 28 day course of Truvada 1tab qd + Raltegravir 400mg BID (End day ___ for HIV Post-exposure prophylaxis -consider further workup of anemia as outpatient -recheck LFTs on ___ after INH restarted and monthly thereafter, to be followed by patient's PCP -___ referral to hepatology given diagnosis of HCV for consideration of treatment -will need repeat HIV Ab testing ___ six weeks and four months to confirm seronegative after finishing post-exposure PPX -continued on INH/Pyridoxine for latent TB to be continued for 9 months as patient was not previously taking consistently -started on fluticasone/albuterol for asthma -given multivitamin, thiamine, folic acid ___ EtOH use -given hydrocortisone cream for venous stasis changes -given nicotine patch for cigarette smoking # CONTACT: mother, ___, ___ # CODE: presumed Full, did not assess ___ setting of suicidal ideation
summary: You came to the hospital with thoughts of ending your life after being sexually assaulted. While you were ___ the emergency room the doctors ___ emergency contraception and medication to prevent sexually transmitted disease. You also had tests sent for HIV which returned negative. Your Hepatitis C test did not show active disease. You got a chest Xray that was concerning for pneumonia and Tuberculosis. Tests came back saying that you do not have active Tuberculosis. However ___ the past you have had a test that showed you may have Tuberculosis hidden ___ your body. Please continue to take you medication called Isoniazid to prevent an active infection of Tuberculosis. Please continue taking Cefpodoxime until ___ for your pneumonia. While you were ___ the hospital you had some swelling of your legs. It is most likely due to your veins not pumping the blood up. This should improve by walking throughout the day and wearing the stockngs provided to you ___ the hospital. We will also provide a prescription for cream that you can rub on your legs which will also improve the return of the blood.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old male with past medical history of alcohol abuse, complicated by prior withdrawal episodes, bicuspid aortic valve s/p bioprosthetic AVR, hypertension, depression, several recent admissions related to alcohol, as well as one admission for C difficie colitis, admitted ___ with epigastric pain felt to relate to alcoholic esophagogastritis, course complicated by alcohol withdrawal, treated medically with improving pain and resolving withdrawal, subsequently leaving against medical advice # Acute gastroesophagitis / Chest Pain - Patient with prior similar episodes in setting of alcohol abuse presenting with chest pain. Workup without signs of ACS or focal cardiac or pulmonary process, but CT did show findings concerning for acute esophagitis. Etiology was suspect to relate to alcohol consumption given his history, but infectious process such as ___ esophagitis could not be ruled out without endoscopy. Patient was treated with PPI and H2 blocker, as well as carafate. GI recommended EGD, but patient left against medical advice before this could be performed. Would consider completing as an outpatient. # Alcohol abuse / Complex alcohol withdrawal - Patient's course was complicated by alcohol withdrawal, initially requiring Valium 10mg every 2 hours, with subsequent improvement in symptoms. Continued thiamine/folate. During prior hospital stays a section 35 had been discussed, but patient requested discharge against medical advice on the weekend (courts not open) and demonstrated capacity to make the decision to leave (see daily progress note documentation from ___ for details). C. difficile diarrhea - Patient initially diagnosed ___ and was scheduled to complete a course of PO vancomycin ___, but reported noncompliance as an outpatient. On this admission, while he had loose stools he reported this was chronic and his baseline, did not have signs of acute infection, and Cdiff PCR was negative. Patient empirically finished abbreviated course of PO Vancomycin during his inpatient stay, completed on ___. Depression/Anxiety - While intoxicated, patient reported suicidal ideation, prompting initial placement of ___. On sober evaluation he no longer had SI, reported feeling ___ was discontinued. Conitnued mirtazapine Urinary retention - In setting of initial presentation and intoxication, patient was found to be retaining large amount of urine, prompting initial foley catheter placement and initiation of Flomax. Patient subsequently able to pass trial of void. Continued on flomax at discharge. Would consider additional workup. # Asymptomatic CBD dilatation - Admission CT showed "Mildly dilated common bile duct and proximal main pancreatic duct, new from the prior exam. No obstructing mass is identified." On above evaluation by GI service, they recommended endoscopic ultrasound to better evaluate. Patient left before this could be completed. He was provided contact information to schedule appointment with GI. Would consider outpatient GI referral to follow this up. # Hypertension / Bioprosthetic AVR - Continued ASA, cardevilol, lasix, lisinopril (all per medication reconciliation with patient and his ___ pharmacy) # Peripheral Neuropathy - continued gabapentin Transitional Issues - FYI: Left against medical advice - Had urinary retention during this admission, prompting initiation of Flomax; would consider further workup as outpatient - Was recommended for EGD for workup of esophagogastritis, but left against medical advice; would consider as outpatient - CT showed "Mildly dilated common bile duct and proximal main pancreatic duct, new from the prior exam. No obstructing mass is identified." Recommended for endoscopic ultrasound by GI, but patient left against medical advice before it could be performed; would consider as outpatient
summary: You were admitted with chest pain. You underwent testing that was suggestive this pain was related to irritation in your stomach and esophagus. This is most likely from alcohol, but could also be from other causes. We recommended an endoscopy to find out more, but you opted to leave the hospital against medical advice. During your time in the hospital you also had alcohol withdrawal. You were treated with medications and improved. It is very important that you abstain from future alcohol use. You had a CT scan that showed mild enlargement of your biliary ducts. You were seen by GI specialists who recommended a procedure called an endoscopic ultrasound to investigate this further. Although you are leaving the hospital against our advice, we recommend you make a gastroenterology appointment. To make an appointment at ___, please call ___. During your hospital stay you also had difficulty emptying your bladder. You were started on a new medication to help with this. We recommend speaking with your primary care doctor further about this.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #. Acute Hepatitis: Patient with ALT/AST in 6000s on admission and no underlying liver disease. Her liver synthetic function was intact and although initially seemed to be mildly confused on arrival had no asterixis, no waxing and waning mental status and was not felt to be encephalopathic. A work up for viral hepatitis (Hep A, B, C, EBV, CMV, HIV), and ischemic hepatitis (Budd Chiari) was negative. Shock liver was felt to be likely given the rapid improvement in LFTs though no hypotension was noted while in the ICU. Tylenol level was low on arrival but NAC was given empirically as it was felt to cause little possible harm. Toxin mediated (either one of the medications she ingested or something mixed in with the cocaine) was also felt to be likely contributing given her concominant ATN and skin findings. She was managed supportively and her LFTs trended down but not to normalization prior to discharge. Her statin was held at discharge, with plan for PCP follow up to help check LFTs to decide when this should be restarted. . #. ATN: Creatinine rose from 4 to 5.1 on the day of admission. Urine lytes suggested a prerenal etiology and patient was treated with IVF. Renal was consulted and spun his urine which revealed muddy brown casts suggestive of ATN. Paient was aggressively hydrated with bolus fluids until her urine output was >30cc/hr. Her creatinine continued to rise to 7.7, but urine output improved tremendously without further boluses being required. Cr trended down to 4.8 prior to discharge. . #. Bullous erythematous lesions: Patient presented with several patches of well demarcated bullous lesions on an erythematous base (on her elbow, her hip, her knee and her right cheek). Patient had no lesions on her mucous membranes. DDX considered included cocaine-related lesion versus trauma. Patient was given one dose of Vancomycin and Keflex on arrival but this was discontinued as it was felt unlikely to be infectious. Dermatology was consulted and felt her lesions were most consistent with trauma. A skin biopsy from one of her fingers was done and was still pending prior to discharge. She has dermatology followup for these results early next week. . #. Depression: Patient with a history of depression and a toxic ingestion which was concerning for suicidal ideation. However the patient denied any SI on multiple occasions. She was continued on her home sertraline. . #. HTN: Patient low/normotensive on arrival and her antihypertensives were held. They were restarted and she was discharged on metoprolol 50 mg BID. She has PCP followup the day after discharge so that beta blocker can be adjusted as necessary. . #. GERD: She was continued on PPI. . # UTI: Patient had positive UA on ___, and was empirically started on Cipro. Urine culture was negative, but patient was discharged on Cipro to complete a 7 day course.
summary: You were transferred to the ___ from ___ with liver and kidney failure. You were monitored and treated in the intensive care unit and seen by the team of kidney doctors. Your condition improved and you were transferred to the general medical wards. You were also treated for a urinary tract infection. You were also seen by dermatologists for the blisters on your skin- they felt these lesions were likely related to trauma when you passed out. We recommend that you stop using cocaine, alcohol, opioids and other drugs to avoid risking your life and causing further damage to your liver and kidneys. It is eseential that you not use any of these substances.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #GI bleed: Patient did not have an episode of BRBPR while hospitalized. His HCT remained stable throughout the hospitalization and trended up from 33 to 36. He underwent an EGD which showed mucosal irritation consistent with gastritis. His colonscopy showed small internal hemorrhoids and diverticulosis. He was subsequently started on omeprazole for gastritis and a high fiber diet to help with internal hemorrhoids. - repeat colonoscopy was recommended in ___ years. . #Depression/Suicidal Ideation: Patient had specific thoughts of walking into oncoming traffic during the week prior to admission and continued to express suicidality during the hospitalization. He had 1:1 sitter throughout the time on the floor and was continued on his home psychiatric medication of depakote and citalopram. Psychiatry was consulted and recommended inpatient psychiatric care after medical issues have been stabilized. At the time of discharge, his medical issues were resolved and he is to be discharged to an inpatient psychiatric facility. . #Bipolar Disorder: Patient had depressive symptoms but denies suicidal ideation. He was continued on depakote and citalopram as above. He is to be discharged to an inpatient psychiatric facility as above. . #Alcohol Abuse: Patient had no history of delirium tremens in the past. His last drink was on ___. While hospitalized he was monitored on a CIWA scale and did not have signs or symptoms of withdrawal. . #Hypertension: His blood pressure was well controlled throughout the hospitalization; he was continued on his home dose of lisinopril. . #Hyperlipidemia: He was continued on home regimen of fenofibrate. . #Diet: Regular initially; his diet was changed to high-fiber after the finding on colonoscopy. . #Diet: High-Fiber Diet . #Code: Full
summary: You came to the hospital because you were not feeling well due to bleeding from your lower GI tract and excessive alcohol consumption. You also had suicidal thoughts during the week prior to hospitalization. During your hospitalization, you underwent an endoscopy which showed gastritis (stomach irritation), internal hemorrhoids and diverticulosis (outpouching of colon that can also bleed sometimes). You were started on a medicine called omeprazole (prilosec) to treat gastritis. You initially had a productive cough during the hospitalization. An X-ray of your lungs showed no infection. Your cough resolved on its own during your stay. We think the cause of your cough is smoking.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man, with a PMHx of alcoholic hepatitis, with severe early life trauma and subsequent debilitating PTSD, EtOH use disorder with hx of withdrawal seizures, depression with prior suicide attempts, multiple psychiatric hospitalizations, who presented intoxicated to the ___ on ___ with suicidal ideation with established plan to slash his neck. He was found unconscious on the street and initially admitted to the psychiatry service for alcohol withdrawal. He was treated with chlordiazepoxide, however hospital course was complicated by worsening altered mental status and obtundation requiring brief MICU admission for airway monitoring. He did not require an advanced airway and was further monitored on the general medicine floor. He was treated with benzodiazepines for further alcohol withdrawal. He was stable and then discharged back to ___ 4. ACUTE MEDICAL ISSUES ======================= #Alcohol Use Disorder #Alcohol Withdrawal - Patient has chronic history of alcohol abuse complicated by withdrawal, typically drinks 1 gallon of vodka daily. He has a history of withdrawals requiring hospitalization and withdrawal seizures. He presented with an alcohol level of 231 and was admitted to the psychiatry service due to suicidal ideation and acute withdrawal. There he received high dose chlordiazepoxide. He was ultimately transferred to the ICU for obtundation and airway monitoring that was felt to potentially be related to combination of chlordiazepoxide and other sedating medications. While on the general medicine floor, he was treated with CIWA protocol with lorazepam. He was also given thiamine, folate, and multivatiman, and continued on his home naltrexone. He will continue to be treated for alcohol withdrawal while on Deaconess 4. #Altered Mental Status - His hospital course while on Deaconess 4 was complicated by altered mental status and subsequent obtundation only arousable to sternal rub. He was transferred to the ICU for airway monitoring however ultimately did not require an advanced airway. The etiology is likely secondary to a combination of sedating medications given his home medications of Haldol, Seroquel, and Mirtazapine as well as the chlordiazepoxide used for alcohol withdrawal prevention. Workup did not identify an infectious or other toxic-metabolic etiology. There was a low concern for Wernicke's as memory appeared intact, low concern for cranial bleed, low concern for DTs given his relative hemodynamic stability. Haldol, Seroquel, and Mirtazapine were thus held. Patient's mental status improved over the next ___ hours to his baseline. #Suicidal Ideation #Depression - He presented to the ___ with active SI with a concrete plan, at high risk. He has a history of multiple suicide attempts. He was placed on a 1:1 sitter. His citalopram was continued. Psych has been following him since admission, and he will be discharged to Deac 4 for further care. CHRONIC ISSUES #HTN - Not on any medications at this time, BP in normal limits. #HLD - We continued his atorvastatin. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES [ ] NEW/CHANGED MEDCAITIONS - Held haloperidol 10mg PO BID prior to deaconess 4 transfer given sedation - Held mirtazapine 15mg PO QHS prior to deaconess 4 transfer given sedation - Started thiamine 200mg QD continued from prior transfer - Started folic acid 1mg daily continued from prior transfer - Started multivitamin 1mg daily continued from prior transfer - Continued lorazepam 1mg PO Q4H:PRN for alcohol withdrawal to be managed by psychiatry team [ ] Please continue to work with case management/social work about his homelessness and current living situation (e.g. shelters to transition to at discharge).
summary: You were brought to the hospital after you were found unconscious on the street after consuming alcohol. You were admitted to the hospital because you had ongoing thoughts of killing yourself with a distinct plan. You wanted inpatient treatment so that you would not harm yourself. You were transferred to the internal medicine services because of another episode of unconsciousness after receiving treatment for alcohol withdrawal We monitored your feelings of depression and desire to harm yourself due to concern for your safety. We treated your alcohol withdrawal to prevent you from having seizures or feeling unwell.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ woman with history of CKD, anxiety, overactive bladder, cervical myelopathy, osteoporosis, and recently diagnosed depression in the setting of her husband's death, who presents with severe lower urinary tract symptoms impairing sleep and difficulty coping at home in the setting of her depression. #Overactive Bladder Patient reports over one year of symptoms, for which she was recently evaluated by urology. The symptoms of frequency and urgency without objective evidence of significant retention are consistent with OAB. Medical therapy was not started at her recent urology appointment, and cystoscopy was being considered as an outpatient. However her symptoms have worsened to the point where she felt unable to go on managing them at home. I discussed the case informally with urology and decided to trial trospium, given her debilitating symptoms. However this was not available on inpatient formulary, so patient will pick up at outpatient pharmacy and trial as outpatient. Also of note, the patient was started on mirtazapine (see below) and slept much better on the night prior to discharge. She has outpatient urology scheduled in 4 weeks, which may be rescheduled to an earlier date should her symptoms fail to improve. #Major depressive disorder / complicated grief Patient endorsing weeks/months of sadness, anhedonia, altered sleep, eating, concentration, and other symptoms since her husband passed away in ___. Not actively suicidal. She was started on paxil 6 days ago, although has only been able to tolerate 5 mg daily due to GI side effects. Evaluated by psychiatry and started on mirtazapine 7.5 mg HS for now. She seemed to tolerate this well and reported improvement in her sleep and bothersome overnight GU symptoms after starting this. She has psychiatry follow-up scheduled and can also be considered for an inpatient geripsych admission if her symptoms fail to improve. She was also continued on her home valium. She continued supplements that she has been taking due to worsened appetite in the setting of her depression. #Mild hypercalcemia / suspected primary hyperparathyroidism Calcium mildly elevated since ___ (10.4-11). It was felt to be unlikely that this was playing a role in her symptoms given the mild elevation. PTH was high-normal at 51, and vitamin D elevated at 154. Vitamin D and calcium were previously recommended by endocrinology for her osteoporosis, but she has only been taking the vitamin D recently. She was advised to stop the vitamin D for now and follow-up with her PCP and likely endocrine for further management. #HTN Continued amlodipine 5 #CKD - stable near baseline #Mild leukocytosis: 10.2->9.4->12.1 during admission. She appears to have chronically high-normal/mildly elevated WBC. Her UA was not suggestive of infection and she lacked other concerning focal symptoms, so infection felt to be unlikely. (especially since symptoms improved somewhat prior to discharge) #Urine culture with diptheroids Suspected to be contaminant, particularly given unremarkable UA Patient offered home services but declined. ========================================== ========================================== TRANSITIONAL/FOLLOW-UP ISSUES: - assess response to trospium and for any side effects; patient has follow-up with urology on ___, but may consider expediting this if symptoms remains severe - assess response to mirtazapine; patient has follow-up with psych; if depression symptoms remain severe she could be considered for voluntary geripsych admission -recheck calcium as outpatient and consider endocrine referral for management of primary hyperparathyroidism and concomitant osteoporosis ========================================== ========================================== >30 minutes in patient care and coordination of discharge
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of worsening bladder symptoms. This is most likely due to a condition called Overactive Bladder (OAB). We spoke with the urology team, who recommended a trial of a medication called Trospium (also called Sanctura), which you can take once nightly for now and see how your symptoms respond. They will look into getting you an earlier appointment if possible, although if the medication helps your symptoms, then it may be preferable to stick with your original appointment with Dr. ___. You were also evaluated by our psychiatry team for your ongoing depression since your husband passed away. In addition to the paxil that you have been taking, they also recommended a medication called mirtazapine (also called remeron), which we think will also help with your recovery. As we discussed, you are scheduled to see a psychiatrist soon. If these therapies are insufficient then you can discuss with your psychiatrist whether an inpatient geriatric psychiatry program could be beneficial. We also discovered that your calcium levels are slightly high, which is something to discuss further with Dr. ___ possibly also an endocrinologist. This is most likely due to elevated levels of "parathyroid hormone". For now we recommend that you stop taking vitamin D, since this can potentially worsen the calcium levels, at least until you discuss this issue further with your outpatient doctors.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PRIMARY REASON FOR ADMISSION ___ yo male with significant CAD history s/p 4 stents who presents with chest pain in the setting of heroine/alcohol withdrawal. . ACTIVE ISSUES . # Chest pain- Patient noted to have central non-radiating chest pain while in ED observation. Patient has a long complex coronary artery disease history with several stents in the past. His pain was not felt to represent acute coronary syndrome. EKG showed Q waves in V1-V4 consistent with a previous anterior MI that was unchanged from previous. Patient was ruled out for an MI with troponins that were negative x 3. The patients pain was felt to be atypical in nature and most likely related to withdrawal from alcohol and heroine. However it was felt that there may be a component of anginal pain in the setting of hypertension. Therefore he was started on Imdur 30 mg daily. The patient ultimately decided to leave against medical advice and was advised to make an appointment to ___ with his primary care physician. . # Withdrawal- Patient was noted to be intoxicated on admission with an EtOH level of 355. Per the patient report he had been on a 5 day binge of several quarts of vodka, heroine and cocaine previous to being brought to the hospital by EMS. His serum toxic screen was positive for ethanol as above and urine toxicology screen was notably positive for opioids but negative for cocaine. The patient was started on a CIWA scale with 10 mg Valium every 2 hours. He was noted to have significant withdrawal symptoms most notably tremors and diarrhea but denied any hallucinations or suicidal or homicidal ideation. The patient was also hypertensive with SBP in the 150s and tachycardic with HR to 106. Symptoms persisted however the patient ultimately decided to leave against medical advice. Patient was informed of and expressed understanding of the risks of leaving while in active withdrawal including seizure, cardiac disease, and death. He was seen by social work who provided him with information regarding rehab facilities two of which may have beds available tomorrow. The patient was encouraged to call these facilities and reports he will make theses calls tomorrow. . # Hypertension- Patient noted to have elevated blood pressure on admission. Per the patients report he had not been taking his home anti-hypertensives for the past several days. The patient was restarted on his home lisinopril. His metoprolol was held due to concern for recent cocaine use. He should ___ with his PCP regarding restarting this medication. He additionally was started on Imdur 30 mg daily. . STABLE ISSUES . # Coronary Artery Disease- As above the patient had not been compliant with his home regimen. He was restarted on his home ASA 325 mg, Plavix 75 mg, pravastatin 40 mg and lisinopril 10 mg. As above his home metoprolol was held during this admission and at the time of discharge. . # Hyperlipidemia- patient was continued on his home pravastatin. . # Anxiety and Depression- Patient was continued on his citalopram. His clonazepam was held while he was getting Valium. He was continued on his home clonazepam at the time of discharge. As above the patient was denying any homicidal or suicidal ideation currently or in the past. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Patient should call rehabilitation facility tomorrow - Patient should call his PCP to make an appointment on ___ - Patient remained full code throughout this hospitalization
summary: As you know you were admitted with Chest pain. There was initial concern that this was due to your heart however blood tests and an EKG showed that you were not having a heart attack. The pain you are having is likely related to withdrawing from alcohol, heroine and cocaine. You have decided to leave against medical advice. You were informed of the risks of leaving while you are still having withdrawl symptoms including but not limited to problems with your heart, seizures, hallucinations and death. You have expressed understanding of these risks and by leaving you accept these risks. You were provided information about rehab facilities. We strongly recommend that you call these facilities tomorrow.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ yo woman with ___ CAD s/p IMI complicated by cardiogenic shock and subsequent 4 vessel CABG in ___, PVD, CKD, poorly controlled HLP (LDL in 200s), poorly controlled DM (last HgbA1c 11.8 in ___ who presents with chest pressure on exertion and at rest x 3 days. Pt ruled out for ACS but was in acute on chronic systolic HF responding to diuresis, however renal function worsened. Patient was diuresed an effective 3 liters with a decrease in Cr. Patient was discharged on a decreased dose of PO lasix and with intensive follow up. . #. CAD - Pt with negative enzymes x 3, negative serial EKGs, and PMIBI without reversible ischemia. Pt recently complaining of occasional CP with normal EKGs and self-limited and admits pain is similar to her GERD - unlikely to be cardiac. Lisinopril was held in light of acute renal failure. Patient was kept on aspirin baby dose, tricor, crestor, and sublingual nitroglycerin as needed. . #. Pump Acute on chronic systolic CHF. Pt's peripheral edema and JVD significantly improved. Patient had a prior echo with an EF of 35%, team was initially holding lasix due to aggressive diuresis -- however, patient was given 80mg PO lasix day prior to admission (equivalent to home dose) and diuresed over 3 liters with no subsequent Cr bump. Patient was also started on carvedilol in light of decreased EF. Patient was set up with heart failure follow up in Dr. ___. . #. Acute on chronic renal failure - Patient's ARF resolved after fluid hydration and then had no bump in serum creatinine after administration of home dose of PO Lasix. Patient was diuresed a net 3 liters, was sent home on a lower dose of PO lasix than originally prescribed (60mg), and was sent home with appropriate follow up and a serum Cr measurement in 3 days. Lisinopril was held during this time due to ARF -- it will be restarted at outpatient primary care provider/cardiologist discretion. . #. Poorly controlled DM - Blood glucoses were poorly controlled during stay, with occassional AM ___ in the low 60's and up in the high 100's during the day. ___ was consulted, and after titrating lantus and humalog scale, patient was D/C'ed on 52 units of lantus at breakfast and bedtime with a tailored humalog insulin sliding scale. Patient was discharged with a follow up appointment in the ___. . #. Hypothyroidism: Continued Levoxyl 75mcg PO Daily . #. Dysphagia: Question of unequal action of the vocal chords. Pt was scheduled for outpatient ENT follow-up . #. PE/DVT Prophylaxis - supratherapeutic INR to 4.0, warfarin was held, came down to 3.3 at discharge, patient had warfarin restarted with a coagulation studies to be checked 3 days after discharged and faxed to ___ clinic. Patient also scheduled with follow up in ___ clinic. . #. Mood/Depression - Pt showed depressed mood and unmotivated for managing her multiple health problems. citalopram was initially started but discontinued due to complains of anxiety and restlessness. The patient was to discuss with her PCP about starting another antidepressant.
summary: You have been diagnosed with acute decompensated heart failure. Your heart failure is the most likely reason for your weight gain and ankle swelling. We have caused you to lose water by providing you with furosemide or "water pills", which have caused you to lose over 10 pounds of water weight. The water pill is called Lasix (also known as Furosemide) -- we are starting you on 60 mg per day. You will need to You also came in complaining of chest pain. After undergoing EKG exams, checking for blood tests, and repeating a nuclear stress test, we have concluded that you have not had a heart attack. Your diabetes, high blood pressure, and high cholesterol were all poorly controlled. We have controlled your diabetes by significantly increasing your insulin dosages and having the ___ diabetes team provide us with their expertise. We have controlled your blood pressure by adding a stronger regimen including starting you on a drug called Carvedilol (Coreg). Finally, we have improved your high cholesterol by starting you on a drug called Crestor.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with h/o depression with SI, anxiety, HLD, HTN, HCV and other medical conditions was transferred from psychiatry for recent chest pain on ___ and transferred back to psychiatry on ___. 1. Atypical chest pain. Patient saw his PCP ___ couple days prior to admission to ___ for depression and suicidal ideation. At that time, he was noted to be tachycardic and hypertensive, but did not complain of chest pain. EKG there showed lateral Q waves, early R wave transition and slight ST depression in V2-5 with TWI in aVL. He was admitted to psychiatry for evaluation of depression and suicidal ideation instead after first set of cardiac enzymes being negative. However, the following day, he complained of chest pain while on psychiatry floor. Cardiac enzymes were stable; 0.06 on admission to 0.03 the next day which were in the setting of increasing/ elevated creatinine. It was noted that ST depressions persisted with and without chest pain. Cardiac catheterization was considered, but called off due to concerns that he would not be compliant with aspirin and plavix if intervention were to be performed given psychiatric issues. He was started on a heparin drip on the floor which was discontinued when ACS was ruled out. He continued on aspirin, beta blocker, statin, CCB, and ___. Instead he underwent a nuclear stress on ___ that showed no concerning findings. Since arriving to the cardiology service, he did not complain of chest pain, shortness of breath, or palpitations. He was medically cleared to return to psychiatry on ___. He should have his lipid panel followed up by his PCP as his LDL was elevated to 149. HgbA1c was at goal at 5.5. 2. Depression and anxiety with suicidal ideation. Pt was initially admitted to psych for suicideal ideation. He was transferred to medicine for evaluation of chest pain and returned to psych when he was medically cleared. He had a 1:1 sitter for suicideal ideation. Initially on the cardiology floor, he initially continued on his fluoxetine, gabapentin, and quetiapine. However, after consulting with psych, quetiapine was discontinued due to anticholinergic effects. Pt was given trazodone qhs as needed for sleep. After he was medically cleared, he was discharged back to psychiatry for further evaluation of depression and anxiety with suicidal ideation. 3. CKD, chronic. Stage 3. On admission, Cr was 1.7. It is unclear what is baseline is as he fluctuates greatly between 1 and 3. ___ last Crt in ___ was 1.4. It does not appear to be pre-renal based on urine electrolytes. His renal function was monitored during his stay, and it was 1.8 upon discharge to psychiatry. He should follow up with his primary care physician regarding his chronic CKD. 4. HTN. His SBP remained within acceptable range while on home medication. He will need to be followed up by his primary care physician after discharge from the hospital for re-assessment 5. HLD. His lipid profile showed elevated total cholesterol, TGA, and LDL. He was kept on home dose of pravastsatin 80 mg. He should have his lipid profiled followed up by his PCP again after he is discharged from the hospital. 6. Alcohol abuse. Patient reports heavy drinking, up to 1 pint of Vodka. He was admitted with CIWA scale prn diazepam as well as standing diazepam, folate, and thiamine. He did not show signs of withdrawal during his stay. Given his stability, the prn diazepam was discontinued, and plan was to gradually taper him off of diazepam. This should continued to be followed by the psychiatry team. 7. Tobacco abuse. Patient reports decreasing tobacco use prior to admission. He was given nicotine replacement therapy during his stay on cardiology. This should continue to be addressed by the psychiatry team.
summary: You were transferred from psychiatry to the medicine floor at ___ on ___ because you developed chest pain. Your lab work did not show injury in your heart muscle, and your EKG did not change significantly without the chest pain. Your chest pain appeared to be somewhat atypical for coronary artery disease. However, you received an stress test to evaluate the function of your heart during stress. It did not show any concerning findings. It is thought that you are medically stable to return to psychiatry, so that the management of your mood can be fully evaluated and treated. Please note the changes in your medication since your discharge from psychiatry on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ y/o male with a history of depression, anxiety, and PTSD who presented with increasing abdominal distension for 1.5 months found to have new onset ascites, elevated LFTs, hyperbilirubinemia, elevated INR, and hyponatremia. # Alcoholic Hepatitis: Patient with new onset ascites in the setting of significant alcohol use likely representing new onset cirrohsis. His AST/ALT ratio was consistent with alcoholic liver disease. He also initially presented with abdominal pain. Dianostic paracentesis done in the ED showed no evidence of SBP. The hepatology team was consulted and he had therapeutic paracentesis performed twice with resolution of his abdominal pain and SOB. First therapeutic para with 6.25 L taken off and 50g of albumin given. Second therapeutic para ___ guided and with 1.5L taken off. His AFP was negative. He was started on lasix and spironolactone for diuresis. HBsAb and HBcAb were positive, indicating previous but cleared HBV infection, and HAV IgG was positive as well. HCV antibody was negative. HIV negative. Thiamine, folate, and a multivitamin were started and a low sodium diet intiated. # Asymptomatic Hyponatremia: His sodium was 125 on admission and improved to 130 with fluid restriction. His hyponatremia is likely due to hypervolemic hyponatremia in the setting of liver disease. He was educated about continued fluid restriction at home. # Alcohol Abuse: He has a significant history of alcohol abuse and 'shakes' with withdrawal. No history of DT. The patient was placed on the CIWA protocol and required diazepam one time for CIWA of 10. Social work was consulted and was given a list of AA locations and discussed a plan to stay sober and attend AA meetings. # UTI: He presented with an elevated WBC count, dysuria, and urine cultures growing pansensitive E. coli. He also had blood cultures drawn, which were not finalized, but showed no growth to date. He was started on ciprofloxacin for his UTI and his last day of treatment will be ___ to complete a total 7 day course. # Nutrition: He presented initially with albumin of 2.9 and several risk factors for nutritional deficiency given his alcohol use and liver disease. Nutrition was consulted and he was started on ensure 4x daily along with thiamine, folate, and a multivitamin. He was given information about what to buy and what to avoid with a low-sodium diet. # Constipation: He had constipation on arrival present for the past 1.5 months. He was started on standing senna/colace and PRN miralax/lactulose and his bowel movements became more regular. # Hemorrhoids: He had some blood-streaked stools and was found to have non-thrombosed external hemorrhoids on exam. He was started on the bowel regimen as above and will follow-up with GI surgery as an outpatient for possible banding. # Depression: He has a history of depression and was not on medication when admitted. He was seen by social work and started on citalopram. His mood was stable over the course of his stay. Transitional issues: - Ciprofloxacin started for UTI - last day ___ - Started on a number of new medications at this visit - assess ability to adhere to complicated new treatment regimen (started: furosemide, spironolactone, folate, thiamine, multivitamin, senna/colace, citalopram) - Given information about AA groups and says he will follow-up - Found to have external hemorrhoids on exam - has follow-up with GI surgery as an outpatient - Started on citalopram due to depressed mood and history of depression - consider if it needed and/or tolerated -Blood culture with NGTD, final pending -peritoneal fluid culture from ___ pending at discharge
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having abdominal pain and distension of your belly. We found that you have liver disease due to alcohol use and fluid in your belly causing the pain. While you were here we took fluid off of your belly and you felt much better. We also started you on medications to help keep the fluid off called lasix and spironolactone and started you on vitamins. It will be very important for you to stop drinking alcohol. We also found that you have hemorrhoids while you are here. This is causing the blood you see when you go to the bathroom. We started you on medications to help move your bowels.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M ___ HCV cirrhosis (c/b portal hypertension), depression, Alcohol abuse, HTN, ___ syndrome (demyelination of Corpus callosum associated with alcohol use), recent ___, who was referred to ___ with abnormal labs found to have ___ and complicated cystitis. #COMPLICATED CYSTITIS: Patient visited PCP ___ ___ and labs were checked. UCx from this visisted revealed E.coli, susceptible to ceftazidime. Given abnormal labs and positive u/a, pt was sent toe ED for evaluation. Pt reported urinary urgency. Exam was not concerning for prostatitis or pyelonephritis. Due to possibility of pyelonephritis and obstruction, renal ultrasound was obtained and was normal with no obstruction or hydronephrosis. He received Zosyn in the ED and was switched to Ceftazidime IV to complete 10 day course (last day ___ for complicated UTI. His WBC downtrended from 12.0 to 9.3. Urinary sx improved. ___: Pt presented with ___, with creatinine of 1.6 (baseline <1.0). The patient reported nausea/vomiting and poor PO intake for many days prior to admission. Etiology of his ___ was thought to be prerenal (especially given his hypovolemia and hyponatremia). ___ resolved with intravenous fluids and albumin 75gm x2. #Hyponatremia: Likely hypoosmolar hypovolemic due to pre renal/dehydration. Normalized after albumin and fluid. #Depression: Pt is debilitated by chronic depression and anhedonia. At home, he has trouble sleeping and reports he has not left his room for weeks. He was initiated on sertraline 50 mg daily. CHRONIC PROBLEMS: #EtOH/HCV Cirrhosis: History of complications with encephalopathy and non-occlusion PVT but no history of varices. MELD-Na 29 on admission. MELD-Na 19 on ___. Hep C viral load undetectable. -Continued lactulose, thiamine, magnesium -Continued Ledipasvir/Sofosbuvir for HCV #Anemia: Hgb at baseline. Previous iron studies reveal ACD. No active signs of blood loss. #Coagulopathy: Most likely related to liver disease. No active signs of blood loss. #HTN: Continued amlodipine **Transitional issues** - Will require outpatient screening EGD/colonoscopy after discharge (scheduled with Dr. ___. - Needs Harvoni delivered to ___ because ___ does not have - He should have repeat labs in 1 week (CBC, BMP, ___ at his follow up appointment on ___ NEW MEDICATIONS: Ceftazidime 500 mg IV every 12 hours x10 total days (day ___, last day ___ Sertraline 50 mg daily
summary: You came to the hospital with abdominal pain and abnormal labs measured at your primary care visit. While you were in the hospital, you were found to have a urinary tract infection and decreased kidney function. Your infection was treated with intravenous antibiotics. Ancatheter was placed in your left arm so that you can finish your antibiotics when you leave the hospital. Your kidney function improved with intravenous fluids and albumin. You were also started on a new medication for your depression. When you leave the hospital, you will go to ___ where you will complete your antibiotic course for urinary tract infection (Ceftazidime 500 mg IV every 12 hours, LAST DAY ___. You will also continue taking sertraline for your depression.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M w/EtOH cirrhosis (Childs C) c/b ascites, hepatic encephalopathy, esophageal varices who presented to liver clinic with volume overload likely in the setting of medication non-adherence and dietary indiscretion s/p paracentesis, restarted on diuretics with improvement in overload. # Volume overload, ascites, weight gain: On presentation, patient was noted to have a significantly distended abdomen and 3+ lower extremity edema. Per patient, he has not had a paracentesis "in some time" and has not been compliant with coming to appointments or taking his medications because he "knew I'd be hospitalized." In addition, patient reported he was not adhering to a low sodium diet and has been actively drinking approx. 3 beers/day. In the ED, he underwent a diagnostic paracentesis that was negative for SBP. Infectious work up including blood culture, urine culture, and CXR were negative. On the floor, he underwent therapeutic paracentesis w/6L removed. He received aggressive diuresis on 40mg IV Lasix qd and spironolactone 200mg qd. Given good response to diuretics, he was transitioned to 80mg po Lasix and spironolactone 200mg. He will continue on these medications as an outpatient and was counseled by Nutrition about appropriate diet. # Anemia: Unclear baseline H/H given recent hospitalizations with low levels from bleeding. His PMHx was notable for GIB with imaging c/f 4 cords of small, grade I, varices and colonic ulcer, polyps, external hemorrhoids. On presentation, patient denied melena, hematemesis, BRBPR. MCV >100 c/f EtOH/malnutritional component. Rectal exam with brown stool, guiaic negative. Anemia work-up was initiated and notable for low transferrin, low TIBC, normal iron, high ferritin c/w anemia of chronic disease, likely due to his liver disease. In addition, there is plan for patient to undergo band ligation of his esophageal varices given inability to be on nadolol, concerning for oozing. He received 1u PRBC during admission. He was continued on a MVI, thiamine, and folate. He will follow up with Hepatology with plan for EGD. # EtOH Cirrhosis: Childs C. Originally presented in ___ w/EtOH hepatitis s/p prednisolone which was discontinued in the setting of PNA. Cirrhosis has been complicated by hepatic encephalopathy, esophageal varices, and HRS. During this admission, he was continued on lactulose/rifaximin. He was restarted on diuresis. His nadolol was no restarted. Given his ongoing EtOH use, he was determined to not a transplant candidate at this time. He was seen by Social Work and referred to a relapse prevention program. He will follow up with Hepatology as an outpatient. # EtOH abuse: Patient reported that his last drink was x1 day prior to admission; he has been having approx. 3 beers/day for the past month. Per patient, he has a history of withdrawal in the past. He was monitored on the CIWA scale; he did not score and did not require medical intervention. He was continued on multivitamin, thiamine, and folate. He was seen by Social Work and referred to a relapse prevention program. # Social stressors/ depression: Patient reported a history of depression and has been on sertraline, but admitted medication non-compliance on admission. In addition, he noted increasingly depressed mood in the setting of a number of social stressors. His sertraline was restarted, and he was seen by Social Work as above. #Malnutrition: albumin 2.6, pt reports poor intake at home along with non-compliance to a low sodium diet. He was evaluated by Nutrition, who assisted the patient in understanding how to monitor his sodium intake. Transitional Issues: [] Discharge weight = 131.6kg (___) [] D/c on Lasix 80mg/spironolactone 200mg [] Plan for CBC, CHEM-10, LFTs on ___, will fax to ___ ___ [] Continue in relapse prevention monitoring program [] Please follow up pending blood cultures, anaerobic peritoneal fluid culture Code status: FULL Contact: ___ (girlfriend) ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ due to significant volume overload and ascites in the setting of your known alcoholic cirrhosis. You received a diagnostic paracentesis on ___ which was negative for infection. You received a therapeutic paracentesis with 6L of fluid removed on ___. While in the hospital you received intravenous diuretics to help improve the swelling in your legs and abdomen. You should continue taking oral diuretics at home; Lasix 80mg every morning and spironolactone 200mg every morning. In addition, to prevent confusion from your liver disease, it is important that you continue to take lactulose three times per day along with rifaximin twice daily. While you were in the hospital you met with our social work team, who provided you with resources to help you stop drinking. It is very important that you abstain from any alcohol use.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ y/o M with PMH including hx of multiple CVAs (residual right sided weakness), CAD (s/p PCI), DM, and recent TKA, who presented for episode of dizziness and facial droop. He received workup for an acute stroke with CTA and later MRI, which did not show any acute stroke. His believe the symptoms were recrudescence of his old stroke. He was also evaluated for weight loss, dysphagia, and frequent falls, with the below findings. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # Weight loss 48 lbs of unintentional weight loss over the past ___ months. CT T/A/P obtained in ED is reassuring for no obvious malignancy. Likely multifactorial, from dysphagia, depression, and failure to thrive at home. He was able to tolerate his diet well in the hospital, and he will be transitioned to rehab facility where he will be able to get ___ and ensure adequate caloric intake. # Dysphagia Patient with reported dysphagia for last few months, frequently vomits food he feels caught in esophagus. Has reportedly had balloon dilitations of esophagus in past (BWH in ___. Last EGD ___ showed normal esophageal mucosa and gastritis. Barium swallow showed some evidence of dysmotility without any achalasia. GI was consulted-- did not plan for further inpatient evaluation, but plan for outpatient upper and lower endoscopy and possible manometry. He continued home pantoprazole. He tolerated all meals well in the hospital without recurrence of his reported symptoms. # R facial droop # Hx CVA Evaluated by Neuro in ED: notable for R NLFF. CTA did not show any acute abnormalities. DDx included TIA or recrudescence of old stroke deficit (his recent stroke ___ years ago presented with R facial droop). Neuro symptoms have resolved to his baseline. MRI checked without any acute stroke. He was continued on home ASA 325 and Plavix. # Dizziness/Presyncope # Recent Fall Orthostatics wnl. Telemetry showing intermittent bradycardia and PVC's, concerning that potentially his symptoms are due to medication side effect. Has had some improvement in symptoms with decreased metoprolol dosing, which he will continue at discharge. He will continue with aggressive ___ at rehab. # CAD s/p PCI ECHO performed this admission showed EF of 45% and focal apical wall motion changes, while prior from ___ (___) showed EF 55% without focal changes. Concern for prior missed ischemic event sometime in the past few years. Patient does note intermittent chest pain symptoms which are atypical in nature. Troponins, ECG at admission within patient's baseline. He continued ASA 325, Plavix, atorvastatin 80 QPM, and metoprolol as above.
