He should have cardiology follow up as an outpatient, where a stress test should be considered. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # COPD Fluticasone IH while inpatient, but will transition back to home budesonide (which is nonformulary at ___ at d/c. Continued home tiotropium bromide IH daily, albuterol inhaler # DM Hoed home metformin, but will restart as outpatient. # HTN Continued home lisinopril and metoprolol # Depression Holding home diazepam (patient reportedly takes rarely at home). Continued home bupropion, citalopram. Initiated mirtazapine given continued depressed mood. Transitional Issues ================== [ ] Consider stress test as outpatient given newly depressed EF and WMA noted on TTE on admission [ ] F/u with GI, consider upper endoscopy, colonoscopy, manometry given ongoing dysphagia symptoms [ ] patient should be connected to outpatient psychiatrist for severe depression symptoms; please consider titrating depression regimen [ ] initiated on mirtazapine this admission; please consider adjusting as necessary [ ] consider ongoing nutritional supplementation and dietitcian evaluation [ ] please ensure appropriate support for patient at home at time of discharge; consider outpatient SW [ ] please consider weaning benzodiazepines as tolerated; this is important given patient's risk for falls
summary: You came to the hospital because you had an episode of dizziness, worsening speech, and facial droop that was concerning for a possible stroke You had workup for a possible stroke, which did not show any new strokes. It is believed that all your symptoms were a flare up of your old stroke symptoms. We investigated the cause of your recent weight loss. We looked into your dizziness and reduced the dose of your metoprolol. our GI team evaluated your difficulty swallowing, and felt that you could have an EGD and a colonoscopy as an outpatient. we did an ultrasound that showed an area of weakening in your heart; we don't think that this was related to why you came to the hospital, but you should see your cardiologist.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ yo male with a history of HTN, DM2, COPD, diastolic HF, gout, OSA who presents with syncope due to dehydration, now resolved, but course c/b acute gout flare. # Syncope, s/p fall: Most likely ___ dehydration after diarrhea. His CE were neg x3, and recent TTE and stress in ___ were negative, making cardiac causes less likely. Reported diarrhea at home since starting colchicine. Colchicine dose was adjusted to QOD. Patient had some diarrhea at the end of his hospitalization, of which imodium prn was given. Orthostatics negative. # Bilateral Knee/Ankle tenderness: Pt with continued pain of unclear etiology during the early part of his hospitalization. His right knee was erythematous and painful on admission. He reported that he fell on the knee during the fall, however there was concern about gout flare vs. septic joint given his history. Rheum was consulted, and R knee was tapped. Crystals were present, but no signs of septic arthritis. Colchicine dose was decreased to QOD because of diarrhea, but there was concern that his pain may be due to a gout flare so he was started on a short course of prednisone. While on prednisone, pt reported feelings of paranoia and pain did not improve, making gout flare an unlikely etiology. He was restarted on maintenance allopurinol and colchicine (at QOD dose). However, then allopurinol was held due to low thought of gout, but this instigated an acute gout flare. He was restarted on allopurinol. The patient was dosed with IV solumedrol x 1 then started on prednisone. His pain was better controlled with this regimen. He should continue on prednisone taper, allopurinol daily, and colchicine 0.6 mg QOD on discharge. # Depression: ___ called ___ and reported that pt had recently expressed suicidal ideation to the visiting nurse. Pt was kept with a 1:1 sitter. Psych was consulted and he was cleared by psych since not currently suicidal. He was started on Wellbutrin per psych recs and continued on citalopram. He has outpatient behavioral neurology and psychiatry followup. #. Facial Rash: Pt was noted to have a rash that was c/w seborrheic dermatitis. Ketoconazole topical cream was started BID. #. Black stools/diarrhea/anemia/weight loss: On HD 2, he reported that he was having black stools. This is concerning for the possibility of cancer. Hct was in the ___ in ___, and has come down to ___ in past year. Iron studies here suggests mild iron deficiency. Hct stable here. An outpatient GI appointment was scheduled for colonoscopy. # DM2: HISS while in house, and restarted on glipizide on d/c # Hypertension: Continued Carvedilol and nifedical # Gout: As described above, rheum was consulted. Rheum felt that pt did not have a gout flare. However, the next day the primary team thought that there might be a gout flare so he was empirically started on prednisone. After 3 day course of prednisone, symptoms did not improve so pain was felt to be unlikely due to gout flare. He was continued on maintenance colchicine and allopurinol. However, allopurinol was held for a few days, which instigated a flare. He was started on IV Solumedrol x 1 and then on prednisone taper, which helped resolve his pain. Note that he should not be given NSAIDs since developed ARF with NSAIDs in the past. # Diastolic CHF. Hypovolemic on admission and euvolemic after HD 2. Continued carvedilol and held lasix since he appeared euvolemic. # Asthma: intermittent wheeze that cleared after neb. He was also continued on advair. # Dispo: Home.
summary: You were admitted after a fall. Tests showed that you did not have a heart attack. The fall was likely due to dehydration after having diarrhea. Somtimes colchicine can cause diarrhea, so the colchicine was stopped for 1 day. When your diarrhea resolved, you were restarted on colchicine. When you fell, you landed on your knee causing right knee pain and swelling. There was concern that this swelling could be due to infection or gout so your rheumatologist came to see you and she took fluid from the knee. It was not caused by infection. You then had a gout flare and were treated with steroids. Because of your chronic black diarrhea, there was concern for malignancy within the colon. To further evalute this, it is very important that you follow up with a gastoenterologist who will likely recommend colonoscopy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with a history of DM2, hypothyroidism, hypertension, and a previous CVA who presented with syncope vs. stroke, hypotension, slurred speech, and question of a facial droop. Acute stroke was ruled out in the ED on head CT and subsequently on MRI, and carotid ultrasound showed no significant carotid stenosis. His slurred speech resolved prior to transfer to the floor. Workup was unrevealing for cardiac or neurologic causes of syncope. ====================== ACUTE CARE ====================== #Syncope: Mr. ___ presented with an episode of syncope c/b hypotension to 60/40 per EMS, slurred speech, and facial droop. Blood pressure improved with fluids. Stroke workup was performed in the ED and noncontrast head CT showed no acute hemorrhage or ischemia. Carotid doppler showed 40% stenosis bilaterally but no acute occlusion. EKG showed a known first degree heart block. He was admitted to the floor for further workup of his syncopal event. Overnight tele showed first degree AV block but no other arrhythmias and he was hemodynamically stable for the duration of the admission. Although the syncopal episode was most likely vasovagal, additional etiologies were considered. Given the constellation of anhedonia, syncope with perisistent hypotension, adrenal insufficiency was assessed with a cosyntropin stimulation test, the results of which showed no evidence of adrenal dysfunction. TSH was also normal. A MRI/MRA performed prior to discharge showed (on wet read) areas of chronic ischemia consistent with his prior strokes, but no acute ischemia or hemorrhage. Of note, he did have a normal anatomical variant of the circle of ___, hypoplasia of the A1 segment. # Hypotension. Mr. ___ was hypotensive per EMS, but in the hospital he was normotensive in the 110s/60s. He does take blood pressure medications at home for hypertension, but given that he was normotensive during the admission while off medications, these were not restarted on dischage given concern for causing hypotension. # Diabetes mellitus type 2 c/b diabetic neuropathy and retinopathy. Home diabetes medications were discontinued and patient put on ISS. ====================== CHRONIC CARE ====================== # Depression. Mr. ___ complains of anhedonia and depression, currently on bupropion. Major life stressors include wife with ocular melanoma and loss of independence secondary to vision loss from vitrectomies. # Hypothyroidism. Mr. ___ has hypothyroidism, treated with levothyroine. Given depression and hypotension in the setting of hypothyroidism, TSH was sent (pending). # Hypertension. HCTZ and lisinopril d/c'ed on admission given syncopal episode. ====================== TRANSITIONS IN CARE ====================== -F/U MRI/MRA final read -d/c'ed HTN medications given normotensive during admission
summary: You were admitted to ___ after you fainted. In the emergency department, to rule out a stroke, doctors did ___ scan of your head and ultrasound of your carotid arteries, which did not show evidence of a stroke. You were admitted so we could try to determine the cause of your fainting episode. We tested a number of things, including monitoring of your heart (which showed only a benign arrhythmia), monitoring of your blood pressure (normal, even while not taking blood pressure medications), and several blood tests. These blood tests included a test for function of your adrenal glands, which showed no adrenal deficiency. Tests of your thyroid showed that your supplemental thyroid hormone levels appear to be appropriate. Finally, an MRI of your head and neck showed no stroke. Of note, you do have a normal variant of the vessels in your brain called "anterior cerebral artery A1 segment hypoplasia," which may be helpful to know in the future. We believe that your fainting episode was most likely caused by low blood pressure. We would like you to stop taking your blood pressure medications until you meet with your primary care doctor. The only CHANGES in your medications is that we recommend STOPPING your blood pressure medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Anorexia/Depression: Ms. ___ was admitted for a rapid decline of her weight in the last 2 weeks in the setting of multiple social stressors in her life, including the anniversary of her mother's death, the recent inpatient psychiatric hospitalization of her youngest daughter, and her husband's continued alcoholism. She endorsed suicidal ideations and a plan for death via starvation. She was aggressively hydrated with IVF and started on the eating disorder protocol. Due to her suicidality, she was maintained on 1:1 observation. Nutrition was involved and MVI, thiamine, and folate supplementation was started. She was very compliant with the protocol, eating 100% of her meals and not requiring liquid nutrition or NGT. Her weight did not make a significant change. Her admission weight was 118 lbs. Her discharge weight was 118.5 lbs. She was offered low doses of either olanzapine or quetiapine at meal times to assist with meal related anxiety. She continued to be followed by psychiatry daily. In a multi-disciplinary meeting with psychiatry, nutrition, social work, nursing, and the medical team it was decided that transfer to an inpatient psychiatric facility for further treatment of her depression/suicidality was the best treatment for her. She was continued on her home fluoxetine and lorazepam at night. She was medically stable for transfer at discharge with normal electrolytes, and stable vital signs. Orthostasis: The patient remained persistently orthostatic despite aggressive IVF resuscitation. Clinically, she was volume replete. After further investigation, she was noted to have been previously diagnosed postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome and autonomic dysfunction. Thus her continued orthostasis was not due to volume depletion. This is not a sign of medical instability. She was started on midodrine and titrated up with improved effect. She should also continue to wear TEDS stockings at all times. Thyroid: Mildly elevated TSH with mildly depressed free T4 without major signs of hypothyroidism. Given the risk of weight loss with thyroid supplementation, we held off on treatment. Further decisions regarding thyroid supplemenation deferred to PCP. Chest pain: Ms. ___ had one episode of chest pain in the ED that resolved with IVF. Her EKG was without signs of ischemia. Two sets of cardiac enzymes were negative. Osteoporosis: We continued calcium and vitamin D. Anxiety: Continued lorazepam in the evening. She was given the choice of olanzapine or quietiapine with meals. Precautions: She had a 1:1 sitter.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of weight loss and worsening depression with suicidal thoughts. While you were in the hospital, we monitored your vital signs and nutrition. You are being discharged from the hospital to an inpatient psychiatric unit. You physicians and counselors will help you with your depression. You have orthostatic changes in your blood pressure. This means that your blood pressure will decrease when you are standing. This had been diagnosed by a neurologist several years ago. Given your continued orthostasis, a medication called midodrine was added to support your blood pressure. You should continue to wear the TEDS stockings.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ cholangiocarcinoma (perihilar type), stage ___ (T2b, N2, Mx) on gemcitabine and cisplatin (C3D15) who is admitted for anxiety and dehydration. #Anxiety/Akathisia: This was thought to be most likely due to a combination of anxiety, withdrawing from compazine, and high dose steroids. The rapid improvement in her symptoms make serotonin syndrome unlikely. Within 12 hours of admission, she was at her usual state. - continue to hold compazine - prn benzodiazepines - avoid steroids for now - f/u psychiatry this week #Diarrhea: DDx includes opiate withdrawal vs. infectious (noro, cdiff). Her diarrhea had resolved prior to admission. Although she was yawning, having teary eyes, and loose stools, it is unlikely opiate withdrawal as she was on her usual oxycontin and only missed her prn oxycodone doses. We gave her stool softeners and suppository to help stimulate a bowel movement but she did not have any more. It is likely this diarrhea resolved but to help preserve her sanity, she will be discharged home and she is aware she must return to the hospital if the diarrhea recurs. I also provided her on disease containment. - hold senna/colace today, resume tomorrow if no diarrhea (need to resume in setting of high dose narcotics) #Cholangiocarcinoma Disease is responding to chemo and on C3D14 palliative gemcitabine and ciplatin. - C4D1 will be ___ #Abdominal pain Chronic, ___ malignancy, unchanged from baseline - cont home regimen OxyContin 120 mg every 8 hours with oxycodone 30mg q.3h.prn - cont gabapentin (pt reduced daytime dose to 100 due to sedation) - cont bowel regimen #Depression Patient is having symptoms of hopelessness, tearfulness, fatigue, and anxiety. She feels sad and spontaneously cries at home. (no SI/HI) And although some of this is an adjustment reaction, many of these symptoms preceded her diagnosis of cancer and impacting her quality of life. I reiterated the importance of her seeing the psychiatry team this week. We discussed ativan (ok to take 0.5 mg up to four times a day if not sleepy) and possibly changing this to valium as an outpatient. I did not increase citalopram to 40 mg due to concern of possible qtc prolongation with her antiemetics perichemo. - cont citalopram at 20 - f/u psych this week # Code: FULL Confirmed # Dispo: Home w/o services # Billing: more than 30 min were spent on coordinating care for discharge
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with severe anxiety and what seems to have been a withdrawal from Compazine. We would recommend that you stay off this medication until Dr. ___ ___ you it is ok to start taking again. In addition, you had diarrhea which resolved on admission. You did not have any fevers or elevated white count. It is likely that this was related to taking stool softeners and stimulants while being off your as-needed oxycodone. You did not have any bowel movements while in the hospital, so we were unable to send your stool for cultures. If you start having diarrhea again (since you had several stool softeners and laxatives in the hospital to help you move your stools and you haven't yet, it will be ok if you have some loose stools on ___, you need to return to care to have your stool checked for c.diff and for stool cultures. If you are having diarrhea, you should always wash your hands and use bleach to clean the toilet seat to avoid spread of disease to family members.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ year old right handed woman who has remote history of colon cancer, now thought to be in remission, who presented after several staring spells as well as a witnessed generalized tonic clonic seizure. At an outside hospital, she received 2 mg IV ativan and was loaded with levetiracetam 750 mg. She was then transferred to ___. By the time she arrived to ___, she was essentially back to normal with intact mental status. Her examination was otherwise notable for bilateral IP and hamstring weakness, with diffuse hyperreflexia but downgoing toes, likely suggestive of a cervical spondylytic myelopathy. Laboratory evaluation during admission was unrevealing for any acute toxic metabolic process or infection. MRI brain showed generalized atrophy as well as chronic microvascular changes. She also reported symptoms of very poor sleep, as well as depressed mood and anhedonia, without suicidality. We feel that the etiology of her seizures is likely due to chronic microvascular changes, exacerbated by sleep deprivation. She was started on Levetiracetam 750mg BID, and tolerated this medication well. There were no further seizures during admission. She did complain of symptoms of a urinary tract infection on ___. Urinalysis was consistent with UTI. She was therefore started on Cefpodoxime and will complete a 3 day course (___). Importantly, she did not have symptoms until well after her seizures, so UTI is felt to be less likely a contributor to her seizures. Physical therapy recommended discharge to acute rehab. Driving restrictions and seizure safety awareness was reviewed. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Started on Mirtazapine due to symptoms of depression and poor sleep. Please monitor her symptoms and adjust medications as necessary. Should be referred to therapist/social worker. -Blood pressure 130-150 systolic during admission. Please monitor and adjust antihypertensive regimen as needed. -She has mild paraparesis on exam, likely suggestive of a chronic spondylytic myelopathy. We would suggest an outpatient evaluation, possibly with MRI cervical spine. She may symptomatically treat this with a soft cervical collar. -No driving for 6 months post seizure. -Complete 3 day course of Cefpodoxime ___
summary: You were in the hospital after you had a few episodes of staring and unresponsiveness. You also had an episode of shaking of all of your limbs. Based on the description, we feel these episodes were likely seizures. In the hospital, we performed several studies to determine why you had a seizure. The MRI of your brain showed that you have some findings that are typical as you age. However, these changes can cause seizures. We started you on a medication called Keppra to help prevent further seizures. We also started you on a medication called Mirtazapine which can help with sleep as well as your mood. When you leave the hospital, you should continue taking Keppra to prevent seizures. In order to prevent future seizures, it is important to get regular sleep, stay hydrated, and avoid excessive alcohol (more than one drink per night). According to state law, you cannot drive for 6 months from your last seizure. Also, you should avoid heights or ladders, as well as swimming or bathing unsupervised.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PLEASE NOTE THAT PATIENT LEFT AMA ON ___, VERBALIZING UNDERSTANDING OF RISKS OF THIS DECISION, UP TO AND INCLUDING DEATH. REASON FOR HOSPITAL ADMISSION: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with a PMH of Hep. C, depression, ADHD, and bipolar dz who presents from Arbour with 3 days of joint pain, fatigue, and rash. HOSPITAL COURSE: # Leukocytoclastic vasculitis (per dermatology biopsy), in setting of chronic HCV and new HBV infection. Her HIV, Lyme, ANCA, ___ testing was negative. She is clinical much improved with resolving rash, pain, and swelling. On the day of her AMA departure, cryoglobulin was still pending. Per dermatology, she will need a weekly Bun/Cr and urinalysis checked for 1 month. For the subsequent 5 months, she will need monthly BUN/Cr and urinalysis to ensure no renal sequelae from the LCV (i.e. glomerulonephritis), for which she would require systemic steroids. #Transaminitis: LFTs have been rising from 100's to 500s. Likely related to an acute HBV co-infection. Medication induced liver injury unlikely, as quetiapine is the only drug that causes AST/ALT elevation and this is unlikely to be the case without steatosis or abdominal pain. Acetaminophen was discontinued, however her tox screen was negative and this is an unlikely contributor. It was our strong recommendation that the patient stay in the hospital until her LFTs stabilized, but she left against medical advice. A good deal of time was spent counseling her regarding the risks of leaving AMA up to and including death. # HCV, newly diagnosed HBV with HBV IgM positive, likely cirrhosis. Her HCV viral load is >900,000. Her last hepatitis treatment was ___ years ago per patient, with interferon. With thrombocytopenia and splenomegaly on US, cirrhosis is a serious concern. Although US liver on this admission was not suggestive, a biopsy is needed for diagnosis. She has liver appointment at ___ ___. She should be immunized to ___. Interferon treatment would be contingent on stabilization of her mood disorder and recent suicidal ideation. Her acute HBV infection, however, will likely warrant treatment. On her AMA departure, her HBV viral load and AFP were pending. # Psych (h/o suicidality. bipolar, depression): Does not endorse suicidality. Apparently has taken pills that she hides on body in past hospital stays (per sisters). ___ and urine tox both negative. Her 1:1 sitter was discontinued after one day. Her home regimen (lithium (immediate release), quetiapine, clonidine, methylphenidate and citalopram was continued in the hospital. # R-sided pain. She initially complained of diffuse right sided tenderness to palpation. She never had peritoneal signs and the pain resolved by the time she left the hospital. Her pain may have been related to acute HBV infection. Without a ___ count, n/v or fevers, appendicitis was unlikely. Her normal AlkP and Tbili and unremarkable were less suggestive of a biliary process. # Opiate abuse. Her serum and urine tox were negative, and she had no withdrawal sx. There was a concern that patient may have history of hiding opiates on her person in prior hospitalizations. This was not thought to have occured during this admission. ___ psych RN arranged an outpatient substance abuse appointment. # Pain was controlled with Ibuprofen. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Needs weekly BUN/Cr and UA for one month, then monthly BUN/Cr for 5 months. HBV viral load pending, please follow Cryoglobulin, AFP pending at the time of discharge, please follow.
summary: You were evaluated at ___ for joint pain, swelling, and rash. The preliminary report from the biopsy of the skin on your leg is consistent with a condition called leukocytoclastic vasculitis, likely related to your liver hepatitis. The condition appears to be resolving without any new intervention, however, you will need to have kidney function checked EVERY WEEK FOR THE MONTH and then EVERY MONTH FOR THE ADDITIONAL 5 MONTHS, as this disease process can lead to severe kidney damage (even after you no longer have symptoms of joint pain and rash). You You were also found to have an acute HBV infection, in addition to your chronic HCV.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ h/o AIDP p/w progressive weakness and paresthesias concerning for a third flare, now most likely representing CIDP. [] Weakness, Sensory Change - The patient presented with proxmal muscle weakness (worse on the left than right) concerning for a relapse of AIDP versus chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy. He also had persistent sensory disturbances worse on the face and feet with patchy pinprick sensory loss. He was treated with plasmapheresis for 5 courses with good response. He was evaluated by ___ and OT who cleared him to go home; he was walking up several flights of stairs and riding on the exercise bike by the time of discharge. He was started on gabapentin to help with neuropathic pain. He did endorse significant depression and even suicidal ideation without a plan or intent; Psychiatry was consulted and assessed that he was not an acute safety risk for returning home, but that he would likely benefit from outpatient followup with a therapist or psychiatrist with possible benefit from an antidepressant (possible Bupropion for its stimulatory effect as well as he might have ADHD-like components to his mood disorder). He also may look into the option ___ as he reports having failed multiple antidepressants in the past. He will followup with Drs. ___ for neurological reassessment and consideration for possible maintenance pheresis. PENDING STUDIES: None TRANSITIONAL CARE ISSUES: [ ] Please determine whether the patient would benefit from maintenance plasmapheresis. [ ] Please check to see if the patient has followed up with the ___ clinic or a therapist/psychiatrist near his home town.
summary: You were hospitalized due to recurrent symptoms of weakness and sensory disturbances that most likely represent a recurrence of your prior syndrome. Given this recurrence but also the persistence of prior symptoms, it is difficult to determine with certainty whether this represents a relapsing form of ACUTE INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY (also known as ___ Syndrome) or if it represents CHRONIC INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY. Nonetheless, the immediate treatment is the same. While both IVIG and plasmapheresis are equally effective, we opted to pursue plasmapheresis this time because you had IVIG twice previously without full resolution of symptoms and with some associated adverse effects.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ==================== PATIENT SUMMARY: ==================== Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of MDD, BPD, and EtOH use disorder who was transferred from ___ for mechanical ventilation in the setting of alcohol withdrawal and concern for delirium tremens. She has a significant alcohol history (averaging ___ drinks daily) with a history of complex withdrawal and delirium tremens. Her last drink was 3 days prior to admission. She initially presented to ___, brought in by ambulance. At ___, she was confused,tremulous and tachycardic. She was given Ativan and phenobarbital load, however she continued to be restless, experiencing visual hallucinations and was intubated for worsening mental status and delirium, using etomidate and succinylcholine for induction, and was sedated with propofol and fentanyl. She was transferred to ___ for airway control. On arrival in the FICU she had a RASS of -5. Her propofol was weaned slowly, changed to precedex, and on day 2 she was able to be extubated. Given a history of prior suicide attempt she had a 1:1 sitter while in the ICU. After extubation she was seen by psychiatry who felt she did not have any suicidal ideation or intent so sitter was discontinued and she was transferred to the floor. ==================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [ ]LFTS Remained elevated on discharge and should be rechecked as outpatient if still elevated would do a complete hepatitis workup ==================== ACUTE ISSUES: ==================== # Alcohol use disorder # Acute alcohol withdrawal # Delirium tremens Has history of severe withdrawal requiring hospitalization. Received phenobarbital dose at ___. Psychiatry has evaluated. Initially required intubation for airway protection and agitation and was in the ICU. She was extubated ___ without difficulty and monitored on CIWA. She was treated with thiamine 500 mg PO daily, Folic acid 1 mg PO, MVI daily. SW was consulted to assist with resources for outpatient EtOH use disorder treatment. # Passive suicidal ideation # MDD # BPD Per report from ___, patient was endorsing passive SI and was placed on ___. Evaluated by psychiatry, who felt there was no indication for 1:1 supervision or inpatient psychiatric care. Pt currently denying active SI. - Outpatient psychiatry follow up with known providers - ___ home medications, including venlafaxine ER 150 mg daily, hydroxyzine 25 mg PO TID, trazodone 100 mg PO qHS, Adderall 20 mg PO daily, gabapentin 800 mg PO TID # Anemia Likely ___ myelosuppression in the setting of EtOH. - Trend Hb # Elevated LFTs may reflect EtOH hepatitis. She remained without evidence of synthetic dysfunction. Discussed with her that she should have her liver function tests rechecked in 2 weeks and if still elevated should undergo a full work-up for hepatitis. She currently is working with financial to reinstate her mass health but understood that this was important to follow-up on. Greater than 30 minutes spent on discharge
summary: You were admitted after you were found to be in alcohol withdrawal. Initially you had severe altered mental status and we were concerned that you could not protect your breathing. You had a breathing tube (also known as intubation) and were in the ICU. As you improved the breathing tube was removed and you were able to breathe normally. The alcohol is seriously harming your body. You were found to have high liver function tests likely from the alcohol. These were improving on your discharge but were not yet back to normal. You should have these rechecked in 2 weeks. Our social worker recommended several options to help you abstain from alcohol. We also suggest you follow closely with your therapist.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ male with a history of depression, anxiety and remote history of EtOH abuse who presented with tremors, mental status changes and hypertension, and was found to have electrical (but not clinical) seizures on EEG (without clinical correlate) at initial presentation. He was admitted to Medicine, then transferred to General Neurology. He was started on valproic acid and lacosamide, had a non-contrast head CT which showed no acute process, and further EEGs showed only moderately severe encephalopathy with no seizure activity. His brain MRI showed "no evidence of acute hemorrhage, infarction, or enhancing mass lesion, with minimal nonspecific T2/FLAIR signal hyperintensity in the periventricular and subcortical white matter which in a patient of this age may be reflective of chronic small vessel ischemic disease." Despite treatment of his seizures, ___ interactions continued to be inconsistent, with severe anhedonia, low speech output, flat affect, and low appetite. He consistently endorsed profound depression and hopelessness, but denied desire or plan to hurt himself. Infectious, metabolic, vascular, and paraneoplastic etiologies were pursued, with no evidence on labs or imaging to suggest a specific etiology for his cognitive decline. Psychiatry was involved and recommended restarting some pharmacotherapy for his depression. He was started on citalopram and seroquel, achieving a dose of 30 mg citalopram and 100 mg seroquel by the time of transfer. By transfer, ___ was speaking more fluently and with more output, and completing accurately all of the tasks in the mental status exam. He still had some deficits in memory occasionally. Ultimately, in conjunction with Mr. ___ and his family, determined that an inpatient psychiatry admission could be helpful for his severe depression and anhedonia. ___ also had some mild hyponatremia during the admission, which corrected after his food and drink intake increased. He also had some scrotal swelling, and an ultrasound showed a likely epididymal cyst vs loculated hydrocele. The swelling subsequently improved. Of note, ___ had a chest CT to eval for malignancy (given concern for paraneoplastic process) which revealed no tumors, but did show a small 6 mm right upper lobe nodule which will require follow-up.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of increasing confusion and memory problems over the past few months. Here, we found that you were having seizures. You were started on medications to stop the seizures and had no further seizures here. Your depression and hopelessness continued, however. We sent off many labs and did many tests to evaluate for other causes of depression and cognitive changes, but everything was negative. Because depression can cause severe memory problems and confusion, our colleagues in psychiatry evaluated you and together with you and your wife, determined that you could benefit from an inpatient psychiatric admission to better manage your depression symptoms and optimize treatment. We are hopeful that you will continue to improve with further care.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Psychiatric: Patient was admitted with psychotic depression with catatonic features five days after being medically admitted to ___ ___ for symptoms of worsening head tremor, paucity of speech concerning for psychotic depression that were at that time were attributed to delirium caused by electrolyte imbalance and leukocystois. She was started on Ativan on admission with subsequent dramatic improvement in her mental status, paranoia, and symptoms of catatonia. Ativan was titrated up to a dose of 1mg TID, then tapered off as the catatonia resolved and the depression improved. She was continued to citalopram. Given her history of doing well on quetiapine 200mg daily in the past, quetiapine was titrated back up to 200mg daily. This was initially given in 100mg BID and then changed to 200mg qhs when she had some daytime sedation. She was also restarted on mirtazapine, initially at a dose of 7.5mg qhs and later titrated to 15mg qhs (the dose she had been on previously). She tolerated these medications without side effects other than the daytime sedation documented above. Symptomatically, patient's paranoia, paucity of speech, and PMR improved steadily over the first week of the hospitalization. Her fears that the treatment team was trying to kill her, that someone was trying to harm her husband/daughter, and that she may have harmed her family dissipated quickly, which is consistent with prior hospitalizations. Despite this improvement, however, she continued to have evidence of depression including depressed mood, hopelessness, slowed thought process and PMR. Given the recent history of rapid decompensation following hospitalization, ECT was pursued. Patient had a rapid positive response to ECT with rapid lifting of hopelessness and increase in her energy and ability to interact with others on the unit. She received three ECT treatments as an inpatient and tolerated them without side effects. She will continue to receive ECT as an outpatient. Family: Patient's husband was closely involved in her care throughout her course. He reported that the patient had been at her best on a combination of quetiapine, citalopram, and mirtazapine. He also reported that she had responded postivitely to ECT courses in the past. He will be transporting the patient to outpatient ECT and either he or the patient's daughter will be with her following treatments. Medical: Patient had previously been on HCTZ, but this was held during the medical admission due to concerns that it had been contributing to hypokalemia. She developed 1+ ___ edema over the course of this hospitalization; HCTZ was not restarted due to concern for hypokalemia and in the setting of ongoing ECT treatment. She was also found to have glucose 291 on ___ and HbA1C 7.4%, concerning for development of DM. Patient will f/u with her PCP for further ___ and treatment of these conditions. Legal: ___ Risk Assessment: Risk assessment at the time of discharge include ongoing risk factors of history of rapid decompensations following hospitalization that tend to lead to psychotic depression with paranoia and suicidal ideation, including history of a suicide attempt by cutting her wrists in ___. Protective factors include her support system of her husband and daughter, the plan to continue ECT as an outpatient, her history of seeking help, and history of rapid response to treatment. She is currently future-oriented, has a bright affect, denies hopelessness/suicidality/paranoia, and is eager to follow up with outpatient providers. While she does have some chronic risk given the rapidity and severity of decompensations in the past, at the present time she does not require an inpatient level of care and is appropriate for discharge home w her comprehensive outpatient plan Disposition: To home with her husband with plans for outpatient ECT and for psychiatric care through ___.
summary: Do not drive, use heavy machinery, or make major life decisions during your course of ECT. You plan to continue your ECT course as an outpatient. In order to have ECT as an outpatient, someone must take you back and forth to and from the ECT appointment and stay with you after the treatment. You had a pilondial abscess on your back that was incised and drained. You should ask your primary care physician to check on this. You should discuss the swelling in your legs with your primary care physician. Please inform him/her that HCTZ caused hypokalemia and so it was discontinued. Your glucose was measured at 291 on ___ and HbA1C 7.4% during this admission, which meet criteria for DM. You should also discuss this with your primary care physician. If you are having difficulty making your appointments, you can discuss applying for The Ride with your outpatient providers. The application can be found on the ___ website (___).

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with persistent depressive disorder with intermittent major episodes and history of suicide attempts, and opioid use disorder in sustained remission, alcohol cirrhosis (Child A), and HCV who presented with loss of consciousness event. She was found on the ground at home by family, after report of recent worsening of depressive symptoms. She reported drinking a pint of alcohol prior to being found down and had a blood alcohol level of 291 on arrival to E.D. She was evaluated by psychiatry in the ED and reported suicidal ideation. She also was noted to have ___, hypernatremia, and hypokalemia. She was admitted to medicine service where she received IV fluid for correction of hypernatremia, electrolyte repletion and was placed on a CIWA scale. She initially had mild withdrawal symptoms and required Ativan PRN for alcohol withdrawal, but recovered and was no longer in withdrawal by time of discharge. Her vital signs and mental status were normal by time of discharge. She required 1:1 sitter and was transferred to the psychiatry service once medically cleared. Problem-based: #Depression with SI: Patient with a long history of depression and SI with current passive SI. Patient has recently been missing psychiatry appointments and is not taking her medications. She was evaluated by psychiatry and placed no 1:1 observation. She was continued on her home meds of Mirtazapine 15 mg PO QHS and Sertraline 150 mg PO QHS. She was transferred to psychiatry unit. - Transfer to inpatient psychiatry unit as patient is now medically cleared #Alcohol Use Disorder: Patient with long hx of alcohol use disorder with no history of DT or seizure but does have hx of tremors and anxiety if stops drinking. Placed on CIWA protocol, and received thiamine repletion, folate and multivitamin. #Hypernatremia -resolved: Likely in the setting of poor po intake. #Macrocytic Anemia: Likely in the setting of poor nutrition. No signs of bleeding. vitamin repletion as above. #COPD: Continue advair. #Esophagitis: Continued PPI. #Hypertension/Palpitations: Continue amlodipine, metoprolol. Transitional issues: [ ] 3 mm pulmonary nodule at the apex of the left lung. If the patient is high risk for pulmonary malignancy, recommend chest CT in 12 months. [ ] Patient should continue on thiamine, folate and multivitamin daily for poor nutrition. [ ] No changes were made to psychiatric medications in house. # CODE: Presumed Full # CONTACT: HCP- ___ ___
summary: Your family found you after you had fallen. You were admitted to the hospital medical service because we were worried that you were dehydrated and had an injury to your kidneys and some changes in your electrolytes. The psychiatry team saw you and were very worried about your depression. They thought it was important for you to stay in the hospital and go to the Psychiatry floor to get treatment for your depression. We gave you some IV fluids for your dehydration and gave you electrolytes. We monitored your for signs of alcohol withdrawal and treated you to prevent further problems from withdrawal. The Psychiatry team recommended that you go to the inpatient psychiatry team. We have not changed your medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: =================== PATIENT SUMMARY =================== ___ h/o GERD, ___ esophagus, IBS, anxiety/depression, ___ disease, hyponatremia, who presents as a PCP referral for suicidal ideation, found to be hyponatremic. Her hyponatremia was thought to be predominately SIADH, with contribution from low solute intake. She was treated with fluid restriction and salt tablets. She developed a simple cystitis and was treated with ceftriaxone for this. Psychiatry placed this patient on ___, and she was admitted to inpatient psychiatry after medical stabilization. =================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [] Hyponatremia: Please continue to monitor the patient's serum Na regularly. Patient should have chem 7 checked in 7 days ___ discharge Na 136. [] The patient has iron-deficiency anemia. She received 2 doses of 125mg ferric gluconate while admitted. She may benefit from additional IV iron repletion given her chronic GI symptoms. [] Please ensure that the patient has age-appropriate GI cancer screening given iron deficiency anemia. [] Weight loss: The patient endorsed weight loss this admission. Please continue to monitor her weight and determine whether she would benefit from age-appropriate cancer screening if her weight does not improve. [] GI: The patient is scheduled to see an outpatient gastroenterologist for chronic dyspepsia/GERD symptoms. She may benefit from 24h esophageal pH monitoring or other testing. Please ensure that the patient is able to see her gastroenterologist. =================== ACTIVE ISSUES =================== #Euvolemic, hypotonic hyponatremia: Labs were most c/w SIADH, though a component of low solute intake may be at play. Recently upped her dose of sertraline as outpatient, which correlated with the rise in Na. AM ___ low, but review of ___ records from ___ reviewed a normal ___ stim test. Renal was consulted. She was placed on fluid restriction at 1.5L and NaCl tablets at 2g TID. #Depression #Suicidal ideation Psychiatry saw patient and placed her on ___. They felt her presentation c/w MDD. Per ___ chart also some c/f anorexia and laxative abuse in the past. Zoloft was stopped given hyponatremia. She was given home dose of Ativan, 0.25-0.5mg BID PRN for anxiety. Psychiatry reevaluated patient and felt that she would benefit from inpatient psychiatry admission. #Simple cystitis UA positive with symptoms. No systemic s/sx or leukocytosis. Urine culture grew out Citrobacter Freunii complex. She was treated with 3d ceftriaxone 1g daily. No systemic s/sx. As Citrobacter Freunii may have inducible beta-lactamase, repeat UA and culture were obtained, which showed that her simple cystitis had cleared. #Iron-deficiency anemia Patient's IV iron store deficiency is 880mg from hgb 8.7 to 12 accoutning for 500mg iron store repletion. Patient received 2x 125mg ferric gluconate while admitted. She may benefit from ongoing IV iron repletion given her chronic GI symptoms. #GERD #Dyspepsia Patients with Parkinsons have a high prevalence of GERD. Already has outpatient follow up with a gastroenterologist where she may benefit from 24h esophageal pH monitoring and H pylori testing since BID PPI doesn't seem to be effective. A component of this may be associated with IBS as well. Her symptoms have been a point of distress for the patient. She was put on 40mg BID pantoprazole and standing BID ranitidine. Additionally given Maalox and simethicone PRN. =================== CHRONIC ISSUES =================== #Weight loss Her weight loss is likely in the setting of depression and possibly decreased appetite with severe GERD symptoms. If weight does not improve, she would benefit from age-appropriate cancer screening. #Thrombocytopenia Per review of OSH records, plt fluctuated between ___ to 200s. Etiology unclear. Stable in-house. ___ disease Not on meds at home. #CODE: presumed full #CONTACT: ___ Phone number: ___
summary: You were having suicidal ideation and abdominal/GI discomfort at home. You were found to have a low sodium level. This is likely due to the Zoloft (sertraline) you were taking. We stopped this medication and you should not take it in the future. To treat your low sodium level, we also put you on a fluid restriction and gave you tablets of salt, per discussion with our Renal doctors. Our psychiatrists saw you and determined that you should be admitted to an inpatient psychiatric facility once your sodium level was corrected. You developed a urinary tract infection, and we gave you antibiotics for this. Please continue to work with the psychiatry colleagues to improve your mood.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old male with history of depression, alcohol abuse, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hyperlipidemia and GERD who presents after two witnessed seizures at ___ today and another seizure in the ED, likely secondary to EtOH withdrawal. # Seizures / EtOH withdrawal: On admission from the Emergency Department, he was slightly tachycardic and hypertensive, but extremely disoriented. He was only oriented to person and believed he was at the ___ in ___. He was started on diazepam 10mg IV every 4 hours as needed. During his first day of admission he required at least this regimen, with a few extra doses for CIWA scores greater than 20. Over the course of the day, his CIWA scores dropped to below 10 and he did not receive any more diazepam for the duration of his visit. His vital signs were stable and not indicative of current alcohol withdrawal. He was ruled out for other causes of seizures with a negative infectious work-up (no leukocytosis) and a negative CT head. He did not have any neurologic deficits on exam. There was some concern for hyperglycemia and poorly-controlled t2DM as a contributing metabolic cause. Neurology exam does not show any focal neuro deficits. He was kept on seizure precautions, but did not have any subsequent seizures while admitted. His electrolytes were repleted as necessary and he was started on supplementation of a multivitamin, thiamine and folate daily. #. CT head finding of lytic lesion: On the CT of his head, a incidental finding of a 1cm lytic, well-circumscribed lesion on calvarium was noted, considered by Radiology to be more likely benign than malignant. He has no previous malignancy in history, but they recommend MRI for further work-up as outpatient. # Depression: During his admission, he still had active suicidal ideation, in the context of his recent suicide attempt on ___. He did not have a specific plan, but endorses that he "would do a better job next time." No homicidal ideation, but he is ___ and will be transferred to a psychiatric facility for close monitoring. Given that his seizures were most likely secondary to alcohol withdrawal, he was restarted on his regimen of buproprion, citalopram and risperidone. He was kept on a 1:1 sitter throughout his admission. # Hyponatremia: Likely secondary to hypovolemia and poor PO intake. Corrected gradually with IV fluids. # Macroytic Anemia: Unclear baseline, but improved. Iron studies showed iron deficiency (low TIBC, normal ferritin), but RDW is normal and macrocytosis is more likely from ongoing alcohol abuse and vitamin deficiencies. B12 was not decreased, but he will be continued on folate and thiamine upon discharge for his alcohol abuse. # Diabetes mellitus, type II: Recently diagnosed and not well controlled currently, with FSBG in high 200s to 300s. He began to refused fingersticks to monitor his glucose. His home glyburide was held and he received an insulin sliding scale. We will defer starting metformin until he sees his primary care physician once again.
summary: You were admitted here after you had seizures at an outside facility. You had another witnessed seizure while you were in the Emergency Department and we determined that these were likely caused by the withdrawal from alcohol. Since you have been drinking so heavily lately and you did not have any alcohol at the outside facility, you began to withdraw from this and had seizures as a result. We treated you with medicines to keep you calm and prevent you from having further seizures. On the CT of your head, we did not find anything that would cause your seizures, but we did find a small area that will need to be followed up with further testing as an outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ F with h/o HTN and hyponatremia causing "passing out" presenting after witnessed seizure at home thought ___ ETOH but possibly also hyponatremia. . 1) Hyponatremia: Unclear etiology at time of presentation as it was diffcult to assess patient's volume status. TSH wnl. Sodium did correct after IVH. On admission 122; improved after receiving 1L NS to Na 139. NA remained stable throughout the rest of hospital course. Patient orthostatic on hospital day 2, so likely hypovolemia hyponatremia secondary to low solute from alcohol abuse. Received additional IV hydration for orthostasis. No further episodes of orthostasis. Sodium monitored and were stable. Urine electrolyte abnormatilies also corrected after fluid bolus. . 2) Seizure: Possibly secondary to EtOH withdrawl or hyponatremia. In addition, neurology consulted for ___ time seizure. CT head neg. Plan for outpatient follow-up with MRI of head +/- gad, EEG. To follow with Dr. ___. No seizure activity in house. . 3) ETOH withdrawal: Patient has h/o ETOH abuse but no h/o seizures from withdrawal in past (although she is a poor historian). Placed on CIWA, however did not require Valium. Thiamine, folate, MVI PO given daily. Patient has an outpatient counselor for her alcohol abuse whom she will continue to see after d/c. . 4) HTN: Patient was normotensive on admission. Home dose of atenolol was continued. Become hypotension and orthostatic on hospital day 2 which corrected with additional IV hydration. Atenolol held that day and resumed on HD3. Blood pressures were monitored and pt was normotensive throughout admission. . 5) Depression: Home dose of citalopram and trazodone PRN continued.
summary: You were admitted to ___ after experiencing a seizure thought secondary to alcohol withdrawl. We monitored you in the ___ and on the medical floors. You remained stable and you received Valium as needed for withdrawl symptoms. Your sodium as found to be low which we attributed to dehydration. You received IV fluids with correction of your electrolytes. You had a neurology evaluation and they recommended you have an MRI and an EEG to further evaluate a source of your seizures. These can be done as an outpatient and we have placed orders in the computer for you to have them done. They also recommended seeing a neurologist as an outpatient and your primary care provider is aware and ___ help set up the tests and appointment with neurology.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx of ileocolonic and perianal Crohn's s/p ileocecectomy and partial colectomy (on Humira), IBS-D, GERD, vestibular neuritis presenting with N/V, abdominal pain, and diarrhea x 4 days, likely secondary to a viral gastritis. # Abdominal pain: # N/V: # Diarrhea likely # Viral gastroenteritis in setting of: # Crohn's disease (___) # IBS-D: Patient presented with 4 days of abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, and diarrhea. Given her hx of Crohn's disease, IBS, and C.diff, GI was consulted. They attributed her symptoms to a viral gastroenteritis (although norovirus PCR negative). Ddx includes bacterial infectious colitis/enteritis, although stool studies were negative, including C.diff testing. A Crohn's flare was felt to be unlikely in the setting of a normal CRP and documentation of remission on Humira. Ddx includes post-infectious IBS flare. Nl lactate was reassuring against abdominal catastrophe or ischemia. She was treated with IVFs, bowel rest, anti-emetics, and judicious narcotics. Her diet was gradually advanced, and she was tolerating a regular diet at discharge without nausea/vomiting or diarrhea. Abdominal pain was improving at discharge on infrequent PO dilaudid. She was discharged on Tylenol (she was advised to avoid NSAIDs), her home hyoscyamine and loperamide, and 8 tablets of dilaudid 2mg PO q6h PRN (PMP checked). She will f/u with her outpatient gastroenterologist, Dr. ___ her routine surveillance colonoscopy and with her PCP's office. Home rifaximin was continued, and she will resume her weekly Humira on discharge. She was advised to eat small frequent meals and to notify Dr. ___ persistent or worsening abdominal pain. # Depression/insomnia: Continued home venlafaxine, aripiprazole, Seroquel, Ativan daily PRN, and trazodone QHS PRN. QTC was checked and was 407 on ___. # Chronic vestibular injury: Continued home meclizine PRN. # Hernia, likely incisional: Outpatient follow-up for elective repair. ** TRANSITIONAL ** [ ] ensure resolution abdominal pain GENERAL/SUPPORTIVE CARE: # Contacts/HCP/Surrogate and Communication: ___ (husband) ___ # Code Status/ACP: FULL (presumed)
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, and diarrhea. You were seen by the gastroenterology service, who thought your symptoms most likely from a viral gastrointestinal infection. They were not concerned about a Crohn's flare. You were treated with pain medication and intravenous fluids, and your symptoms slowly resolved. Please stay hydrated and eat small, frequent meals. You are being discharged with a short course of dilaudid for pain control. Please trial Tylenol our hyoscyamine before taking the dilaudid. Do not drive while taking the dilaudid. Should your pain worsen or fail to improve in the next few days, please contact Dr. ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with a past medical history significant for headaches and anxiety who presented to the ___ Epilepsy Monitoring Unit ___ for long term monitoring for recurrent spells concerning for epileptic versus nonepileptic seizures. While in the hospital, EEG showed epileptiform discharges indicating pt was at risk for seizure. Lamotrigine was therefore started. MRI (Seizure) protocol was performed and did not identify any anatomical abnormality suspicious for seizure focus. Given signs of depression, including sleep disturbance and anorexia, patient was started on Mirtazapine (Remeron) with good effect. While plan had been to replace EEG leads after MRI in hopes of capturing an event, patient elected to leave Against Medical Advice on ___. Prescriptions for Lamotrigine and Mirtazapine were provided at discharge. Follow up with Neurology and Psychiatry will be arranged.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital to characterize events concerning for seizure. While we did not capture any seizures on EEG there was evidence of an area of your brain that may have had seizures recently or is otherwise not fucntioning properly. We obtained an MRI that did not show any damage or abnormal development of your brain. While we would have liked to collect more EEG information to make a more precise diagnosis, you declined to have the EEG leads replaced and are being discharged Against Medical Advice (AMA). In order to protect you from having seizures in the future, you were started on a medication called Lamotrigine (Lamictal). In order to reduce the risk of side effects, this medication was started at a low dose and will need to be increased slowly over the next several weeks in order before optimal protection will be achieved. Please increase the medication dose according to the plan below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ male with history of multiple myeloma on Daratumumab/Pomalidomide/Dex who presents from clinic with dyspnea, abdominal pain, and elevated bilirubin. # Elevated Bilirubin: Unclear etiology. ___ medication related, possibly doxepin or pomalidomide. Abdominal pain has resolved. RUQ US negative for biliary process. LFTs improving at time of discharge. Possibly intermittent hemolysis as indirect was elevated. Please continue to monitor. # ___: Patient found to have evidence of ___ esophagitis given oral thrush on exam and odynophagia. He was prescribed a 14-day course of fluconazole for total duration to be determined by outpatient providers. Also checked baseline QTc which was 400. Please continue to monitor. # Dyspnea: Normal O2 sats at rest and ambulation. Does not appear in respiratory distress. CTA chest unremarkable. Resolved at time of discharge. # Acute Kidney Injury: Cr 1.3 on admission, baseline 0.9-1.1. Likely due to poor PO intake. Improved with IVF. # Hyponatremia: Mild. Likely hypovolemic due to poor PO intake. Improved with IVF. # Multiple Myeloma: Relapsed, refractory IgG Lambda multiple myeloma currently on Daratumumab/Pomalidomide/Dex. Continued Bactrim and acyclovir for prophylaxis. Follow-up with outpatient Oncologist # Depression/Anxiety: Multiple stressors in life. Follows with Psychiatrist Dr. ___. Severe anxiety and insomnia. Held home doxepin. Continued Lexapro and clonazepam. # Anemia/Thrombocytopenia: Counts at baseline. All lines down on ___ likely ___ IVF and stable on recheck. # Abdominal Pain: Unclear cause. Currently resolved. # Gout: Continued home allopurinol. # Chronic Pain: Continue gabapentin and Tylenol. # Severe Protein-Calorie Malnutrition: Patient with weight loss and poor PO intake. He was seen by Nutrition. ==================== Transitional Issues: ==================== - Patient found to have evidence of ___ esophagitis given oral thrush on exam and odynophagia. He was prescribed a 14-day course of fluconazole for total duration to be determined by outpatient providers. - Patient had baseline EKG with QTc of 400 given interaction between fluconazole and escitalopram. Please continue to monitor QTc. - Patient with mildly elevated bilirubin on admission which normalized without intervention. Please consider intermittent hemolysis and continue to monitor LFTs. - Please note CTA chest with mild bibasilar fibrotic changes. - Please note abdominal ultrasound with splenomegaly of 15.5cm. - Patient's doxepin held at time of discharge. Please ensure follow-up with Psychiatry. - Patient was seen by Nutrition given evidence of malnutrition. - Please ensure follow-up with Oncology. # BILLING: 45 minutes spent completing discharge paperwork, counseling patient, and coordinating with outpatient providers.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath, abdominal pain, and elevated liver numbers. For your shortness of breath, you had a chest CT scan which did not show any blood clots in your lungs. It did not show any cause for your difficulty breathing. Most importantly, your breathing improved while in the hospital and were having no symptoms when being discharged. Your abdominal pain also resolved. You had an ultrasound of your liver that did not show any cause of your elevated liver numbers. This improved the following day and your Oncologist will continue to monitor. You also reported throat discomfort with swallowing. You had signs of ___ infection in your mouth. This throat pain is likely due to a ___ infection in your esophagus. You were started on a medication called fluconazole which you have been on in the past. This should help your symptoms improve. Please discuss how long you should continue this medication with your Oncologist. We did stop your doxepin which may have been causing some of your side effects.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with PMH signficant for COPD, Behcets disease, and depression who presents from ___ s/p elective L TKR with a ___ anterior STEMI, s/p 1 DES, now receiving post-STEMI care and pain control/safe dispo plan for TKR. #) ANTERIOR STEMI: The patient presented from an OSH after elective L TKR with perioperative anterior STEMI with STE in V2-V3, s/p 1 DES. Risk factors include prior smoking history, family history of MI. Initial troponin was 1.12, and upon arrival to ___ was 0.27. The patient was initially started on ticagrelor but out of concern for bleeding risk with ASA + ticagrelor +lovenox (for knee prophylaxis), on ___ discontinued ticagrelor and the patient was loaded with 600 mg clopidogrel and subsequently continued on 75 mg daily instead of ticagrelor. She also was started on metoprolol and lisinopril. #) Systolic heart failure: The patient's TTE on ___ demonstrated akinesis of apex/distal anterior septum and hypokinesis of the basal mid anterior septum and distal inferior wall, with an EF 35-40%. She was started on metoprolol and started lisinopril on ___. She was started on furosemide 20mg daily to help manage her hyperkalemia of 5.4 on ACEi, but then she became mildly dehydrated and furosemide was stopped. However, over time she began to become volume overloaded and developed mild pulmonary edema and an oxygen requirement and received several doses of 20mg IV lasix and was discharged on oral lasix 20mg daily. Due to LV apical hypokinesis found on TTE, the patient was started on warfarin with a presumed course of 6 months. #) S/P L TKR: The patient underwent elective L total knee replacement ___. She was started on enoxaparin with a bridge to warfarin which should be continued for 6 months due to her cardiac condition (apical hypokinesis). For pain control was on PCA, converted overnight ___ to oxycodone. Per recommendations from her OSH orthopedist, the patient continued with physical therapy (weight bearing as tolerated). Physical therapy recommended rehab after hospital discharge. #) COPD: The pt is a former smoker, also has Behcet's disease which may cause obstructive pulmonary symptoms. On admission she was saturating 97% on 2L NC. She was continued on home albuterol, tiotropium, and fluticasone. #) DEPRESSION: The patient was continued on home escitalopram oxalate 20 mg daily. She developed some anxiety during hospitalization requiring lorazepam; however, due to waxing and waning mental status, this medication was discontinued. #) BEHCET'S DISEASE: Per OSH notes, the patient had a Behcet's flare 10 weeks ago that responds to azithromycin PRN. Behcet's disease may cause coronary arteritis, which may lead to MI. Her Behcet's disease was not an active issue during this hospitalization. #) ALTERED MENTAL STATUS: Patient had waxing and waning mental status which developed during her hospital stay. She appeared somnolent and was receiving lorazepam for anxiety and oxycodone for pain. Her lorazepam was stopped and her oxycodone was decreased in dose. Her mental status and level of consciousness improved significantly suggesting her altered mental status was due to medication side effects. She had a UA which was negative with culture pending at discharge and chest xray had no infiltrate so infectious causes were felt to be unlikely. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Please check Chem-7 and INR on ___. -Weight bearing as tolerated. -Anticipate less than 30 day stay -Monitor weights daily. If gains more than 3 pounds in 1 day or 5 pounds in 3 days then would start diuretics. Discharge weight 109kg standing with knee brace on -For any questions regarding post-op care of knee replacement call Dr. ___ ___
summary: You were admitted with a heart attack and a blockage was found in your left anterior coronary artery. A drug eluting stent was placed to open the artery and you have done well since the procedure. It is extremely important that you take aspirin and clopidogrel every day for the next year to prevent the stent from clotting off and causing another heart attack. Do not stop taking aspirin and clopidogrel for any reason unless Dr. ___ ___ you it is OK to do so. Your heart is weaker after the heart attack and it is possible that you will retain fluid. Check your weight daily and report a weight gain more than 3 pounds in 1 day or 5 pounds in 3 days to Dr. ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with significanct PMH of opioid and alcohol abuse who presented with altered mental status and agitition, intubated for airway protection, now completely resolved. #ALTERED MENTAL STATUS: Secondary to acute opioid intoxication and withdrawal. No intracranial abnormality. No evidence of infection, seizure, or acute neurological process. Altered mental status resolved in ICU. Patient improved over time. No signs of withdrawal for 24 hours before discharge. At times he complains of shaking and sweating, but the shaking appeared volitional and the sweating and anxiety were more consistent with an acute stress disorder (he is very anxious about impending incarceration). Patient never becomes tachycardic or hypertensive during these episodes. Never any frank tongue fasciculations or true tremors. #POLYSUBSTANCE ABUSE: Opioid withdrawal in ICU. Treated with valium and clonidine. Also, heavy alcohol use and monitored on CIWA. Gave thiamine, folate, MVI. Patient did not truly show signs of withdrawal for 24 hours before discharge. #URETHRAL DISCHARGE: Yellow discharge noted from near foley site and patient reported penile pain with discharge; UA with copius WBCs. Administered pyridium for pain. Treated empirically for gonorrhea and chlamydia with ceftriaxone and azithromycin. Ultimately urine culture, RPR, gonorrhea/chlamydia returned as negative. Symptoms resolved. #ASTHMA: Patient was provided albuterol PRN. #DEPRESSION: Continued on home Celexa 40mg daily. #ANXIETY: In the setting of social situation. Administered Diazepam PRN. Have started citalopram as patient was taking his mother's medication before. Transitional Issues -Patient needs treatment for his substance abuse -Has anxiety (that can manifest as shaking) that should be followed as an outpatient. His anxiety appears organic and there were no signs of true withdrawal.
summary: You were admitted to ___ ___ for substance withdrawal. When you arrived, you had a breathing tube which was removed after several hours of monitoring. You were treated with medications to treat the high blood pressure muscle cramps, shakes, and other symptoms of withdrawal. It is VERY IMPORTANT that you stop using illegal substances. You were found to be anxious and depressed about your legal situation. You were continued on Celexa and treated with anti-anxiety medications while in the hospital. You are no longer withdrawing from medications/illicit drugs. You were also found to have pain and discharge from your penis. You were treated with pain medicine. You were treated for gonorrhea/chlamydia while tests were sent and ultimately negative for gonorrhea/chlamydia You are now safe to leave the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with history of HTN, IDDMII c/b gastroparesis, peripheral neuropathy, GERD, constipation, cholelithiasis, history of abdominal necrotizing fasciitis now presenting with abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting consistent with prior gastroparesis flares. HOSPITAL COURSE BY PROBLEM: # Gastroparesis flare The patient presented with abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting, suspected due to flare of gastroparesis. Other possible contributions including constipation and GERD. Patient has history of cholelithiasis, but pain atypical of biliary colic. Lipase within normal limits. KUB without acute process. She improved with conservative/supportive treatment including bowel rest with slow advancement of diet, pain medications (initially IV morphine, then oxycodone/APAP/ibuprofen), and antiemetics (lorazepam due to prolonged QTc). She improved with these measure and was tolerating a regular diet and pain-free at the time of discharge. # IDDMI # Hyperglycemia: Last A1c 7.2% on ___. Home regimen is Levemir 14 units Qam and 12 units QPM. She was treated with a reduced dose of her insulin while not tolerating PO. Her home regimen was resumed on discharge as she was eating and drinking normally. # Dysuria Patient recently completed course of antibiotics for UTI, but reported ongoing dysuria. Repeat UA was not consistent with UTI. # Acute kidney injury (resolved with IV fluids) Pre-renal azotemia in setting of poor oral intake and vomiting. # Prolonged QTc Likely due to medication effect from multiple Qt prolonging medications including metoclopramide and citalopram. Her QTc improved after holding these meds. On discharge, her citalopram was changed to sertraline and her metoclopramide was held. # Leukocytosis (resolved after IV fluids): Likely due to hemoconcentration. No fevers. Patient was recently treated for urinary tract infection. CHRONIC/STABLE PROBLEMS: # Hypertension: - Not on any agents # GERD: - PO famotidine and omeprazole # Anemia: Hb was normal in setting of hemoconcentration on admission. H/H was trended. # Post-partum depression. She was recently started on citalopram which was held on admission due to prolonged QTc. Citalopram was changed to sertaline on discharge. >30 minutes spent on complex discharge
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for abdominal pain, nausea, and inability to take in oral liquids/food due to a gastroparesis flare. Most likely this was triggered by your recent urinary tract infection. You were treated with supportive care (bowel rest, anti-nausea medicines, pain medicines) and your symptoms improved. You also had some kidney injury due to dehydration which resolved with IV fluids. You had a segment of your EKG (heart electrical conduction) that was slightly prolonged due to the effect of multiple medications you were taking. This can be dangerous because it can cause a fatal rhythm problem in your heart.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ yo RH man with history of CAD s/p CABG, HTN, HLD, DMII, history of multiple strokes/TIAs in the past and gait disorder and recent hospitalization for a small stroke with discharge to rehab facility who now presents with an episode of unresponsiveness followed by lethargy after a fall, initially concerning for hemorrhage but CT without evidence of bleeding. Some initial concern for seizure given EEG on previous admission showing slowing/epileptiform discharges from L temporal lobe #NEURO: On initial exam, mental status was notable for non-fluent speech, and orientation to person but not time or place. Patient appeared withdrawn on admission. Physical exam was notable for mild right NLFF (chronic) as well as weakness in Right upper extremity as well as lower extremities bilaterally R>L worse than baseline. CT without evidence of bleed, and due to concern for seizure given previous EEFs with slowing/eppileptiform dischcharges from L temporal lobe, patient was given Keppra 1000 mg x1 load, and subsequently maintained on Keppra 500mg po BID. EEG was repeated and showed no evidence of clinical or electrographic seizures by preliminary read, although formal read is pending at time of discharge. The patient's keppra was discontinued on day of discharge at the request of Dr ___, ___ patient's outpatient neurologist, who did not feel this was consistent with a seizure. We continued ASA 81 mg and Plavix 75mg daily. Patient was assessed for stroke risk factors on previous admission with fasting lipid panel (LDL 69) and HbA1c (7.1%). He was continued on his home dose of Simvastatin. Patient showed improvement of speech and cognition on discharge. Patient discharged to rehab facility with Neurology and PCP follow up in place. #CV: On admission patient was bradycardic to ___ but asymptomatic. ECG with RBBB and 1st degree AV nodal block, with is unchanged from prior. On discharge patient HR in the ___. Patient was monitored on telemetry with no pathological dysrhytmias.We initially allowed his BP to autoregulate with goal SBP < 180 (goal SBP 140-180s). #ENDO: Finger sticks were checked qid and patient was put on insulin sliding scale with goal of normoglycemia. HbA1c on previous admission 7.1%. Restarted home diabetic regimen on discharge #ID: UA was negative for infection. #HEME: CLL Patient with mild leukocytosis, likely due to recently diagnosed CLL. Patient will continue to be followed by outpatient provider. #GI: Patient with history of ___ esophagus. Patient will continue home dose of ranitidine. #PSYCH: Patient will continue home dose of sertraline for depression.
summary: You were admitted on ___ due to unresponsiveness and confusion after falling at your rehab facility. A CT of your head did not show any bleeding in your brain. We were also concerned that your symptoms might have been the result of a seizure, however on further testing we did not find any evidence to suggest that your symptoms were due to a seizure. Your symptoms improved by ___ and you were felt appropriate to be discharged to acute rehab. We discussed your case with your Neurologist, Dr. ___ felt that you should not be started on anti-seizure medications at this time. Based on our current EEG reads we agree with this assessment although you should review the final results of this study with Dr. ___ you see him in clinic.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o female with H/O seizure disorder, obesity, and significant tobacco abuse presenting with inconsistently localizable chest pain reproducible by palpation with extension to "whole body." # Chest Pain: No acute cardiac cause identified. Pain lasted over 24 hours at a reported ___ intensity without EKG changes or biomarker elevation for myonecrosis. Also, pain was reproducible on exam, although her history and exam were quite variable from examiner to examiner. Patient was able to fall asleep despite complaints of ongoing pain. She was unable to perform nuclear stress testing due to inability to lay still on her back, which according to the patient is a chronic problem for her. She was also unable to cooperate with a non-imaging exercise stress test. Since ischemia was unlikely given ongoing chest pain without evidence of infarction, other pharmacological stress testing or imaging modalities were not pursued. An echocardiogram was obtained which was unrevealing for any acute wall motion abnormalities but did note grade I diastolic dysfunction. A GI cocktail and PPI did not improve the pain. She was treated with standing Tylenol and ibuprofen for presumed costochondritis. A subclinical seizure leading to fall was a possibility, but CK's were not elevated and pt could not remember any such episode. Patient also had no evidence for pulmonary embolus or aortic dissection by ___ angiogram. The radiology department there felt the 3.9cm ectasia of the ascending aorta seemed chronic and stable and was very unlikely to be causing any of her symptoms. The plan was to have repeat outpatient CT scan of chest in 6 ___ year to assess stability and size. She eventually became pain free prior to discharge. # Seizure disorder- continued divalproex per outpatient schedule # Depression/anxiety - continued quetiapine, clonazepam, escitalopram, and trazodone. Social work was consulted to ensure adequate community resources were available prior to discharge. # Atypical cells on Differential - After discussion with heme/path, the initial report of 12 atypical lymphoctes was changed to 2 reactive lymphcytes, and an automated differentual the next day was reported as negative for atypicals. Plan was to have repeat surveillance WBC differential performed as an outpatient. # Smoking- Patient was given a nicotine patch and encouraged at smoking cessation. Social work was consulted.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain. We made sure you did not have a heart attack by checking EKGs and several blood tests. We also made sure you did not have a blood clot in your lungs by performing a special type of CT scan. We tried to have you perform different types of stress tests but you could not lie flat because of chronic back discomfort. You did have an echocardiogram which did not reveal any acute cardiac pathology. Although we are not certain what the cause of your pain is, it is most likely musculoskeletal given that your whole body hurts, and we could reproduce the pain by examining you. The pain is is not related to your heart, lungs or major blood vessels in your chest. We incidentally found a small widening of your aorta which is not causing your pain, but it should be monitored with another CT scan in 6 months to make sure it is not getting bigger. Dr. ___ schedule this study for you. We also found very few atypical blood cells on your bloodwork. These are also not the cause of your pain, but a repeat blood test can be performed as an outpatient to make sure they resolve. We will give you prescriptions for pain control.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o F with PMHx of Breast CA, prior CVAs/SDH with Cerebral angiopathy, possible cirrhosis and colon polyps who p/w approximately 2 mths of RUQ pain, obstructive LFTs, biliary dilation and multiple liver lesions concerning for metastatic disease who underwent ERCP on ___ for CBD stricture now s/p sphincterotomy with PD and CBD stent placed. Pt/family and PCP are all aware of these highly concerning findings and likelihood of metastatic biliary or pancreatic malignancy. Path pending at discharge and pt returned home with her daughter. I will contact them early next week once the final path is available. Biliary stricture, obstructive LFTs with RUQ pain: now s/p ERCP with sphincterotomy and PD/CBD stents. Pt did well post procedure. LFTs were downtrending and pt was tolerating regular diet without difficulty. CBD cytology was pending at discharge. RUQ pain with diffuse liver lesions and right rectus muscle mass: I spoke at length today with Ms ___ daughter/HCP about the concern for metastatic disease, possibly of biliary origin. Of note, her breast exam did not reveal any abnormal masses and recent mammogram was reassuring. Pt underwent additional biopsy of the right anterior abdominal wall lesion that was identified on imaging as a likely met. I explained to the patient that we are concerned about cancer and will know more once the biopsy returns next week.
