Pain was managed with Tylenol ___ TID and pt was given a prescription for OXycodone 2.5-5mg for breakthrough pain in addition to aggressive bowel regimen. #Cerebral amyloid angiopathy #History of CVAs and recent SDH after fall at ___: Per daughter has both expressive and receptive aphasia, though pt is able to answer most simple questions appropriately on exam. Pt is not on any anticoagulation. Hypertension: continued amlodipine Anxiety/Depression: continued escitalopram 10mg daily #HLD - continue fenofibrate #GERD - continue omeprazole 40 mg PO DAILY Transition issues: - new finding of multiple liver mets in setting of CBD stricture. Family, pt and PCP were all notified of high suspicion for metastatic biliary malignancy. Path pending at discharge, plan to discuss further with daughter once path available early next week. - will need referral to ___ clinic once path complete - recommend f/u LFTs next week with PCP to ensure continued downtrending > 45min spent on clinical care today including > 50% spent with patient and family reviewing the findings, concern for new diagnosis of metastatic malignancy and discussing next steps.
summary: You were admitted after an ERCP with placement of two stents to open up a narrowing in the common bile duct. You underwent CT imaging that revealed concerning lesions in your liver and you underwent a biopsy to obtain additional tissue to review in pathology. These results are currently pending and I will continue you as soon as these are available to discuss next steps. You are welcome to use Tylenol for pain and can use oxycodone 2.5mg for pain not responding to Tylenol. It is important to continue stool softeners to prevent constipation while you are taking oxycodone. We hope you have a pleasant weekend with family.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo male with h/o HIV (CD4 552, VL 65 copies/ml on ___, HCV c/b stage II cirrhosis, COPD GOLD stage II, chronic pain and spinal stenosis on methadone/narcotics who presents with right arm pain/redness/swelling and fever x3d. # Abscess: The patient's abscess appears consistent with abscesses he has developed in the past to minor trauma, notably in ___ after a cat scratch to his left leg, for which he was started on doxycycline and augmentin. The patient initially had an I&D in the emergency department, but unfortunately cultures were not sent from his I&D. The patient was started on IV Vancomycin in the ED, and was continued on IV Vancomycin for a day's duration while on the medical floor, with improvement in the pain and erythema of the 6x6 packed abscess. Packing was changed on the day of discharge, with minimal pain controlled with a one time dose of oxycodone 5 mg in addition to the patient's home regimen. As culture data was not sent, it was unclear what his underlying microorganism was; however, based on prior culture data, he appeared to have an MSSA, and after discussion with his outpatient providers, it was decided to treat him with Keflex ___ mg BID for a total antibiotic course of 7 days (patient was sent home with 5 days antibiotics). Blood cultures x 2 sent in the ED were still pending at the time of discharge. He refused to have home ___ for the packing and unpacking of his wounds, indicating that his PCA will do this for him. He was sent home with packing supplies for dressing changes until his meeting with Dr. ___ on ___. #H/o spinal stenosis w/ chronic pain: Has narcotics contract in ___ and has been seen by the pain service in the past. He is on methadone as well as home narcotics, ___ clinic contacted on admission and pt was given a letter with last dose on discharge. The patient's oxycontin 40 mg QID PRN was made standing during his hospitalization. Per his PCP/ID MD, he was dsicharged with 4 x 1 doses of 5 mg oxycodone to supplement his current medication for pain while changing and packing his abscess wound. # HIV: CD4 552, VL 65 copies/ml on ___. Hx of opportunistic infections, but now well-controlled with efavirenz, lamivudine-zidovudine, and tenofovir; this regimen was continued while he was in-house. #Chronic nausea: Per pt, this is secondary to his anti-retroviral therapy. He was controlled in house with IV Zofran, and was discharged on his home promethazine #COPD: Stable throughout his course. He was written for his home inhalers and nebs prn. #Depression: No SI. Per pt, this was well controlled and his mood was stable. Home citalopram was continued. #HTN: The patient was noted to be hypertense during his admission, with BPs occasionally 160/100s. Given the patient's pain and recent infection, blood pressure was not lower with anti-hypertensive agents for fear of dropping BPs much lower upon discharge with improved pain and infection control. As an outpatient, the patient's BP should be serially monitored with an eye on potential pharmacologic intervention. # CODE STATUS: Full (confirmed with patient) # CONTACT: ___ (his home PCA) ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because a bug bite which you sustained on ___ developed into an abscess. Your abscess was drained in the emergency department, and you were admitted to the medical floor for further monitoring and treatment with IV antibiotics. After IV antiboitics, you infection improved a great deal, and we felt comfortable sending you home on oral antibiotics, to be followed closely by your infectious disease doctor. START oxycodone 5 mg as needed for dressing changes; please time your dose of oxycodone to your PRN doses. START Keflex ___ mg twice a day for the next 5 days (for a total 7 day course of antibiotics). CHANGE your dose of OxyContin 40 mg every 6 hours as needed for pain to OxyContin 40 mg every 6 hours We did not make any other changes to your medications, so please continue to take them as you normally have been.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Focal neurological findings, right arm: According to patient, she began to experience right arm weakness soon after the syncopal event in the shower on ___. Her arm feels "not right" and "floppy." Neuro exam shows weakness in R arm with give-way and R pronator drift. Initially there was concern for TIA/stroke, ___ paralysis (if syncopal event was seizure), cervical stenosis and neurology was consulted. Inpt neuro consult team thought the PE findings were functional, given strength exam with give-way weakness and sensory findings with non-dermatomal distribution. She underwent MRI head & MRI c-spine which were unremarkable. Given functional nature of strength testing, conversion disorder is possibility. Pt was reporting improvement in symptoms with conservative care and there was no organic cause discovered to explain her symptoms. . # Syncope: According to report, she had episodes of syncope which were not thought to be cardiogenic and assumed to be vasovagal. The patient reports that she has not had an episode of syncope since high school. Differential diagnosis includes vasovagal syncope exacerbated by dehydration, reduced PO, viral illness; complicated migraine; seizure; cardiogenic causes (although EKG not suggestive of WPW, HOCM, QT prolongation). Outside records from Children's, which are from ___, demonstrated normal echo, EKG, and exercise stress test. Overnight telemetry showed no events, but friend of patient witnessed another event of almost "passing out" which sounds like orthostatic syncope (occured upon standing and associated with some lightheadedness). EKG obtained at time was normal. Therefore, Holter monitoring/repeat echo does not seem necessary. The patient has been informed that she may follow up for autonomics testing. . # Anemia with microcytosis: Most likely iron-deficiency anemia, especially since patient describes having menstrual bleeding for five weeks. Patient's serum iron and TIBC were normal (Iron studies as follows: Iron: 98, calTIBC: 445, Ferritn: 6.6, TRF: 342). Ferritin low at 6.6, supportive of iron-deficiency anemia. Patient was provided iron supplementation. . # Hypothyroidism: Continued home dose of levothyroxine. TSH normal @ 2.8 . # Depression: Continue home dose of citalopram.
summary: You were admitted because you had an episode of passing out, and you also developed some right arm weakness while you were in the hospital. We reviewed your medical records from ___ ___, which reassured us that you did not have any problems with your heart. Your EKG and telemetry were also not concerning. We got MRI pictures of your brain and neck, which were both normal. Again, that is reassuring news. We had Neurology come to see you, and they did not feel you had any neurological findings. Your episode of passing out may have been because of dehydration or the aftereffects of your recent illness. We did discover that you had a mild anemia, likely due from iron-deficiency after your recent long period of menstruation. You should take a iron supplement three times a day to help with the anemia. Add an iron supplement three times a day.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ has had several recent admissions for chronic abdominal pain here at ___. We initially considered surgical possibilities for her pain, but none were apparent upon review of her clinical status and laboratory assessment. A CT scan at an OSH showed no evidence of ileus or obstruction. Given a slowly decreasing hematocrit, GI was consulted; there was however no evidence of frank melena or hematochezia and her anemia was felt to be secondary to malabsorption/iron deficiency in the setting of her complex bowel anatomy. Her G tube output was also clear. We felt constipation may have been contributing to her pain so we started her on an aggressive bowel regimen. Her pain was otherwise managed with dilaudid 4 mg q4. She had no evidence of hepatitis given the prior history of tylenol ingestion; her APAP level was negligible and there was no need for NAC administration. Her abdominal pain improved slightly with improved bowel movements. Her G tube was kept to gravity and remained clear (no need for suctioning). We felt that a lot of her abdominal pain was exacerbated by her underlying psychiatric comorbidities including depression and significant family stressors at home. In the setting of prior tylenol intake (although she claims was non-intentional) and self-inflicted injury (per OSH report and numerous bruises throughout her body and RN witnessed self abuse - pinching, etc) we felt her depression needed to be addressed directly in the inpatient setting rather than as an outpatient. Working with social work, we discussed her depression and suggested an inpatient voluntary psychiatry admission. Psychiatry was consulted; after a multidisciplinary discussion, Ms ___ did feel that inpatient psychiatry would help her with her depression and acknowledged depression as a major barrier for improvement in her quality of life. Her celexa was increased to 40 mg from 20 mg and she was given trazadone for assistance with insomnia. She did endorse passive suicidal ideation intermittently; she was initiated on seroquel for anxiety and her lorazepam was weaned to 1 mg TID. She was then transferred to ___ for ongoing inpatient psychiatry care.
summary: You were admitted because of increasing abdominal pain and increasing need for tylenol and pain medications. You had reported blood in your stool; to investigate this possibility further, we consulted the GI team and the general surgery team. Although you did have low blood counts, we did not feel this was because of active bleeding from your gut. Your low blood counts are probably because of low absorption of iron; for this reason we continued your iron supplementation. We also gave you an aggressive bowel regimen to keep your bowels moving; constipation may have been playing a role in your increasing abdominal pain. You also had an elevated tylenol level at ___ prior to your admission here; our tests revealed your tylenol level was non-toxic so this did not require further work-up. We had psychiatry see you because of your long-standing depression and they started you on Seroquel and decreased the amount of Ativan you were needing. We agreed as a team and support your decision to pursue inpatient psychiatry as a way to help bring your depression under control so that you are more able to manage some of the other medical and social issues that are present in your life.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M h/o CAD s/p CABG ___ and recent BMS (___) on ASA, Plavix, as well as h/o PUD dx ___ ago presents with daily melenotic black stools since ___ and now a drop in Hb from 11.3 (___) to 8.2 (today). . # GI Bleeding: Given nausea, melanotic stool, significant hematocrit drop without any bright red blood per rectum suggested upper GI bleeding. Patient was started on IV BID PPI and was given 2 units of pRBCs with appropriate hematocrit response. He had an EGD which showed gastritis and 1.5 cm clean based non-bleeding duodenal ulcer. Even though the ulcer was non-bleeding, it was most likely the source of bleeding. Patient has known diverticulosis based on colonoscopy in ___ however diverticulosis was considered to be less likely source of bleeding. Biopsies were taken during EGD which were pending at time of discharge. Patient's hematocrit remained stable during the rest of this hospitalization and he did not have melanotic stool on the day of discharge. He tolerated his diet well. He was switched to high dose po PPI and will follow up with is gastroenterologist for further management. Per the recommendation of our GI team, if patient's biopsies are H. pylori negative then his gastroenterologist may consider repeat stool Ag or urease breath test in 2 weeks to assess for h-pyrlori infection. . # CAD: s/p BMS stent in ___. No chest pain during this admission. Continued aspirin and plavix. # Type II Diabetes: HbA1c of 9.9 in ___. Glucose levels well controlled. Continued patient on home dose of lantus and kept patient in sliding scale insulin. . # Hypertension: Continued Losartan, continued isosorbide mononitrate. Metoprolol initially held in the setting of anemia to allow appropriate HR response. Metoprolol added on the day of discharge. . # Chronic Kidney Disease: Most likely secondary to diabetes and hypertension. Cr remained at baseline level of 1.5. . # Hyperlipidemia: Continued Atorvastatin . # BPH: Continued Terazosin . # Depression: Continued Venlafaxine . # CODE: Full Code . Transitions of Care: - GI biopsy pathology results pending at time of discharge. - Patient was started on BID high dose omeprazole - He will follow up with his gastroenterologist for further monitoring. If GI biopsy is negative for H. pylori then his gastroenterologist may consider repeat stool Ag or urease breath test in 2 weeks when further out from acute bleed.
summary: You were admitted with black melanotic stools suggesting gastrointestinal bleeding with low blood counts. You were transfused two units of blood with appropriate increase in your blood counts. You had an endoscopy which showed inflammation in your stomach as well as a duodenal ulcer. You were started on twice daily dose of protonix which you should continue to take on daily basis. Biopsies of your stomach and doudenum were pending at the time of dischage. Our gastroenterology team will follow up on the results of those biopsies. If your GI biopsy is negative for H. pylori infection than you gastroenterologsit should consider repeat stool Antigen or urease breath test in 2 weeks. On the day of discharge you did not have any significant black stools and your blood counts were stable.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: ___ is a ___ year old woman with cholangiocarcinoma metastatic to the liver (on palliative chemo), prior episodes of ascending cholangitis and C. difficile admitted ___ for biliary obstruction and recurrent ascending cholangitis, s/p ERCP ___ with biliary stent replacement c/b post-procedural septic shock requiring ICU transfer/pressors, transferred to the floor ___. ___ blood cultures from OSH growing Pseudomonas. Subsequently with rising leukocytosis and obstructive jaundice (in absence of fevers, n/v or abdominal discomfort) in spite of ongoing antibiotic treatment with pip/tazo, found to have recurrent stent obstruction, now s/p repeat ERCP on ___ with metal stent placement. Briefly improved but then developed recurrent obstruction and cholangitis, ultimately requiring PTBD on ___. HOSPITAL COURSE BY PROBLEM: # Recurrent malignant biliary obstruction with cholangitis s/p ERCP decompression ___ and ___, with subsequent recurrence of obstruction and cholangitis, s/p PTBD on ___ # Pseudomonal bacteremia (+ bcx ___ # Septic shock # Underlying cholangiocarcinoma metastatic to liver Stent placed on ___ for recurrent obstruction and cholangitis. Post procedure course initially complicated by sepsis requiring ICU. Stent obstruction recurred and patient required new stent placement on ___ (only able to stent L side). Several days later, again developed recurrent obstructive/cholangitic picture, and ERCP team recommended external drainage by PTBD. Patient underwent L and R PTBDs on ___ with ___ with subsequent improvement in her WBC and LFTs. After sustained improvement, she subsequently underwent ___ drain exchanges on ___ and ___, underwent additional ERCP on ___ where one metal stent was removed. She will follow-up with ___ in ~1 week for biliary drain removal. She was evaluated by ID for pseudomonal bacteremia. She was recommended to continue zosyn through ___ (2 weeks from source control on ___ Outpatient onc team: Dr. ___ at ___ and Dr. ___ at ___; touched base with Dr. ___ on ___, and plan is likely to switch to ___ given progression of disease # Macrocytic anemia, multifactorial, in absence of overt blood loss Contributing factors likely include inflammation, underlying malignancy, prior chemotherapy, hypothyroid. Received 2 U pRBCs # Hyponatremia: Na 128 on admission, fluctuating since that time. Seemed to correlate with times of intravascular volume depletion and improved with fluids. #Coagulopathy Likely nonspecific in setting of malnutrition, sepsis, and possibly her malignancy # Hx of C.diff: No evidence of C.diff currently. Continue PO Vanco initiated in ICU for C.diff ppx while on broad-spectrum antibx (___). Can stop PO vancomycin when no longer taking systemic antibiotics (___). # Hypoalbuminemia # Malnutrition Albumin 1.8->2.0, likely in setting of ongoing infection and malignancy. Nutrition consulted, patient offered supplements. #Depression - Continued home citalopram 40mg PO daily #Hypothyroidism - Continued levothyroxine 137mcg PO daily - Endocrine consulted - TSH improving on ___ patient should follow-up with PCP vs local endocrinologist in ___ after discharge #GERD - Continued pantoprazole 40 mg PO QD ========================= Transitional issues: -Follow-up with ___ within 1 week for drain removal -Recommend CBC with diff, chemistry, LFTs checked within ___ days -Continue zosyn and PO vancomycin through ___ -Recommend Close on follow-up with Dr. ___ -Monitor TSH and titration of levothyroxine -Nutritional counseling and monitoring -Monitoring of anemia ========================= >30 minutes spent on complex discharge
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with an obstruction of your bile duct from your bile duct cancer. A stent was placed on ___, but this became blocked, and you needed another stent on ___. However your bile duct became obstructed again, and so you needed a biliary drain to be placed by the interventional radiologist. You were also treated with an antibiotic for the infection in your bile duct, as well as a bacteria that traveled into your bloodstream from the bile duct. You will need to continue this antibiotic, called zosyn, until ___. While you are taking zosyn, please also take antibiotic vancomycin to prevent recurrence of c diff of c diff. You can stop vancomycin on ___ as well. You improved with the above interventions. After you were stabilized, the ERCP team was also to remove one of the stents. The Interventional Radiology team placed new stents and converted the internal biliary drains into external biliary drains that you can go home with. They will arrange for you to follow-up with them in clinic in about one week.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with history of noninsulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, hypertension/hyperlipidemia, and extensive psychiatric history who presented from ___ with chest pain. Active Issues: (1)Chest pain: Despite low suspicion for primary cardiac etiology in the setting of young age, female gender, multiple reassuring EKGs, and negative cardiac enzymes, pharmacologic stress test was performed for more definitive exclusion of coronary artery disease, resulting in no anginal-type symptoms or ischemic EKG changes; 2-day cardiac perfusion study was similarly negative (though extensive attenuation and motion artifact was present), with increased left ventricular cavity size with normal systolic function. CXR on admission was negative for focal infiltrate while CTA in the setting of concurrent back pain was negative for dissection and proximal pulmonary embolus. There were no events on telemetry. Ultimately, given reproducibility on palpation, musculokeletal etiology in the setting of recent fall was suspected, as was gastrointestinal etiology in the setting of concurrent postprandial right upper quadrant pain as below. Organic pain likely was augmented by significant anxiety. She received pain control and anxiolytics as needed. (2)Right upper quadrant abdominal pain: She experienced intermittent right upper quadrant abdominal pain, typically preceded by meals. Although CT abdomen/pelvis on the day prior to the current admission was negative for intraabdominal, including gallbladder pathology, concern remained for gallbladder cholic. Right upper quadrant ultrasound was negative for gallbladder pathology, and frequency of painful episodes decreased with restriction to low-fat diet. Outpatient gastrointestinal and/or surgical follow-up is advised in the event that she continues to experience symptoms, with consideration of HIDA scan if indicated. (3)Mental health: She has an extensive psychiatric history, including bipolar disorder, major depressive disorder, suicidal ideation with wrist cutting, posttraumatic stress disorder, and borderline personality disorder, and is presently admitted to ___ under ___, having presented reportedly voluntarily for suicidal ideation. She denied suicidal/homicidal ideation throughout admission and did not require a 1:1 sitter. Home psychiatric regimen, including Abilify, Cogentin, Luvox, lamotrigine, and Topamax, was continued without modification. She was evaluated by the psychiatry service and was not felt to require further inpatient psychiatric care, with discharge home advised. (4)Status post fall: She experienced a mechanical fall, striking the right side of her head without loss of consciousness, on ambulation to the bathroom on hospital day 4. Detailed neurologic exam was nonfocal and head exam atraumatic/normocephalic at that time, hence noncontrast head CT was deferred. She remained neurologically intact throughout admission. (5)Gastroesophageal reflux: Home omeprazole was increased from 20mg to 40mg daily, without significant relief of chest or right upper quadrant abdominal pain, hence she was discharged on home dose of 20mg daily. Inactive Issues: (1)Asthma: Home Symbicort and albuterol were continued. (2)Hypertension: Although she carries a history of hypertension, she remained normotensive on the floor off antihypertensives; she does not appear to be on a home antihypertensive regimen at present. (3)Hyperlipidemia: Home Zocor was continued. (4)Hypothyroidism: Home Synthroid was continued. (5)Noninsulin-dependent diabetes mellitus: Home Actos was held in favor of gentle Humalog insulin sliding scale, with Actos resumed at discharge. Transitional Issues: - Outpatient gastrointestinal and/or surgical follow-up is advised for right upper quadrant pain, with consideration of HIDA scan if clinically indicated. - Pending studies: None. - Code status: Full.
summary: As you know, you were admitted for chest/upper abdominal pain, back pain, shortness of breath, and lightheadedness. Multiple studies of your heart, lungs, abdomen, and major blood vessels showed no evidence of injury/dysfunction. Your stress test of the heart showed that your heart is okay. It is likely that your symptoms related to residual pain from your recent fall, exacerbated by anxiety. Given that your chest and abdominal pain seemed to relate to meals, further evaluation of your gastrointestinal tract may be needed in the outpatient setting. However, you did have a CT scan and an ultrasound of your abdomen, both of which showed NO gallbladder problems, NO acute abdominal problems. In the meantime, you are advised to eat a low-fat diet to minimize recurrence of pain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: A/P: ___ p/w syncope and upper respiratory symptoms found to have influenza A. . # Influenza A: The patient tested positive for influenza A. Likely contributing to syncopal episode and explaining URI symptoms, decreased PO intake and left shift. She was started on Oseltamvir 75mg BID x5d given <72h symptoms in hospitalized pt with asthma and diabetes. She was given supportive care with fluids, and nebulizers, zofran prn nausea, tylenol prn fever, guaifenisen prn cough. . #Syncope: Likely secondary to influenza A and decreased PO intake. Patient had witnessed episode and did not have tonic-clonic movement, and was without post-ictal phase or loss of control of bladder or bowels. EKG was unchanged, cardiac enzymes were negative. CT head/spine in the ED were non-concerning for acute injury. U/a was negative. Ucx neg, bcx pending. Syncopal episode may have been exacerbated by polypharmacy with oxycodone and clonazepam. Pt was given IVFs and orthostatics were negative prior to dispo. PO intake was encouraged. Pt was continued on home doses vicodin, tylenol prn pain from fall. . #DM2: Controlled on metformin monotherapy with aspirin ppx for CVD. Normal alb/cre at baseline. mild neuropathy at baseline. Last A1c (___): 7.1, last urine alb/creatinine (___): 3.9, LDL ___. Metformin was held and pt was covered with ISS. Discharged on home metformin. . # Anxiety/Depression: continued home clonazepam, citalopram . # Pain: on home vicodin ___, gave hydrocodone 5mg q6h prn, tylenol ___ q6h prn pain/fever, celebrex ___ daily. . # GERD: continued ranitidine 150mg BID, omeprazole 20mg daily . # Asthma: Gave albuterol nebs prn wheezing, increased home flovent 110mcg to Flovent 220mcg to be continued x1mo and return to previous dosing. . # HTN: held home lisinopril, restarted on dispo. . # HL: continued home simvastatin. . # Allergies: continue loratadine 10mg daily . # FEN: diet, replete electrolytes prn, IVFs/encourage PO fluid intake . # PPX: PPI+H2 blocker, heparin sq/pneumoboots . # Code: full . # Social issues: pt stated that during admission she slept better than she has since her husband died because she has been sleeping on the couch because she cannot bear to sleep in the bed where they used to sleep together, but cannot afford to move to another apt. Should continue to follow-up with social work as an outpt.
summary: You were admitted for an episode of fainting and found to have the flu. The flu likely caused this episode as you were weak from infection and had not been eating and drinking enough. We did CT scans of your head and spine and found that there was no injury to your bones from falling. We did tests to show that your fall was not due to a heart problem. We have been treating you with intravenous fluids and antibiotics. You were also found to have some worsening of your asthma due to flu infection. We treated this with increased frequency of your home medications. You will be discharged on a higher dose of Flovent which you should use for a month and then return to your regular dose. You should expect to continue to feel sick, with muscle aches, and some weakness for the next several days. It is very important that you continue to drink a lot of fluids to prevent feeling lightheaded or passing out.

text: Brief Hospital Course: []BRIEF CLINICAL HISTORY: Assessment: ___ yo M with metastatic renal CA s/p IL-2 and Avastin, Sunitinib with recent cord compression s/p T8 laminectomy and radiation,s/p C6 Corpectomy and C5-7, anterior fusion on ___ now with progressive disease, thoracic cord compression with urinary and fecal incontinence, urinary retention, acute renal failure. []ISSUES: #Home Hospice needs: Patient to go home with hospice for CMO care. He is DNR/DNI/CMO. His only current PO meds are dexamethasone, synthroid, and sertraline. There is a concern for aspiration with pills, so his only critical med that needs to be converted is dexamethasone; he has been taking long term and we want to avoid adrenal crisis with abrubt cessation. The hospice liason is working on a concentrated liquid formation of dexamethasone. He will be sent home with dex and dilaudid and methadone. Plan tentatively for dispo today or ___. The IV nurse team and the interventional radiology teams both attempted PICC placement and were unsuccessful. The plan is to send him home with a subcutaneous PCA. # metastatic RCC: progression of disease (particularly rib, spine lesions, chest wall, mediastinum) despite everolimus and with new cord lesions/pathologic fracture and cord compression, functional status is likely to be very limited. The patient and family was seen by palliative care during a family meeting where Dr. ___ was present. They presented to the patient that the only option is radiation therapy, chemo and surgery are no longer viable options. # spinal cord compression: His presentation and very limited imaging is consistent with thoracic cord compression. Given his progression of metastatic RCC elsewhere, he was deeemed not to be a surgical candidate and he was admitted primarily for palliation. Given his urinary retention, a foley was placed. Patient will receive palliative XRT for stabilization of lower thoracic spine lesion. He received his first dose of palliative thoracic spine XRT on ___ with the plan for a ___ut he refused further treatments after the ___ session. Patient originally has need to be in hard cervical collar until after 3 months from his cervical operation (around ___ however, he is now CMO and has it removed. # Acute renal failure: resolved. initially pre-renal in etiology. Patient's Cr 0.8 on ___. # Anemia: chronic, HCT 20.5 on morning after admission, down from 28, likely ___ hemodilution. Improved s/p 2 units of blood on admission, with Hct ~31. # UTI: patient w/ ua initially that was suggestive of UTI. Culture demonstrated staph aureus coag negative. All antibiotics stopped on ___ once patient became CMO. # Depression/anxiety: cont sertraline if patient tolerates PO. # Hypothyroid: cont levothyroxine if patient tolerates PO. # FEN: Regular diet if patient tolerates PO. # PPX: PO diet #CODE STATUS: DNR/DNI/CMO []TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: 1.) Patient is CMO. 2.) Patient is taking steroids and must continue taking them as prescribed. It there is a clinical indication to stop, he MUST be slowly tapered off the steroids to prevent adrenal crisis. 3.) Patient may remove c-collar for comfort with the understanding that his neck will be unstable.
summary: You were admitted to the ___ ___ ___ for incontinence and spinal cord compression. You were evaluated by neurosurgery and neurology and were found to be a sub-optimal candidate for surgery. The radiation oncology service attempted palliative radiation therapy to the thoracic spine. A family meeting was held with your primary oncologist Dr. ___ and in the subsequent days, steps were taken to make you more comfortable and transition you to home hospice care. You were placed on a patient controlled analgesia (PCA) pump to provide you more effective pain relief. You will go home with this pump and will continue with steroids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ y/o M with h/o CAD (s/p CABG), atrial fibrillation, dCHF, pulmonary HTN, chronic upper GI bleed from GAVE, chronic kidney disease presenting in the setting of anemia. # Acute on Chronic Normocytic anemia: The patient has known GAVE with multiple recent admissions with EGD and thermal therapy. Last EGD on ___ showing duffse erythema and nodularity in antrum with active oozing c/w GAVE s/p APC thermal therapy also with grade II non-bleeding varacies. Pt with Hct of 24 on admission, which is down from 28 on discharge on ___. Pt was given 1 unit of PRBCs. Initially treated with IV PPI and transitioned back to home PO BID PPI on HOD 2. GI evaluated patient and felt that EGD should wait until previously scheduled EGD on ___. Hct was stable at 24.9 discharge. # Asterixis, Hepatic encephalopathy, Cirrhosis: CT chest on ___ demonstrated evidence of liver disease and possible cirrhosis. Hepatitis panel negative and iron studies not consistent with hemochromotosis. EGD on last admission showed grade II varacies. Abd US this admission showed fatty liver, splenomegally, trace ascites, and patent hepatic vasculature. AFP normal. Given asterixis on exam pt started on lactulose; he improved with therapy. Pt to follow up with GI as an outpatient. # Chronic SOB: Likely multifactorial, from diastolic heart failure, pulm HTN, and anemia. Per patient transfusions help shortness of breath however patient poor historian. Patient able to speak in full sentences and was saturating at baseline on 2L NC. Pt with BNP 2400 (down from 3600), however appeared volume up on exam and weight up 2 kg since last admission. He was given one dose of IV lasix and then maintained on home regimen. # Chronic kidney injury: Admission Cr of 2.0 at recent baseline of 1.7-2.0. Cr trended and 1.6 on discharge. # OSA/Pulmonary Hypertension: Patient with TTE e/o severe pHTN however has never undergone a right heart cath. Pt continued on CPAP and home 2L NC. # dCHF: pt with BNP of 2400 which is down form 3600 previously. Weight up 2 kg since last admission. Pt feels SOB improved after transfusion, however appeared volume up on exam. Recieved one dose of IV lasix and then maintained on home lasix regimen. Discharge weight of 117 kg. # Atrial fibrillation: Rate controlled with metoprolol and digoxin. Was on coumadin in the past (~ ___ year ag) but this was discontinued given gastric bleeding and he now takes baby aspirin. Pt with bradycardia to ___, asymptomatic. Metoprolol decreased to 6.25 mg BID (and discharged on 12.5 long acting daily) and continued home digoxin and ASA. # Coronary artery disease: S/P CABG. Patient was without cardiovascular complaint. Of note, lisinopril had been discontinued on previous admission ___ ___ however had been restarted at rehab (5mg daily) was initially held and then restarted on ___. Continued home ASA, statin, and BB (reduced dose). # Chronic pain: Chronic pain in lower back and hips. Likely pain in legs related to chronic hip and back pain. CK checked and initially minimally elevated to 43 and down trended to 29 on recheck. Home oxycodone and lidoderm patch continued. # Gout: continued renally dosed allopurinol # Neuropathy: continued renally dosed gabapentin # Depression: Continued citalopram, mirtazapine, and lorazepam # BPH: continued tamsulosin Transitional: - Consider outpatient sleep study and right heart cath - EGD to be done ___ - Needs GI follow up re: GAVE and liver disease - Repeat lab work to monitor CBC and Cr - Consider up-titration of metoprolol in light of atrial fibrillation
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after you felt short of breath and leg pain at your iron infusion. You were found to have a blood count that was a little low. We gave you a blood transfusion and your shortness of breath improved. We treated your pain and feel that your leg pain is related to your chronic back and hip pain. You also reported tremors. Your lab work was reassuring, not revealing a cause of your tremors. We started you on a medication called lactulose to keep your bowel movements regular as in people with liver disease the build up of toxins can cause tremors. Your tremors are most like related to your kidney disease, liver disease, and heart disease.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Palpitations: Unclear etiology but possibly due to sinus tachycardia of unclear etiology. Cardiac ischemia unlikely given negative troponins across 24 hours and lack of significant EKG changes. Cardiac function unlikely given lack of structural abnormalities on echocardiogram. Pericarditis considered given mild initial elevation in troponin (0.04) and possible pericardial effusion seen on bedside echo in ED however no evidence of pericarditis on follow up echo. No evidence of tamponade physiology, pulsus ~8, follow up troponins all < 0.01. PE exceedingly unlikely given lack of risk factors, negative D-dimer, and oxygen saturations > 98% on room air. Pheo unlikely given overall symptoms. Buproprion was reduced given possibility of causing palpitations. Given low likelihood of this etiology, however, and patient's history of depression, this medication should be increased back to the prior dose if this reduction fails to improve the patient's symptoms. Patient was discharged on a trial of an increased dose of metoprolol and follow up with endocrinology. # Dyspnea: Patient gives slightly varying story that ranges from significant dyspnea on exertion to just overall fatigue. Discussed collectively and felt overall to be likely consistent with fatigue rather than actual dyspnea on exertion. CXR clear, no evidence of PE (as above), and patient able to ambulate without drop in oxygen saturations below 97%. Of note, on ambulation patient reported feeling tired, but did not experience a significant rise in HR. # Fatigue, Anxiety, Somnolence: Unclear etiology, but likely psychiatric in nature. Symptoms concerning for hypothyroidism, however, TFTs all normal, morning cortisol level normal as well. Psychiatry consulted. Patient discharged with outpatient psychiatry follow up. # Tachycardia: Patient observed to be in sinus tachycardia during most of hospitalization with HR ~ 80s to 110s. Etiology most likely psychogenic/psychiatric. No concerning arrythmias on telemetry or EKGs. No evidence of hypovolemia, with stable blood pressures, negative orthostatics, good PO intake, no lab abnormalities concerning for hypovoemia, and good urine output. No evidence of PE as discussed above. No evidence of sepsis. Of note, patient was observed to be anxious during much of this admission, especially in the presence of his father, and his heart rate was higher during much of the time his father was present (which was during much of the afternoons of both days). # Depression: Patient actively denying feelings of depression on current admission. Bupropion reduced to 300 daily with concern for cause of etiology as discussed above. Patient to follow up with psychiatry within week of discharge. Denying any suicidal thoughts. # History of Hypothyroidism: Euthyroid, TSH 3.6. Patient continued on current dose of 100mcg thyroxine daily. # Hypogonadism: Continued testosterone cream.
summary: You were admitted for palpitations, fatigue, shortness of breath and difficulty concentrating. While you were here you were here we ran several tests on you to try to determine the cause of your symptoms. Our tests suggest that you were not having a heart attack. Your heart rate is generally fast, which can cause feelings of palpitations, but we did not see any arrythmias that are concerning at this time. As we have discussed, we have reduced the dose of your buproprion to 150mg twice a day, as this medication is sometimes known to cause palpitations. If this does not help you should discuss raising the dose back to your previous dose with your psychiatrist. We have also raised the dose of your metoprolol, since this medication may help with some of your symptoms. If this medications does not help you should discuss stopping it or changing the dose with your primary care doctor or endocrinologist. We did not change any of your other medications while you were here.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with ___ notable for PUD s/p Bilroth II, CAD s/p quadruple bypass, asthma sent from PCP with melena and found to be orthostatic. # Melena/BRBPR: Initial ddx included erosions/ulcers vs post-op ischemia - if this represents an upper GI bleed. On the other hand, he gives a history of sucralfate induced constipation that led to straining and large volume bright red blood once a few days ago (hemorrhoidal v diverticular). Patient underwent serial Hcts which showed a drop on admission from prior outpatient Hct but was stable throughout the hospital admission. He had no recurrence of BRBPR or melena though did have a guaiac positive stool. Patient maintained on BID IV PPI and his carafate was held. Upper endoscopy and colonoscopy were performed. Colonoscopy showed a prominent vein which may have bleed (BRBPR) with straining. Patient instructed/Rx provided for improved bowel reg to prevent straining. Upper endoscopy showed severe gastritis likely ___ bile reflux from previous Bilroth II. Patient was discharged with Rx for bowel reg, continue Carafate, BID PPI. While initial report recommended reglan and to consider ursodial, per conversation with GI, preference was to hold Ursodial until trial as outpatient if needed. Additionally, as reglan interacts with sertraline, pt was started on erythromycin instead at discharge per GI verbal instructions. # Orthostasis: Patient noted to be orthostatic at ___ office prior to admission. Likely ___ to acute blood loss (see above). Was not orthostatic prior to discharge. Chronic issues: # IBS: Held home Librax as NF. Restarted at d/c. # Asthma: Continued home albuterol PRN, flovent, ipratropium # HLD: Continued home zetia, rosuvastatin # CAD: Continued metoprolol, ASA # Depression: Continued home sertraline # Chronic pain: Continued home gabapentin # Health maintance: Continued MVI, Omega 3; Held phytosterol as NF - restarted at discharge. Transitional issues: -CODE: DNR/DNI (confirmed with pt and reports that wife is aware) -EMERGENCY CONTACT: ___ (wife) ___ ___ -Pathology pending at discharge but now finalized: Gastric mucosal biopsies: Oxyntic mucosa with changes of marked reactive (chemical-type) gastropathy -Will f/u with GI and will likely need repeat EGD in 8 weeks.
summary: You were admitted with hypotension when changing position (called orthostasis) and black stools and bleeding with a bowel movement. The black stools also represent bleeding in your GI tract. The hypotension was also due to the bleeding that you had. We monitored your blood counts closely and they remained stable, though they were lower than is normal for you. We consulted the gastroenterology service who performed a colonoscopy and upper endoscopy. They found severe gastritis. Your colonscopy showed no evidence of bleeding but you did have prominent veins, which could bleed if you strain to have a bowel movement.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ metastatic KRAS mutated NSCLC who presents from clinic with fatigue and confusion. #BRAIN METASTASES: #___: current presentation with difficulty related to short term memory, following multi-step directions, writing likely all related to new left occipital mass with significant vasogenic edema, and multiple new cerebellar masses likely representing metastasis from ___. Current ___ treatment (nivolumab) on hold since ___ for autoimmune hepatitis (LFTs now normalized). Seen by neurosurgery on admission, who recommended dexamethasone and keppra. s/p dex bolus. Initial concern for hypophysitis (given nivolumab) on admission - TSH 2.7, LH 47, FSH 99, FT4 1.4, Cortisol 13.4 - making likelihood of hypophysitis less likely. Extensive discussions on ___ with outpatient oncology team (Dr ___, radiation oncology, neurosurgery, family. Outcome was to pursue palliative WBRT - initiated on ___. She will receive 14 treatments to C2 whole brain, followed by short break and then a CyberKnife boost to occipital region. Started on keppra 500mg BID and dexamethasone 4mg q6hrs - will continue for at least one week with potential taper following. WIll be done at the discretion of radiation oncology. #SVC SYNDROME: Related to disease progression, improved after XRT ___. Switched home dose of enoxaparin 1mg/kg bid to 1.5mg/kg daily (90mg daily). #EMPHYSEMA: No e/o exacerbation on admission. Continued Tiotropium Bromide 1 CAP IH DAILY, albuterol sulfate 90 mcg/actuation inhalation QID:PRN. #HYPOTHYROIDISM: TSH, free T4 WNL on admission. Continued home synthroid 50mcg daily #CKD STAGE III: Cr 1.4 on admission, baseline 1.2-1.3. Cr 1.2 on discharge. #PRURITIS: Unclear etiology per her onc team. Continued home topical medications. #DEPRESSION: continued home bupropion 150mg BID TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== #MEMANTINE: started on ___ for cognitive preservation while receiving radiation. Uptitrating schedule as follows: - ___: 5mg in AM and 5mg in ___ - ___: 5mg in AM and 10mg in ___ - ___: 10mg in AM and 10mg in ___ (at this point, you will continue to take this dose each day) #DEXAMETHASONE: started on ___. Will continue 4mg q6hrs until ___, then potential taper, but will be done based on clinical status. Will be followed by radiation oncology daily while she receives treatment, their team may decide to taper. #KEPPRA: started on ___. Will continue for seizure prophylaxis. #ENOXAPARIN: discharge dose 90mg daily (1.5 mg/kg/day), to minimize injections at home. #RADIATION: will receive 14 treatments to C2 whole brain, followed by short break and then a CyberKnife boost to occipital region. First treatment on ___. #HOSPICE: per family, will initiate discussion with local hospice house following discharge. #CODE: full code
summary: You were admitted to ___ from ___ to ___ for new difficulty with memory and writing. An MRI of you brain revealed several new masses within your brain, as well as some associated swelling. Your MRI was evaluated by radiation oncology and neurosurgery, who, along with Dr ___ Dr ___ felt that the best course of action would be to radiation your whole brain, with the intention of shrinking these masses. This treatment was started on ___, and will continue when you leave the hospital. You were started on memantine, which may help preserve your memory You will continue to receive radiation treatments as prescribed by Dr ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female with PMH significant for dementia, with recent unsteadiness, multiple falls, and difficulty standing from seated position, now presenting with unwitnessed fall. # s/p fall: Given that fall was unwitnessed and patient's baseline dementia, it is difficult to tease out the exact mechanism. However, given the husband's description of recent unsteadiness, we felt mechanical fall was most likely. Patient also denied any preceding prodrome, chest pain, palpitations, or loss of conciousness (though she has baseline dementia and may not be accurate). Seizure was unlikely given lack of post-ictal state or history of incontinence. EKG in the ED looked stable from baseline, and non-contrast head CT showed no acute process. Blood pressure was 130s/80s on admission and remained normotensive throughout admission, and orthostatics were negative. Pt did complain of some right knee/hip and lumbar back pain, but XRs were negative for any acute fractures or dislocations. Her pain was well controlled with standing tylenol which should be continued as needed as an outpatient. She worked with ___ who felt she was best ___ for rehab. # Urinary tract infection: Was noted on ___ UA. Pt afebrile, no CVA tenderness. Given 5 day course of bactrim to complete as an outpatient. Urine culture is still pending as of discharge. Of note, had a leukocytosis of 12.2 on admission which normalized by HD #1 # Tachycardia: Pt noted to be tachycardic to 110s on the night of admission, but responded well to 500cc NS bolus. Heart rate remained in the ___ for the rest of admission. Her home atenolol dose of 25mg daily was changed to metoprolol 12.5 mg BID in house for better titration but changed back to atenolol on discharge. # Proximal ___ weakness: Per husband, pt with recent difficulty rising from seated position, including night prior to admission trying to get up from toilet. CK was also 260 on admission but could be secondary being down for extended period of time. We felt her proximal weakness was likely to be secondary to general deconditioning and did not pursue further workup. Pt to be discharged to rehab as above. # Dementia: Pt with progressive cognitive decline over past few years. No acute changes noticed by husband. In house she was mostly A/O x1 (self), would repeat questions to her husband and staff, and would oftentimes not recognize staff members after meeting them. We continued her home aricept 10mg daily. # Depression: Continued sertraline 50mg daily. # HTN: Pt normotensive in house and orthostatics normal. We continued home amlodipine 5mg daily, and beta blocker as above. # Code: Pt is confirmed full code both with patient and husband. # Healthcare proxy: Patient named her husband to be her HCP. # Follow up issues: -Blood culture ___ PENDING final results -Urine culture ___ PENDING final results. Pt should continue on bactrim for her UTI for now until sensitivities return.
summary: You were admitted to ___ after being found down at home after a fall. Here you were evaluated for several possible causes of your fall, and it seems most likely that you tripped and fell. We did a head CT and XRAYs of your leg and back which were negative. We feel that you most likely had a mechanical fall. Because of your recent falls, you were seen by our physcial therapist who recommended that you go to rehab to improve your strength and balance. During your stay, you were found to have evidence of a urinary tract infection. For this we are prescribing an antibiotic bactrim for you to take for 5 days.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is ___ with history of dementia (AAOx2 at baseline), HTN, HLD who was sent in from his ___ with SOB and chest pain, found to have NSTEMI which was medically managed, course complicated by tachy-brady syndome s/p PPM and GI bleed. . # NSTEMI: The patient ruled in for NSTEMI with trop of 0.16. Given the patient's dementia and goals of care, it was decided that medical management would be the best option. He was loaded with Plavix and completed 48 hours on a heparin gtt. He had an ECHO done which showed normal systolic function. - follow up with cardiology . # Tachy-brady syndrome: Patient found to have episodes of atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response in addition to episodes of bradycardia. Goals of care discussion elicited that it would be in the patient's benefit to undergo PPM placement. PPM was placed on ___ without complication. After PPM placement beta blockade was initiated to control further episodes of afib with rapid ventricular response. . # Kidney injury: Patient with acute kidney injury in ___ was 1.4 that per office records he was supposed to repeat and had not, of unclear etiology despite multiple attempts to elucidate prior baseline and course of kidney injury. Patient was admitted with Cr of 2.0 and thiazide was held. FeNa was 0.57% with urine sodium of 58. He responded minimally to fluid challenge. Upon discharge, Cr was 2.6. - recommend repeat labs drawn in one week to monitor Cr, labs to be sent to MD following at ___ . # HTN: The patient was initially continued on metoprolol and amlodipine when he was first admitted. His thiazide was held in the setting of his kidney injury. After developing bradycardic episodes metoprolol was held until PPM placed and then restarted. Hydralazine was increased for improved BP control. . # Anemia/GI bleed: Patient was noted to have downtrending hematocrit and guaiac positive stool. He received 1u PRBCs and his hematocrit stabilized. GI was consulted, who felt risk of colonoscopy in the initial 30 day period from ___ outweighed potential benefits. Colonoscopy was deferred to the outpatient setting should the family wish to pursue it. - f/u with GI for colonoscopy 3 weeks after discharge . # Alzheimer's dementia: The patient has a history of Alzheimer's dementia, AAOx2 at his baseline. While in house, he was continued on his home medications, donepezil, memantine, and olanazapine. Delirium precautions were taken, including minimizing tethers, ensuring adequate pain control, frequent reorientation, and orientation to day/night cycle. . # Delirium: Patient developed delirium, likely in the setting of PPM placement and extended hospital stay. Per family, patient had become combative in the past and often a sign the patient had the urge to urinate. No evidence of infection or leukocytosis, and delirium resolved with frequent reorientation. . # Depression: Continued on his home citalopram 20 mg daily. . # Fungal infection: The patient was noted to have fungal infection in skin folds and he was continued on antifungal powder. . # GERD: The patient's omeprazole was changed to pantoprazole in the setting of starting Plavix and the potential interactions between plavix and omeprazole. . Transitional Issues: - Contacts: Dr. ___ ___ - Code: DNR/DNI - follow up with cardiology - f/u with GI for colonoscopy 3 weeks after discharge - recommend repeat labs drawn in one week to monitor Cr, labs to be sent to MD following at ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had a heart attack. We started you on blood thinning medications to help treat your heart attack and adjusted your blood pressure medications. You also had an abnormal heart rhythm that required a pacemaker. You tolerated the procedure well. You were also admitted with worsening kidney injury which was noted in a previous doctor's visit in ___. This may be because you are not eating and drinking as well as you once were. Your blood levels dropped and you were found to have occult blood in your stool. The gastroenterologists were consulted and did not feel it would be necessary to intervene so soon after your heart attack due to the risks.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ M with HCM, CP syndrome with multiple admission, PAfib s/p PVI s/p PPM implantation s/p AV nodal ablation, HTN, HL, OSA, who p/w chest pain x 1 week here for ___. . # Chest pain / CAD Patient has chronic chest pain, and presenting CP is similar though progressing for past week. Appears to be chronic small vessel disease. Troponin mildly elevated at 0.05 but CK and MB flat. Patient also has acute renal failure at this time, likely accounting for mild trop elevation. He was monitored on tele, had cardiac enzymes x 3 which were significant for troponin of 0.05--> 0.07 --> 0.07 in the setting of an elevated Cr. We continued plavix and aspirin. He went for stress which was largely unchanged from ___ (akinesis of apex with LVEF of 31%). As this LVEF was markedly lower than the previous EF we had from ECHO (which was 60%), he was sent for cardiac catheterization which was negative for any focal occlusions. A repeat ECHO done on the date of discharge showed a LVEF of >55%. His chest pain was not able to be controlled on tylenol, and he eventually became chest pain free with tramadol. He was discharged with a short course of tramadol to be f/u as an outpt. . # Left arm numbness/shooting pain Patient notes that he has shooting pains lasting only seconds, associated with numbness down his left arm intermittently with after moving around in various positions, unclear which positions. He does have chronic back pain associated with shooting pains down left leg for many years, so he may also have spinal stenosis or arthritis of the cervical spine causing similar symptoms in his arm. Appears neuropathic in nature. Alternatively, he may have compression of nerve root further out. No weakness of muscle, no need for urgent intervention. We felt outpatient evaluation would be best for him. Pt was reassured his pacemaker was not shocking him. . # Acute Renal Failure Patient had acute renal failure during previous admission up to 3.1. He presents today with creatinine 1.5, up from 1.2. He appears dry on exam, likely pre-renal etiology. Recently 1.6 on ___. We trended his Cr over his admission and hydrated him with 2L of NS during his admission. His Cr returned to 1.1. . # Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation s/p ablation and pacemaker placement. Patient is A-V paced. Patient believes he was switched from warfarin to clopidogrel for Afib because he failed warfarin therapy and had presented to hospital with very elevated INRs, difficult to control. Per previous OMR note, patient may have overdosed on warfarin intentionally, though he states now that he did not. We continued his home amiodarone, metoprolol, and verapamil. . # Hypertension Patient previously on lisinopril, held starting with last hospitalization because of acute renal failure. In addition to continuing his metop and verapamil, we started a 5 mg dose of lisinopril as his Cr returned to baseline. . # ?COPD Patient doesn't recall having a diagnosis of COPD but it is likely because of his strong tobacco history. He notes that he was previously on inhalers, unsure which ones, but he never got a new prescription and stopped taking them years ago. Harsh end-expiratory wheezing today. We treated him with ipratoprium/albuterol nebs and this should be discussed in the outpatient setting in terms of his COPD medications. . # Chronic Back Pain with Neuropathic Symptoms - continued gabapentin . # Restless Leg Syndrome - continued ropinirole at bedtime . # Depression - continued sertraline . # GERD - continued pantoprazole . # Smoking cessation: patient counseled about smoking cessation - used nicotine patch in house. . CODE: Full (confirmed with patient)
summary: You were admitted to ___ with chest pain. We also noticed that your creatinine (something we measure to check your kidneys) was high. For those reasons, we controlled your chest pain with pain medications and also gave you IV fluids to help bring the creatinine down. We checked your cardiac enzymes which were mildly elevated, thought to be due to your renal function, but within normal limits. Also, we did a stress test which showed some decreased movement in the apex of your heart, which is similar to the test you had done in ___. We did an ECHO on the day you were discharged which looked normal. Finally, we did a cardiac catheterization, which showed minimal disease in the vessels of your heart.

text: Brief Hospital Course: 1. Angioedema: Related to recently started ace-inhibitor without evidence of airway compromise. Used oxygen in the ICU for comfort only without evidence of hypoxia and stable on room air at discharge. Improved on famotidine and benadryl and resolved by discharge. Received steroids in the ED. Ace-inhibior added to allergy profile. 2. Acute on chronic renal failure: Cr close to baseline at discharge. Followed by renal transplant team with history of BK virus nephropathy. BK level elevated at ___. Question pre-renal cause in setting of recent vomiting and also started on ACE which could cause initial bump in CR. 3. s/p hepato-renal transplant: complicated by BK nephropathy and rejection felt to be secondary to cidofovir. ___ level wnl. Continued on home bactrim, sirolimus, prednisone. 4. Hypertension: Recently started on ACE-I for HTN but this has been added to his allergy list and stopped. His BP was normal on toprol XL. Avoid ___ given this angioedema. 5. Hypercholesterolemia: Continued Tricor 6. Depression: Continued sertraline 7. Chronic Steroid Use: Ergocalciferol monthly and start calcium supplementation
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with swelling of your lips and face. We think this was due to a condition called angioedema which is a known complication of the medication lisinopril. We have stopped this medication and you should not restart it. You were found to have slightly worsened kidney function which was likely due in part to the medication and dehydration. This improved with IV fluids and stopping the medication. We have also started you on calcium supplements since you are on prednisone chronically which can cause bone loss over time. No other changes were made to your medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is a ___ F w/ HCV/EtOH cirrhosis on transplant list, chronic pancreatitis s/p multiple ERCPs and Puestow procedure in ___, ___ s/p RFA ___, TACE ___ presenting with abdominal pain. ACUTE ISSUES: ===================== # Chronic Pancreatitis: LFTs stable, lipase not elevated. RUQ unrevealing. Has h/o chronic pancreatitis, which is consistent with her current epigastric pain. The patient was initially kept NPO with IVFs and pain treated with PO oxycodone (has a history of respiratory depression with IV narcotics.) The patients home creon was continued. Her pain improved and diet advanced prior to discharge. # Left lower quadrant pain: This pain is new per patient with a broader differential for its etiology, including colitis, diverticulitis, pelvic process, constipation. Given ___ status she was initially treated with cipro + flagyl to cover GI source. MRI abdomen/pelvis obtained for ___ staging/exclusions points was obtained that showed no etiology of abdominal pain. Abdominal US unrevealing. Stool studies negative. Colonoscopy was with poor prep but no signs of colitis or diverticulitis. The patients pain improved with increasing bowel regimen. Abx were discontinued 24 hours prior to discharge and she was put back on cipro ppx. # HCV/EtOH Cirrhosis: Complicated by ___ s/p RFA and TACE (___). Patient on transplant list. No varices on last EGD ___. Last drink was ___ years ago per patient's report. The patient was continued on home lactulose and rifaxamin. Home spironolactone and lasix breiefly held in setting of mild elevation of Cr and colon prep but then restarted prior to discharge. She was continued on home cipro ppx. MRI abd/pelvis and CT chest non-con obtained for transplant workup. CHRONIC ISSUES: ====================== #Leukopenia: chronic issue and stable during admission. #Anxiety/Depression: continued home citalopram #GERD: pantoprazole given in place of home nexium (non-formulary) TRANISTIONAL ISSUES: ========================== -follow up in transplant clinic -continued work up and management of chronic abdominal pain
summary: You were admitted with several days of worsening abdominal pain. Your mid-abdominal pain was likely due to your chronic pancreatitis. This was treated with bowel rest, fluids and oral pain medications. The cause of your left sided abdominal pain was unclear but may be due to a spasm of your colon. Workup for this pain included an abdominal ultrasound, an MRI of your abdomen and pelvis, and a colonoscopy, all of which were negative for a concerning etiology of your left sided abdominal pain. A routine CT of your chest was also performed to update your MELD exception score and was negative for any concerning findings. Your abdominal pain improved and your diet was advanced such that you were able to tolerate eating regular food.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMHx of HTN, HLD, DM, PVD, CKD (baseline creatinine of 1.3-1.5), CVA, extensive CAD s/p CABG, sCHF (EF35-40%, ___ MR in ___, and known plavix resistance and ___ ischemic CVA presenting with fever and weakness in the context of elevated troponins, but no EKG changes, concerning for NSTEMI. # NSTEMI: Given history of CAD and extensive cardiac risk factors, concerning for MI (NSTEMI Type 1). Patient has known multi-vessel disease and is s/p CABG w/last cath in ___. Patient reported fever at home of 100.8, no fevers recorded in hospital. Infectious workup negative, likely reactive to acute MI. Was ultimately decided that catheterization would not provide significant benefit due to diffusely severe disease, and patent LIMA leading to stenosed LAD. Patient was given 48 hours of heparin gtt and then transitioned to optimal medical therapy. Patient also noted that last time he had an MI, he did not experience chest pain or shortness of breath, but did have fatigue, consistent with this event. # Acute on Chronic CKD Patient baseline Creatinine is 1.0, during hospitalization was around 1.6 which is concerning for Acute on Chronic CKD. ___ is likely in the context of demand ischemia, but could also represent a new baseline for the patient. Will need to follow as an outpatient. - please check labs twice weekly to confirm stability # Hx of Ischemic Stroke Patient has known weakness on right side of body, these symptoms appear to have recrudesced in the context of his troponin rise. Neurology believes that this is due to NSTEMI, and no further imaging is needed. He has neurology followup scheduled as an outpatient. # HLD/CAD/PVD: Statin and medical management as above. # OSA: Continued on CPAP for him during hospitalization. Please maintain CPAP every night. # Depression/Anxiety: Stable, no active issues. Home medications continued. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - please ensure patient presents to follow up cardiology and neurology appointments - please assist with PCP appointment upon discharge from rehab - continue CPAP at night - please see med rec for medication changes - note Viagra is DISCONTINUED and should be discussed with physician before reinstating given that he is started on Nitroglycerin SL - Pantoprazole started instead of Omeprazole due to data on interaction with anti-platelet agents - Code: Full - Contact: wife ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Acetaminophen 500 mg PO Q6H:PRN pain 2. Ascorbic Acid ___ mg PO BID 3. Aspirin 325 mg PO DAILY 4. Atorvastatin 80 mg PO DAILY 5. Bisacodyl 10 mg PR HS:PRN constipation 6. Carvedilol 50 mg PO BID 7. Citalopram 20 mg PO DAILY 8. ClonazePAM 0.5 mg PO QAM 9. ClonazePAM 0.25 mg PO BID 10. Cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg PO DAILY 11. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID 12. Fish Oil (Omega 3) 1000 mg PO DAILY 13. Fluticasone Propionate NASAL 2 SPRY NU DAILY 14. Losartan Potassium 100 mg PO DAILY 15. Omeprazole 20 mg PO DAILY 16. Potassium Chloride 10 mEq PO DAILY 17. Senna 17.2 mg PO HS constipation 18. Spironolactone 12.5 mg PO DAILY 19. TraZODone 25 mg PO HS 20. Cetirizine 10 mg PO DAILY 21. dextran 70-hypromellose 0.1-0.3 % ophthalmic q2h:PRN dry eyes 22. Glucosamine Sulf-Chondroitin (glucosamine ___ 2KCl-chondroit) 500-400 mg oral qd 23. Torsemide 60 mg PO DAILY 24. Viagra (sildenafil) 100 mg oral prn intercourse
summary: You came to ___ due to fatigue and weakness. You were found to have elevated "troponins" indicative of damage to your heart from a heart attack. You were seen by the interventional cardiologists who determined that performing a cardiac catheterization at this time would have risks that outweigh the potential benefits. For this reason, we kept you on anticoagulation (heparin drip) for 48 hours, and ensured that at the time of discharge you were on an optimal medication regimen for your heart. In addition, due to concern that your stroke symptoms had worsened, you were seen by our neurology experts. They determined that you did not have a new stroke, instead having some temporary worsening of your prior stroke symptoms in the context of your heart attack. These should begin to resolve in the coming weeks. Physical therapy saw you and recommends that you go to rehab, and it is through rehab that you can regain your strength and return to your normal activities.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with a history of HCV cirrhosis on the transplant list, with recent esophageal variceal bleed s/p banding, who presents following 4 episodes of bilious vomiting and BRBPR. # GI Bleed: Appears to be lower in source, as the patient experienced bilious vomiting without evidence of hematemesis. Blood per rectum is also bright red and scant in amount in a hemodynamically stable patient. Her BRBPR resolved in <24 hours. She underwent EGD which revealed persistent esophageal varices and colonoscopy which revealed large hemorrhoids. She was discharged on PPI, carafate, and an aggressive bowel regimen to help avoid constipation. # HCV cirrhosis: Complicated by past variceal bleed. She was continued on her home regimen of lasix, spironolactone, nadalol. Her potassium continued to rise throughout her hospitalization, and as a result, we instructed her to stop taking aldactone for 2 days post-discharge, and then to resume the same dose, but with only once daily dosing. She was discharged with a prescription for an outpatient lab check for ___ prior to meeting with her PCP. # History of opiate abuse: On admission she was not truthful about her current use of suboxone. Upon additional discussion, she revealed that she has been taking suboxone, but does not currently have a provider. It is not clear where she obtains the suboxone that she currently takes. Urine tox screen from admission was positive for opiates and we additionally tested her admission urine specifically for subxone. The result of this test is pending. Of note, the positive result for opiates cannot be explained bu suboxone. She reports that she formerly was prescribed this medication by Dr. ___ but she severed her relationship with that physician ___ long time ago, and has been since intermittently taking suboxone unsupervised. Per discussion with her outpatient pharmacy, the last prescription for suboxone from Dr. ___ was picked up on ___. We placed a social work consult for substance abuse counseling and assistance in finding a new suboxone provider. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Depression: She was continued on her home regimen of citalopram. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - ___ need enrollment in a suboxone program given her current opiate abuse and unsupervised use of suboxone - Outpatient adjustement of aldactone dosing based on labs and volume status on ___ - Urine suboxone test pending
summary: You were admitted for work-up of your rectal bleeding. You underwent an EGD and a colonoscopy. On EGD you were noted to have varices, one of which was banded. On colonoscopy, you were noted to have large hemorrhoids. Because of the hemorrhoids, we recommend that you take medications to ensure that you avoid becoming constipated. Also, you will need repeat EGD in 2 months to monitor your varices. Also, your potassium has been rising during your stay in the hospital. On discharge, we would like you to not take your aldactone (spironolactone) dose for 2 days. Then, on ___, please restart taking aldactone 100 mg daily (not twice a day).

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient Summary: ================= ___ with metastatic gastric cancer (last chemo ___ line taxol/ramicirumab on ___ obstructive uropathy s/p B/L percutaneous nephrostomy tubes, h/o DVT on apixaban, GERD, H. pylori infection, chronic constipation and known hemorrhoids who was admitted for ?pyelonephrosis as well as BRBPR found to be related to hemorrhoids. CT abdomen imaging with findings concerning for possible pyelonephritis. However, patient with stable urinary symptoms for the past few months without fever/chills and hemodynamically stable. She has had multiple prior cultures with with UA with pyuria and bacteria consistent with chronic colonization iso nephrostomy tubes. No antibiotic treament given clinical picture. Had palliative paracentesis and nephrostomy tube replacement while inpatient. # Pyuria # ? pyelonephritis Patient afebrile and well-appearing with chronic urinary symptoms of dysuria,urgency and tenderness at PCN site. UA with pyuria, neg nit and pos leuk and bacteria that was consistent with previous UA. She had multiple previous positive cultures with similar UA that is more consistent with colonization with bacteria iso nephrostomy tubes. She received a dose of ceftriaxone in the ED though would have required Cefepime for appropriate coverage of previous cultures. CT abdomen with evidence of renal stranding concerning for pyelonephritis but given clinical picture, antibiotics were discontinued. She remained afebrile. ___ evaluated nephrostomy tubes without concern for infection. Bilateral nephrostomy tubes replaced ___. Will be due for replacement again in 3 months. # Lower GI bleed # hemorrhoids # constipation BRBPR consistent with previous episodes of hemorrhoids in the setting of significant constipation. Patient only use prune juice at home for management of constipation. GI evaluated in ED and concurs that bleeding consistent with hemorrhoids. CT abd/pelvis without other site of bleed established. Remained HD stable with stable H/H. Started on bowel regimen with Senna, colace, miralax. # metastatic gastric adenocarcinoma # malignant ascites last chemo ___ line taxol/ramicirumab on ___. Awaiting clinical trial appointment at ___ as well as further molecular characterization. She had an appointment for paracentesis on ___ but had an inpatient paracentesis performed for comfort. Pain managed with home regimen of Fentanyl patch and PRN oxycodone. #H/o malignant obstructive uropathy- ___ evaluated nephrostomy tubes in ED. Stable mild pain around nephrostomy tubes without evidence of infection. Will need nephrostomy tube exchanged as outpatient by end of ___. # DVT # Pulmonary embolism: Continued on Eliquis # Hypothyroidism- Continued synthroid # Depression- Continued citalopram and prn Clonazepam. Transitional Issues: ======================== - Nephrostomy tubes exchanged on ___ and due for 3 month exchange by ___. - ___ consider suppressive/prophylactic antibiotics given recurent UTI - Follow up with Dr. ___ for ___ #HCP/Contact: First contact: ___ Second ___ ___ #Code: Full (confirmed)
summary: You had bloody stools for a few days. Your CT abdomen showed concerning signs of a possible kidney infection You had abdominal imaging that showed a lot of abdominal fluid and had the fluid removed during your stay. You were given antibiotics shortly for concern of an infection but since you were not having any new symptoms from your previous burning sensation with urination, antibiotics were stopped. You were evaluated by gastroenterology who believed your bleeding was related to hemorrhoids. You underwent a paracentesis, a procedure where the fluid in your abdomen is removed for comfort. Your nephrostomy tubes were replaced You should continue taking the stool softeners Senna and Docusate daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old male with PMHx AIDS (CD4: 141; VL less than 20 copies/mL from ___ complicated by multiple OIs including ___ and PCP pneumonia, chronic respiratory failure requiring trach and PEG, and recurrent c. diff, who was admitted with fever, tachypnea, and increased sputum production. Urine culture growing Pseudomonas. Sputum growing multiple GNRs with high levels of antibiotic resistance. He appears significantly improved compared with admission. # Severe Sepsis due to Healthcare Associated Pneumonia vs aspiration pneumonia: Patient presenting with fever, leukocytosis, ___, and lactic acidosis consistent with severe sepsis. Lactate downtrended from 2.9 to 1.1 to 0.9 after 2L IVF in ICU. He was hemodynamically stable while on the floor. His metoprolol was held throughout this admission. His sputum grew out resistant E coli, so he will finish a 2 week course of cefepime. He was seen by the ID service. # Recurrent aspiration: Per his facility staff, he is known to surreptiously sneak food by mouth, and is well known to aspirate. He has also had a lot of oropharyngeal exercises at his facility and his ability to swallow safely without aspirating has NOT improved. Given no improvement with oropharyngeal exercises/rehab and his overall poor health he is unlikely to be able to eat by mouth safely. I discussed with his sister whether he would wish to eat for comfort and accept the risk of aspiration, but she feels that he would want "everything done". He remains NPO and on tube feeds. Continue tube feeds: Jevity 1.5 @ 105ml/hr, infusion time ___ (x20hr/day) to provide 3150kcal and 134gprotein/day. A laryngectomy would enable him to eat safely, but it is a procedure with high associated morbidity and may not be offered to someone in in his poor overall health. - # Recurrent C. diff infection - H/o severe ___. No severe leukocytosis or significant abdominal pain or distention to suggest severe/complicated C. diff infection. Per ID, will continue 125mg PO Vancomycin Q6h and will plan to continue PO vanc at this dose for 7 days following completion of other antibiotics # Tracheostomy problems: stoma site appears large in diameter, but does not appear to be actively infected, but reportedly unable to swallow/phonate. He was seen by ENT in house and they advised that RNs carefully packing between stoma and trach tube (around trach tube) with Kerlex soaked in betadyne, changed once daily, to encourage granulation tissue to form and possibly shrink the diameter of the stoma They hope that this will stimulate him to form his own granulation tissue and that the existing tracheostomy will fit better. They will see him as an outpatient to see if this is working, or if they need to upsize his trach. He is more likely to be able to phonate with a smaller tracheostomy tube. # Acute Kidney Injury: resolved with initial IV fluids and antibiotics. # AIDS: Patient has history of AIDS (multiple OIs); last CD4 was 141 and HIV VL < 20 copies on ___. We continued his home ___ 1 tab daily, Darunavir 800 mg PO DAILY, RiTONAvir 100 mg PO daily and home prophylactic meds: atovaquone and azithromycin. # Chronic abdominal pain: Continued hyoscyamine. # Seizure history: Continued on home Keppra 1250mg PO BID. # Depression/Anxiety/Insomnia: Continued home citalopram, Ativan PRN. # Osteonecrosis / Chronic Right Leg pain: Continued home tylenol for pain. # ? UTI: Urine culture grew out pseudomonas, but patient asyx. The cefepime that is treating the aspiration pneumonia will also cover the UTI. # Hypertension: Metoprolol held throughout hospitalization, and systolic blood pressures in the ___. WIll continue to hold. # Goals of Care: Facility should continue to engage with patient and family regarding his goals of care, especially given his clearly demonstrated urge to eat. I spoke to sister ___ on ___ and called his sister ___ on the day of discharge to review his care.
summary: You were admitted with with shortness of breath, fever, and increased sputum production caused by pneumonia. You were treated with intravenous antibiotics and your pneumonia improved. You also developed an infection in your colon called C. diff colitis, which was treated with oral antibiotics. You were also evaluated by the ENT doctors and they advise packing the area of the stoma around your tracheostomy so that you grow more of your own tissue to hold the trach in place. Also, we feel that it remains unsafe for you to eat unless you are willing to accept the risk of worsened aspiration. You had a PICC line placed so that you can get your iv antibiotics. This should be removed after your antibiotics have been completed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ was admitted with concern for acute on chronic cognitive decline and encephalopathy. She was evaluated by neurosurgery, neurology, and psychiatry, all of whom felt that there was no acute process or change in her functioning in corroboration with her family, and she was discharged home to follow-up with her outpatient providers for further management of her depression, cognitive decline/dementia, and thalamic mass. ACTIVE ISSUES # Worsening mental status She presented initially out of concern of a new PCP who felt that she was more severely altered.
