During his episodes of bradycardia he was completely asymptomatic when awake and while sleeping. His electrolytes were monitored and replaced as needed. His home ASA was continued. # Concerns for falls at home: He worked with ___ during this admission, who recommended it was safer that he go to rehab for further recovery and ___ after discharge. He has a ___ for when he goes home. Per ___, he should ambulate with a walker. # Change of code status: The patient decided to become full code during this admission. He cited as his reasons the desire to help those who would be treating him learn from the experience. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Depression / Dementia: His mood was bright during this admission and he remained well-oriented and communicative during this admission. His home sertraline and risperidone were continued. # Benign Hypertension: Pressures were stable during diuresis. He was continued on his home Losartan. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - He should follow-up with Atris cardiology as an outpatient. ## Contact information: Outpt case manager, ___ (___) Phone ___, ___ HCP and power of attorney ___ ___
summary: You were admitted to the ___ because of increasing shortness of breath and leg swelling. We determined that you have an exacerbation of congestive heart failure (a condition in which fluid backs up from the heart), resulting in fluid in the lungs as well as, quite prominently, in your legs. We treated you with intravenous diuretics (like your water pill) with close monitoring of your electrolytes. Your swelling diminished markedly and you are ready to be discharged. You have been switched to a new oral lasix regimen (increased dose) that you can take at home. Before going home, though, we have discharged you to a rehab facility where you can work with physical therapists to regain your strength and balance. Please review your medication list closely.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old woman with HTN, HLD, depression, hypothyroidism who presents with 3 episodes of left hand and face tingling, with mild dysarthria, on head CT found with a small right frontal convexal SAH. Three episodes prior to admission-- ___ spell: 2 weeks ago with 30 seconds of slurred speech and LUE circumferential tingling, ___ spell: day PTA with tingling in the fingers/hand 1 min, ___: more pronounced slurred speech and hand tingling. No episodes were associated with headache and no history of recent trauma. Of note, a few years of minor memory issues-- forgetting names, misplacing objects, etc. Throughout her hospital course her neurological exam remained normal, however she continued to have brief episode similar to the ones that brought her in. The MRI appeared consistent with convexal SAH but the etiology was not known. Our initial CTA showed prominence of R frontal vasculature suggestive of possible AVM and Neurosurgery took her for cerebral angiogram on ___ which was negative. We suspect that this bleed may be related to early amyloid angiopathy, given her age and cognitive complaints, although the MRI GRE sequence did not show diffuse GRE signal. During this hospitalization we stopped ASA (plan to hold indefinitely given weak indication for ASA). We are continuing home statin, close BP control with goal <140 systolic. The question of events related to possible seizure was addressed throughout this admission. Dr. ___ was the admitting attending, felt that the symptoms were most likely related to cortical spreading depression (migrainous phenomenom) rather than seizure and EEG/AEDs were deferred. On day of discharge the service was taken over by Dr. ___ preference was to treat empirically, especially if events persist. She will follow up with Dr. ___ call his office to start keppra if bothersome events persist. At discharge she had refused AEDs. Incidentally throughout this hospitalization she had asymptomatic SVT to 140s mostly during sleep. She was given a home holter monitor and discussed with primary care doctor who will follow up. Transition Issues - SBP <140 - holding ASA indefinitely given bleed - if persistent events will call Dr. ___ initiation of AED (low dose keppra preferred) - Holter monitor for SVT and PCP follow up ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Fluticasone Propionate NASAL 1 SPRY NU DAILY:PRN congestion 2. Pravastatin 5 mg PO QPM 3. Sertraline 50 mg PO DAILY 4. Acyclovir 400 mg PO 5X/DAY FOR 5 DAYS AS NEEDED 5. famciclovir 125 mg oral BID:PRN rash 6. Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (250/50) 1 INH IH BID 7. Levothyroxine Sodium 100 mcg PO DAILY 8. Albuterol Sulfate (Extended Release) 90 mcg PO Q6H:PRN sob/cough 9. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 10. Ca ___ bor 250 mg calcium -500 unit oral Other 11. Vitamin D 400 UNIT PO DAILY 12. Estrogens Conjugated use a small amt on tip of finger directed once per week and when exchange of pessary gm VG ASDIR 13. lisinopril-hydrochlorothiazide ___ mg oral DAILY 14. ___ (cranberry extract) 400 mg oral DAILY 15. coenzyme Q10 100 mg oral DAILY 16. cranberry 600 mg oral DAILY 17. Fish Oil 1200mg-144mg-216mg oral BID 18. Glucosamine-Chondroitin Complex ___ mg oral DAILY 19. Multivitamins W/minerals 1 TAB PO DAILY 20. Vitamin B Complex w/C 1 TAB PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted to the Neurology Service with episodes of left sided paresthesias. On CT scan you were found to have a small bleed that likely caused your symptoms. On MRI we found that you had a small, stable hemorrhage called a subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH). This kind of blood can sometimes cause brief episodes of numbness/tingling like the kind you report. We do not think these were seizures, and we did not give you medications to control them. You may continue to have intermittent spells like the one that brought you to the hospital, but we do not think these are dangerous and are likely to improve as your brain heals from this injury.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ h/o syncope x~6, the last of which was in ___ now s/p unwitnessed syncopal episode. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Syncope: Given how often urination is involved, increased vagal tone suspected. Question as to whether this causes pure vasovagal, leads to sinus pauses, or whether this is truly neurocardiogenic. Given his possible orthopnea and PND, there is a question of cardiac involvement. The episodes do not sound neurologic (CVA, epileptic), nor hypoglycemic. Though he has mechanical problems and orthostasis, these are not at all historically compatible with the episodes the patient describes. Notably his tox was negative and chemistries nl in the ED. He does state that sometimes he wakes up immediately after a fall but feels so fatigued that he falls back asleep on the floor. None of his medications are strongly anticholinergic (notably, he has not been taking his diphenhydramine or vitamins recently due to financial difficulties). The patient's telemetry was unrevealing and EKG showed only nonspecific anterolateral T wave changes. After fluid resuscitation for ___ (below), the patient's heart rate was noted to increase from 100 to 140-160 on standing by his nurse. This suggested an orthostatic component that is likely representative of an autonomic neuropathy as he was volume replete. This is certainly consistent with his peripheral neuropathy secondary to alcohol. His TSH and zinc were normal. Vitamin B studies had yet to return at the time of discharge. It was thought that the patient's syncope was multifactorial and could be attributed to components of both micturition syncope and autonomic neuropathy-mediated orthostasis. . # ___: Cr 1.3 from baseline ~1. Likely pre-renal given pt's history of orthostatic episodes. The patient was volume resuscitated and his creatinine resolved to its baseline. . # Back pain: chronic problem with no acute progression in sensory deficits or weakness. No incontinence, making acute SC processes unlikely. He was treated with acetaminophen and opiates. . # Headache: the patient received analgesia as above with speedy resolution of his headache. . # Depressed mood: The patient denied suicidal ideation. Social work was not able to see him over the weekend and they were similarly occupied while he remained inpatient on the day of his discharge. . INACTIVE ISSUES: none . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: # Syncope: The patient was discharged with moderate-pressure compression stockings in an effort to decrease the orthostatic component of his syncope. We recommend tilt table testing to parse out whether there is a definite vasovagal component to his episodes that might be successfully treated with low-dose propanolol. Although he had not been taking his Benadryl, this was formally discontinued. . # Depression: the patient was given a social work phone number to call so that he can be referred to appropriate psychiatric services.
summary: As you recall, you were admitted after you were found on the floor having sustained a syncopal episode. It was thought that your episode was related to the need to urinate, as have many of your past episodes. Initial heart testing was unremarkable and your episodes did not seem consistent with low blood pressure on standing, seizure, stroke, or simply a mechanical fall. Your hospital course was remarkable for an episode of low blood pressure on standing, but you did not lose consciousness. For the near future, you should urinate sitting down. You should take your time getting up from a sitting or lying position, to let your body equilibrate.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old with a history of COPD and bronchiolitis obliterans in addition to prior episodes of septic arthritis of her L ankle who presents with L ankle pain and shortness of breath. # Dyspnea: likely component of acute COPD exacerbation in setting of baseline bronchiectasis and bronchiolitis. Green sputum production concerning for pneumonia, specifically with pseudomonas given that patient has structural lung disease. However, patient does not have leukocytosis or evidence of pneumonia on CXR. Patient was given prednisone 60mg for presumed COPD exacerbation along with increasing azithromycin to 500 daily x 5 days and then may return back to her prior doses of those medications. Patient received standing albuterol/ipratropium nebulizers as well as her home maintenance medications. -cont spiriva -cont advair 500/50 -Prednisone 60mg PO daily ___ (chose ___ given patient on longstanding steroids). -Continue Bactrim 3x/week -Increase azithromycin to daily for 5 days, then back to 3 days/week (___) # Ankle swelling: unlikely to be septic joint, however was aspirated by orthopedics,and gram stain shows no organism, culture no growth. Most likely related to RA flare. Elected not to cover with antibiotics at this time given exam not consistent with septic joint, no sign of sepsis. That being said, patient on steroids which minimize symptoms of infection. CT ___ was non specific and could indicate osteo, however more likely related to prior destruction of the joint. Bone biopsy would likely be only way to definitively rule out osteomyelitis, however this appears to be overly aggressive for something that has low pre-test probability. This was discussed with Dr. ___ ___, outapatient rheumatologist who stated he would have her see infectious disease as an outpatient. #Rheumatoid Arthritis: severe. patient on hydroxychloriquine as outpatient as well as chronic steroids. Also with Ibuprofen. Ankle likely represents RA flare. No indication for acute change at this time. -cont hydroxychloroquine -cont prednisone as noted above -cont ibuprofen # GERD: complicated by ___ esophagus, continued omeprazole 20 mg BID (especially given prednisone use) # anxiety/depression: continued fluoxetine, trazodone, PRN lorazepam. # HCM: - continued home ASA, folic acid. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Patient should follow up with PCP and pulmonologist regarding COPD exacerbations and indications for daily azithromycin versus her current regimen -Patient should follow up with her rheumatologist and infectious disease regarding her ankle
summary: You were admitted to ___ because of difficulty breathing and pain in your ankle. While you were here you were thought to have an exacerbation of your COPD (for which you received prednisone and azithromycin) and your ankle does not appear to be infected. Please take the prednisone 60mg and azithromycin daily for 2 more days (last day ___. At that time you can change your prednisone to 40mg per day for 2 days, then 20mg for 2 days, then back to your home 15 mg. Your ankle was tapped for fluid but not found to have any evidence of infection. A Ct scan was done, which was non specific given that your joint already had a lot of damage.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female with PMH notable for L knee replacement in ___, recent admission for cellulitis in end of ___ now direct admit for persistent cellulitis, also C diff pos. Active issues: # Cellulitis: Initialy on 3 weeks of oral antibiotics (keflex/bactrim). MRI neg for osteo. Ultrasound showed no abscess/pocket for drainage. Per ID, patient was started on PO cipro and IV vancomycin. Blood cultures negative (some pending at time of discharge). Patient to follow-up with infectious disease who may adjust planned 2 week course of antibiotics. # C.diff colitis: Found to be C.diff positive in the setting of 3 weeks of antibiotics. Patient started on Flagyl 500mg PO q8hrs, to be continued for 2 weeks after stopping the Cipro/Flagyl as above. Chronic issues: # Depression/anxiety: Continued home bupropion and ativan # Left shoulder pain: Patient with known rotator cuff tear. Followed by orthopedics as an outpt and s/p steroid injection ___. Tylenol PRN. Transitional issues: -CODE: Full (confirmed) -EMERGENCY CONTACT: ___ (husband) ___ -Needs follow-up with PCP and ID to assess for resolution of this resistant cellulitis
summary: You were admitted with left foot cellulitis. We were concerned that ___ could have osteomyelitis (an infection of the bone). An MRI scan was performed which showed no osteomyelitis. We had the infectious disease specialists see ___ in the hospital. They recommended a combination of an IV antibiotic called vancomycin and an antibiotic by mouth called ciprofloxacin. We started ___ on these medications in the hospital. You were also found to have an infection in the bowel called C. difficile colitis (sometimes just called "C. diff"). We started ___ on an antibiotic by mouth called flagyl to treat this infection.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr ___ is a ___ y/o patient with a complicated medical history significant for alcohol abuse, cirrhosis, hepatitis C, diabetes, HTN, CAD s/p NSTEMI in ___ (refused cath at the time) who initially presented at ___ for chest pain and shortness of breath and was found to have a NSTEMI with troponin peak 2.0 and a platelet count of 50,000. He was transferred to ___ for evaluation for possible platelets transfusion prior to catheterization and possible PCI. He underwent catheterization on ___ and received two BMS in proximal and mid RCA lesions. . #NSTEMI: Patient with a history of CAD s/p NSTEMI in ___ presented at OSH for chest pain and shortness of breath. He was found to have troponin elevations with a peak at 2.___epression in V5. His pain was relieved with morphine and he was started on a heparin drip. As his CXR showed mild pulmonary edema, he receives 20 mg lasix IV. His echo showed normal LV wall motion with an EF=65% and normal RV function. At our hospital, we continued his heparin drip, started him on pravastatin 10 mg and continued his home medication. Patient was catheterized on ___. For contrast dye allergy he received three doses of Methylprednisolone 40 mg IV Q4h and benadryl 50 mg IV once one hour prior to cath. For his nephropathy, he received fluids prior to cath. His RCA has a 80% proximal lesion and a segment of 80% ulcerated mid disease. He received two BMS of proximal and mid RCA lesions. His LAD has a segment of mid ___ disease, his circumflex has a 70-80% lesion and his ramus has 70% proximal disease. He received 600 mg clopidogrel once and was started on clopidogrel 75 mg. He will take clopidogrel 75 mg for one month ending on ___, and continuing aspirin 81 mg indefinitely. #THROMBOCYTOPENIA: Likely due to cirrhosis and alcohol. Patient has history of thrombocytopenia and was found to be thrombocytopenic at the OSH with platelet count of 50,000. His platelet count on ___ was 52,000 and the cardiologist felt comfortable doing cath at that platelet count without transfusion. #HYPERTENSION: We continued his home medication: amlodipine 10 mg, metoprolol succinate 25 mg and hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily #DIABETES II: Patient has poorly controlled diabetes with last HbA1c from ___ of 9.0. He has a diabetic ulcer on his left foot. Is on insulin at home: insulin aspartat( novolog), insulin detemir (levemir). In the our hospital, he was on a insulin sliding scale, reduced dose long-acting while NPO. #DEPRESSION/ANXIETY: We continued his home sertraline 50 mg daily and clonazepam 0.5 mg TID PRN for anxiety. #CHRONIC BACK PAIN/NEUROPATHIC PAIN: We continued his home gabapentin 800 mg TID and he received home oxycodone 10 mg Q6H PRN or oxycodone ___ mg Q4H PRN. #CIRRHOSIS: His cirrhosis is likely secondary to HCV and alcohol. He denied history of prior bleeding. His INR at discharge on ___ was 1.4. #ALCOHOL USE: He received thiamine IV, multivitamins and folate. We strongly recommended alcohol and smoking cessation. #PANCYTOPENIA: Patient was pancytopenic with WBC 1.8, Hgb 10.9 and ___ ___ on ___. Possible causes are chronic splenic congestion, secondary to cirrhosis, myelosupression from chronic alcohol abuse and for thrombocytopenia, ITP due to hepatitis C. We monitored his CBC daily. ## TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: We recommend endoscopy (EGD) as an outpatient to check for varices and assess bleeding risk. We asked patient to call our Gastroenterology Department ___ for an appointment for an endoscopy (EGD).
summary: You initially presented at ___ for chest pain and shortness of breath. Your heart marker in the blood, called troponin, was elevated. In combination with chest pain, this suggests a blockage in one of the vessels, that supply your heart with blood, called coronary vessels. You were also found to have a low platelet count. Your CXR showed that you had some fluid in your lungs, called a mild pulmonary edema. A cardiac ultrasound showed that your heart is pumping well. You received morphine for your pain, a medication that removes fluid from your body, called lasix and were put on a blood thinner, called heparin. You were transferred to our hospital for catheterization.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ F with hx of schizophrenia and recent GI bleed from ulcer at gastric bypass junction who p/w one day of progressive, limiting, lower extremity edema. Active Issues: #Bilateral leg edema: Patient with swelling while inpatient prior that was not this severe. ___ edema for which she's on Lasix at home. Started on Augmentin for possible cellulitis (although thought unlikely, Day 1 of ___ was ___. Negative lower extremity U/S in ED. Her Lasix was increased to 60mg daily and she was given Spironolactone 25mg BID; this, along with leg wraps and elevation and her edema improved. She was discharged at a weight of 106kg and should follow up with her PCP to continue to assess edema and her electrolytes checked. . #Abdominal Pain: Patient with intermittent abdominal pain not related to eating. Sharp pain in URQ. Lasts about 30 minutes and goes away. Initially described as "crampy," similar to when she's having her menses. Was non-tender on exam, labs were not concerning for hepatobiliary process. #Right subclavian dermatitis: Patient with area of dermatitis after R subclavian removed. Appears allergic to the tape used. Low dose steroid was applied to the area with improvement. Was not sent home on the cream because did not want to weaken the skin. #Incontinence: Patient states she has had a recent history of incontinence, most likely stress incontinence per her history. With further difficulty due to her edema, she was prescribed adult diapers for home. Chronic Issues: #S/p GI Bleed- from marginal ulcer at the location of her gastric bypass anastamosis. She had no varices on exam. Patient received her 5 days post GI bleed antibiotics. As an inpatient, she received Pantoprazole 40mg BID. An H. pylori stool antigen was collected, result should be followed up on. No evidence of bleed while she was in house. Iron and folate supplements were continued. #Alcoholic Cirrhosis- LFTs slightly elevated from previous discharge. Patient has denied any use since she left the hospital. LFTs trended down while she was in the hospital. Thiamine and Lactulose were continued. #Depression: Continue Fluoxetine 20mg PO daily #Chronic back pain: Pt with complaints of back pain on previous admission. Ordered for a lidocaine patch and tylenol prn Transition Issues: #Patient should follow up with PCP to see how lower extremity edema is progressing. Her discharge weight was 106kg, still with significant edema. She should also have her labs checked with her PCP since ___ dose was increased on discharge. #S/p GI bleed: Will need to follow up with hepatologist to discuss recent GI bleed from an ulcer at junction of gastric bypass surgery. #H. pylori stool antigen sent to see if she should be treated, although expectation is that the ulcer is a result of poor blood flow to anastamosis. Will need to follow up this result. #Hx of EtOH and drug abuse: Patient states she does not want to abuse substances anymore. She should be encouraged and assisted as an outpatient.
summary: You came to the hospital with swelling in both of your legs that decreased your ability to walk. Using ultrasound, we showed it to be unlikely that you have a clot in your legs. Also, we started antibiotics because you had a fever while you were here, and while that is precautionary, we think it to be unlikely that you are infected. The swelling improved with elevation of your legs, water pills to help you urinate excess fluids, and wraps around your legs. This most likely indicates that your swelling is due to Venous Stasis (a problem with your veins getting blood flow back from your feet). This should continue to improve with compression, elevation, and taking the water pills (Furosemide and Spironolactone). The rest of your labs looked normal while you were here and your liver enzymes were still improving from your previous admission.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Right perioral numbness: Originally thought to be due to progression of large-B cell lymphoma with CNS involvement, however, patient developed a cold sore on her right lower lip so it was more likely due to HSV. Resolved with acyclovir. MRI head was obtained which did not show conclusive evidence of involvement. # Large B-cell lymphoma: Patient had bone marrow biopsy to evaluate for progression of lymphoma. Second cycle of methotrexate was administered. In addition to a bone marrow biopsy, she had a biopsy of an inguinal lymph node. The pathology came back as high grade B cell lymphoma for both the bone marrow and the inguinal lymph node. She received 3 days of ICE chemotherapy, which she tolerated well. She was started on neupogen on the day of discharge. # Hyperlipidemia: Continued simvastatin 20 mg po qdaily. # Asthma: Continued monteleukast 10 mg po qdail. # Depression: Continued citalopram and fexofendine. # GERD: Continued omeprazole.
summary: You were admitted because you had numbness around your right lower lip. It was thought this was due to progression of your large-B cell lymphoma with CNS involvement, however, you developed a cold sore on your right lower lip so it was more likely due to HSV. You were treated with acyclovir and it resolved. MRI of your head was obtained along with bone marrow biopsy to evaluate for progression of your lymphoma. Second cycle of methotrexate was administered which you tolerated well. In addition to a bone marrow biopsy, you had a biopsy of an inguinal lymph node. The pathology came back as high grade B cell lymphoma for both the bone marrow and the inguinal lymph node. You received 3 days of ICE chemotherapy, which you tolerated well. You were started on neupogen on the day of discharge and you should continue giving yourself one injection daily until directed otherwise by your oncologist.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with metastatic endometrial adenocarcinoma who presented with abdominal cramping and constipation found to have interval progression of intraabdominal tumor burden with mass partially encasing distal sigmoid colon causing partial large bowel obstruction s/p diverting ostomy ___ complicated by worsening abdominal pain, intermittent fevers, and leukocytosis found to have post-op abscess s/p revision and abscess drainage on ___ complicated by difficult to control cancer-related pain. # Cancer-Related Abdominal Pain: # Large Bowel Obstruction: Presented with abdominal cramping and constipation found to have interval progression of intraabdominal tumor burden with mass partially encasing distal sigmoid colon causing partial large bowel obstruction. Staging CT chest without interval change. Gastrograffin enema revealed high grade stricture in distal sigmoid colon and upper rectum at least 5.2 cm in length. She is s/p elective diverting ostomy on ___ and underwent revision on ___. CT with PO contrast shows no obstruction on ___. Palliative Care was consulted for assistance with pain control. Patient discharged on Tylenol 1g TID, ibuprofen 200mg TID, oxycontin 40mg q12h, and oxycodone ___ q4h PRN. Patient also discharged on lorazepam for insomnia and anxiety. Also on dicyclomine. She was counselled extensively on pain medication usage and pain management techniques. Suspect component of pain related to anxiety. Pain was controlled at time of discharge. Medications were delivered to bedside prior to discharge. # Abdominal Wall Abscess: Following initial surgery had fever and leukocytosis found to have abscess s/p cavity drainage. ___ drain removed by surgery on ___. She received 7-day course of cefepime/flagyl. # Metastatic Endometrial Adenocarcinoma: # Secondary Neoplasm of Lung: Previously on tamoxifen/megace. She received carboplatin on ___. Treating with palliative intent. Will follow-up with Dr. ___. # Vaginal Bleeding: # Anemia: Secondary to disease recurrence. She received Ferric Gluconate 125mg IV x 4 doses. # Fatigue in Malignancy: In setting of cancer recurrence, surgery, and infection. Seen by ___ and will have home ___. # Moderate Malnutrition: Due to poor PO intake and weight loss. Seen by Nutrition. Recommended supplements. # Depression: Continued home Celexa. # Insomnia: Discharged on Ativan PRN. # BILLING: 60 minutes were spent in preparation of discharge summary, coordination with outpatient providers, and counselling patient and family. ==================== Transitional Issues: ==================== - Pain Control: Patient discharged on Tylenol 1g TID, ibuprofen 200mg TID, oxycontin 40mg q12h, and oxycodone ___ q4h PRN. Patient also discharged on lorazepam for insomnia and anxiety. Please ensure follow-up with Palliative Care. - Please ensure follow-up with Therapy and consider uptitration of citalopram.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with abdominal pain and constipation. You were found to have a blockage in your large bowel. You had a surgery to relieve the blockage. You then had an abscess around the surgery and this was drained. You completed a course of antibiotics for the infection in the hospital. We also modified you pain medications while in the hospital. Please continue to take the pain medications as indicated. Please keep a log of your pain as well as when and how many oxycodone you are taking. Please bring this log to all of your clinic appointments. For your as needed oxycodone you are written for a range of ___ every 4 hours. For moderate pain, you can take 10mg. For more severe pain, you can take 15mg. Also if after you take 10mg and still having pain, you can take an additional 5mg. You can use the lorazepam for insomnia as well as anxiety. This medication can cause more sedation when taking with oxycodone so please be cautious.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with a history of COPD (intermittent home O2 2L), seizure disorder, frequent falls, HTN, insomnia, who presented with dyspnea for several days despite oxygen, albuterol and spiriva therapy at home. She had URI symptoms about a month ago that exacerbated her COPD and she is s/p azithromycin for 5 days in early ___. She says she never fully recovered from that infection. She continues to have mildly productive cough and significant wheezing. #COPD: On home O2 PRN. She was not put oral steroids for exacerbation earlier in the month when she had URI like symptoms. She is also smoking more this month than previously, up to ___ pack per day from ___ pack per day. She is also not taking her Spiriva or Steroids daily. She was started on azithromycin for 5 days, prednisone for 5 days, and duoneb treatments. While in the hospital her O2 sats remained >95% when not on O2 and while walking. She was discharged with a prescription for a nebulizer and duonebs. She will take the prednisone and azithromycin for a total of 5 days (last day ___. #frequent falls: She has been falling more lately. She was seen by neurology and diagnosed with BPPV. She was shown the Epley maneuver which improved her balance and gait. ___ evaluated her and recommended outpatient vestibular ___. # Seizures: Last seizure-like event was ___ and frequency has decreased tremendously since she has been transitioned to Keppra. She is followed by Dr. ___ in ___. Continued Keppra 1500mg BID # Depression: Continued Sertraline 200mg daily # GERD: Continued Pantoprazole 40mg daily # HLD: Continued pravastatin 40mg daily # Insomina: Her medications were titrated up by her neurologist due to difficulty sleeping at night. Continued home dose of clonazepam and seroquel as above ========================= TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ========================= -Pt reports difficulty paying for medications. Not taking spiriva, budesonide at home. -given a nebulizer along with duonebs Q6 and albuterol neb q2h prn. Spiriva stopped as patient not able to pay for it. Will need to follow up with her PCP/pulmonologist. -HCTZ stopped for low blood pressure, as concerned it was contributing to falls -encouraged to quit smoking and has been using nicotine patches. She should continue to seek help with smoking cessation. -PCP to arrange outpatient vestibular ___ for treatment of BPPV. -Patient recommended cane for ambulation. -Sedating medications kept the same, as patient said she would take that dose anyways at home. No falls with home medications. -DNR/DNI during hospitalization, but within goals of care to have short round of CPR. Does not want intubation. Should discuss again as outpatient. -EMERGENCY CONTACT: ___ (Husband, ___) ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list may be inaccurate and requires futher investigation. 1. LeVETiracetam 1500 mg PO BID 2. Sertraline 200 mg PO DAILY 3. Diltiazem Extended-Release 240 mg PO QAM 4. Lisinopril 40 mg PO DAILY 5. Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO DAILY 6. Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q24H 7. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 8. Pravastatin 40 mg PO QPM 9. Calcium Carbonate 1250 mg PO DAILY 10. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY 11. Potassium Chloride 20 mEq PO DAILY 12. Ascorbic Acid ___ mg PO DAILY 13. Fioricet (butalbital-acetaminophen-caff) 50-300-40 mg oral PRN headache 14. Senna 8.6 mg PO BID:PRN constipation 15. Tiotropium Bromide 1 CAP IH DAILY 16. ClonazePAM 4 mg PO QHS 17. Pulmicort (budesonide) unkown mg/2ml inhalation PRN 18. Albuterol Inhaler ___ PUFF IH Q4H:PRN shortness of breath/wheezing 19. Nasonex (mometasone) 50 mcg/actuation nasal DAILY 20. QUEtiapine Fumarate 600 mg PO QHS
summary: ___ came to the hospital for trouble breathing and frequent falls. A chest XRAY was negative for pneumonia. A CT of your head did not show any damage from your fallsYou were admitted to the hospital for further treatment of your COPDYou were given steroids and an antibiotic to help your shortness of breath. In addition, ___ recieved nebulizer treatments, that helped your breathing significantly. ___ will continue your prednisone and azithromycin for 2 more days after discharge (end date ___You were also provided a nebulizer to start using nebulizer treatments every 6 hours as needed for wheezing, shortness of breath.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ====================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [] We increased Imdur to 60mg daily [] We also increased diltiazem to 120mg XR daily [] Patient has follow up on ___ with interventional cardiology, as previously scheduled. We will communicate with the interventional team ahead of time to determine options for PCI vs bypass. [] Instructed patient to take acetaminophen 1g Q8 as standing for one week instead of PRN for musculoskeletal injury to left upper quadrant [] Discharged patient also with lidocaine cream 5% for symptomatic relief of musculoskeletal pain ======================= ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with CAD (RCA stent in ___ complicated by restenosis in ___, HTN, DM2, and depression who presents with both exertional and non-exertional chest pain that are separate from one another. # Angina with established CAD Pain is exertional, right-sided, relieved by rest, and lasts a few minutes. He has multi-year history of the same. pMIBI without EKG changes but did show regional WMA. His troponins have been negative but are only 2.5 hours apart. EKG is without ischemic changes. We continued with outpatient antianginal medications: - Atorvastatin 80 mg PO QPM - Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY - Ranolazine ER 500 mg PO BID - Metoprolol Succinate XL 100 mg PO BID - Nitroglycerin SL 0.3 mg SL Q5MIN:PRN chest pain And we increased the home Imdur and Diltiazem: - Isosorbide Mononitrate (Extended Release) 30 mg PO DAILY --> 60mg PO DAILY - Diltiazem 30 mg PO BID --> 120mg XR daily # Pain in left lower chest and LUQ Pain is reproducible by palpation on exam, non-exertional, and not concerning for cardiac etiology. Most likely this is musculoskeletal injury. Patient is passing gas. Treated with: - PPI w/GI cocktail PRN - Tylenol 1 gram TID - Lidocaine patch - DCed with Lidocaine cream 5% # HTN He is hypertensive here to SBP 170. We think that increasing the Imdur and Diltiazem as above could aid with this. We do not know his blood pressures at home. Otherwise we continued with: - Lisinopril 20 mg PO DAILY # HLD (Last LDL 62, goal less than 70 per outpatient cardiologist) - Continued with high intensity statin # DM - Last A1c 6.5 Was previously taking Jardiance but it was DC'ed by PCP in ___ of this year according to the patient. - We held MetFORMIN and treated with Insulin SS # Constipation x4 Days - Senna/Colace # Depression: Continued with home medications: - Sertraline 200 mg PO DAILY - BuPROPion XL (Once Daily) 150 mg PO QAM # Palpitations According to outpatient documentation, patient had sensation of palpitations in the past and he has been taking Flexeril with successful control of those symptoms. - Continued with Cyclobenzaprine 10 mg PO BID # FEN - Continued home Vitamin D ___ UNIT PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had chest pain, both your chronic pain as well as new pain. We examined your heart with a blood tests, EKG, and ultrasound. We found evidence of blood flow that is restricted to a portion of the heart, which is consistent with what we have known from previous procedures back in ___. There is a chance we can fix this, and we would like you to see our interventional cardiologists as an outpatient. You already have an appointment. We will be communicating about your case as well to determine the options. We also addressed pain on your left side, and found no evidence of anything wrong with any of your internal organs. We think this is an injury to the muscle in the area, and we are providing symptomatic treatment.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ yo female with diabetes, HTN, DM, extensive PVD requiring bypass, admitted for cardiac catheterization for unstable angina and positive stress test. She received single DES to RPDA, and balloon rescue of the jailed branch of RPL through radial artery. # Coronary Artery Disease: patient has multiple risk factors for coronary artery disease including HTN, obesity, HLD, tobacco use, PVD, DM, and family history. Her troponins and EKG results were negative for MI at ___. However, her stress test was positive and she was found to have flow limiting lesion of RPDA. She underwent DES to RPDA and PTCA of jailed RPL. She was given integrillin for 12 hours post procedure. She was started on aspirin 81 mg po and clopidigrel 75 mg po daily. She will follow up with Dr. ___ in one month. # Rhythm: Patient was thought to be in atrial fibrillation on an EKG post-procedure. No known hx. No other episodes of atrial fibrillation and she was on normal sinus rhythm for the rest of her hospitalization. Repeat EKG showed normal sinus rhythm. She was started on metoprolol PO 25 mg daily for rate control. Her CHADS2 score=3 but will continue to be on aspirin/plavix for now. She does not wish to start coumadin. Patient has a h/o rheumatic fever when she was ___ years ago. An ECHO was obtained, which showed LVEF>55%, no valves abnormalities, and no change in ventricular function. # Chronic problems: remained stable and she was continued with her home medications -Hypertension: amlodipine, chlorthalidone, losartan -Hyperlipidemia: continue with pravastatin -Diabetes Mellitus Type II: NPH, FSS, sliding scale -peripheral neuropathy: Gabapentin -COPD/asthma: albuterol, fluticasone, ipratropium bromide, adviar -GERD: ranitidine -Depression: bupropion, citalopram -Lumbar back pain: codeine PRN -Neck dermatitis: desonide cream
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain. You underwent several tests that showed that you were not having a heart attack. However you chest pain was most likely from unstable angina. You had an abnormal stress test and you were referred to ___. We found that you had blockages in your coronary arteries. One stent was placed to open a blockage and one was opened with angioplasty. You will need to take aspirin and plavix for at least one year to keep the stents open. Please do not stop taking these medications without talking to your cardiologist first. You were also started on metoprolol, which is a beta blocker to slow your heart rate.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient presented to ED on ___ with a 5 day history of rash that began at his right axilla and subsequently spread to his arms, legs, groin, pannus, and hands. Had been given prednisone and Keflex at outside hospital on ___. Upon presentation, rash was confluent, erythematous and maculopapular in axillae with small papular lesions throughout rest of body causing pruritus. Hands had subcutaneous nodules at knuckles and left great toe was inflamed and painful. Rash: Patient given Salmeterol 125 mg IV and Dilaudid 1mg IV in ED and right axilla skin biopsy performed. Derm concern for ___ ___ prompted admit to floor. Patient was able to ambulate on his own and reported pruritus and left great toe pain upon arrival to floor. Patient given 180 mg daily fexofenadine and 1mg/kg/day prednisone for rash per derm recs and home medications of sertraline and lorazepam held due to concern as causative agents for SJS. Suspicion for SJS decreased upon obtaining results of ___ skin biopsy and dermatology suspected drug reaction with sertraline and lorazepam as likely culprits. Patient given nystatin and miconazole due to suspicion of fungal cause of rash in axillae. Patient reported improvement in pruritus and rash appeared less erythematous. Patient prednisone dose changed to 60 mg daily to be tapered in ___ weeks. Rash continued to improve with decreased erythema and pruritus throughout hospital stay. Patient discharged home on hospital day 3 with prescription for prednisone and fexofenadine and appointments for follow-up care with PCP and dermatology. Steroid-related hyperglycemia/leukocytosis: Lab report on ___ demonstrated elevated glucose level of 280 that trended down to 233 on ___ and 123 on ___. Glucose levels monitored throughout hospital stay to assess potential need for insulin and attributed to high dose steroids. Patient's WBCs were also elevated to 24.5 on ___ which decreased to 19.8 on ___ and was also attributed to high dose steroids. Constipation: Patient given docusate and senna for constipation. Depression/Anxiety: Mental health stable during hospital stay. Patient advised to follow-up with PCP regarding new ___ agents. Restless leg syndrome: Condition stable throughout hospital stay. Continued on home dose of pramipexole. Obstructive sleep apnea: Patient uses a CPAP machine intermittently at home and is unable to tolerate using it consistently. Condition monitored throughout hospital stay without need for CPAP. Asthma: Condition stable throughout hospital stay. Patient continued on home dose of fluticasone-salmeterol, montelukast, and albuterol as needed.
summary: You were admitted for a diffuse rash that was concerning for a possible reaction to medications that you have been taking. Of concern were your usage of Sertraline, Lorazepam, and the recent usage of Keflex. We were unable to determine which medications was the culprit. You were treated with several different steroids during your hospital stay and the rash responded appropriately. You will need to continue the steroids for approximately one month after leaving the hospital. You are not being sent home with either Sertraline or Lorazepam. You should not take any of these medications if you have them at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ F with Wegener's, hypothyroidism, osteoporosis who presented with hypoxia and stridor from pulmonary clinic felt secondary to Wegener's flare. # Hypoxia. She has progressively worsening Wegener's for which she has required chronic high-dose steroids and Rituxan in addition to frequent admissions for flares. Patient greatly improved throughout admission on high dose steroids (Solumedrol for three days and prednisone 60 mg and ultimately discharged at 40 mg daily). Patient had a total of four bronchoscopies with first one being a flexible bronch demonstrating complex extensive right and left main stem 80% obstruction. She was taken back to the OR three more times for rigid bronchoscopies where the left main bronchus was electrocautarized and then balloon dilated. The right side was much stenosed and fibrosed where dilation would be too risky. The site was injected with Dexamethasone to decrease inflammation and she was taken back to the OR five days later where the right bronchi was successfully dilated. During this procedure, she received an additional dose of succinylcholine. Post-extubation, was noted to have a weak inspiratory effort and LMA placement was attempted but the patient was noted to be bronchospastic and so she was reintubated. She was then admitted to the MICU for further management. She was monitored in the MICU for several hours and was extubated without incident. The remainder of her hospital stay was without incident with good saturations in the high ___ on ___ liters supplemental oxgyen. Patient will continue on high dose steroids with guidance on taper by her pulmonologist in the outpatient. She will be discharged to an acute rehab facility where she can partake in pulmonary rehab as she will require aggressive chest ___. # Wegener's granulomatosis. Followed by Dr. ___. She was continued on bactrim ss ppx, vitamin D and calcium and will continue alendronate in the outpatient. # Weight loss, malnutrition: Patient has had a significant weight loss over the past several months. Nutrition was consulted and felt tube feeds were an option for patient. She refused this. It is advised she drink supplements with each meal as she states she enjoys this. # Memory Loss: Patient became more confused and forgetful during her admission stay. Patient's symptoms were similar to last year when she was on high dose steroids. A full work up was performed last year for this and no other obvious causes of her memory changes were found. She also improved once her steroids were decreased. During this admission her memory problems were felt to be due to high dose steroids. Given the significant benefit this had for her respiratory status these were continued with frequent re-orientation for patient. She understood the risks and benefits of steroids. # Hypothyroidism. TSH within normal limits. Continued levothyroxine # Anxiety/Depression. Started citalopram 20 mg during this admission as patient was felt to be significantly depressed and anxious given her clinical picutre. She was also re-started on diazepam 5 mg BID prn anxiety. She was reportedly on this at home. # Leukocytosis: No signs of infection. Felt secondary to procedure and steroids. This should be repeated in rehab to ensure resolution. # Goals of Care: Given patient tenuous respiratory status and overall clinical picture, goals of care were discussed with both patient and health care proxy. It was decided that patient will be DNR/DNI. She would still want to proceed with bronchoscopies as this is helpful to her comfort and shortness of breath.
summary: You were admitted due to difficutly breathing. You had a procedure called a bronchoscopy that showed significant narrowing of your airways. The lung doctors ___ of your bronchi, or airways. These interventions along with high dose steroids taken orally significantly helped your breathing. You were also noted to have an infection of your trachea called tracheitis. You completed a course of an antibiotic called Augmentin. The pulmonary doctors ___ were safe to go to rehab as your breathing has been quite stable in the hospital. You may need a repeat bronchoscopy in the future and the pulmonary doctors ___ contact ___ for scheduling of this. Also, it was noted that you are not eating enough and are quite malnourished. Tube feeds were recommended, but you declined. You should continue to take liquid supplements with your meals. You had problems with memory during this admission. This was felt secondary to high dose steroids. We decreased your steroids as your respiratory status improved. This will be closely monitored by Dr. ___ in the outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ epilepsy on oxcarbazepine, chronic hyponatremia (132-135), chronic headaches, and asthma p/w weakness presents with acute on chronic hyponatremia to 125. # Hyponatremia: Patient presented with acute on chronic hyponatremia to 125. She was euvolemic on exam (vital signs normal and stable, JVP flat, moist mucous membranes, no peripheral edema, BUN/Cr normal). Her only symptom was increased fatigue and subjective weakness. Urine osms were inappropriately elevated at 383 with low serum osms at 267. This is consistent with SIADH, potentially due to medication side effect (oxcarbazepine, trazodone, fluoxetine, omeprazole, ranitidine). Her baseline hyponatremia was likely exacerbated by her large intake of relatively hypotonic gatorade prior to admission, after being initially told she was hyponatremic. CXR was done to rule out possibility of lung malignancy given her nighttime cough and was negative. She is being treated for laryngopharyngeal reflex as well as GERD with ranitidine and high-dose omeprazole and her cough may be secondary to either of these etiologies. TSH and AM cortisol obtained and pending to rule out hypothyroidism or adrenal insufficiency. Although she does have a headache associated with nausea, these are similar to her chronic headaches and there are no focal neurologic signs on exam. Other etiologies seem more likely at this point. Serum sodium improved with free water restriction and she reported feeling less fatigued and weak on discharge. She will continue on oxcarbazepine with free water restriction to 1.5-2L and follow up with her neurologist as an outpatient to consider changing medication regimen. # Epilepsy: Patient has a history of epilepsy, followed by Dr. ___ at ___. She is currently controlled on oxcarbazepine and her last seizure was ___. Per email correspondence with Dr. ___ continued oxcarbazepine with fluid restriction and follow-up as an outpatient to consider alternatives. # Headache: Patient has a history of chronic headaches that have been well-controlled with gabapentin. She reported a severe headache on admission that was more unilateral than her usual headaches, associated with nausea but no vomiting. No warning signs (not particularly bad in the morning, chronic nausea is not usually associated with headache but more often with food). Home gabapentin was continued. # Hypertension: Patient was concerned for high blood pressure to 150/90. She is on home atenolol. Recent outpatient BPs have been 110-130s/70-80s. She has been well-controlled since admission. Home atenolol continued. CHRONIC ISSUES: ================ # Depression: Continued fluoxetine. # Insomnia: Continued trazodone. # Asthma: Continued home meds. # GERD: Continued home meds. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== - Fluid restriction: 2 liters per day -Patient will see Dr. ___ at ___ ___ post-discharge appointment. -Patient will be contacted by neurologists' office with appointment for next week. Alternate AEDs (to oxcarbazepine) will be discussed at that time. -Please consider alternate therapy for GERD and LPR as omeprazole and ranitidine can lead to hyponatremia. -Please follow up blood pressures as an outpatient.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because your sodium level was low. Your sodium level has been low in the past but it was even lower than usual when it was checked in the ED. You were feeling fatigued and weak, which can be due to low sodium levels, so you were admitted. We think that your low sodium might be caused by one of the medications that you are taking. In particular, it might be caused by the trileptal. We think it was made worse when you drank a lot of gatorade because your body held onto the water part of the gatorade, but not the electrolyte part. This causes dilution of the sodium in the body and lowering of the sodium concentration.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with depression, anxiety and history of alcohol abuse was admitted after presyncopal event and found to have multiple lab abnormalities including ketoacidosis, hypokalemia and acute renal failure. She had cardiac catheterization given concerning EKG changes with elevated troponins which showed normal coronary vessels. Echo showed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy of elderly. She is encouraged to maintain adequate hydration and discharged in stable condition with follow up appointments. . #Presyncope: hypotensive by EMS and at triage that was fluid responsive suggests hypovolemic etiology of presyncope vs vasovagal; lack of focal neurological deficits makes primary CNS event unlikely; history not consistent with seizure; there was initial concern for cardiac origin (she has LBBB that seemed rate dependent) given new EKG changes (T wave inversion in V3 and flat T wave in V4 that were not present on prior EKGs). She was in sinus rhythm and she did not complain of chest discomfort or shortness of breath during her stay. She had 20 beats of NSVT per telemetry which made it necessary for her to be transferred to inpatient cardiology service. However, this can be a real NSVT or anxiety related tachycardia with LBBB looking like NSVT. Otherwise, she was in sinus rhythm. There was no coronary artery disease on cardiac catehterization (please see results). ECHO was pursued which showed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy of the elderly in which part of the septum is hypertrophic and causes obstruction when patient is dehydrated. She was discharged with instructions to maintain adequate hydration and to get a new cardiologist. . # Psychiatric issues: She has history of alcohol abuse but reports stopping drinking for the last 4 months. Also had history of valium abuse in the distant past per patient which was used to treat her anxiety. During her stay, she was very tearful and anxious expressing some paranoid ideations about the staff. She was reassured and also was evaluated by social worker who recommended some therapists and the patient seemed receptive. She will be seeing Dr ___ soon who is aware of her situation. The patient did not seem unsafe to follow up as outpatient. . #Ketoacidosis: likely starvation ketoacidosis given rapid weight loss (per PCP ___ 185 lbs on ___ now ___ lbs (pt reports 20 + lbs wt loss since stopping alcohol); diabetic unlikely given no previous diagnosis and glucose < 200; alcoholic ketoacidosis also unlikely if patient truthful about not drinking (serum ethanol negative); osmolar gap negative for other ingestions; serum toxicology was negative. She received thiamine and folate during her stay with good hydration. Her gap closed. . #Acute renal failure: likely prerenal azotemia due to poor oral intake. Received IV fluids and renal function improved. Lisinopril was held in the setting of worsening kidney function. . #Leukocytosis: Resolved. Afebrile. It was likely acute phase reactant, no signs or symptoms of infection. Urine culture showed no growth. CXR no signs of infection. Antibiotics were not administered possibility of infection was low. . #Depression/anxiety: We continued home fluoxetine 40 mg once daily. Social worker followed her during her stay as above. She will follow up with psychiatry as outpatient. . FULL CODE Emergency contact: ___ (wife) ___ Email sent to PCP ___ not current PCP, ___ PCP) and sent letter
summary: As you know, you were admitted to the ___ ___ after nearly fainting. We examined the arteries that supply blood to the heart and did not find any blockages. We performed an ultrasound of the heart which showed that a part of your heart is slightly larger than it should be which can cause decreased blood flow when you are dehydrated. We believe that this is the reason that you nearly fainted. We recommend that you maintain adequate hydration, especially on warm days or in warm environments. We recommend that you are seen by a cardiologist please see your primary care provider for ___ recommendation for a new cardiologist.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Primary reason for hospitalization: Mr. ___ is a ___ with hx of CAD, HTN and AAA s/p endovascular repair who presents with LLQ/left flank pain consistent with renal stone, found to also be hypoxic. Active Diagnoses: COPD: Patient has felt gradually more dyspneic over the past half a year and acutely more dyspneic over the past week, which he attributes to a URI. Differential includes COPD vs. CHF vs. untreated OSA. He was ruled out for a PE by CTa in the Ed. The diagnosis of COPD was supported by emphasematous changes on CT thorax and wheezing on exam as well as a significant smoking hx. His body habitus is concerning for a component of untreated OSA. Peak flow in the ED was low at 300. CHF is less likely because patient's last echo in ___ showed an EF of 70%. The patient was maintained onoxygen by nasal canula with standing combivent nebulizers. On the day of discharge, the patient was less dyspneic and able to maintain ambulatory sats in the ___. Renal Stone/Forniceal rupture. Differential for patient's LLQ/L flank pain included renal stone vs. incarcerated hernia leading to obstruction. Renal stone is supported by imaging findings of visualized obstructing stone, hydronephrosis and ruptured fornix, although the patient's pain did not sound like colic. Patient seemed obstipated/constipated, raising the possibility of obstructing hernia at the time of increased intraabdominal pressure from straining, but this could also be explained by an ileus from the renal inflammation. A urology consult was called for forniceal rupture, he was given fluids to facilitate stone passing, and his lisinopril was held. The patient's Cr continued to trend up the next day, so urology performed a cystoscopy with stone removal and stent placement. Subsequently, his creatinine dropped to within normal range (1.2). Chronic Diagnoses: Hypertension. The patient was not hypertensive during the entire hospital course. He was continued on his home meds Lisinopril 20mg PO qd and Atenolol 50mg PO qd. Hyperlipidemia. The patient was continued on home simvastatin. Depression. The patient was continued on home fluoxetine. Transitional Issues: Code status: Full code He will follow up with urology for stent removal.
summary: You were admitted from home left sided abdominal pain and flank pain. A CT scan of your abdomen showed that you had a kidney stone that was blocking the passage of urine into your bladder and your lab tests showed that your kidneys were not working as well as they had before. You were seen by the urology doctors, and they took you to the operating room where you had the stone removed and a stent placed to help the urine drain. We also started you on a medication called flomax to help your urine drain from your bladder. After the procedure your kidney function returned to normal, your constipation and abdominal pain both resolved and you were able to be discharged home. During your hospital stay we also found that your oxygen levels were on the low side so we started some medication to help you breath. We think is likely due to COPD, you should see your primary care doctor for further evaluation of this after you leave the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # ACUTE RENAL FAILURE: Patient presented in ARF with Cr of 4.7 from 1.2 10 days prior. This was thought to be due to a combination of multiple myeloma progression and hypercalcemia causing pre-renal ARF. Renal U/S negative for obstruction and FENa was 1.1%. He was treated with IVF for his ARF as well as his hypercalcemia (see below) and his renal function improved. He was seen by nephrology who agreed with management. His Cr was 1.1 on the day of discharge. . # HYPERCALCEMIA/HYPOCALCEMIA: Patient presented with Ca of 15. His only symptom was shortness of breath when he presented to the ED. On admission he was treated with aggressive IV hydration, calcitonin, steroids and pamidronate (90mg once). His Ca level returned to ___ after 2 days and calcitonin was stopped. Subsequently his Ca level continued to decrease and he met the criteria for hypocalcemia. Again he was asymptomatic. He was treated with aggressive calcium repletion, both IV and PO, and vitamin D. He had persistent hypocalcemia thereafter requiring aggressive repletion. This was thought to be due to pamidronate. This medication was given when the patient was in ARF, he was given the max dose and it probably was not being excreted at the normal rate so it caused hypocalcemia. His PTH was found to be high while patient had hypocalcemia. This was thought to be a reaction to hypocalcemia, not a primary parathyroid problem. PTHrp was pending upon discharge and should be followed up. Upon discharge his corrected Ca level was 7.2, he was continued on oral Ca repletion, vitamin D and had follow up appointments for Ca checks the day after discharge and the day after. . # ELEVATED INR: Patient presented with an INR of 16. The etiology of this was unclear but it was thought to be due to decreased PO intake causing vitamin K depletion and the patient taking too much coumadin as an outpatient. On presentation he received FFP, vitamin K and his coumadin was stopped. His INR continued to trend down and when it returned to ___ and his ARF had resolved he was restarted on coumadin. Initially at 5mg daily and on discharge he was continued on his outpatient regimen. . # MULTIPLE MYELOMA: Patient's presentation was concerning for disease progression, given that it was known that his disease had relapsed. Patient's treatment with revlemid was stopped on admission given his ARF. Once his renal function was improving his revelemid was re-started at 5mg ___ of home dose) on ___. Once his renal function was back to ___ his dose was increased to 10mg. His MM markers were found to have improved since last assessment. He was instructed to take revlemid 10mg for 3 days after discharge to complete his 28 day cycle. . # DYSPNEA: Initial concern was for infection (viral vs bacterial) although patient had had no fevers or any other localizing symptoms. Given his recent admission for pneumonia he was treated for HAP with vancomycin/cefepime (7 day course) and for CAP with azithromycin (4 day course). He also received standing nebulizer treatments for his COPD as this could have contributed to his presentation. . # AFIB: Patient had a history of atrial fibrillation. His HR remained in the ___ range. His coumadin was restarted prior to discharge and his INR was 1.8 at the time. . # CAD s/p MI: Patient asx and currently NS bradycardia. His metoprolol was held due to his persistent bradycardia and his aspirin stopped due to elevated INR. The patient was instructed to re-start his metoprolol as an outpatient. Patient was instructed not to continue treatment with aspirin as he was already being anticoagulated with coumadin. . # DEPRESSION: Continued outpatient citalopram.
summary: You were admitted to because you were having shortness of breath at home. Upon admission you were found to have an elevated calcium level in the blood, an elevated INR (coumadin number) and acute kidney failure. We treated you with IV fluids and medications to lower your calcium and this resolved. We treated your kidney failure with IV fluids and your kidney function returned to normal levels. We treated your elevated INR with transfusions of plasma and vitamin K administration and this decreased to normal levels. We re-started your coumadin on the day before your discharge. We treated your shortness of breath with antibiotics, this resolved and the antibiotics were stopped. Your calcium was then found to be low and you needed daily calcium repletion. You also had bacteria in your urine and were given antibiotics for this.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old female with past medical history of eating disorder, adjustment disorder, admitted ___ with hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis in setting of ongoing purging behavior, subsequently placed on eating disorder protocol, electrolytes repleted with improving electrolytes and acid-base status, able to be discharged to the care of her parents and plan for inpatient treatment initiation at ___ in ___. # Hypokalemia: Patient was referred from PCP's office for admission with a K of 2.4. She stated that she has not been taking her prescribed amount of K supplements at home in the setting of restrictive eating habits. She was placed on telemetry without signs of arrhythmias. She initially refused IV potassium and was supplemented with powdered K. Throughout her stay the potassium trended upwards and has stayed between 3.1 and 3.8. She was otherwise managed on eating disorder protocol as below. She was discharged on 40 meq po TID potassium powder. #Metabolic alkalosis: Admitted with bicarb 41. This was thought to relate to purging behavior. As below she was placed on eating disorder protocol with slow improvement---it was unclear if this related to slow correction versus ongoing intermittent purging behavior (which patient denied). Discharge bicarb = 39. # Anorexia/bulimia / Severe Protein Calorie Malnutrition - As above, she was placed on the eating disorder protocol upon admission. She was given thiamine and phosphate repletion per the protocol. She was ostensibly compliant with the eating disorder protocol, taking in all meals and complying with all guidelines. As above, there was concern for intermittent purging behavior given poor weight gain and ongoing alkalosis. Given electrolyte stability on repletion, it was felt that she was medically safe for discharge to care of parents for planned admission to ___ inpatient ___ in ___ ___. # Orthostatic hypotension: On initial presentation she had positive orthostatic vitals with her Heart rate increasing up to the 140-150's upon standing, but remaining without symptoms during these times. Her vital signs would recover upon siting on laying supine. Following above interventions with diet, her vitals improved so that she was no longer orthostatic per vital signs, although remained with some mild findings suggestive of chronic dysautinomia from her eating disorder. #tobacco abuse: She was give nicotine gum per protocol #Anxiety: She was prescribed her home clonazepam #Depression: She was continued on her home venlafaxine. Her home Quetiapine decreased to 50 mg PO/NG TID and 200 mg PO/NG QHS due to excess sedation per psychiatry's recommendations. #PTSD/Nightmares: She was given her home dose of Prazosin # CONTACT: Mother ___ ___ TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ================== - Discharge weight: 44.8kg - Would ensure that she is taking ___ Meq potassium by mouth three times per day - given prior variable potassium levels, would continue to follow her potassium blood levels regularly and adjust her daily potassium dose accordingly; would follow bicarb as well, as this may be helpful indicator of ongoing purging behavior - Encourage continued hydration and appropriate nutrition
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had low potassium. While you were her we gave you potassium and also gave you a strict eating regimen to treat your anorexia. Your potassium has now returned to a stable level. Please take the Potassium supplement that we have prescribed you three times a day. When you arrived your blood work showed slight elevation in your creatinine levels which measures the function of your kidneys. We believe that it was elevated because you were dehydrated. It has now returned to normal. Please refrain from vomiting after meals as this can cause you to become dehydrated. Please go to the ___ Treatment Center in ___ ___ for further treatment. The nutritionist has given you a package of food to eat between leaving the hospital and arriving at ___ Treatment Center on ___. Please eat everything in the package.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMHx significant for CAD s/p multiple MIs and 5 stent placement, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia presented with approximately a week of chest pain and elevated troponin, concerning for NSTEMI. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # Chest Pain | NSTEMI | CAD: Patient has a history of CAD s/p multiple MIs with 5 stent placements. Concern for NSTEMI was raised on admission given elevated troponin, chest pain described, and improvement with nitroglycerin. Patient was taken for cardiac catheterization that revealed 50% OM and 100% RCA occlusion. 2 DES were placed. Hospital stay was complicated by episodes of chest pain post catheterization that resolved with morphine, ativan, nitroglycerine gtt. Slight desaturation during chest pain requiring ___ supplemental O2 that subsequently improved to 92% on RA. Chest X-ray was performed that demonstrated no acute process or pulmonary edema. Repeat EKGs showed no acute ischemic changes or changes consistent with pericarditis. Troponins post catheterization were stable and MB was downtrending. BNP was decreased compared to admission. Chest pain was not felt to be due to additional NSTEMI and ddx included volume overload, pericarditis, microvascular ischemia, anxiety. Echocardiogram was performed that demonstrated mild regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with focal hypokineiss of the basal to mid inferior and inferolateral walls and normal contraction of the remaining segments (LVEF= 40-45 %). Given complex overlapped stent geometry, anticoagulation was changed from clopidogrel 75mg daily (which patient was on at time of admission) to prasugrel in order to decrease the risk of late stent thrombosis. Patient was loaded with 60mg prasugrel on day of discharge with plan to take 10mg prasugrel daily thereafter. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # Depression: Continued mirtazapine 45 mg daily, amitriptyline 25 mg QHS # History of Seizures: Continued lamotrigine 150 mg BID # BPH: Continue finasteride 5 mg daily TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: =================== #Chest pain: Patient advised to call Dr. ___ he experiences additional episodes of chest pain. Can consider prn furosemide, colchicine, imdur as clinically warranted. #Anticoagulation: changed from Plavix to prasugrel. Patient loaded with prasugrel 60mg on day of discharge with plan to start 10mg daily thereafter. Patient to take aspirin 81 mg and prasugrel 10mg daily indefinitely. Provided with coupon for prasugrel. #Diltiazem changed to metoprolol given mild ischemic cardiomyopathy #Atorvastatin increased to 80mg daily #Please monitor for radiation induced skin injury in scalp and hair area. Prolonged exposure to xrays during cardiac catheterization procedure. # CODE: FULL (Confirmed) # CONTACT: ___ (wife, ___
summary: You came to the hospital due to chest pain that we felt was due to a heart attack. We performed a cardiac catheterization that revealed blockages in the blood vessels in your heart. We placed 2 stents in your heart. You also received an echocardiogram of your heart that showed that the pumping function of your heart was slightly low, probably due to the heart attack. You had additional episodes of chest pain the day after your catheterization. This chest pain resolved with medications including nitroglycerine. We checked additional EKGs, labs, and do not think that this was due to another heart attack. We checked a chest xray that did not show any evidence of infection. You should call Dr. ___ you have more episodes of similar chest pain to consider adding additional medications. We changed your anticoagulation from Plavix to Prasugrel. You should take this every day. You should also take a baby aspirin (81mg) for the rest of your life. It is very important to take these medications every day, or else your stents may become blocked and you could have another heart attack. If you have any problems paying for the prasugrel, please take your Plavix instead every day and call Dr. ___ so they can try to get you insurance clearance for the prasugrel. We also changed your diltiazem to metoprolol and increased the dosage of your atorvastatin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: AA/P: ___ F c hx of NSCLC and COPD presenting with dyspnea, much improved with treatments as below: . #. Probable COPD Flare - multiple admits with similar presentation in the past. At discharge, O2 sats excellent on home O2 level of 2LNC. Likely precipitant was previous URI with hx of nasal congestion, dry cough, post-nasal drip. Ruled out for pneumonia as CXR without any focal consolidations, afebrile, nl WBC. Used prednisone 60 mg, with decrease to 40mg and then a taper. Continued Levofloxacin 750mg x ___ontinued nebulizer treatments with atrovent/albuterol q6h standing . #. NSCLC - Further treatment as per primary outpatien oncology team. Continued PRN compazine, fentanyl patch, and PRN oxycodone . #. Depression - Continued venlafaxine, trazodone . # Headaches - chronic. Increased fioricet to q4h prn . #. HTN - Continued nifedipine and lisinopril . #. Ulcerative Colitis - asymptomatic currently, Continued asacol . #. Chronic Anemia - Stable from prior, so unlikely to account for new dyspnea symptoms. . # FEN - ate an unrestricted diet per onc recs . # Code - Full . # Ppx - sQ heparin, PPI as per home regimen . # Dispo - ___ consulted, no acute ___ issues. Has home ___.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with an exacerbation of your chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). You responded well to treatment with high dose steroids, nebulizers, oxygen, and an antibiotic called levofloxacin. You will complete your course of antibiotics today (a total of 5 days). You will also be taking a slow taper of your steroid (prednisone) as detailed on your prescription. While on prednisone, you should also take calcium and vitamin D to protect your bones. With these treatments, you were quickly able to return to a normal oxygen saturation on your home level of oxygen. We also had our physical therapists work with you and they did not find any acute physical therapy issues.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old gentleman with a history of chronic pancreatitis s/p Whipple procedure, IDDM, and HTN presenting with abdominal pain. # Chronic pancreatitis flare- The patient presented with abdominal pain consistent with prior flares of chronic pancreatitis. He was treated with bowel rest, pain control, and IVFs. His diet was advanced as tolerated to regular at the time of discharge. He was discharged on his home regimen of Perocet and ___. He has outpatient GI follow scheduled in ___ his outpatient gastroenterologist. # Weight loss- The patient reported a 20 lbs weight loss over the last 6 weeks. However per review of his sheets he was 154 lbs in ___ and 160 lbs in ___. However it is unclear if he gained additional weight from ___ to ___. While his weight loss may be from decreased PO during his 6 week pancreatitis flare, would want to rule out malignancy. Per review of his prior records a colonoscopy was attempted in ___ which was aborted secondary to inadequate prep. This should be repeated and re-ordered by PCP. He also had an MRCP in ___ ordered by his outpatient gastroenterologist which commented on a "Enhancing T2 hyperintense focus seen just to the left of the lateral aspect of the left lobe of the liver most likely an exophytic hepatic hemangioma, but not fully characterized." with six-month follow up liver MRI is recommended for re-assessment which was not done. Repeat MRCP should be considered as well. #Insulin dependent diabetes - The patient is usually on levemir 18 units and sliding scale. He was hypoglycemic on admission to 40 and had an AM sugar of 60 on HD 1 treated with amps of dextrose and D5LR. Episodes of hypoglycemia are likely related to decreased PO intake. His basal insulin was decreased to 5 units while admitted without recurrent hypoglycemia. The patient usually self titrates his insulin at home when his PO intake is decreased. He was discharged on 5 units levemir with instructions to increase by 2 units every two days if fasting sugars are > 120. He has outpatient follow up with PCP for further insulin titration. #HTN - The patient's blood pressures were a little on the soft side, likely from decreased PO. Orthostatics were checked on the day of discharge and his SBP went from 90 while laying down to 118 with standing. He was continued on lisinopril at discharge, and atenolol and HCTZ were held until PCP follow up for blood pressure check. # Acute on CKD - The patient was admitted with a Cr of 1.7 which improved to 1.4 the day of discharge suggesting pre-renal azotemia. His baseline appears to around 1.4-1.5, however there are not many interval labs. PO intake was encouraged and repeat labs should be done at PCP follow up. # Depression- the patient was continued on his home bupropion TRANSITIONAL ISSUES # CODE STATUS: Full code (confirmed) # CONTACT: Daughter ___ ___ ___ decreased to 5 units with instructions to increase by 2 units every two days if fasting sugars are > 120. Expect his insulin requirement will increase with increased PO intake #BP's were borderline in the mid ___. Atenolol and HCTZ were held at discharge; may be resumed at PCP follow up as pressures tolerate # Patient instructed to decrease ASA from 324mg to 81 mg daily
summary: You were admitted to the hosptial with chronic abdominal pain. This was likely related to a flare of your chronic pancreatitis. You were treated with bowel rest, pain control, and ultimately able to tolerate a regular diet. You also reported some weight loss over the past months. While this is likely from not eating, we want to make sure that you do not have a underlying cancer. It is important to speak with you PCP to help ___ rescheduling a colonoscopy. Per review of your records it appears that you were supposed to have a follow up MRI of your abdomen from ___. Please speak with GI doctor about getting this repeated. Also your sugars were on the lower side during this admission. This is likely related to you not eating as much as you normally do. We reduced your levemir to prevent your sugars from dropping to low. We decreased your levemir to 5 units. You should increase your levemir by 2 units every two days if your morning sugar is greater than 120.

text: Brief Hospital Course: 1. Weakness/difficulty concentrating/word slurring: These symptoms had resolved by the time the patient arrived on the medical floor. The patient's transient difficulties were likely secondary to severe hyperglycemia in setting of uncontrolled diabetes, volume depletion secondary to polyuria, and anxiety. . 2. Chest/Left shoulder pain: The patient's chest pain was clearly reproducible with palpation of the left shoulder, and was thought to be related to an old shoulder injury. The patient received aspirin in the emergency room, and he had three negative sets of cardiac enzymes. The patient will follow up in orthopedics clinic. . 3. Diabetes mellitis, poorly controlled: The patient was noted to have very poor glycemic control and was symptomatic with polyuria and polydipsia. The patient received 4 units of regular insulin and 2 liters of normal saline in the emergency room. On the medical floor, the patient's blood glucose was controlled with a Humalog insulin sliding scale. The ___ diabetes service was consulted and provided counseling on diet and exercise. Home insulin was not recommended because the patient was unwilling to check his blood glucose frequently. The patient was discharged on metformin and glipizide, dosed daily for patient convenience. The patient will follow up in the ___ ___ clinic, where he has endocrinology, nurse educator, and exercise appointments. . 4. Anxiety/Depression: These were thought to be contributing the patient's presentation and to his poor diabetes control. The patient described many recent stressors. The patient denied suicidal ideation and was not thought to pose any danger to self or others. The patient has history of panic attacks, for which he takes diazepam. The patient was discharged on his home regimen of bupropion, sertraline, and diazepam. He will follow up with his psychiatrist. . 5. Right knee pain: This problem was stable. The patient's pain was controlled with ibuprofen and a lidocaine patch, per his outpatient regimen. . 6. Hypercholesterolemia: The patient continued his home regimen of simvastatin and fenofibrate.
summary: You came to the hospital with chest pain, weakness, and confusion. Your symptoms were not due to a heart attack. The chest pain seems to be referred from your left shoulder. Your weakness and confusion were probably caused by high blood sugars and dehydration. Because of your high blood sugars, you have been producing large amounts of urine, and this is making you dehydrated. It is very important that you get your diabetes under better control. Diabetes has serious complications, if not controlled. With improved control of your diabetes, you will be able to minimize your risks of experiencing serious complications. You were seen by doctors from the ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs. ___ is an ___ yo F with dementia, depression, PUD, dysphagia admitted from nursing home with bilateral lower lobe pneumonia. She completed a course of vancomycin and cefepime for her PNA and developed c.diff colitis while in the hospital requiring treatment with oral vancomycin and IV flagyl.
