She will need to continue oral flagyl 500mg tid until ___. . 1)Healthcare Associated Pneumonia: Confirmed based on clinical picture with cough, hypoxia, and also with bilateral infiltrates on CXR. Since patient comes from ___, must be concerned for HAP and therefore resistant pathogens. No obvious pleural effusion on CXR. No other obvious sources of infection. BNP elevated though no priors to compare. She was initially transferred to the ICU given concern for oxygen requirement and possible decompensation given her age. She did well and did not develop SIRS/sepsis or significant respiratory distress. She was stable for floor transfer the morning following admission however stayed in the ICU for 2 days given lack of floor beds. Sputum from admission with GPC's in pairs and clusters as well as GNR's, speciation revealed MRSA and pan-sensitive Klebsiella. She completed a course of Vancomycin and Cefepime (was also initially on levofloxacin which was discontinued). Pt was evaluated by Speech/Swallow service and it was determined that she should be supervised during all meals, she should have a soft dysphgia diet, and can have regular thin liquids. . 2) CDIFF colitis: likely developed as a consequence of treatment with broad spectrum antibiotics for PNA. WBC continued to rise and peaked at 28 with treatment with po flagyl, thus oral vancomycin and IV flagyl were given concurrently for seven days and her white count returned to normal. An abdominal CT was done demonstrating prominent rectal wall thickening (which was described as unusual for c.diff). At the time of discharge, oral vancomycin can be discontinued, but oral flagyl (metronidazole) should be continued at 500mg TID until ___. WBC and anion gap should be monitored every two days until completion of flagyl course. . 3)Anemia: She has h/o iron deficiency anemia. HCT on admission at baseline, with slight drop on the morning of ___, however no signs of bleeding with guaiac negative stools. Iron low (13), ferritin wnl, TIBC low normal (likely mixed deficiency / chronic dz). Once bowel movements return to normal, please initiate iron supplementation. . 4) SKIN RASH: On perineum and sacral area. Incontinent and requires frequnet sheet/diaper changes. Rash appears fungal, continue miconazole powder until rash significantly improved. . 5) Dementia: Cont Zyprexa, mirtazapine. Her daughter does not accept this diagnosis. 6) HTN: Pt with no prior hx of HTN, BP has been in 150-160/80-90 range. Did not start antiHTN in the setting of acute illness, would consider adding antiHTN if high BPs persist. 7) Depression: Cont mirtazapine, lexapro. 8) S/p unhealed humeral fx (last year): Cont tylenol for pain control. Please hold Percocet until bowel movts return to normal. 8) PUD: Cont PPI
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with a pneumonia which may have been caused by a problem with your ability to swallow. You completed a course of antibiotics for this. You acquired an infection of the colon while you were in the hospital called c. difficile. You were given oral vancomycin and IV metronidazole. At the time of discharge, you should continue to take ORAL metronidazole for one week--you will receive a prescription for this. None of your previous medications have been discontinued. We have been holding percocet and you should continue to hold this until you are having regular solid bowel movements.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Outpatient Providers:Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of seronegative neuromyelitis optica, now confirmed to be anti-MOG encephalitis (on rituximab + methotrexate), C. difficile colitis, Salmonella colitis, IBS and depression who presented to the ___ ED on ___ with diarrhea and poor PO intake for several days as well as blurry vision. Acute Issues Addressed ======================= #Neuromyelitis Optica #Anti-MOG encephalitis #Chronic relapsing inflammatory optic neuropathy Patient was diagnosed with seronegative NMO in ___. She is seen as an outpatient by her neurologist Dr. ___ ___ Dr. ___ primarily manage her condition. While admitted here for diarrhea, the team was informed of this patient's anti-MOG titer returning markedly positive. Given her retro-orbital pain and questionable blurry vision, an MRI w/ and w/o contrast was obtained of the brain/orbits which showed some enhancement. She was therefore started on 1g of methylprednisolone IV and prescribed a 6 week taper of prednisone. She will f/u in one week with Dr. ___. She is on a PPI at home and she reports she takes calcium and vitamin D. We stressed the importance of also taking the trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis, though Ms. ___ is not in favor of taking this medication. #Diarrhea Patient has a history of IBS. Presented with diarrhea up to 10x/day that self resolved. She was treated symptomatically and given IV fluid resusscitation. On discharge, her diarrhea had resolved, she was able to tolerate POs and improved. #Anemia Her hemoglobin was 7.9 on admission. She was also anemic during her last hospitalization as well, but this was lower than she had been previously. Iron studies were performed which revealed low iron, low ferritin and normal TIBC. Because the patient reported significant GI upset with PO iron supplementation, we did not provide this during the admission. #Abnormal urinalysis The patient had multiple urinalyses w/leukocytes only. She was afebrile throughout her admission, had normal WBC count, and had no dysuria or frequency. Urine culture was consistent with skin contamination. We refrained from treating given that she was asymptomatic, having diarrhea, and has a history of C. diff (and multiple recent courses of Abx). Chronic Issues Addressed ======================= #Weight loss Per chart review her weight was 236 on ___ and 204 on admission (-32 pounds). This may be related to avoidance of eating due to IBS, which the patient endorses. #Depression Continued home fluoxetine and ziprasidone. #Stiffness/Back pain Continued home tizanidine. #Migraines Continued home topirimate and oxcarbazepine. Transitional Issues ======================= [ ]Rituximab: next scheduled dose of Rituximab is ___. Given that may be causing her diarrhea, pt will have to decide in conjunction with her neuro/neuro-optho team whether to continue this medication given her new diagnosis of anti-Mog antibody + disease. [ ]Anemia: Consider iron infusion given history of not tolerating PO iron [ ] PCP ppx with SS Bactrim for the 6 week course of steroids [ ] Prednisone taper week 1: 40mg 2:40mg 3: 30mg 4: 20mg 5: 10mg 6: 5mg [ ] Steroid treatment ppx: PPI rabeprazole 40mg bid. Osteoporosis w/ vitD and Calcium. PCP ___: with Bactrim SS
summary: You were admitted because of diarrhea and blurry vision. While in the hospital, you were given IV fluids for hydration. We performed tests on your blood and stool that did not reveal an infection causing your diarrhea, and believe that it was likely caused by your Rituximab, based on conversations with your outpatient gastroenterologist. You were also evaluated by our Neurology and Neuro-Opthalmology teams. We received results from a test that Dr. ___ sent out in ___ indicating that you likely have a condition called "Anti-mog associated encephalomyelitis" rather than Neuromyelitis Optica. You had an MRI scan of your eyes and their related nerves which revealed a possible flare of this condition that was likely responsible for your blurred vision.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old gentleman with history of orthostatic hypotension, frequent falls, IDDM complicated by autonomic dysfunction and charcot joints, and CAD status post CABG x5 who presented to the ED after a fall with head strike but no LOC. # Fall: Mr. ___ fall is likely related to his known autonomic dysfunction and orthostatic hypotention that are currently followed by Dr. ___ neurology here at ___, further complicated by dehydration from not having eaten or taken fluids for most of the day of presentation due to his longer than expected doctor's appointment. Imaging did not show any fractures or intracranial bleeding. Upon arrival, he showed mild/moderate signs of dehydration so he was administered IV fluids as needed. He was continued on his home dose of midodrine 2.5 mg PO daily. Orthostatics were measured and found to be consistant with his baseline of SBP 100s-110s when standing and SBP 160-180s when supine, post midodrine administration. Vital signs were monitored and remained stable at his baseline. # LLL opacity/mild cough: In reviewing Mr. ___ medical records, it appears that this opacity had been noted at least as far back as ___, and appears stable since. A slowly growing malignancy is a serious possibility that should be considered, but the absence of any systemic B-symptoms is reassuring. Absence of elevated WBC, fevers, chills, productive cough, chest or back pain and SOB were reassuring for the absence of an infectious process. He reported a dry, non-productive cough for the last ~3 weeks that was thought to be likely a side-effect of the ACE inhibitor he had been recently started on, which was held for the length of his admission and also at discharge. Further evaluation of this opacity should be considered by outpatient provider. # ___: Creatinine on presentation was elevated at 2.1 from his baseline of 1.2-1.4, which likely reflected ___ in response to poor PO and fluid intake on the day of presentation. Enalapril was discontinued on arrival given ___. He received IVFs and tolerated appropriate PO intake. Creatinine downtrended throughout his admission and upon discharge was 1.8. # Right leg cellulitis: Mr. ___ reported some soreness of his right leg upon arrival, which was initially thought to be secondary to his fall. He later developed some erythema and upon discharge physical examination the anterolateral aspect of his leg was suspicious for a cellulitis infection. He was discharged on a regimen of cephalexin 500 mg PO twice daily and Bactrim DS PO twice daily for a total of 5 days of treatment. # IDDM: Mr. ___ blood glucose was monitored carefully and remained withing appropriate levels. He was continue on his home dose of Novolog 70/30 34 units twice daily, and was also placed on a humalog sliding scale for tighter control. # CAD s/p CABG: He did not report any CP, palpitations, or SOB throughout his admission. He was continued on home dose of aspirin 81 mg PO daily. # Supine HTN: Enalapril was held given ___ and ___, non-productive cough. Vital signs were monitored closely. His SBP was noted to be in the 160-180s while supine after administration of midodrine. He would likely benefit from substitution of his enalapril by ___ if supine blood pressure control is deemed necessary, but will leave this to the discretion of his PCP and cardiologist. # HLD: He was continued on his home dose of Atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily. # Chronic pain/depression: He was continued on his home doses of Duloxetine 60 mg PO daily, Fluoxetine 20 mg PO daily, and Topiramate 100 mg PO daily. Gabapentin was held given his ___. ============================ Transitional issues: ============================ - PCP: followup on right leg cellulitis - PCP: consider further evaluation and imaging of LLL opacity - PCP/cardiologist: consider initiation ___ in replacement of his ACEi - Emergency Contact: Wife (___) ___ - Full code
summary: You were admitted to the Medicine service at ___ after you suffered a fall, striking your head. Your fall was likely related to your prior history of blood pressure drops when you get up from the sitting or laying position, and was probably exacerbated by the fact that you had not eaten much or drank any fluids for a long period of time. Imaging studies did not show any evidence of bleeding or bone fractures. You were given IV fluids and were continued on your home dose of midodrine. We held your other blood pressure medication, enalapril, given your recent fall, as well as the possibility that it might be contributing to the dry, non-productive cough you have been feeling for the last ~3 weeks.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY FOR ADMISSION: ====================== ___ year old woman w/ hx of HTN, orthostasis, with no history of seizure who is transferred from ___ for evaluation of syncope. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # Syncope # Orthostasis EKG and TTE performed in ED, no concern for cardiac etiology. Profoundly orthostatic to SBP ___ in ED while working w/ ___. Neurology evaluated patient and was not concerned for seizure activity. Recently started on acetazolamide w/ ophtho which can worsen orthostasis. Was given 1L LR bolus on arrival to floor. Her felodipine was discontinued. We began tapering her off her clonidine. She was initially on home dose of 0.1mg TID. This was decreased to to 0.05mg TID. Changed to BID on ___. Plan to have her continue BID dosing with consideration of further decreasing frequency/discontinuing at the discretion of her primary care physician. Her home fludrocortisone was increased from 0.1mg to 0.2mg daily. She did not have any dizziness, weakness, seizure activity, or further episodes of LOC. She remained asx throughout her stay, and her orthostasis was improved, but was not resolved. Orthostatics on discharge ___ included Lying SBP: 158 -> Standing SBP: 96 without dizziness. # Asymptomatic Bacteruria: Received dose of IV CTX in ED. Patient was afebrile and asx throughout her stay. UCx from ___ was consistent with skin and/or genital contamination. Did not continue any antimicrobial therapy. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # HTN: - Discontinued home felodipine, - Weaning off clonidine as above # Hypothyroidism: - Continued home levothyroxine # Depression: - Continued home fluoxetine TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [] Clonidine Taper: Discharged ___ on 0.05mg BID. Continue BID dosing until follow up with PCP and consider tapering to QD dosing x 4 days before discontinuing. [] Discontinued felodipine this admission [] Increased fludrocortisone from 0.1mg to 0.2mg. ___ uptitrate as tolerated. [] Please reinforce counseling regarding lifestyle changes to minimize orthostasis, ie. time between position changes. [] Depression screening (pt declined on initial interview)
summary: You felt lightheaded and had a brief loss of consciousness. You were transferred here from ___. You were seen by our neurologists who wanted to rule out any seizure activity. They did not feel that your episode was caused by a seizure. We got an EKG to look at the electrical activity of your heart, as well as an ultrasound image of your heart. Both of which were not concerning. Our physical therapists worked with you since your blood pressure was very low when you were changing positions (from lying, to sitting, to standing) which likely contributed to your episode. We adjusted your blood pressure medications to help keep this more stable.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ woman who is otherwise healthy who presented with abdominal pain, nausea and vomiting in setting of heavy alcohol use and family history of liver disease found to have possible but not classic alcoholic hepatitis versus other viral infection. ACUTE/ACTIVE PROBLEMS: # Alcohol Hepatitis # Abdominal Pain # Nausea and vomiting: LFTs initially in AST>ALT pattern, but subsequent pattern was not classic for etoh hepatitis and no bilirubin elevation. APAP level 0 (was briefly treated with NAc in ED, which led to lip swelling so this was stopped and NAc listed as allergy). HBV and HCV negative ___ to HBV, will need vaccination), ferritin not elevated, AMA, ___ neg. She had normal TBili and INR and no encephalopathy, so no evidence of acute liver failure. If this is etoh hepatitis, DF was very low so no steroids. Her LFTs were rapidly improving. She did report that for 2 weeks prior to admission she was having abdominal pain with eating, feeling like something was stuck (in abdomen, not esophagus), without changes in bowel habits. Abdominal exam was improved. She does use NSAIDs, so pain related to eating could be due to gastritis or ulcer. She has also been using 3 OTC diet pills, which she had nausea with in the past, and did restart taking several weeks ago, so there is a possibility it could be related or could be just a coincidence in terms of timing. She was counseled on discussing future medications such as diet pills with her new PCP. She was treated with IV fluids and Tylenol was avoided in acute setting. She was counseled on importance of abstaining from alcohol use. HAV was considered given GI illness beforehand and the fact that she is a student; IgG positive, IgM pending on discharge. Advised her on precautions in case HAV IgM positive (but does not live in dorm/handle food). If her IgM is positive, she will be contacted by state ___. # Cholelithiasis: RUQ ultrasound showed cholelithiasis without choledocholithiasis or cholecystitis. ACS was consulted in the ED and did not recommend inpatient cholecystectomy. Elective outpatient surgical consultation could be considered if her abdominal pain recurs. # Adjustment disorder with depressed mood # Alcohol abuse # History of passive suicidal ideations: She endorses severe stress especially over the past week. She has no family in the country and had problems with the friends she was living with, which caused her to feel upset and have passive suicidal ideations prior to admission. She has since moved into her own apartment and feels much better. She denied current passive or active SI while inpatient, so did not pose a risk to herself or others. She has cut herself in the past. She was very receptive to getting help and social work and psychiatry were consulted. She did not need ___ and psychiatry felt she had adjustment disorder with depression. She would benefit from outpatient therapy. She was given resources by social work for signing up with an IOP and/or dual diagnosis program. # iron deficiency: continued home PO iron =============== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Needs LFTs rechecked at next visit - HAV IgM pending at time of discharge; if positive, would be contacted by state as reportable illness - She was set up with new PCP - ___ needs outpatient therapy for coping, stress management, adjustment disorder with depression - Consider outpatient surgical consultation for elective cholecystectomy if pain recurs, but not urgent - Patient will need HBV vaccination since ___ - monitor iron deficiency =============== ___ is clinically stable for discharge today. The total time spent today on discharge planning, counseling and coordination of care today was greater than 30 minutes.
summary: You were hospitalized with abdominal pain, nausea and vomiting. You were found to have abnormally high liver function tests. You had negative testing for hepatitis B and C and autoimmune causes. It is most likely that your liver function tests were abnormal due to heavy alcohol use, causing mild alcoholic hepatitis. The most important treatment is to abstain from alcohol to avoid liver damage. You are being set up with new primary doctor and should have your liver function tests rechecked within the next week. If you have pain, for now, would avoid taking Tylenol since it is processed by the liver. You can cautiously use lower doses of ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin are brand names) ___ every ___ hours, but avoid using for more than a few days, because this can put you at risk of ulcers and stomach irritation.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ left-handed man with a history of ___ with corticobasal degeneration, HTN, pulmonary sarcoidosis, and migraines presents with recurrent episodes of a constellation of findings including total body pain, headache with phono/photophobia, and expressive aphasia. ACUTE ISSUES: =============== # Neurologic constellation, NOS Unclear etiology of his neurologic complaints. Differential included complex migraines, seizures, and other structural brain abnormalities, however diagnostic procedures were still underway when patient left AMA. Of note, the patient reported an even on the night following admission, however this was unwitnessed. He had head imaging performed in the ED including a head CT and CTA which did not show acute changes. An EEG was performed prior to discharge, but official read was still pending when patient left against medical advice. Neurology was consulted and also recommended an MRI with FIESTA sequence, however this was unable to be completed prior to the patient leaving AMA. B12, folate, and TSH were notably within normal limits. # Nausea/vomiting # Loose bowel movements Unclear etiology, though the persistence of symptoms for several weeks is inconsistent with food-borne illness. Patient had recently traveled to the tropics and eaten foods that were potentially undercooked, raising the possibility of parasitic infection. Stool culture and ova/parasites was pending at the time patient left AMA. He received Zofran for nausea. # Normocytic anemia Patient noted to be anemic though not far off recent value at OSH (13.3 in ___. Unclear etiology currently. Does have a diagnosis of MGUS though not of frank myeloma and does not have other findings suggest of MM. Improving throughout hospital course. Iron studies within normal limits. Recommend further outpatient assessment. CHRONIC ISSUES ================ # HTN - Continued home lisinopril, nifedipine, HCTZ, and metoprolol #Depression - Continued home fluoxetine # ?Carotid dissection No evidence on CTA. - Continued atorvastatin 20 - Restarted ASA 81mg as primary CAD prevention ___ disease with corticobasal degeneration - Continued home sinemet TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== # Microscopic hematuria - noted on admission eval, should be followed up by outpatient providers. # Normocytic anemia - unclear etiology. Iron studies within normal limits, as above. Should be further evaluated on an outpatient basis. # Centrilobular nodules noted on chest imaging should also be further evaluated and compared to previous chest imaging. # CODE STATUS: Full (presumed) # CONTACT: Name of health care proxy: ___ Relationship: Fiance Phone number: ___ Addendum: Patient decided to leave AMA in morning. Of note, patient complained the hospital had lost his CDs with images. I explained to patient that all we had were the paper copies. The teams in the ED did not have a CD and neither do we on the floor. He was frustrated about not getting the CDs and left AMA. We explained to the patient the risk of AMA but assured him we would document all the tests he needs to get done. We expressed our concern but patient insisted on leaving AMA.
summary: You were admitted to ___ from ___ - ___. You were admitted for neurologic complaints including sudden onset, progressive total body pain and inability to speak. We were able to complete some head imaging that was negative and an EEG that was still pending at the time of discharge. The neurologists wanted you to get an MRI of your head, however you decided to leave against medical advice before we could have that done. We explained to you the risks of leaving against medical advice, which include but are not limited to persistence or worsening of your symptoms that may result in personal harm, injury, or even death. Despite these risks, you decided to leave against medical advice.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT & PLAN: ___ yo man h/o HTN/HLD, recurrent syncope ___ intermittent CHB, Anoxic brain injury from cardiac arrest s/p DDD ___ (___), recently diagnosed PE (+RH strain) ___, Urothelial CA presented with light headedness and significant hematuria. # Hematuria: Mr. ___ was admitted with hematuria and associated acute anemia ___ blood loss, orthostatic hypotension. he was seen by urology in the ED and CBI was recommended. Due to his past (difficult) experiences with foley, he refused. However, he had no need for bladder irrigation as he had no production of urinary clots. The hematuria occurred in setting of anticoagulation (Lovenox/Coumadin), further exacerbated by UTI (possibly from recent Catheter from recent admission) and potentially from underdiagnosed GU malignancy. Bladder scan showed 2 thickened foci in posterior wall c/f for cancer. The prospect of cancer was felt unlikely given recent cystoscopy which was negative. urology was following the patient and signed off as no intervention was felt necessary. Due to significant anemia, he required 3 units of pRBC transfusion during this admission. Workup for other potential etiologies of anemia included the following: Guaiac negative; Abd/pelvic CT scan without signs of hematoma; hemolysis labs negative. His UTI was treated initially with IV ceftriaxone and then later switched to oral ciprofloxacin. Due to the complicated UTI, the decision was to treat for 10 days total. Urine culture returned mixed flora - however, the likelihood of bacterial UTI was considered high with the positive Nit. He was observed while being on Coumadin alone. There were no further bleeds - and once his INR reached 1.9, he was deemed ready for discharge. He will be followed by ___ clinic as an outpt. oF note, he described being quite sensitive to Lovenox: it lead to taste loss, rash (not seen on exam here), and excessive bleeding (including bloody nose - which resolved when Lovenox was stopped). This was not considered a true allergy, but worth noting in case Lovenox would be required in the future. # Recent PE (a/w RV strain): No O2 requirement, but pt may be preload dependent. He was continued on anticoagulation during the admission. He was initially placed on IV heparin gtt, but then switched to Coumadin alone as his INR was close to therapeutic (when it was stopped). Heparin was avoided to minimize chance of hematuria. # CV: HTN/HLD, recurrent syncope, intermittent CHB s/p DDD ___ (MRI incompatible) ___ - no ASA, fish oil given bleeding. Cont lisinopril # Depression: cont mirtazapine # OTHER ISSUES AS OUTLINED. . #FEN: [] IVF [X] Oral [] NPO [] Tube Feeds [] Parenteral #DVT PROPHYLAXIS: heparin gtt #LINES/DRAINS: [X] Peripheral [] PICC [] CVL [] Foley #PRECAUTIONS: [X] Fall #COMMUNICATION: pt, wife ___ (___), ___ daughter ___ #CONSULTS: urology #CODE STATUS: [X]full code []DNR/DNI .
summary: As you know, you were admitted with bloody urine while being on Lovenox and Coumadin. You were found to be anemia - and required 3 units of blood transfusion. Over time, the anticoagulation was switched from Lovenox to Coumadin. Once the Coumadin was used alone, there were no further urinary bleeding. The red blood cell count steadily increased with time with the discharge Hematocrit of 32. You were also treated with iv antibiotics (ceftriaxone) and then transitioned to oral antiobiotics (cipro). The plan is to treat for a total of 10 days. The final day being ___. Please continue with the Coumadin as prescribed. The ___ clinic will help monitor the levels while you are at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ year old woman with a PMH notable for breast CA, hyponatremia, and recent uncomplicated diverticulitis who presents recurrent abdominal pain and diarrhea. Clinically stable, pain markedly improved with antibiotic therapy, continued liquid diarrhea. Likely current recurrence vs. continuation of uncomplicated diverticulitis. Clinical picture possibly complicated by concurrent antibiotic-induced diarrhea. The patient was started on cipro and flagyl and her symptoms improved. She was able to tolerate PO on the day of discharge and was not complaining of ongoing diarrhea. ACUTE ISSUES: ============ #Diverticulitis: On admission, had LLQ pain, diarrhea, and elevated WBC consistent with diverticulitis, further supported by negative stool cultures and ___ CT abdomen/pelvis showing wall thickening and fat stranding in sigmoid colon without signs of complication. Since she had a recent admission for diverticulitis, this presentation is either a recurrent or unresolved diverticulitis. Initially, lipase was elevated, raising the question of pancreatitis. However, repeat lipase on the same day was normal so this was likely spurious or secondary to something else. Due to the initial concern for pancreatitis, she had a RUQ US on ___ that showed stable ductal dilation in comparison to previous imaging. She was treated with IV CTX and flagyl since she was not taking good PO and had ___ diarrhea per day. She also received continuous D5LR fluid given her diarrhea and poor PO intake. She also received IV zofran for nausea and vomiting as well as IV dilaudid for pain. However, her diet improved after three days and she was transitioned to PO antibiotics and a full liquid, soft diet. She will go home with PO cipro 500 mg BID and PO flagyl 500 mg TID to finish a 10 day course on ___. Due to the persistent diverticulitis, surgery was consulted and they recommended continued medical management. Since she continued to have some diarrhea after her abdominal pain improved, she was started on Imodium 2 mg BID as needed. #Metabolic acidosis: On ___, her HCO3 increased from 16 to 23 on ___ AM. Since her lactate was normal and she is not uremic, this was likely starvation ketosis given ketones on urinalysis and poor PO. Her acidosis resolved with D5LR. #Hyponatremia: Patient has a documented history of frequent / baseline hyponatremia. Patient had hypovolemic hyponatremia on admission (Na 129) and this improved with IVF (Na 134) the next day. However, it dropped down to 129-130 over the next two days. She had osmolar tests done on ___ that showed osm 395, urine Na 89, and serum osm 271. This is consistent with euvolemic hyponatremia and SIADH. Her SIADH may be related to pain or possibly citalopram use (which was held until her day of discharge). Sodium improved to 132 prior to d/c. #IV Access: Patient had very poor peripheral access with unusable left arm secondary to history of breast cancer. She received a midline on ___. This was removed prior to discharge. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #HTN: Had borderline BP on admission so metoprolol and valsartan were held. These were restarted upon discharge. #Depression: Held home citalopram w/ concern for SIADH, but this was restarted upon discharge. #Glaucoma: Continued home eyedrops TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: =================== -___ need to recheck blood sodium to evaluate for continuing hyponatremia. -Will complete 10 day course of cipro 500 mg BID and flagyl 500 mg TID on ___. -Started on Imodium 2 mg PRN for diarrhea and Zofran PO PRN for nausea. -Will have follow-up with Dr. ___ clinic (PCP) on ___. -Will need to have a follow-up colonoscopy to evaluate for sigmoid mass as a cause for recurrent diverticulitis. PCP should arrange this once she improves clinically. -Code status: Full -Contact: ___ (son) ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for worsening diarrhea, nausea, and abdominal pain. You had a CT scan of your abdomen and it showed either unresolved or recurrent diverticulitis. You were treated with IV antibiotics and IV fluids and your symptoms improved. You were transitioned to oral antibiotics and you will go home with these. These are the same medications that you took on your last admission and you need to continue taking them until you complete a 10 day course on the evening of ___. The first antibiotic is called ciprofloxacin and you will take 500 mg (one tablet) twice a day until ___. The second antibiotic is called metronidazole and you will take 500 mg (one tablet) three times a day until ___. You were also started on a medication called Imodium (2 mg per tablet) and you can take this twice a day if you have diarrhea. Finally, you can go home with Zofran 4 mg, which dissolves in your mouth and you can take when you become nauseous.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr ___ was admitted to our hospital for the treatment of dehydration, anorexia as well as fatigue that was ongoing. # SOB/Dyspnea/O2 dependence - He was also found to be hypoxic and was started on oxygen therapy. He endorsed wet cough with green sputum production, and had a leukocytosis, and as result was started on Azithromycin and Ceftriaxone for presumed Community aquired pneumonia. Patient was discovered to have cavitary lesions in his lungs that have progressed due to progression of his disease. He was found to have a hemo/pneumothorax in his right lung. A pig tail was placed into the right lung and 800+ ccs of fluid was drained. He was covered with - ipratroprium/albuterol nebs q6H and Guaifenesin ___ mL PO/NG Q6H:PRN and started inhaled lidocane NEB. Given progression of disease hemo/pneumothorax is likely to recur. Since Pig tail was pulled out, Interventional Pulmonologists discussed options with patient, who is to go home with hospice care. It was indicated to patient and family that they can come back for repeat drainage if this becomes necessary in the future. . # Anorexia/Depression: Likely in setting of progression of his renal cell carcinoma. Renal function is normal. Has known brain metastases in L front lobe which are stable s/p Cyberknife no new dysphagia or other concerning neurologic symptoms. Patient reports poor appetite. He was continued on mirtazapine and megase. . # Renal Cell Carcinoma: Metastatic to Lungs, Brain. s/p Cyberknife treatment to solitary brain met with good effect. On off label pazopanib. CT Torso revealed worsening renal disease. Dr. ___ options with patient and family, and patient will go home with hospice care. . # Hypothyroidism patient was continued on home dose of levothyroxine.
summary: You have been admitted to our hospital for the treatment of your shortness of breath as well as decrease in appettite. While you were admitted we have performed a CT scan of your chest and found that you had increase in the fluid levels in your right lung. Interventional pulmonology was contacted and performed a procedure to take the fluid out of your lung. The following medications have been added to your regimen: Increase Fluticasone to 50mcg/Spray Two sprays 2 times a day START Guafensin syrup (without codeine) ___ every 6 hours as needed for cough. START LEVAQUIN 750mg Tablet once a day for 2 days START Benzonatate 100mg capsule three times a day as needed for cough START DUoNEB 1 nebulizer treatment every -46 hours as needed for shortness of breath/wheezing. START Ondansetron 4mg tablet every 8 hours as needed for nausea START Fexofenadine 60mg Tablet one tablet twice daily START Mirtazapine 15mg one tablet every evening prior to bedtime START Ativan 0.5mg tablet one tablet every 8 horus as needed for anxiety START Lidocaine 2.5ml of solution every 8 hours as needed for cough, do not eat for 1 hour after taking this medication. Morphine 20mg/ml take ___ every 2 hours as needed for shortnes of breath or pain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY: ================ ___ PMH of AML (s/p MiDAC consolidation C1D1 ___, who presented with neutropenic fever (cause unknown), with hospital course complicated by maculopapular rash (leukemia cutis), with e/o peripheral blasts on smear now planning to start IDH2 inhibitor Enasidenib. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # AML # Leukemia cutis Reportedly in CR after 7+3 induction. Had completed first cycle of MiDAC. Hemepath identified a blast on peripheral smear from ___, and skin biopsy from ___ (abdomen and left antecubital) showed leukemia cutis. No evidence of disease in the marrow based on repeat bone marrow biopsy. Based on extensive discussion with primary oncologist, planning on starting Enasidenib (IDH2 inhibitor) in the outpatient setting. The patient was continued on acyclovir and fluconazole prophylaxis in-house. He was briefly placed on hydrea 500 mg PO Q12H which was discontinued prior to discharge. # Febrile neutropenia Presented with reported fever > ___ F without clear source, possibly flossing, as he noted gum irritation at home prior to presentation. He was initially broadly covered with vancomycin, cefepime, and flagyl (given history of odontogenic infection) but was later narrowed to cefepime as CT mandible/sinuses and blood/urine cultures were all negative. Initial ANC was 0. Respiratory viral PCR negative. Patient's highest fever in house was 99.8 on morning of ___. Because of this, antibiotics were discontinued. Patient appeared well clinically throughout hospitalization. ANC normalized to 3470 prior to discharge. # History of RUE DVT Continued enoxaparin 70mg q12h in house. STABLE ISSUES: ============== # HTN # CAD s/p PCI Normotensive and asymptomatic without symptoms and signs of ACS. Continued ASA 81 and carvedilol 3.125mg bid # Major depressive disorder Currently asymptomatic. Continued bupropion # GERD Continued pantoprazole. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== # Please follow-up official bone marrow biopsy read. Still pending at time of discharge. # Working on arranging financial assistance for Enasidenib. Per discussion with social work, patient has been approved for financial assistance in order to afford drug. Patient will follow up with Dr. ___ in the outpatient setting. NEW MEDICATIONS: - Enasidenib (to be started early next week). DO NOT START UNTIL ___. ___ YOU TO. CHANGED MEDICATIONS: - Acyclovir changed to 400 mg PO/NG Q12H HELD MEDICATIONS: - Ciprofloxacin HCl 500 mg PO Q12H CODE: Full (presumed) CONTACT: ___ (Brother): ___
summary: You came to the hospital because of a fever at home. You were placed on antibiotics for a possible infection. You had no further fevers and there was no clear source of infection. Because of this, your antibiotics were stopped and you continued to feel well. Your had a rash, which had developed on your thighs and abdomen. This was biopsied by the dermatology team. This showed leukemia cells in the skin. Because of this, you had a repeat bone marrow biopsy. Fortunately this showed no evidence of leukemia in the bone marrow. The decision was made to start a new medication called ENASIDENIB in order to treat the leukemia and your skin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with a PMHx significant for hepatitis C cirrhosis s/p liver transplant in ___, recurrent cirrhosis and portal hypertension s/p TIPS in ___, and s/p Hep C treatment and sustained viralogic response who presented to the ER ___ with left sided chest pressure and pain that radiated to the arm as well as diffuse abdominal pain. These symptoms had been present without remission for a few days. She also described chills, diffuse fatigue, depression, anhedonia, and low energy. She denied trauma to the chest wall. In the ER she had CT abdomen-pelvis that showed no cause of her symptoms. A CXR was normal. EKG and troponins x2 were not indicative of acute coronary syndrome. She was admitted for inpatient stress testing. Given the nature of her exam, which included tenderness over the entire chest wall and entire abdomen, and her historical description of chest pain that had been present for days without change, we felt that this was highly unlikely to be anginal. She requested oxycodone repeatedly and we thought that she did not have a clear indication. Her pain may have been due to musculoskeletal strain of the intercostal muscles, but this would not explain her diffuse abdominal pain without imaging correlate. She has a history of recent discontinuation of her buprenorphine opiate abstinence therapy due to high insurance copay. Overall, we felt that angina was unlikely and that she could have an outpatient stress test safely. We also recommended to her that she seek psychiatric therapy for depression treatment, which appeared to be a major background complaint. She would also benefit from alternative opiate abstinence therapy if there are more cost effective options, such as naltrexone. PROBLEM LIST: #Atypical Chest pain #Cirrhosis due to hepatitis C #History of liver transplant #History of opiate addiction #Abdominal pain #Hypertension #Depression #Chronic opiate addiction, in remission MEDICATION CHANGES: none TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: []behavioral health follow up for titration of antidepressants []reinitiation of opiate abstinence therapy []follow up with PCP and liver clinic in ___ weeks []consideration of outpatient stress testing
summary: you were admitted to the ___ because of chest pain. You had an EKG and blood work to look for heart attack and these were negative. You had no arrhythmias on the cardiac monitor. A CT scan of your abdomen showed nothing to explain symptoms of abdominal pain. A chest Xray showed no broken bones or pneumonia. The ER admitted you to be evaluated for a stress test to look for coronary artery disease. After further questioning, the chest pain that you described was clearly reproducible with pushing on the chest and did not sound like chest pain associated with heart disease. Because of your age and history of high blood pressure, it may be reasonable to get a stress test to look for heart disease, but this can be done by your primary care doctor in clinic. You also described symptoms of depression in the hospital. We would strongly recommend that you see a psychiatrist in the future to augment your depression treatment as citalopram (Celexa) does not appear to be adequate.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Bacteremia: Pt found to have blood cultures positive for MRSA on admission ___. Etiology unclear, suspected HD cath infection. TEE and TTE were negative for evidence of endocarditis. X-rays of L-spine and feet showed no evidence of osteomyelitis. MRI or L-spine showed no evidence of abscess or osteomyelitis. Ultrasound of site of previous HD catheter (R subclavian) was negative for DVT. On admission pt was started on vancomycin and infected HD tunnel catheter was removed. Blood cultures remained positive until ___. Pt was placed on line holiday. After six days of being afebrile and having negative cultures pt was scheduled for HD catheter placement. The patient however declined this HD catheter placement and hemodialysis. Pt was continued on IV vancomycin which was dosed daily per level. Infectious disease team was consulted. They recommended switching to daptomycin for easier dosing. They also suggested further imaging of his feet. MRIs of both feet were perfomed and results suggested osteomyelitis of the L foot. Podiatry was consulted. They agreed with continued antibiotics but did not feel any surgical intervention was necessary at this time. He will follow up with podiatry in clinic after discharge. PICC line was placed. Pt was to be discharged on daptomycin IV 6mg/kg q48h per ID recs, however, his home services were unable to deliver this dosage. In substitution pt was discharged on IV vancomycin 1000mg q48h for seven weeks. Pt is to follow up with Infectious Disease in clinic after discharge. . # Hypotension and Dyspnea: Hypotension and tachycardia are explainable by line sepsis. He recovered very quickly and was hemodynamically stable with 95%on RA on the floor. CTA was negative for acute pulmonary embolus. Patient has obstructive sleep apnea and occasionally desats at night. . # ESRD: Contaminated HD catheter was removed on admission. Patient placed on HD holiday. Renal was consulted and electrolytes were closely monitored. Given bacteremia and no need for urgent dialysis HD was postponed until ___ when a new tunnel catheter could be placed. However, at this time pt declined catheter placement and hemodialysis. Pt was counseled on the risks of not continuing hemodialysis. During his hospitalization there was not acute need for hemodialysis. He was evaluated and found to be competent to make decisionf regarding his medical care. He continued to be followed closely by the Renal team and will continue to follow up with them as an outpatient. . # HTN: Lasix was initially held in setting of sepsis and restarted with resolution of hypotension. Once hypotension and fevers resolved pt was restarted on home dose lasix and blood pressure remained stable. . # DM2: Continue NPH 75QAM 60QPM and sliding scale here while in house. Pt currently no active foot ulcers. Pt was continued on lyrica for neuropathy and dose was adjusted for renal function. Pt was continued on daily aspirin regimen. . # Hepatitis C: on review of old notes, does not appear to ever have been treated. Patient reports that he would like to initiate treatment if options are available. - genotype 1b - no viral load appears to be on file - re-check viral load. . # Hypothyroidism: Stable; pt was continued on home dose 50mcg levothyroxine. . # Depression: Patient has history of depression and was previously treated with fluoxetine and haldol, however he reports that he hasn't been taking anti-depressants for 6 months per his psychiatrist and his mood has been stable. . # Chronic low back pain: Pt was continued on home pain management regimen of oxycodone ER and percocet. Pt did experience a brief episode of confusion and lethargy when he was given the oxycodone ER and percocet at the same time. After this the medications were administered separately and the symptoms did not return. . # CODE: ___, d/w pt . # ___: w/ patient . # DISP: home w/ services .
summary: You were admitted with fevers and chills. You were found to have an infection of your blood that was likely due to the hemodialysis catheter. The catheter was removed on admission and you were treated with IV antibiotics during your entire hospitalization. You should continue IV antibiotics for a total of 8 weeks. The antibiotics will be administered through your ___ line by a home nurse. You will require weekly labs to be drawn and forwarded to your primary care, infectious disease, and renal physicians. After several days of antibiotic treatment you were cleared for placement of a new HD catheter but at this time you did not wish to undergo this procedure. You made the decision that you were not ready to restart dialysis. Both your primary team and the renal team reviewed with you the implications of this decision. You were informed that you were not in any emergent need of dialysis during this hospitalization, however you will likely require dialysis in the near future.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female with history of chronic pancreatitis, pancreatic divisum diagnosed with ERCP ___, s/p sphincterotomy, who presented with acute on chronic pancreatitis # Acute on chronic pancreatitis: Her readmission to the hospital appeared similar to past acute on chronic pancreatitis flares. It was felt that she likely advanced her diet too quickly at home. She was managed with bowel rest, IV fluids, and pain control with IV dilaudid transitioning to po dilaudid. CT showed new small fluid collection that was likely a pseudocyst that appeared to enlarge slightly based on follow-up ultrasound. Both GI and surgery felt the fluid collection would be best medically managed. Unfortunately, her diet was not able to be advanced past clear liquids during her 2 weeks of hospitalization. There was concern from a nutritional standpoint and she received a PICC line and was started on total parenteral nutrition (TPN). On discharge, she was tolerating oral medications and clear liquids with mild-moderate pain and was advised to advance her diet very slowly at home. She was followed by Dr. ___ pancreatic surgery while in the hospital and should followup with him in 3 weeks for possible surgical intervention. She was discharged on TPN with home infusion services and will need weekly lab checks to ensure her TPN regimen is sufficient. # Bacteremia: Approximately 3 days after admission, she had an episode of high fevers to 103.1 and altered mental status. Blood cultures were sent which grew pan-sensitive Klebsiella pneumoniae. She was initially empirically treated with Cipro/Flagyl overnight, then switched to Zosyn for one day, and then switched to IV meropenem. She continued meropenem for 9 days and then was transitioned to po cipro based on the sensitivites of the bacteria. It was felt that the most likely source of her bacteremia was her GI tract and likely her pancreas. Her subsequent blood cultures were negative and she remained afebrile after antibiotics were initiated. She will continue taking ciprofloxacin orally until ___ for a two week total course of antibiotics. # HTN: In the first week after admission, she had high blood pressures with SBP>180 likely secondary to pain superimposed on essential HTN. She was managed with her home blood pressure medications, and occassional supplementation with hydralazine. At the time of discharge, she was normotensive. # Psych: She has a history of anxiety, bipolar disorder, and alcoholism (quit ___. She had marked depression during this hospital stay and her fluoxetine was increased to 80mg po daily. We continued home topamax, abilify, and trazodone prn for sleep. # Code Status: She was full code during this hospitalization.
summary: You were admitted due to a pancreatitis flare. You had a CT scan that showed that you have a small cyst in your pancreas. You also had a bacterial infection in your blood that may have been caused by an infection in your pancreas. You were treated with IV antibiotics, and then switched to antibiotics by mouth. The GI team and the surgery team participated in your care in the hospital and felt that there was no need for drainage of your pancreatic cyst or surgery at this time. Since you could not eat due to pain and nausea, you were started on IV nutrition called TPN. We treated your pain with pain medication by mouth and IV fluids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY STATEMENT: ==================== ___ female with diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, presenting with chest discomfort, positive troponin/NSTEMI with apical hypokinesis focal akinesis of the distal third of the LV not consistent w/ single vessel territory with mildly reduced EF of 45-50% without evidence of thrombus and only with mild LAD disease on coronary angiogram (40% at multiple points in the LAD). Pt chest pain free off nitro drip and was discharged home with follow up. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - Consider follow-up echo due to apical hypokinesis in the absence of significant CAD - Atorvastatin dosage has been increased from 10mg to 40mg daily - Consider initiation of Carvedilol as an outpatient for BP control/cardioprotection ACTIVE ISSUES: ============== #NSTEMI Presenting with chest pain, positive troponin/NSTEMI, pt was put on tele, heparin gtt, nitro gtt (CP went away), given full dose ASA, started on metop, got atorva 80, and was sent for cath and TTE. Found to have apical hypokinesis focal akinesis of the distal third of the LV not consistent with single vascular territory with mildly reduced EF of 45-50% without evidence of thrombus and only with mild LAD disease. Following procedure, chest pain resolved and nitro/heparin drip were not resumed. Takotsubo is a possibility given apical hypokinesis without significant CAD although no precipitating factor. She does have cardiac risk factors including DM, HTN, HLD, obesity, early FH MI in her father, and on low-dose statin although LDL was recently in ___. Intensified statin to 40mg on discharge. Given HTN iso mildly reduced EF, will recommend starting a beta blocker outpatient. While hospitalized, was given metop tartrate 6.25 q6h. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # T2DM - A1c 6.4, put on ISS and diabetic diet while inpatient, and metformin and glipizin held but restarted on discharge. # HLD - increased atorvastatin # HTN - continued lisinopril 5 while inpatient, continued on discharge # Glaucoma - continued home eye drops # Depression - continued fluoxetine CONTACT: ___ ___ CODE STATUS: Full Code (confirmed with patient)
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had chest pain. In the hospital, our blood work was concerning for injury to your heart. Fortunately, your heart vessels were not blocked and no further intervention was necessary. Ultrasound imaging of your heart showed some parts of the heart were not moving as much as expected and that the heart wasn't pumping blood quite as effectively as normal. It is unclear exactly the cause of these changes, but your condition improved and you should have additional imaging of the heart in in the future to see if the heart's pumping improves. We made some adjustments to your medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with diastolic cardiomyopathy, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on amiodarone and Coumadin who is admitted with evidence of decompensated diastolic heart failure in the setting of diarrhea, found to have unresolved urinary tract infection (s/p Bactrim) and mild c diff colitis who was briefly diuresed to euvolemic and discharged stably with close follow up with PCP, cardiology and home services. # Acute on Chronic Diastolic heart failure (EF >= 55%): Ms. ___ presented from home following subacute onset of dyspnea. Stated that she went to visit her boyfriend at his ALF 9 days before admission which had Noro outbreak. Subsequently developed diarrhea and nausea which resolved. However, also endorsed dysuria and was treated with short course of Bactrim. In the interim, for about the week PTA, she endorsed been feeling short of breath and unsteady on her feet with associated dyspnea. Then presented to ___ where found to be in acute on chronic diastolic HF and. Some nonspecific abdominal bloating on ROS. Infectious work up revealed very dirty UA and in setting of bloating and recent abx for prior (?unresolved) UTI, a positive C diff. She was gently diuresed with iv Lasix x48 hours and treated for multiple infectious etiologies. She was remarkably stable and functional while admitted. Given 40mg PO Lasix on day of discharge and discharged with 20mg Lasix PRN for weight gain and close follow up. # C diff colitis As above, likely developed in setting of norovirus altering gut flora and recent UTI treatment with Bactrim leading to superimposed C diff colitis. Afebile w/o leukocytosis. No abdominal tenderness and only 2 BM in last 24 hours, thus no ileus. Initiated on PO flagyl for mild c diff colitis ___. -Will complete 14 day course Flagyl 500mg PO q8h for mild c diff colitis (d1: ___ end ___ # Urinary tract infection: Evidenced by grossly positive UTI, despite treatment earlier this month with Bactrim. Macrobid started in ED. F/u urine culture negative. At no point did pt have complaints of dysuria. Completed 4 day course of Nitrofurantoin 100mg PO q12h (___). ================ CHRONIC ISSUES: ================ # Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation: CHADS2 score is 4, on Coumadin with therapeutic INR. Was well within range on admission and very stable. TSH checked in setting of CHF exacerbation and amioderone use and was wnl. - continued Amiodarone 200 mg PO/NG DAILY; - continued Warfarin 4 mg PO/NG DAILY # Hypertension: BP mildly elevated on admission to 160s systolic. Did not want to overcorrect and used threshold of SBP <150. Increased amlodipine to 10mg daily, added Carvedilol 6.25mg twice daily and continued home losartan at 160mg daily. # Aortic stenosis: Last TTE ___: Mild aortic valve stenosis (valve area 1.2-1.9cm2). Trace aortic regurgitation is seen. Mild to moderate (___) mitral regurgitation is seen. It was felt that her AS was not contributing to her current exacerbation given infectious etiologies and response to relatively gentle iv diuresis. CONTINUED: # Diet controlled diabetes: Diabetic diet. # HLD: Atorvastatin 10 mg PO/NG QPM # GERD: Continue Omeprazole 20 mg PO BID # Depression: Continued Sertraline 37.5 mg PO/NG DAILY # Chronic pain: Continued gabapentin and lidocaine patch *****TRANSITION ISSUES***** # CONTACT: daughter, ___, ___ # CODE STATUS: full, confirmed # Follow up volume status and weight (discharged on PRN Lasix) # Follow up BP; increased amlodipine FROM 7.5mg TO 10mg daily and added Carvedilol 6.25mg twice daily # c.diff treatment for 14-day course(course of Flagyl 500mg PO q8h ___ # f/u current UTI treated with macrobid # Bactrim may have predisposed to C diff colitis and UTI on admission responsive to Nitrofurantoin. Would recommend avoiding Bactrim and Abx w/anaerobic activity in future if possible. # No changes to Coumadin, routine follow up at PCP ___ # Discharge Weight: 67.8kg
summary: You were admitted to the ___ for evaluation of your shortness of breath. You were determined to have a mild exacerbation of your (diastolic) heart failure. You were given intravenous and pill forms of a medication called "Lasix" to help you remove this excess fluid that had built up causing your difficulty breathing. You responded very well to these medications. Additionally, we noted you were having some loose bowel movements and your abdominal exam was slightly abnormal. We sent a stool test for a bacteria called Clostridium difficile (C. diff) which returned positive. As we discussed with your daughters present, this is a bacteria that many individuals have in their intestines normally. You did not do anything or eat anything which caused it to be there. It is likely that you developed an infection related to this bacteria in the setting of the diarrheal illness you had a week or two ago and the antibiotics you were given for a urinary tract infection called "Bactrim" (sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim). While this medication is NOT an allergy, you should be cautious in taking it in the future as it is not uncommonly indicated in predisposing individuals to this infection. Finally, your urinary tract infection you previously had had not quite resolved. You were given a short course of medication "Nitrofurantoin" or "MacroBid.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with psoriasis here with cellulitis and possible PICC infection, found to have upper extremity DVT. # UE DVT: The patient's PICC site was noted by her ___ to be red and tender. The PICC was removed and tip cultured out of concern for possible line infection. UE ultrasound revealed DVT in the left axillary and basilic vein. Although her self-reported falls are a concern for anticoagulation, it was felt that the benefits of at least short term anticoagulation (likely ___ months) outweighed the risks. She is to be discharged on warfarin with a lovenox sc bridge. The duration of her anticoagulation is to be at the discretion of her primary care physician. # Cellulitis. Patient with recurrent cellulitis ___ and ___. She was recently discharged from ___ on a course of vanco/CTX to end on ___. It appears most likely that her psoriasis is likely the leading to these recurrent infections. Currently, she reports that her legs are slightly more swollen, however overall her legs have improved. She was continued on her planned antibiotic course which finished on the day of discharge. She was also additional diuresis while in the hospital to help decrease some of her chronic ___ edema. # Elevated troponin. Upon presentation, the patient was noted to have a slightly elevated troponin level with flat CK-MB and no EKG changes. She denied any current or previous chest pain. It was felt that this was not secondary to ACS, and her troponin trended downwards. She was continued on her statin, beta-blocker, and aspirin while being monitored on telemetry with no events. # Chronic diastolic CHF: The patient was continued on her home diuretic doses. She was also given additional diuretic for increased ___ edema as above. # Type II IDDM: She was continued on the ___ sliding scale that was established during last admission. # Hypertension: Enalapril and metoprolol were continued at home doses. # Psorasis: Continued clobetasol. # Hyperlipidemia: Continued statin. # Depression: Continued Cymbalta and mirtazapine ====================================== Transitions of Care ====================================== # Anticoagulation: patient was discharged on lovenox and warfarin 5 mg po qday. She is to have her INR checked and faxed to her PCPs office. The full duration of her therapy is to be left at the discretion of her PCP. # Pending labs: Blood cultures (___) remain preliminarily no growth to date upon discharge.
summary: We hope you continue to feel well. You were brought to the hospital as your visiting nurse was concerned that your PICC line might have been infected. The redness and swelling were concerning to us, and your PICC line was removed. An ultrasound of your arm revealed that a clot had developed in one of the deep veins of your arm, which we believe is what caused your symptoms. Due to the clot in your arm, you will need to be on blood thinning medications for at least the short term, but we recommend you speak to your primary care doctor to determine the exact duration. Your legs still appear red and swollen, although it appears the infection is improving.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: Mr ___ is a ___ w/ PMHx HIV on HAART, Stage II NSCLC, and recurrent C Diff colitis who presented with recurrent C. diff. He was treated with PO vancomycin and discharged with a plan for long-term suppression on an extended PO vancomycin taper. ACUTE ISSUES: #C. diff colitis: Third recurrence of C diff colitis, possibly related to recent antibiotic course. Pt had severe abdominal pain, watery diarrhea, and low appetite. CT on admission showed acute hemicolitis, for which he was initiated empirically on cipro/metronidazole/po vancomycin until stool studies were positive for C. Diff. Subsequently, he was narrowed to po vancomycin and metronidazole on ___ for a 10 day course of antibiotics (___). Given that this was third recurrence of C. diff colitis, GI and ID were consulted for consideration of a fecal transplant. However, given risks of colonoscopy required for fecal transplant, decision was made for trial of an extended vancomycin taper for C. diff suppression. # Symptomatic hypotension: Patient with multiple episodes of dizziness, nausea, symptomatic hypotension to the 90/50s, requiring additional IV hydration. Symptoms initially attributed to ongoing infection/inflammation in addition to ongoing diarrhea and poor po intake with building net deficits. Home torsemide was held in setting of net negative fluid balance. # Urinary retention: Thought to be secondary to inflammation and underlying benign prostatic hypertrophy, for which a Foley catheter was inserted on ___ and finasteride was added on ___. Foley removed ___ and patient was able to void independently. #CHF: Held home torsemide. Patient was hypo- to euvolemic during the hospitalization. #CBD 13 mm: #elevated bilirubin on admission: CBD size and elevated bilirubin on admission raised concern for cholestasis in this pt. However, s/p cholecystectomy CBD at 13 mm is normal per GI, and bilirubin quickly corrected with fluid resuscitation; thus no intervention was required. CHRONIC/STABLE ISSUES ===================== #Squamous cell carcinoma of the lung: stage II s/p radiation and chemotherapy. #HIV: CD4 470 ___. Continued on HAART therapy. #CAD: Continued home ASA and statin. #COPD: Continued home regimen. #Depression: continued on venlafaxine, abilify, and quetiapine and follow-up as outpatient TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [ ] Medication changes: - continue taking oral vancomycin every 6 hours and oral metronidazole every 8 hours until ___. On ___, start taking only oral vancomycin 1 pill every 12 hours for a month (___). Discuss with PCP at ___ how to continue with taper - hold home torsemide until PCP determines it is safe to restart - continue tylenol ___ mg every ___ hours as needed - started on finasteride 5 mg for BPH [ ] Follow- up appointments: - with Urology on ___ - with ___ provider ___ ___ [ ] biliary duct dilation: per GI, pt can consider outpatient f/u MRCP, though not formally recommended. # CODE: FULL # CONTACT: ___, ___
summary: because you developed some abdominal pain and diarrhea. We tested your stool and found that you have 'C Diff' (Clostridium difficile bacteria). We treated your infection with two antibiotics called 'vancomycin' and 'metronidazole' ('flagyl'). You developed some inflammation of your bladder and were unable to urinate on your own so we placed a foley catheter to help you pee. We started you on a medication to help you urinate more easily called 'finasteride'. We took out your catheter after giving the bladder time to relax and watched you to make sure that you can void on your own. We gave you some fluid boluses to keep your blood pressure up as you were excreting more than you were taking in. Continue taking vancomycin liquid every 6 hours and metronidazole 500 mg tab every 8 hours for another X days. After that, take the vancomycin liquid twice a day for a month.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year-old male with hx of depression and one seizure in the past who presented with micturition syncope and hypokalemia. #Micturation Syncope - Given IVF. Seen in consultation by neurology who did felt that this presentation was not concerning for a seizure. EKG and telemetry did not reveal any evidency of arrhythmia. Recommended aggressive hydration and precautions when getting up to use the bathroom. # Hypokalemia - Unclear if spurious lab value as subsequent labs revealed normal serum postassium. Patient denied any diuretic/laxative use, diarrhea, or urinary symptoms. Did not have hypertension or metabolic alkalosis. Recommended repeat electrolyte panel soon after discharge. #Depression: Continued fluoxetine. #Psoriasis: Continued hydrocortisone cream.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after passing out while urinating. You were evaluated by neurology for evidence of seizure. They did not feel that your episode was concerning for a seizure. Most likely your episode was due to an entity called "postmictrition syncope" due to changes in your blood pressure and heart rate while using the bathroom. Please make sure that you remain adequately hydrated. You were found to have a low potassium level on your blood work. Your potassium was repleted and your potassium normalized. You were monitored for 24 hours without potassium repletion and the level remained normal. You should try to eat food with potassium regularly such as bananas or orange juice. Please have your blood work checked in ___ weeks at your primary care doctor's office to ensure your potassium level remains normal.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY ==================== ___ old woman with a history of atrial fibrillation on apixaban, HTN, depression, and dysphagia, admitted for workup of dysphagia, found to have new diagnosis of ALS. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== #ALS [] F/u new diagnosis of ALS: pt will call for appointment at next ___ clinic on ___. Discharged with script for riluzole 50mg BID. [] Consider Palliative Care, Social Work consults. [] F/u nutrition, weight. Encourage supplements per Nutrition eval: scandishake BID, magic cup daily, smoothie daily. #CORD COMPRESSION [] MRI C-spine demonstrated cord compression at C2-3, C3-4, C4-5 and C6-7 without cord signal abnormality or abnormal enhancement. Per Neurology, appears chronic and not unusual for pt's age. No indication for urgent intervention, could consider further outpatient workup if pt's chronic neck pain is thought to be related to cervical cord compression. ACUTE ISSUES ==================== #Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis #Choking sensation #Dysarthria #Tongue fasciculations ___ year old woman with a several month history of progressive dysarthria, hoarseness, and to a lesser degree dysphagia. This is superimposed on weight loss in the past 6 months, at least partially stemming from significant anxiety surrounding food intake after an episode in ___ where she choked and required the Heimlich maneuver. Prior outpatient workup included barium swallow demonstrating esophageal dysmotility. During this admission, SLP was consulted and video swallow study demonstrated significantly worsened swallow from last evaluation in ___. Neurology was consulted with concern for motor neuron disease given bulbar weakness and tongue fasciculations. Neuro exam notable for hypophonia, tongue fasciculations, wasting of thenar eminence and intrinsic hand muscles, fasciculations of the upper extremities, and diffuse hyperreflexia. Exam and EMG/NCS study consistent with ALS. MRI brain and C-spine without central pathology to explain her symptoms. New diagnosis of ALS was discussed with patient and family by Neuro attending Dr. ___. LFTs wnl, pt was discharged with script for riluzole 50mg BID and plan for follow up in ___ clinic. #Weakness #Severe malnutrition / weight loss Patient with cachectic appearance and marked 20 pound weight loss over past year, despite reported adequate PO intake with Ensure supplements. Likely ___ ALS as above, perhaps some element of decreased intake secondary to fearing choking sensation. Per SLP evaluation, safe to continue regular diet with thin liquids with chin tuck pt instructed to select softer foods from the menu and take small bites/sips. Nutrition recommended supplements with scandishake BID, magic cup daily, smoothie daily, multivitamin with minerals. CXR without evidence of aspiration. The possibility of a feeding tube will be considered further in follow-up with neurology. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # Afib Continued home apixaban 5 BID. # Depression Continued home sertraline. # Hypertension Continued home carvedilol, lisinopril. >30 minutes in patient care and coordination of discharge
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were losing weight and experiencing choking and coughing sensations. A CXR did not show evidence of pneumonia or aspiration. The Speech and Swallow team performed a bedside evaluation and video swallow study. They felt that your swallow was worse than during last evaluation in ___, and in order to prevent aspiration of food into the trachea it is important to tuck your chin while eating. The Neurology team evaluated you and recommended testing including an EMG and MRI. These tests showed that you have Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). Please start taking a new medication, riluzole, for ALS.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr ___ is a ___ y/o M with PMH of cardiac surgery for repair of anomalous pulmonary artery and ASD (at age ___ and history of skull fracture (complicated by anosmia), who presented to ___ with palpitations, found to be in wide complex tachycardia, was cardioverted into VFib and defibrillated to NSR, and transferred to ___ for further workup. Cardiac MRI on ___ was consistent with RV arrythmogenic cardiomyopathy. EP study on ___ found multiple abnormal right ventricular foci which were ablated. On ___ the patient had an implantable cardiac defibrillator placed. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # Wide complex tachycardia # Arrythmogenic Right ventricular Cardiomyopathy The patient presented with monomorphic ventricular tachycardia having a LBBB, inferior right axis.. The cardioversion at ___ could not be accurately synchronized because of the wide QRS and rapid rate (sine wave configuration), resulting in VFib. The patient remained in normal sinus rhythm during his hospital stay, with occasional PVCs. Cardiac MRI was performed on ___ and ___ with the following overall impression: "Mildly dilated right ventricle with mild global free wall hypokinesis and regional areas of dyskinesis and thinning, with patchy late gadolinium enhancement in the free wall, consistent with arrhythmic right ventricular cardiomyopathy. Persistent left superior vena cava with dilated coronary sinus. No clear flow across interatrial septum seen or hemodynamically significant intracardiac shunt (RV:LV SV = 0.99). Normal left ventricular wall thickness/mass index with mildly increased cavity size and mild global systolic dysfunction. No early or late gadolinium enhancement in the LV consistent with absence of focal fibrosis/scar." These results were consistent with a diagnosis of right ventricular arrythmogenic cardiomyopathy. EP study was performed on ___, with electrical mapping showing multiple areas of low voltage in the right ventricle, which were ablated. On ___, further cardiac MR imaging was obtained to better characterize the right ventricle. On ___, the patient had an ICD placed. He is now being discharged with close follow up with the electrophysiology clinic and Dr ___. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # H/o anomalous pulmonary artery and ASD, s/p repair Monitored as an outpatient by Dr ___ in ___. # H/o skull fracture: neuro exam normal. # Depression: - Continue home sertraline Transitional Issues: None Meds Added: None Meds Changed: None Meds Discontinued: None DC Cr: 1.0 DC Weight: 226 lbs Code: Full
summary: You were admitted because you had an abnormal heart rhythm You were admitted to the hospital because you had an abnormal heart rhythm called ventricular tachycardia. You received an imaging test called a cardiac MRI to examine the structure of your heart. You also had a study of the electrical circuits within your heart, and multiple abnormal areas were "ablated," meaning they no longer can generate the abnormal electrical activity that may have caused the abnormal heart rhythm. In addition, you received an implantable cardiac defibrillator, which can help to terminate abnormal rhythms if they happen again in the future.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with h/o afib, sCHF (EF 35%), DM, CKD (Baseline Cr 2) who presents with ___ episodes low volume, melanotic stools x 1 day and bilateral lower abdominal pain, concerning for ischemic colitis. ACTIVE ISSUES: #Severe sepsis: Patient met ___ SIRS (leukocytosis, HR in high ___ in setting of beta-blocker use) on admission with concern for GI source of infection. On presentation she had signs of end organ hypoperfusion with ___ and a lactic acidosis. In ED had CTA showing evidence of enteritis and was given fluids, empirically started on Vanc/Cefepime/Flagyl. Given hypotension on arrival she was additionally given Hydrocortisone 100mg IV x1 in setting of chronic steroid use and concern for adrenal insufficiency. In the MICU, she was continued on broad spectrum antibiotics with Vanc/Cefepime and Flagyl for concern of intraabdominal infection and was additionally started on PO Vanc until ruled out for C.diff. She continued to receive IV fluids for resuscitation. Surgery was consulted for ongoing concern that this might be an ischemic colitis and performed serial abdominal exams while patient was in the MICU. Patient lactate normalized and her renal function improved. She was transferred to the general medicine floor on ___ where she continued Cefepime/Flagyl. #Enteritis: Concern for ischemic colitis vs infectious colitis as seen on CT. Patient presented with ___ episodes of low volume dark red bloody stools x 1 day and in this setting with a lactic acidosis, patient was admitted to the MICU for close monitoring. Abdominal exam with point tenderness bilateral lower abdomen in the setting of chronic steroid use, which can mask peritonitis concerning for ischemia. She had serial abdominal exams by the MICU team and ACS and continued to improve with treatment of her sepsis as above. She continued Cefepime/Flagyl to empirically treat an intraabdominal infection. ___ on CKD: Patient with ___ on presentation, differential included pre-renal azotemia, ATN in setting of hypotension vs. obstructive given her very low urine output on presetnation. She was given IVF as above to treat her sepsis and renal function improved to baseline. #Transaminitis - Mild transaminase elevation in setting of severe sepsis. LFTs were trended and improved. CHRONIC ISSUES: #Paroxysmal Afib: on amiodarone for rhythm and metop for rate control. Warfarin for anticoagulation. INR 3.3 on presentation, Coumadin was held in setting of possible need for surgical intervention and concern for ongoing GI bleed. Her Metoprolol was additionally held in setting of sepsis. Amiodarone was continued, and Coumadin was restarted with a therapeutic range INR (2.2) on discharge. #HFrEF (EF 35%) - Hypovolemic on presentation in setting of colitis/sepsis. Her home Torsemide and Lisinopril were held on admission. She was given cautious IVF in light of her recent CHF exacerbations. Home torsemide was restarted after concern for an volume overloaded state. Respiratory status remained at baseline. # Pericardial Effusion: Seen on CT, appears stable from prior exams. # COPD: Continued tiotropium. # Depression: Continued venlafaxine. # RUL nodule: Stable from prior, needs repeat CT chest ___ per outpatient plan. Currently scheduled. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Needs to f/u with GI in 1 week. Will need another colonoscopy -Levofloxacin 750mg QD for three days. last dose ___
summary: You were admitted the ___ following an episode of bloody stools. You were found to have a high white blood cell count and other signs of infection. You were started on antibiotics and monitored for improvement. You quickly improved while on the antibiotics. While here you developed some difficulty breathing. A chest xray was done and revealed some fluid in the lungs. This was concerning for a possible pneumonia so you were given additional antibitoics. Your breathing quickly improved over the course of a couple days. You will be discharged with a prescription for antibiotics which should be taken until ___. Based on the CT that was done there was concern for your lower colon. It was recommended that you follow-up with a GI Physician and get ___ colonoscopy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F, hx HTN/HLD/DM2, OA, anx/depr, Parkinsonism/tremor, dementia, somatoform disorder, presents with acute SOB (resolved by the time of arriving the floor) and abdominal pain in context of constipation. # Dyspnea. Unclear. Resolved by the time patient arrived the floor. Her troponin was flat, making ACS unlikely. D-dimer was negative, making VTE unlikely. Lung exam and CXR were not suggestive of pneumonia or other intrapulmonary process. Telemetry did not capture any concerning arrhythmia while in the hospital. An outpatient event monitor would be helpful in this setting. It is also possible that patient had an anxiety/panic attack as mentioned by her family. Her symptoms were validated but reassured. # Abdominal pain/constipation. LFTs were within normal. UA was unremarkable. Based on her exam as well as the abdominal X-ray, she was constipated. The opioids that she was on could certainly have contributed to her constipation. Patient had a large bowel movement while in the hospital. She was given simethicone for gas discomfort. # Osteoarthritis and chronic pain. She was continued on home Tylenol, MS ___, and oxycodone. # T2DM. She was on insulin sliding scale and diabetic diet while she was in the hospital. # Hypertension. She was continued on home amlodipine and atenolol. # Hyperlipidemia. She was continued on home simvastatin. # Anxiety/Depression. She was continued on mirtazapine and lorazepam. However, it is unclear if patient was taking lorazepam at home. # GERD. She was continued on home omeprazole and famotidine. # Parkinsonism, tremor: continue pramipexole. Transitional issues: [] consider event monitor [] managing underlying anxiety [] encourage more aggressive bowel regimen
summary: You were admitted because you felt short of breath and constipated. We gave you medicine to encourage bowel movements, and you felt better after a bowel movement. Your shortness of breath went away while you were here. Your labs did not show heart attach, pnuemonia, or clots. Your breathing may have been affected by pressure from your bowels or by anxiety. You had a bowel movement while you are in the hospital. You should make sure you are taking a stool softener while you are on narcotic pain medication. Simethicone was started for gas discomfort You should make sure to talk to Dr. ___ to discuss anxiety management. You states that you are NOT taking lorazepam (Ativan) at home. Therefore, it is not included in your medication list.
