text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with a history of dementia, HFpEF (LVEF>55% in ___, chronic venous insufficiency, atrial fibrillation on dabigatran, h/o of MVR with porcine valve, and tricuspid valve repair (___) who presents with fall and hypervolemia. # FALL/FOUND DOWN: Patient found down at assisted living home, unwitnessed fall. EKG without new ischemic changes. Etiology likely in mechanical in setting of dementia given no telemetry events and no evidence of ischemia. Patient suffered a L clavicular fracture (described below). Delirogenic meds (alprazolam, codeine) were stopped given concern for worsening confusion. # ACUTE ON CHRONIC DIASTOLIC CHF EXACERBATION (LVEF>55%): On presentation, patient had new oxygen requirement. CXR showed pulmonary edema. Home dose of Lasix was Furosemide 40 mg PO DAILY, recently decreased from 60 mg due to hypotension. Exam notable for bibasilar crackles. TTE here with preserved LVEF and no further evidence of mitral stenosis as was seen on prior TTE at ___ with well seated mitral tricuspid annulplasty ring and normal functioning mitral valve bioprosthesis. She was diuresed with IV Lasix and transitioned back to her home dose of Lasix 40 mg daily and metoprolol tartrate 12.5 mg PO BID. Discharge weight: 53.8 kg. # L DISTAL CLAVICLE FRACTURE: Pt with minimally displaced L distal clavicle fracture. She was seen by orthopedic surgery who suggested wearing a sling for 2 weeks (til ___ with follow-up in their clinic. Pain controlled with acetaminophen. # ATRIAL FIBRILLATION: Patient on home Dabigatran, rate controlled with Metoprolol and Amiodarone. Had been on warfarin (indicated given valvular afib) but had changed to dabigatran due to difficulty managing frequent monitoring of INR with warfarin. After discussion with patient, patient's daughter, and team, weighed the risk/benefits of anticoagulation given her frequent falls and decided to hold off on further anticoagulation and discharged on ASA 81 mg. She will have further discussions with her outpatient cardiologist, Dr. ___ ___ about risks/benefits of anticoagulation. CHRONIC ISSUES: # DEMENTIA: She was continued on her home dose of donepezil and memantine. At night, she did sundown and became agitated for which Seroquel was given instead of her home alprazolam. QTc was 490 and decided to hold further doses of Seroquel. # GERD -Continued home Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q12H -Continued home Sucralfate 1 gm PO QID -Continued home Ranitidine 150 mg PO DAILY # CONSTIPATION -Continued home Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO DAILY # NUTRITION -Continued home Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY -Continued home Vitamin D 1000 UNIT PO DAILY -Continued home Cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg PO DAILY # DEPRESSION: -Continued home Sertraline 100 mg PO QHS Transitional issues: - Patient had been on dabigatran for anticoagulation (had previously been on warfarin but this was discontinued because of difficulty with monitoring). Due to concern for bleeding in the setting of frequent falls, this was held and she was discharged on 81 mg aspirin pending her primary cardiologist's decision RE anticoagulation. - Patient's home alprazolam and acetaminophen with codeine were discontinued due to concerns that they increased her fall risk. She was given Seroquel in the hospital for delirium/agitation with benefit but did have increased QTc to 492 so was not discharged on this medication. This could be considered in the future, but QTc should be followed carefully. - Please follow up on free T4 and consider starting levothyroxine if indicated given elevated TSH. - Discharged on 40 mg PO furosemide - Discharge weight 53.8 kg - CODE: DNR/DNI (confirmed) - CONTACT: ___, daughter, ___ (cell)
summary: You were in the hospital because you fell at home. Due to the fall, you broke your collar bone. Orthopedic surgery saw you and recommended that you wear a sling and follow up with them in clinic in 2 weeks. You should wear the sling at all times until then. You were also found to have a mild build up of fluid due to your heart failure. We gave you some medication through your IV (an IV version of the furosemide that you take at home) to remove this fluid. After leaving the hospital, you will go to a rehabilitation facility to help maintain and build up your strength. After that you may need more help to make sure that you do not continue to fall.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with stage III nonsmall cell lung cancer who presented with chest pain after chemotherapy today, found to have ST elevations with troponin leak to a peak of 0.3. Her chest pain was pleuritic and exacerbated by positional change, most consistent with myopericarditis. ============= ACTIVE ISSUES ============= # MYOPERICARDITIS: Patient developed chest pain after chemotherapy. In ED, cardiology consulted given minimal STE (not meeting STEMI criteria) and troponinemia. Received nitro gtt and heparin gtt overnight, without improvement of symptoms, to minimal improvement of ST elevations on EKG. Echocardiogram on admission without pericardial effusion or wall motion abnormalities. CTA negative for PE. Patient given indomethacin with improvement of symptoms. - Indomethacin 25mg TID x 2 weeks, 25mg BID x 2 weeks, 25mg daily x 2 weeks. - Omeprazole daily during NSAID therapy - Consider 1U PRBC for goal Hgb 8 - F/u with cardiac MRI as outpatient in 2 weeks ===================== CHRONIC/STABLE ISSUES ===================== # DEPRESSION: - Home venlafaxine once daily # COPD: - Albuterol nebs PRN # HYPOTHYROIDISM: - Home levothyroxine # NSCLC, STAGE IIIB: Cisplatin/etoposide regimen as per outpatient heme/onc. Held ___ dose of pegfilgrastim given stable cell counts and possible confounding side effect of chest pain. =================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== # CODE: Full # CONTACT: - Daughter ___, ___ - Husband ___, ___ [ ] MEDICATION CHANGES: - Started indomethacin: 25mg TID x 2 weeks, 25mg BID x 2 weeks, 25mg daily x 2 weeks. - Omeprazole for GI prophylaxis while on prolonged indomethacin taper - Held ___ dose of pegfilgrastim given stable cell counts + possible confounding side effect of CP. Will need a CBC within 1 week (___) to monitor cell counts and dictate redosing as per outpatient hematology/oncology. - Per Pt and daughter conversation, Pt had self-downtitrated her venlafaxine from 75mg BID to once daily. Can discuss with Pt if this is sufficiently helping her depressive Sx. [ ] NEULASTA: - Dose held ___ as above. Will need a CBC within 1 week (___) to monitor cell counts and dictate redosing as per outpatient hematology/oncology. [ ] FOLLOW UP: - Consider 1U PRBC for goal Hgb 8 - F/u with cardiac MRI as outpatient in 2 weeks
summary: You were seen in our hospital because you had chest pain, and a blood test showed that you were having some mild damage to your heart. Based on your history, we thought that your symptoms were due to inflammation of the lining around your heart ("pericarditis"). We treated this inflammation with a strong anti-inflammatory medication ("indomethacin"), which significantly improved your pain. Please continue to take this as prescribed, and take this medication with food to avoid stomach upset. Please schedule a cardiac MRI in 2 weeks to evaluate your heart. Please arrange for one unit of blood to be transfused for your heart.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo F w/ hx of HCV cirrhosis who was on triple therapy with telapavir/ribavrin/interferon, which was complicated by a diffuse drug rash to telapavir, which was stopped ___. Pt then developed a transient MSSA bacteremia, most likely due to scratching some of her lesions. She was started on nafcillin without issue and discharged to rehab. However, two days prior to admission she developed another diffuse rash and was admitted for evaluation. . Skin rash: Likely a delayed rash in the setting of being on nafcillin for MSSA bacteremia. Pt was seen by dermatology who felt although it was both morbiliform on the torso and petichial on the extremities, it is likely due to her nafcillin exposure. This medication was stopped on admission and she was switched to vancomycin, and she showed continued improvement in her rash. She was also treated with triamcinolone ointment and Benadryl and Fexofenadine for the pruritis. . MSSA bacteremia: Pt was seen by infectious disease, who suggested switching the patient from nafcillin to vancomycin. She had a repeat TTE due to a question of a new cardiac murmur, which again did not demonstrate any endocarditis. Infectious disease initially felt that the patient should have a repeat TEE, but cardiology felt that the study was uneccesary given the constant appearance and degree of her mitral regurgitation, her recent TEE demonstrating lack of vegetations, and prior treatment w/ nafcillin. Infectious disease agreed with discharging without TEE. Pt will continue vancomycin 1 gram IV q8h until ___ (for total of 4 weeks after ___ negative blood culture) and has followup in ___ clinic. Pt's repeat blood cultures have remained negative by the time of discharge. . Hepatitis C: Pt had grade 4 cirrhosis by biopsy. Given the extent of her liver disease, it is important to try to maximally preserve her liver function. Viral load <43 on ___. Her last dose of telepavir was on ___. Pt was still on interferon weekly (now at 135mg decreased from 180mcg weekly). Although interferon is very likely contributing to her pancytopenia (see below), we continued interferon 135mcg weekly every ___ since her HCV viral load was not detectable on ___. We also restarted ribavarin at her home dose of 600mg po bid. Pt's dose of ribavirin and interferon as well as her CBC and HCV viral load will be followed by her hepatologist. . Pancytopenia: Pt has chronic pancytopenia attributed to interferon and liver disease. Blood counts were stable. . Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine. . Depression: Continued venlafaxine. Transitional Issues: Pt's CBC will need to be monitored in the setting of restarting her ribavirin. Her dose of ribavirin and interferon may need to be adjusted.
summary: You were admitted with a rash from the antibotic that you were taking (nafcillin). You were seen by our dermatologists, who felt that you had a drug rash. However, given the recent development of melanoma in your sister, they would like to see you in their clinic. We made an appointment for you (see below). You were also seen by our infectious disease specialists, and based on their advice, we started you on a different antibiotic (vancomycin). Your rash is improving. We performed an echocardiogram of your heart, which did not show any infection on your heart valves. You will need to continue vancomycin until ___, and you will need to see Dr. ___ in infectious diseases (see below). Because your hepatitis C viral load was undetectable, we restarted your interferon and your ribavirin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ ___ speaking (poor historian) who p/w nausea and chills and resp distress with infiltrate concern for PNA. . # PNA: on admission had new O2 req of 3L, retrocardiac opacity on CXR, concerning for community acquired PNA. WBC slightly elevated with 90% PMNs. UCx neg. sputum cannot be produced. The pt was treated with ___ renally dosed and tylenol/guaifenesin/tessylon as needed for cough. She was also given nebs prn shortness of breath. Her breathing slowly improved but her lung sounds continued to be rhonchorous. She likely also has some component of upper airway obstruction and perhaps OSA. She was discharged on ___ to be completed for a total of 10 days. Of note, the original CXR for the pt suggested that rpt imaging after resolution might be useful to r/o possible malignancy but this was not seen in rpt CXR. In the past, she has refused w/u of breast mass and other outpt findings. If pt fails to improve in future, rpt imaging or consultation with PCP ___ to talk about rpt imaging could be considered. . # Afib: Per report the pt has a h/o PAfib but on admission she was in NSR on 12.5 bid of metoprolol. During her stay she triggered three times for AF with RVR up to 140s which responded to boluses of IV dilt 10mg. She was also increased to 50 TID metoprolol and spontaneously converted back to sinus with HR in ___. Due to one of her episodes of RVR she went into flash pulmonary edema and required transfer to the ICU for diuresis and potential cardioversion (although pt converted spontaneously to sinus). She was called out of the ICU after one day when she was breathing on 2L. She is discharged to a facility for tele monitoring. She was kept on her home aspirin 325. If the pt has an episode of RVR, per her cardiologist it was recommended to not start amiodarone or dronedarone due to its high risks. She may start PO dilt on top of her metoprolol if needed but it would be most ideal to simply titrate up her metoprolol (at 37.5mg TID on dicharge). . # CAD/CHF: Pt not on lasix at home. EF 40-45. Pt did not have any CP and there was low concern for ACS although new RBBB and TWIs. She ruled out for MI with cardiac enzymes x3. As noted above, if the pt goes into RVR she is prone to flash pulmonary edema given her poor EF at baseline. She responds to IV lasix ___ mg boluses as she is relatively lasix naive. . # Gout: stable without flare this admission. We held her home colchicine 0.6 in acute setting. We treated with tylenol prn. . # CKD: pt is discharged within her baseline of 1.7-2.0 . # Social: Found in feces/urine at home. Concern for elder abuse or poor social situation. Son is in ___ and is HCP. Son is not involved in care as his wife and the pt do not get along. SW visited pt and did not find any concerns for elder abuse at this time. . # Depression: stable. we continued home celexa. . # Contact: son ___ (HCP) ___ #) Code Status: Full (confirmed with pt and son this admission) . ___ on Admission: Metoprolol tartrate 12.5 b.i.d. ECASA 325 Citalopram 20 colchicine 0.6 Voltaren gel p.r.n. fluticasone spray lactulose lidocaine patch SLTNG 0.4 p.r.n. polyethylene glycol acetaminophen vitamin C docusate ___ MVI one daily, senna 8.6 vitamin A and D ointment.
summary: You were admitted due to a pneumonia. You were initially treated with antibiotics. You suffered from some episodes of a fast heart rate called atrial fibrillation which we were able to control with medications. You were in the ICU for one day due to this leading to fluid build up in your lungs which was removed with diuretics. When you returned to the medical floor your breathing was improved and you were working with physical therapy. Your heart rate has been in control as well. Please take levofloxacin as prescribed for your pneumonia. Please take metoprolol 37.5mg three times a day for control of your heart rate. Please take guaifenesin and benzonatate as needed for cough and nebulizers as needed for shortness of breath. There were no other changes to your medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of R infiltrating ductal carcinoma s/p lumpectomy/chemo, past episode of thrombophlebitis while on tamoxifen, who presented now with one day of pleuritic chest pain and found to have bilateral pulmonary emboli. ACTIVE PROBLEMS: 1. BILATERAL PULMONARY EMBOLI: The patient was found to have acute submassive pulmonary emboli with questionable evidence of R heart strain on CTA. Initial exam was notable for tachypnea, borderline tachycardia but no edema, elevated JVP, or other signs of RV failure. She was started on a heparin drip in the ED and transitioned to subcutaneous lovenox and warfarin. A TTE showed moderate pulmonary hypertension and evidence of R ventricular dilation with likely preserved systolic function. Although the patient has had prior episode of superficial thrombophlebitis while on tamoxifen therapy, this episode appears unprovoked. She has had no prior DVT/PE and lacked risk factors for coagulability, including recent immobility, surgery, trauma. There was low clinical suspicion for hypercoagulability secondary to malignancy, particularly in the context of up-to-date mammogram and colonoscopy. Her symptoms of pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea slowly improved, and she began to saturate normally on room air. Due to a likely inability to self-administer lovenox at home and her poor mobility, she was discharged rehab to complete her lovenox bridge to heparin and continue ___. 2. ANXIETY - The patient has OCD with aspects of anxiety/depression, on lorazepam and sertraline. She is closely followed by Dr. ___, and a Clinical Social Worker, ___ ___, through ___. She feels unable to tend to her house and wishes to sell it in favor of moving into an assisted living community. Our social work team tried to direct her to the appropriate resources. She appears to have few social supports. She was continued on home benzo, antidepressant. 3. ?AORTIC STENOSIS - TTE also revealed moderate to severe aortic stenosis, though a significant pre-valvular outflow tract obstruction likely coming from a flailing mitral leaflet precluded an adequate assessment of the aortic valve. On exam, she has a III/VI SEM at the RUSB/LUSB without radiation. A repeat echo should be obtained when the acute PE has resolved and her tachycardia dissipates. She may benefit from cardiology followup. Her PCP was notified of this recommendation at time of discharge. INACTIVE PROBLEMS: 4. BREAST CANCER: She is no longer receiving treatment. Her most recent mammogram in ___ was negative for malignancy. 5. HYPERLIPIDEMIA - Continued on statin Labs/Studies Pending at Discharge: None Transitional Care Issues: - Patient will complete Lovenox bridge with Q12 subcutaneous administration until INR therapeutic - Long-term anticoagulation support. - Given echo findings suggestive of aortic stenosis and outflow obstruction, cardiology recommends repeat Echo when PE resolves.
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath and chest pain with inspiration. You were found to have clots in your lungs. Initially you received a medication through your veins (heparin), which prevents the clots from worsening. You were then transitioned to a similar medication by injection into your belly (lovenox), as well as an oral medication (warfarin). On these medications your shortness of breath and chest pain with breathing improved. You no longer required supplemental oxygen. You also had an ultrasound of the heart (Echocardiogram) which showed increased pressure in the vessels of your lungs. This increased pressure is due to the clots and should improve as you continue treatment. You will be taking the lovenox injections twice a day until your labs indicate that the medication is working optimally.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo man h/o HTN, diet-controlled DM2, ___ Tooth disease with Charcot feet (although genetic test ___ neg for dominant gene), ankle pain s/p L ankle fusion, anxiety, BPAD admitted with R thigh pain and chills c/w cellulitis, abscess. # R thigh abscess/cellulitis: Mr. ___ had developed R thigh abscess/cellulitis likely ___ infected hair follicle. He was admitted with elevated WBC 17.6, increased erythema despite several doses of abx. This abscess was I&D'd twice in the emergency room (prior to admit) and he was given IV vancomycin. On this regimen he did well with reduced pain and redness. Unfortunately, there was no abscess culture available on OMR and thus this was an empiric treatment. Soft tissue U/S here did not show any residual abscess or fluid collection. He was very slow to improve, and on ___ ceftriaxone was added for better MSSA and strep coverage. He was switched to bactrim and keflex on the day of discharge. Prior failure was attributed to retained abscess. The packing and dressing changes instructions were provided to the wife since the infection location was tough for Mr. ___ to reach. He was treated with oxycodone PRN for pain and was discharged on the same dose he was taking at home. # HTN: cont ACEI # ___, Charcot Feet: no new issues - on oxycodone PRN. Clotrimazole for tinea # Psych: anxiety, depression, BPAD, schizophrenia - on lamictal, mirtazapine, risperidone # Headache: fioricets PRN # Ashtma: albuterol PRN #DVT PROPHYLAXIS: heparin sc #CODE STATUS: full code .
summary: As you know, you were admitted with redness, pain, and warmth on your right leg - consistent with a skin infection called cellulitis. There was evidence of an abscess (collection of infected fluid) as well, which was drained twice in the emergency department. You were treated with intravenous antibiotics and demonstrated good response after several days. To ensure that there was no residual abscess, an ultrasound was performed and it showed no abscess. You were transitioned to an oral antibiotic which you should continue for an additional 7 days. Please continue with packing - as taught to your wife - on a daily basis.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with history of EtOH use disorder, previously in remission, who presented with jaundice, currently be treated for alcoholic hepatitis. ACUTE ISSUES # ACUTE LIVER INJURY # PRESUMED ALCOHOLIC HEPATITIS On ___, Mr. ___ presented to ___ with jaundice and elevated liver enzymes. At ___, he had a RUQ-U/S showing hepatomegaly, and splenomegaly. He was subsequently transferred to ___ ___ for subspecialty care. Upon arrival to the ___ ___, he had no signs of hepatic encephalopathy and had labs showing tBili 22, AST and ALT elevation (101/49), INR 1.8 in the setting of recent EtOH use, concerning for alcoholic hepatitis. On ___, he was admitted, and continued on his 6-week steroid course. Per patient, he started steroids on ___. During his admission, Mr. ___ maintained elevated ALT/AST values, however his total bilirubin downtrended, and he showed signs of response to steroid treatment (Lille<0.45). He will require daily steroid medication until ___, then subsequent taper. Per patient, Mr. ___ has been previously counseled about his alcohol use by previous providers. He was counseled by medical team about the importance of abstaining from further alcohol use. He was evaluated by the ___ Liver Service, and is ineligible for a liver transplant a this time. HepC viral load, and HepB and autoantibody serologies were sent, and all returned negative. # COAGULOPATHY: Pt presented with INR 1.8, which was likely elevated in the setting of acute liver injury (as above). He was given two injections of vitamin K, and his INR improved to 1.6 on the day of discharge. #C - Chronic issues pertinent to admission (ex. HTN, held Lisinopril for ___ # EtOH USE DISORDER Per patient, last drink was on ___, prior to admission. Pt reports history of alcohol withdrawal in the past with tremors, palpitations, diaphoresis, but denies seizures. During his hospital admission, Mr. ___ denied withdrawal symptoms and had no signs of withdrawal on exam. He was monitored and treated per ___ protocol, and given thiamine and MV daily. He also met with our Social Work team on ___, and they created a plan to seek out IOP at ___ or ___. Mr. ___ will make contact with IOPs and SW will return to provide assistance with referrals. # HEMACHROMATOSIS Pt with reported history of sub-clinical hemochromatosis, which was discovered when he allowed blood test for research study at ___ (___). Records from partners were limited and did not confirm subclinical significance of lab findings. # DEPRESSION # INSOMNIA Continued home topiramate and mirtazapine which patient takes for sleep. Patient had no issues with sleep during admission. #T - Transitional Issues [] Presented with elevated liver enzymes and jaundice consistent with alcoholic hepatitis, treated with prednisone. [] Prednisone 40mg daily for 6 weeks then taper (___) [] On ___: Primary Care Visit with Lab Work (CBC, LFTs, Chem-7, Coagulation, Serum Tox Screen) [] On ___: Follow-up visit with Hepatologist Dr. ___ [] Ensure adequate daily nutrition with Ensure 4x daily until Dr. ___ [] Follow-up with Partners records to confirm sub-clinical significance of hemachromatosis. [] Follow-up with ___ Social Work for referral aid in connecting to IOP at ___ or ___. - New Meds: None - Stopped/Held Meds: None - Changed Meds: Prednisolone changed to Prednisone 40mg Daily for 6 weeks (started ___ end ___ - Follow-up appointments: Primary Care and Hepatology appointments. - Post-Discharge Follow-up Labs Needed: CBC, Chem-7, Coags, Tox Screen on ___. - Incidental Findings: None - Discharge weight: 237.1 lbs - Discharge creatinine: 0.7 - Discharge hgb: 12.4 # CODE: FULL Code # CONTACT: Health care proxy chosen: Yes Name of health care proxy: ___ Phone number: ___
summary: You were admitted for jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes) and elevated liver enzymes concerning for alcoholic hepatitis. Once admitted to the hospital, you were continued on steroids to suppress the immune reaction in your liver. You were given medication and vitamins to reduce the symptoms of possible alcohol withdrawal. Your blood electrolytes were restored, and we gave you medication to help your blood clot. We performed blood tests to ensure that the steroid treatment was working. We performed blood tests to rule out autoimmune or infection causes of liver injury, and those returned negative. The most important thing you can do once leaving the hospital is to not drink alcohol.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx of bulimia and EtOH abuse (2 large bottles of wine/day, last drink ___, hx c/b withdrawal seizures and DTs), who was found to have pneumomediastinum after severe vomiting on CT at ___, transferred to ___ where barium study revealed no esophageal perforation (pneumomediastinum thought to be due to self-resolving esophageal microperforation), and later found to be in EtOH withdrawal. Withdrawal resolved and no valium was given (per CIWA) since ___ AM. # Delirium tremens: exhibited by patient's tremors, agitation, anxiety, hallucinations, and mild tachycardia / HTN about 5 days after stopping heavy EtOH consumption. She responded appropriately to initial bolus of IV lorazepam, and was quickly transferred to PO valium, before all BZDs were discontinued on ___. Patient also given thiamine, folate, and MVI. SW consulted - patient made plans to enter ___ rehab facility, ___, in ___ after discharge; discharged home on ___ with plan to go to rehab on ___. # Pneumomediastinum: as seen on CT scan from ___. Likely ___ self-resolving esophageal microperforations from vomiting/wretching. As per CT Chest (___), pneumomediastinum with foci of air were identified posterior to the sternum and superior to the left brachiocephalic vein as well as just superior to the left main stem bronchus, improved overall from prior CT. This likely explains the burning chest pain she continues to have. Thoracic surgery recommended conservative management. On discharge, patient was tolerating liquids and a soft diet. She was having difficulty swallowing tough food and hot/acidic food. She will need flexibility in her diet with a liquid (ie ensure, etc) and soft diet until able to tolerate solids at rehab. # Chest pain: possibly from pneumomediastinum as above. Pericarditis also considered given diffuse STE (presumed to be J-point), constant chest pain that improves with leaning forward, and plausible mechanism of injury. However, no pericardial effusion seen on CT Chest, and ECHO was normal. # Eating disorder: Appears to be under reasonable control at this time. Due to recent esophageal injury, patient unable to tolerate many solid foods, and hot/acidic beverages. She did drink ensure while inpatient. Will need flexibility in meal selection at rehab for the next ___ weeks while her esophagus recovers. # Depression: home sertraline was continued. # Seizure disorder: No seizures while in hospital. Home keppra was continued. # Thrombocytopenia: Mild (PLT in ___. Baseline unknown. No evidence of bleeding. LFTs/coags unremarkable. Possibly ___ heavy EtOH consumption. Normalized to 173 as of ___. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: =============== - Patient was discharged and will be attending rehabilitation at ___ in ___. She will be tired for several days while she recovers from her hospital stay. - Due to recent esophageal injury, patient is unable to tolerate tough, hot, or acidic food. She should be allowed to eat a soft diet for the next 2 weeks until her esophagus heals. - Discharged with lidocaine and sulcralfate as needed prior to eating - Patient had mildly elevated phosphate level after receiving PO phosphate repletion, please ensure level has returned to normal as outpatient - If patient continues to drink alcohol, would recommended supplementation with a multivitamin that contains a minimum of thiamine 100 mg daily, vitamin B6 2 mg daily, and folic acid ___ mcg to 1 mg daily #Code: full (confirmed) #Communication: ___ (Father) home: ___, cell: ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after experiencing chest pain, nausea, vomiting, and undergoing a CT scan that showed there was some air in your mediastinum. This was concerning for an esophageal perforation and so you underwent a barium swallow which demonstrated that you had no ongoing perforation. You were observed and were stable. You had lab work that did not demonstrate any signs of infection or distress. You did have some electrolyte abnormalities which are consistent with vomiting and these were repleted and normal prior to your discharge. Please allow us to give you some instructions regarding your discharge: 1) Please advance your diet to normal as tolerated. You may find soft foods are easier to eat while your esophagus is healing. Please stay well hydrated with at least eight 8oz glasses of noncaffeinated fluids daily. Please refrain from drinking alcohol.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ y/o M w/ bladder Amyloidosis, BPH, Afib (theureputic on Coumadin) and diastolic CHF who presents for hematuria x 2 days. ACTIVE ISSUES # Hematuria: Chonic issue due to bladder amyloidosis with temporizing through bladder fulgaration by Dr. ___ in ___. Current bleeding likely due to reaccumulation of amyloidosis in the bladder causing irritation. A UA showed RBCs and a bladder scan showed PVR. A ___ ___ foley catheter was placed in the ED, CBI was initiated, and urology was consulted. His urine failed to clear with CBI, and thus he was admitted to medicine for observation and CBI. Urology irrigated and removed a few clots until urine cleared. CBI was continued. Urine clear O/N with low flow CBI. CBI off in AM, urine remained clear. Foley was d/c'd and the patient was able to void. PVR was checked. CHRONIC ISSUES # Diastolic CHF: Stable, no edema or SOB. Followed closely by his cardiologist. Continued Metolazone, Toresemide, Metoprolol and Spironolactone. # Atrial fibrillation: On warfarin for thromboembolic prophylaxis. Prior bleed earlier this year with epistaxis when INR was theraputic. Held warfarin, continued metoprolol for rate control. Resume warfarin with INR checks as outpatient, follow up with cardiologist. # Sick sinus syndrome: Status post pacemaker implantation 3mos ago, last interrogated on ___. # Hypertension: The patient's blood pressure is currently well controlled. # CKD: Stage III, stable Cr 2.3. # Back pain: Since surgery. Continued percocet for pain and colace for constipation ppx. # Depression: Continued citalopram 15 mg daily. # Gout: Continued Uloric 80 mg daily. CHRONIC ISSUES # CODE STATUS: Full # PENDING STUDIES: None # MEDICATION CHANGES: None # FOLLOWUP PLAN: - Followup with PCP - ___ as previously arranged - Will resume warfarin tomorrow
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had blood in your urine which formed clots which then obstructed your flow and made it difficult to empty your bladder. In the Emergency Department, a foley catheter was inserted to allow for the bladder to be irrigated, we were able to remove many of the blood clots that had formed in your bladder. You were kept on constant irrigation overnight and your urine became clear because you were no longer bleeding. In the morning, the urine was still clear and we removed the foley catheter. You were able to urinate without any problems and had no more bleeding so we discharged you from the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ FTM transgender (on testosterone) w/ PMHx depression, PTSD, question of fibromyalgia, reportedly Ehlers ___, and migraines, admitted with persistent headache, likely secondary to medication overuse. ACTIVE ISSUES ============= # Medication overuse headache: Pt presented with 3-week h/o of persistent headache. Neurologic examination was significant only for bilateral V1 distribution numbness. Pt was seen by neurology, who felt the headache was likely multifactorial secondary to medication overuse headache (increased Fiorcet and Naproxen use), with low suspicion for low pressure headache (possibly ___ CSF leak post LP). Per neurology, Fioricet and Naproxen were discontinued, Topamax was increased to 50mg BID. Pt also treated with Medrol dose pak. Pt discharged with close follow up scheduled with neurology and PCP and short course of tramadol for breakthrough pain. MRI head/spine were performed to r/o low pressure headache and was unremarkable except for incidental findings listed below. # Malnutrition Pt is underweight and severely malnourished in setting of anorexia and reported gastroparesis as evidenced by loss of 26% body weight in ___ year. Pt was seen by nutrition. Pt recommended to have supplements, and start multivitamin with minerals daily. Pt also received 100mg thiamine for three days for refeeding risk. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== # Fibromyalgia: continued home lyrica 125 mg TID # Asthma: continued home Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID # Depression/PTSD: continued home fluoxetine # GERD: Continue home ranitidine. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ============================= 1. Pt needs to complete Medrol taper. Pt to take Medrol 4mg on ___. 2. Pt may benefit from starting an albuterol inhaler in the future for treatment of exercise-induced asthma 3. Area of low signal is identified in the expected location of the gallbladder, suggestive of gallstone, correlation with abdominal ultrasound is recommended for further characterization. 4. Punctate area of enhancement identified in the left temporal lobe, with no evidence of mass effect or edema, follow-up MRI with and without contrast in two weeks, or as clinically warranted is recommended to demonstrate stability or any further change. # CODE: Full # CONTACT: ___ ("like a step mom to me") ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Docusate Sodium 200 mg PO BID 2. Fluoxetine 40 mg PO DAILY 3. Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID 4. Metoclopramide 10 mg PO TID 5. Omeprazole 40 mg PO BID 6. Pregabalin 125 mg PO TID 7. Ranitidine 150 mg PO BID 8. Tizanidine ___ mg PO BID:PRN muscle spasms 9. Topiramate (Topamax) 25 mg PO QHS
summary: You were admitted because of your worsening headaches. Your headaches are likely from medication overuse from too much Fioricet and Naproxen. Overuse of analgesic medications (like Fioricet and Naproxen) can cause medication overuse headaches. It is important to continue to hold these medications to prevent future medication overuse headaches. You were treated with steroids (Medrol dose pak), which you should continue. You will take Medrol 4mg on ___. You underwent an MRI of the head and MRI spine which ruled out a low pressure headache. There was an incidental finding of punctate lesion in the temporal lobe of the brain which is likely unrelated to your headaches.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ obese F with dCHF, asthma, HTN and atrial fibrillation (on coumadin and verapamil) who presents with worsening SOB and fluid retention. . #. SOB/Edema: Patient presented with symptoms consistent with CHF exacerbation. She was treated with multiple prn doses of 40mg IV furosemide and had excellent response with fluid output. Her lower extremity edema improved. We continued to check her electrolyte status during diuresis. Her creatinine levels remained within normal ranges during diuresis. On admission she was very SOB and required 4L of 02 satting at 95%. She was maintained on oxygen for several days. She was also treated with ipratroprium nebulizers PRN for SOB. By discharge, she was satting 93% on 1 L and 86% on RA, so was d/c on ___ L home O2. The patient also had an Echocardiogram performed to evaluate her heart function and establish a possible cause of this acute exacerbation of CHF. Patient had a previous echo but results were limited due to body habitus. New echo showed global hypokinesis and an ejection fraction of ___. Due to her CHF, patient was started on lisinopril 5mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, increased to metoprolol succinate 150mg daily, and started on torsemide 40mg daily. She should continue with these medications as an outpatient. Ms. ___ has an appointment to follow up with her PCP, ___, on ___ ___ at 8:30am. Dr. ___ was informed of all of the changes made to Ms. ___ regimen during her stay. Patient should also follow up with her cardiologist, Dr. ___, on ___ at 2:40. Dr. ___ has also been notified about her hospital course. She was discharged on ___ on home O2, with stable VS. Metoprolol 75mg TID was changed to Toprol XL 200mg daily for ease of dosing and she was also d/c on torsemide 20mg daily. . #. Asthma: Patient had minimal wheezing throughout her stay but was maintained on prn Albuterol nebs for intermittent SOB. Patient should continue on home albuterol inhaler after dishcarge. . #. Atrial fibrillation: Patient was found to be in afib per EKG. Not symptomatic. Patient was maintained on coumadin with therepeutic INR. Home dose of coumadin was changed from 5mg 5x/week and 2.5mg 2x/week to 5mg daily. Her home verapimil was DCed and metoprolol was substituted due to better protection from CHF. Patient should continue these medications at home. . #. EtOH: Patient drinks ___ drinks per night. CAGE (-). No signs of withdrawal during her stay. CIWA scale was calculated to be less than 10. No ativan was given. She was counseled on the contraindications of drinking to her extent, especially related to her heart health. Patient was informed that alcohol use might be contributing to her worsening heart failure, and should discontinue alcohol in the future. She should follow up with her PCP to discuss this in more detail. . #. Anxiety/depression: Patient was maintained on home dose of sertraline and had no symptoms. Patient should continue on sertraline at home.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for increasing shortness of breath and fluid retention that was not being alleviated with no resolution at home with PO lasix. You were treated in the hospital with multiple doses of IV furosemide. You had excellent output of urine in response to the IV furosemide. Your leg edema was much improved. You also received an Echocardiogram of your heart to try to interpret why you had this acute exacerbation of water retention. It looks like you have worsening heart failure since your last echo was performed in ___. We added some new medications which will help to protect your heart in the setting of congestive heart failure. You should also stop your intake of alcohol to help improve your heart failure, as this is likely contributing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient admitted for expedited workup of recent seizures. On review of previous neurology consult notes, there is persistently elicited a history of gabapentin withdrawal and therefore a gapabentin level was drawn on admission. He underwent routine EEG which did not show any abnormal epileptiform discharge. MRI was performed and showed nonspecific subcortical white matter disease without evidence of structural lesions or acute infarct. We reviewed his PMP and also found a history of anxiety and depression that was intermittently evaluated by a provider associated with ___ in urgent clinic. On communication with her, she reported giving gabapentin prescription for anxiety and recent fluoxetine prescription as well. She also clarified that he does not follow regularly with her. There is also suspicion detailed in prior notes that he abuses gabapentin and buys this on the street. Given the unclear history and lack of clear evidence for an underlying primary epileptogenic condition, as well as his poor record of compliance, it was felt that the potential benefit of starting an AED which requires strict compliance and follow up did not outweigh the potential risks in terms of altering his liver metabolism (in setting of HCV) and/or exacerbating his psychiatric conditions. For follow up, he will be scheduled to see his outpatient neurologist. He has follow up scheduled in liver clinic to address his HCV. He was given instructions to continue following with his outpatient Psychiatry provider. Transitional issues: - Please follow up gabapentin level. - Encourage follow up with psychiatry provider. - Encourage follow up with liver clinic provider. - Encourage sobriety.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for a seizure. You have had multiple seizures in the past several months. The majority of these were in the setting of stopping your gabapentin suddenly. However, to exclude a condition that predisposes you to seizures, we admitted you for workup. We performed an EEG, which is a study of the electric activity within your brain. This was normal. We also performed an MRI, which was also normal. We believe that the etiology of your seizures is most likely from abrupt cessation of medication such as gabapentin and/or other substances. Since your anxiety and depression are not well controlled, we restarted you on fluoxetine (or Prozac), which is what your Psychiatry prescriber had recommended you to start.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PRINCIPLE REASON FOR ADMISSION ___ yo male w/ history of ETOH abuse, presented with prolonged ETOH consumption and suicide attempt with ibuprofen ingestion. # ETOH abuse w/ history of withdrawals. Patient was admitted to the MICU initially, and was placed on q2h CIWA of 10mg for score >10. He was also started on clonidine 0.1mg BID. Per psychiatry recommendations, on ___ he was started on 12mg PO q4h standing and then started on a taper: day 2: 10mg PO q4, day 3: 8mg PO q4, day 4: 6mg PO q4, day 5: 4mg PO q4. He was then transferred to the floor where standing valium taper was continued with prn valium 10mg q2-4hours for CIWA>10. He continued score heavily on CIWA for anxiety, but by time of discharge was not scoring for >24 hours. He was also started on thiamine, folate, and MVI and social work met with the patient. # Suicidal ideation: Prior to admission, patient intentionally overingested ibuprofen in a presumed suicide attempt. On admission, he was denying and HI and SI. UTox and Serum tox were remarkable for ETOH only. No other coingestion. He was monitored with a 1:1 sitter. Hydroxyzine was started for persistent anxiety. He will be transferred to inpatient psych. # Abdominal pain: Patient complained of mild RUQ pain on admission and LFTs were mildly elevated. Appeared consistent with alcoholic hepatitis. His MDF was 1 and patient did not require steroids. His LFT's and pain improved. However, he did develop new lower quadrant abdominal pain in the setting of constipation for several days. Aggressive bowel regimen was added and patient received soap suds enema with resultant BM and improvement in his abdominal pain. Would recommend continued aggressive bowel reg, rechecking LFT's in one week to ensure stability, and avoiding acetaminophen. # Thombocytopenia: likely from alcohol induced bone marrow suppression. # Transaminitis: Initially elevated in setting of acute alcohol ingestion. Initially downtrended. Did have additional bump during stay after administration of tylenol. Tylenol was DC'd and LFT's were downtrending on discharge. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Inpatient psychiatric care for depression and substance abuse - Recheck LFT's in one week to ensure stability
summary: You were treated at the ___ for alcohol intoxication and a suicide attempt. You were kept in the intensive care service to monitor you for withdrawal seizures, which you have experienced in the past. You were were given a substantial amount of valium. On the medical floor we kept you on a CIWA scale with valium and got you through detox safely. You were noted to have some abdominal pain, likely related to alcohol use and constipation. Your blood counts are alos a litte low, indicating chronic toxicity from alcohol, but a RUQ US and hepatitis serologies were normal. Now that you have finished detox, you are safe to be discharged to the psychiatry floor for treatment of your depression.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt is a 22 pregnant G1PO female, 11 weeks pregnant, with history of IVDU who is transferred from ___ given new onset fever and concern for endocarditis. . # Fever: Unclear etiology. Recent viral infection which may have contributed to low grade fever. Certainly transient bacteremia in the setting of IV drug could cause an isolated fever. Blood cultures x 5 negative at 48 hours at the time of discharge. Negative urinalysis and chest radiograph. No new murmur or peripheral sequele of endocarditis. . # IVDU: Pt continues to use IV heroin, despite also using Subutex. Parents are very concerned for continued use, but unaware of IV heroin use. Subutex 8mg Daily continued with Clonidine 0.1mg twice daily PRN. Symptoms of withdrawal continued at a mild level and included intermittent abdominal cramping, sweats. . # Abdominal pain: Likely ___ withdrawal. Reassuring that she has had no bleeding or discharge. Exam without concerning features. Improved during hospitalization. . # Depression: No suicidal ideation or symptoms of mania. Continued sertraline 50mg daily. Social work as consulted. . # Pregnancy: Currently 11 weeks pregnant. OB/GYN evaluated and noted no current OB/GYN issues. . # Normocytic anemia: Hct 34.3 on admission and remained stable. . TRANSITIONAL CARE: 1. CODE: FULL 2. CONTACT: PARENTS 3. FOLLOW-UP: - PCP, ___ Rehab 4. MEDICAL MANAGEMENT:Continue Subutex for withdrawal. 5. RISKS TO REHOSPITALIZATION: - IVDU, fair insight into medical problems
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after developing a fever. Given your history of IV drug use we were concerned that you could have developed an infection of your blood stream. After careful evaluation of your blood cultures it appears that you do not have an infection of your blood. Further, you had no other fevers during the time you were hospitalized. You were evaluated by the OB/GYN doctors who ___ that there was no active issues regarding your pregnancy and recommended you follow up with your OB/GYN after discharge from the hospital. You also were evaluated by social work and they gave you phone numbers for multiple rehab facilities in the area. You should contact these facilities to arrange further evaluation and treatment of your addiction.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ year old male h/o CAD s/p CABG (6 vsl in ___, MI s/p DES to ___, HTN, GERD, Anxiety, Depression issues presenting with chest pain and shortness of breath. . # Chest Pain: CP is consistant with stable angina likely complicated by anxiety/depression from his psychosocial stressors (see below). He reports baseline ___ chest pain at home, which had increased leading up to admission. Troponins were negative, and cath was clean for new coronary disease, although he was noted to have SVG to OM stenosis with 9 mm aneurysm (see further details in pertinent results). Pt continued to have ___ pain in-house after cath, so we increased his home imdur from 30mg to 60mg daily, and put him on standing tylenol. Pain was back down to baseline ___ the following morning after cath. He was continued on home plavix 75mg daily, asa 325, rosouvastatin 40mg daily, lisinopril 20mg daily. We changed his home metoprol tartrate to toprol-xl 100mg po daily. His home imdur was increased to 60mg daily as above. . # Depression/anxiety: Pt is known to suffer from anxiety and depressive disorders, which have increased recently with the death of his wife last year and subsequent death of his son 2 weeks ago. He reports depression/anxiety attacks and difficulty coping with current emotional situation which likely have exacerabated his chest pain. Pt insisted that he did not have feelings of self harm or harm toward others. Psychiatry was consulted who recommended increasing his home citalopram from 20mg to 30mg daily. He was also sent home with a small prescription for ativan 1mg q8h prn for anxiety. He was encouraged to follow up with his outpatient therapist at ___ who he will set up an appointment with. It may also be reasonable to set up him with outpatient psychiatric follow up for further medication management. From a psychiatric standpoint, we felt he was safe for dishcarge home. . # HTN : We continued home meds of lisinopril, and changed his home metoprolol tartrate to toprol-XL 100mg daily. Pressures were controlled over night after his cath. . Inactive Issues: . # HL : continued Rosuvastatin 40mg daily . # Gout : continued Allopurinol ___ mg daily and Colchicine 0.6 mg daily. . # GERD : Continued ranitidine 300 mg BID . Follow up issues: -Depression/anxiety should be readdressed with his PCP follow up and consider referral to psychiatrist. Pt should also follow up with his outpatient therapist.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain. You had a cardiac catheterization which did not reveal any significant new coronary disease. We have increased one of your medications (imdur) to help with your pain. We also recommend you take tylenol every 6 hours for the next few days to help with the pain. You should not take more than 3 grams of this daily, however. We also stopped your metoprolol tartrate (lopressor) and started you on Toprol-XL (long acting metoprolol) at 100mg by mouth daily. You also saw one of our psychiatrists who recommended increasing your citalopram to 30mg daily. We strongly recommend seeing your therapist at ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ male with a past medical history of CAD status post CABG with multiple stents, chronic systolic and diastolic heart failure, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, V. tach s/p ICD who presents with dyspnea and fatigue found to be in Afib with RVR. # Afib with RVR: etiology thought to be due to previous ischemic heart disease. Started on a heparin drip. Unable to control tachycardia with oral and IV metoprolol, and had intermittent episodes of hypotension with SBP 70-80s. Went for TEE and cardioverison. Converted into sinus rhythm, HR ___, SBP 100-120s. Heparin stopped and started on rivaroxaban 20mg with dinner. Amiodarone loaded for concurrent rate and rhythm control. 400mg twice a day for 1 week (start ___, 200mg twice a day for 1 week (start ___, 200mg once a day indefinitely (start ___. Stopped sotalol and plavix. Changed aspirin to 81mg daily. # CAD s/p CABG and stenting: Pt has chronic chest pain. Troponin elevated to 0.09 after cardioversion. EKG changes with rapid ventricular rate, thought to be demand ischemia. Plavix stopped since anticoagulation initiated. Switched to aspirin 81mg. Continued on rosuvastatin 40mg daily. Chest pain free. # Chronic systolic and diastolic heart failure (EF ___: JVD elevated but otherwise appears euvolemic on exam without crackles ___ edema. Does not appear to be volume overloaded. Held home antihypertensives while hypotensive in setting of Afib w/RVR. Hemodynamically stable post cardioversion. Restarted on spironolactone and torsemide. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Hypertension: home meds held when hypotensive. Restarted post cardioversion. Home medications: isosorbide mononitrate, losartan, spiriolactone, torsemide. # OSA: Uses CPAP at home. Pt unsure of settings. CPAP ordered with respiratory consult. # HLD: continue Ezetimibe 10 mg PO DAILY and rosuvastatin 40mg qhs # GERD: continue Ranitidine 300 mg PO QHS # Depression: continue Fluoxetine 40 mg PO DAILY ======================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ======================== - NEW MEDICATION: amiodarone 400mg twice a day (start ___, then 200mg twice a day for one week (start ___, and change to 200mg once a day on ___. - NEW MEDICATION: rivaroxaban 20mg daily, take with dinner - CHANGE: Aspirin 81mg daily (changed from 325mg daily) - STOP: Sotalol and plavix - Pt instructred that if SBP <90, he can hold his losartan - All CHF medications remained the same - Will have follow up appointment with Dr. ___ 2 weeks after discharge. Will follow up with Dr. ___ ___ weeks after discharge. - Pending labs: TSH, Free T4 - FULL CODE
summary: ___ came to the hospital because ___ were short of breathYou were found to have a fast and irregular heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation. Oral medication was unable to control your heart rate, so ___ underwent a cardioversion. The cardioversion was a success and your heart rate returned to normal. You were started on a new medication called amiodarone. ___ will take 400mg twice a day for one week (through ___, start taking 200mg twice a day on ___ for one week, and start taking 200mg once a day on ___. ___ will take 200mg once a day until ___ are told by your cardiolgoistYou were also started on rivaroxaban, which is a blood thinner. ___ should take this every day with dinner. ___ should stop taking your sotalol and plavix. Take your blood pressure every day. If the top number of your blood pressure is less than 90, do not take your losartan.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female initially presented to OSH with sepsis and E.Coli bacteremia source thought initially urinary, found to have cholestasis, MRCP with dilated intrahepatic ducts treated for cholangitis and s/p ERCP at OSH with failed cannulation and transferred here for repeat ERCP. ERCP here failed cannulation as well. MRCP showed large mass likely cholangiocarcinoma at liver hilum completely obstructing the biliary ducts, also with an intrahepatic bilioma. PICC was placed for 2 weeks of IV CTX for E.Coli bacteremia resistant to PO meds. PTBD was placed ___ successfully. # Cholangitis: Related to underlying cholangiocarcinoma most likely. E.Coli bacteremia, sepsis and negative UCx at OSH in the setting of dilated biliary ducts all consistent with cholangitis, s/p 2 ERCPs with failed cannulation. Treated with IV Ceftriaxone given sensitivies at OSH with ongoing clinical improvement. MRCP performed due to failed ERCP cannulation x2 which revealed a large mass at the hilum of the liver concerning for cholangiocarcinoma, also with evidence of a bilioma. PTBD was performed on ___ successfully. CT Torso performed for staging without evidence of metastases. PICC placed for 2 weeks of Ceftriaxone to complete ___. Pathology ultimately negative for cancer from tissue and brushings. Interventional Radiology reported that the mass is most likely inflammatory and related to a prior surgical clip in situ at that location; Pt. seen by Dr. ___ surgery and declined any surgical intervention while discussing the possibility of cancer. Given negative pathology, pts advanced age and wishes for no surgery, successful biliary decompression, this 'mass' may not require specific follow up unless new and syptomatic, attributable conditions again arise. She did develop local cellulitis at drain site, being treated with oral bactrim to cover in addition to existing ceftriaxone. # Hypoxemia: Found to be hypoxemic following ERCP on admission. Most likely aspiration pneumonitits given acuity of onset and recent two recent ERCPs increasing risk for risk and rapid resolution in symptoms without medical intervention also consistent. Treated with Albuterol nebs and IS to bedside, CXR without concerning pathology ___: There are persistent low lung volumes and elevation of the left hemidiaphragm. Small left effusion has decreased. Left lower lobe atelectasis is grossly unchanged. There is no pneumothorax. Cardiomediastinal contours are unchanged with widened mediastinum and tortuous aorta. Multiple surgical clips and a catheter projects in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen. Multiple surgical clips project in the left axilla. # HTN: Chronic, poorly controlled during admission though (150-160 systolic). Started on ___ ___ with improvement in BP control. Continued home Valsartan 160 mg PO DAILY. # Depression: Chronic, stable. Continued Venlafaxine XR 75 mg PO DAILY # Hypothyroidism: Chronic, stable, continued Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg PO/NG DAILY Transitional Issues: - Left adrenal adenoma and thyroid nodule found on imaging, letter sent to patient and PCP for surveillance as appropriate - CTX IV and PO Bactrim until ___ - Started HCTZ for hypertension continued during this admission
summary: You were hospitalized at ___ for an infection in your bile ducts called cholangitis. You had a procedure called an ERCP which was unable to cannulate into your bile duct. An MRI of your liver revealed a large mass compressing the bilie ducts which was worrisome for a cancer called cholangiocarcinoma. You had a procedure called a PTBD to drain the dilated bile ducts and biopsy the mass for definitive diagnosis - the biopsies revealed no evidence of cancer. The radiologists here are of the opinion that this mass is more likely inflammation and scar relating to a prior surgical clip, and not a cancer. You were evaluated by Dr. ___ surgery given the concern for cancer, and you declined surgery given your advanced age. The plan was next to open the blockage of the bile duct with a balloon ductoplasty - this was done and an external drain was placed. You developed some cellulitis (skin infection) at the drain site. You also had a PICC IV line placed and treated with antibiotics which will continue until ___, for the blood infection (Rx: Ceftriaxone IV) as well as cellulitis (Rx: Bactrim by mouth, which will work in conjunction with the Ceftriaxone to treat the cellulitis). PTBD management: Interventional radiology Phone: Change dressing daily. Gently cleanse around the skin entry site of the catheter with dilute hydrogen peroxide. Dry and apply sterile gauze dressing. If the catheter appears to be pulling out or pulls out, please notify Interventional Radiology immediately. SAVE THE CATHETER for us to inspect.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with history of HTN, HLD, PMR/GCA on chronic steroids, osteoarthritis, scoliosis, gout who presented with back pain. ACUTE/ACTIVE PROBLEMS: # Acute on chronic low back pain # History of GCA/PMR on chronic prednisone # Osteoarthritis # Possible history of inflammatory arthritis # Lumbar spondylosis and spinal stenosis: Patient with history of chronic lower back pain, likely multifactorial but primarily due to lumbar spondylosis and spinal stenosis who presented with atraumatic worsening of lower back pain with radiation to her anterior thighs consistent with pain secondary to lumbar degenerative joint disease. MRI demonstrated no cord compression or cauda equina compression, and no evidence of spinal infection, but did demonstrate multilevel degenerative spinal disease. Patient with maximum temperature of 100.0 at outside hospital, but no true fevers and no evidence of infection including spinal infection on MRI. Given her known history of PMR which has presented at times as thigh pain as well as an elevated CRP of 126 (ESR was 45, normal for patient age), rheumatology was consulted. They did not find any evidence of active polymyalgia rheumatica or any other inflammatory cause of back pain. They did not recommend any changes to current prednisone dose. Cause of CRP elevation was unclear, but no infection was found as above and CRP had spontaneously downtrended to 80 at time of discharge. Pain spontaneously improved and her exacerbation was felt to be likely musculoskeletal or secondary to lumbar disc disease. She was seen by ___ who recommended rehab. # Possible renal mass: CT A/P at outside hospital demonstrated 2.1 cm lesion on left kidney suspicious for renal neoplasm, as well as renal and hepatic cysts. She will need MRI for further evaluation but this was unable to be performed as an inpatient. Discussed with patient's primary care office who will order scan as an outpatient. Patient and her daughter are both aware of need for renal MRI to exclude malignancy # Encephalopathy, resolved: Patient initially presented with waxing and waning sensorium in the ED. On arrival to the floor, she was lucid and linear. She had no signs or symptoms of infection and basic infectious workup was negative. She also had no metabolic derangements to explain confusion. NCHCT was without acute process but did show chronic small vessel changes that would likely predispose to delirium. Overall her initial altered mental status in the ED was felt to likely be delirium in the setting of severe pain. She had no episodes of confusion while admitted CHRONIC/STABLE PROBLEMS: # Hypertension: continued home metoprolol, losartan, amlodipine # Hyperlipidemia: CoQ-10 help while inpatient as non-formularly # Seizure disorder: continued home Keppra # CKD: Per chart, unknown baseline Cr. Creatinine remained in 1.0-1.2 range # Gout: continued home allopurinol # Depression: continued home sertraline # Urinary incontinence: continued home oxybutinin > 30 minutes spent on discharge coordination and planning Transitional Issues: ==================== - needs renal MRI to further evaluate suspicious renal lesion - should have CRP rechecked as an outpatient to ensure downtrending
summary: You came in with back pain. We think the pain was likely due to a muscle strain or pinched nerve. You had an MRI which did not show any sign of fracture or infection in the spine. You were also seen by the rheumatologists who felt that your polymyalgia was under good control. They did not recommend any changes to your prednisone dose. At Metro West you had a CT scan that showed a lesion on your kidney. It will be very important to have an MRI as an outpatient to make sure that this lesion is not cancer. We spoke to Dr. ___ and they are working on arranging this for you. You are now going to rehab to work on getting stronger.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Impression: ___ man presents with exertional dyspnea and midepigastric pain, found to have likely 2:1 AV block. # Lyme Carditis: Patient presented with symptomatic bradycardia and found to have 2:1 AV block with severe AV conduction delay. PR interval on admission approximately 400ms. Ddx included lyme carditis, intrinsic conduction defect, infiltrative disease such as sarcoid or amyloid, or other CTD. TTE performed which showed only with mild RA enlargement. Patient started on empiric ceftriaxone 2gm IV daily while awaiting lyme titers. He was monitored on continuous telemetry and PR interval decreased to 280ms on day 4 of hospitalization. He was then transitioned to doxycycline to complete a 28 day course (day 1: ___, day 28: ___. Initial blood cultures were no growth. Lyme titers were pending at discharge as they were sent to ___ Clinic for Western Blot testing. # Anemia: Patient found to have an isolated anemia with Hb of 12.1 with normal MCV on admission. He denies history of anemia. RBC smear unrevealing though Fe studies showed iron of 25 and ferritin of 32, consistent with iron deficiency anemia. He was started on Fe supplementation and PPI empirically. # Dyspepsia: Patient presented with mild epigastric tenderness to palpation that resolved during hospitalization. Given ETOH use, concerning for gastritis vs PUD. H pylori was negative. Currently without alarm symptoms, though anemia is concerning for gastritis vs PUD. H pylori serology was negative. Patient was started on pantoprazole empirically. # Depression: home fluoxetine continued # Anxiety: home gabapentin continued Transitional issues: - please monitor PR interval monitoring at next PCP visit - further investigation into iron deficiency anemia likely warrented in this otherwise healthy ___ year old - taper off PPI - will complete a 28 day course of antibiotics for lyme carditis with doxycycline (day 1: ___ day 28: ___ - lyme titers pending at discharge
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath with exertion and were found to have an abnormal heart rate, which reflected a conduction delay in your heart. We believe this is most likely due to lyme disease and you were treated with IV antibiotics. This conduction delay improved. You should continue taking the antibiotic, doxycycline 100mg every 12 hours, to complete a 28 day course (through ___. Please ___ with your primary care physician as listed to evaluate this conduction delay again. He should perform an EKG at that visit. Your lyme titers were pending on discharge. If you would like, you can also see Dr. ___, the cardiologist, in 1 month. His clinic number is ___. You were also found to have an iron deficiency anemia.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female with CLL, history of lung cancer s/p wedge resection, hyperthyroidism s/p recent radioactive iodine ablation, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation s/p PMP who presents with palpitations. . ACTIVE ISSUES # Atrial Fibrillation: Pt has long standing atrial fibrillation that has failed treatment with amiodarone, digoxin, disopyramide, and flecanide and has been on sotalol for the past several months. Pt was felt to be a poor candidate of PVI. She had recent underwent a dual chamber pacemaker placement. She came to the hospital for symptomatic atrial fibrillation. In the ED, pt was given diltiazem for rate control. She was seen by Dr. ___ while on the floor, and decision was made to proceed with AV nodal ablation. She tolerated the procedure very well. Sotalol and diltiazem were discontinued subsequently. We continued her warfarin at 6 mg daily. . # Hypothyroidism: Patient is recently s/p radioactive iodine ablation for Graves' disease. She had a TSH checked on ___ which was 40.6 (H), but with a T4 of 8.8, and a FT4 index of 7.18 (on the ranges provided by ___ T4 and FT4I are both in range). She had her Levoxyl increased from 25 mcg to 50 mcg on ___. Her TSH checked during this admission was 77, with Free T4 at 0.99. We increased her levothyroxine to 75 mcg qd. She should be followed closely by endocrinology as outpatient. . # Hyponatremia: Her sodium was 129 on presentation. Her urine lytes were not consistent with prerenal etiology. Her free cortisol was normal. This is likely a result of hypothyroidism. Her hyponatremia largely resolved on the second day. . CHRONIC ISSUES # CLL: Patient was diagnosed approximately ___ years ago and receives monthly IVIG as an outpatient. The FISH analysis showed only 13q14.3 deletion which is a favorable prognostic factor. She was last seen by ___, ___ on ___, with a plan to continue IVIG given her immunoglobulin deficiency every 6 weeks. WBCs were recently 98 on ___, and 105.3 on ___ . # Anemia: Presumed ___ her CLL. Per her Heme/Onc notes has been stable, and HCT here is stable from DC in ___. . # HTN: Chronic. Stable. We continued lisinopril. . # Osteoarthritis: Chronic. Stable. We continued tylenol. . # GERD: Chronic. Stable. We continued omeprazole. . # Depression: We continued sertraline 25 mg Daliy . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES CODE: FULL (confirmed with patient) PENDING STUDIES AT DISCHARGE: none MEDICATION CHANGES - DISCONTINUED sotalol - DISCONTINUED diltiazem - INCREASED levothyroxine to 75 mcg daily (from 50 mcg daily) FOLLOWUP PLAN - Pt will need PCP, ___ and Endocrine followup. No appointments were made as pt was discharged on the weekend. The ___ attending on service stated that appointments will be set up through ___, and pt will be notified at home. - Pt need repeat thyroid function test in two weeks - Pt need routine INR followup
summary: You came to our hospital for palpitation and fatigue. You were found to have a rapid heart rate as a result of atrial fibrillation. You were treated with medication initially, and later underwent a procedure called "AV nodal ablation". You tolerated the procedure very well, with much better improved heart rate afterwards. We also noticed that your blood test was concerning for very low thyroid level. We increased your levothyroxine. You need a repeat thyroid function test shortly after this dose change. Please notify your endocrinologist of your abnormal thyroid function tests early next week.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with EtOH cirrhosis who presents with clogged NGT and slightly worsened hyponatremia. His last alcoholic drink was ___. Previously evaluated for transplant by ___ but for insurance reasons, he transferred care to ___ ___. His cirrhosis is quite severe; he requires nearly weekly paracenteses (last ___ removing 8L). His ascites is diuretic-refractory (due to hyponatremia). Last in hospital ___ for hyponatremia and serial paracenteses. Due to severity of malnutrition, patient has chronic Dobhoff (advanced to jejunum) for tube feeds and medications. He does not have trouble swallowing. Last seen in ED ___ for clogged Dobhoff, which ___ replaced while patient was in ED. Re-presents ___ for clogged Dobhoff again. Being admitted as ___ unable to replace in ED (___). #SEVERE MALNUTRITION: #CLOGGED DOBHOFF: recurrent issue for the patient, both while admitted and at home. Requiring tube feeds for severe malnutrition that cannot be managed with PO intake alone. ___ was not available to place and advance feeding tube in the ED, so he was admitted. Dobhoff replaced but unfortunately could not be advanced post-pyloric. Following the administration of water via the feeding tube, in an attempt to better dilate the stomach and pylorus, there was a reflux of small amount of blood and water. Mr. ___ blood counts remained stable. His tube feeds were changed to Osmolite 1.5 Cal; Full strength as there may be less clogging with this tube feed. At discharge, patient was tolerating tube feeds at goal rate of 75cc/hr. #EtOH CIRRHOSIS: Childs class C, currently decompensated by ascites. No HE currently, but does have a history of it. Current MELD-Na 23. Continued Lactulose 30 mL PO/NG TID, Rifaximin 550 mg PO/NG BID. He underwent a therapeutic paracentesis on ___ and 9 L were removed. #HYPONATREMIA: likely due to cirrhosis and volume overload. Baseline ~125, down to 121 on admission. Managed with a 1L fluid restriction daily, low Na diet. Received albumin while in house. His midodrine dose was also increased to 15 mg TID. Na 124 on discharge. #RASH: Mr. ___ reported a rash around IV site while getting albumin. He states that he only gets this rash when getting albumin post paracentesis. Of note, he received albumin multiple times without rash. Consider diphenhydramine administration prior to albumin administration. #DEPRESSION: continue Sertraline 50 mg PO/NG DAILY #ASTHMA: stable, does not take any medications for this TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [ ] if Dobhoff is clogged again, would recommend contacting patient's hepatologist who can coordinate with radiology to have ___ replaced (to AVOID returning to Emergency Dept) [ ] sodium on discharge was 124 [ ] increased midodrine to 15mg TID [ ] patient must flush Dobhoff every 4 hours with 30cc water [ ] weekly vitamin D should be changed to daily (1000 mg) week of ___ [ ] continue with weekly paracenteses + 100g albumin with every paracentesis. Next due ___. [ ] Mr. ___ reported a rash around IV site while getting albumin. He states that he only gets this rash when getting albumin post paracentesis. Of note, he received albumin multiple times without rash. Consider diphenhydramine administration prior to albumin administration. [ ] Discharge weight 71 kg [ ] Discharge MELD-Na: 23
summary: Your feeding tube was clogged. You had low salt. You had your feeding tube replaced. You got albumin to help the salt level. It is very important that you follow a low salt diet (no more than 2 grams a day). You cannot drink more than 1 L of fluid a day. You will need to come to the radiology office for paracentesis (fluid removal from the belly) once a week going forward. You will leave the feeding tube and continue to receive additional nutrition at home. You need to flush this every six hours. It is of the UTMOST importance that you participate in counseling and the relapse prevention program that has been recommended to you by our transplant social worker, ___ ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with h/o colon adenocarcinoma s/p sigmoidectomy ___ and nml colonoscopy ___, h/o depression and anxiety, monoclonal gammopathy of uncertain signficance and pernicious anemia presents with weight loss, hyponatremia and recurrent falls. Pt noted to have lumbar compression fracture of unclear duration, head CT negative. . # FALLS AND LUMBAR FRACTURE AND NEUROPATHIC PAIN: Pt with concerning presentation, unable to support own weight. Neuro exam does not demonstrate motor or sensory findings. Pt does have osteopenia, lumbar fracture (unclear if acute) and also decreased range of motion of neck. Cardiac causes for falls unlikely. Pt does have e/o ischemic changes on head CT, but no e/o active bleed. No history of syncope or pre-syncope, mostly appears ___ weakness. Pt has chronic urinary incontinence but no loss of bowel control. No e/o lytic lesions on x-ray of lumbar spine. Pt does not appear to have any clear altered mental status. B12 and folate recently normal. Hip/femur/knee/thoracic/cervical x-rays were negative for fracture. L1 compression fracture noted on x-ray and CT scan demonstarted severe canal stenosis. She was started on calcium and vitamin D. She was started on pain control with diclofenac, tylenol, lidocaine patch and tramadol/oxycodone(prn). She c/o neuropathic pain in bilateral thighs. She will be started on neurontin 300mg at bedtime and this dose can be uptitrated. Physical therapy recommended rehab placement. . # HYPONATREMIA: new since checked ___. Likely ___ poor PO intake, pt appeared hypovolemic. With h/o cancer and gammaglobulinopathy, SIADH could be considered. Her sodium improved well with normal saline. She had no evidence of altered mental status. . # FAILURE TO THRIVE and NUTRITION: concerning for recurrent neoplasm. Source could be colonic, s/p adenoCA with resection. Pt did not have surveillance colonoscopy in ___ as planned. Pt also with symptoms of constipation (chronic) but no melena or BRBPR. She c/o "fullness" sensation with eating and recent dry heaves. No EGD in past. She has a history of monoclonal gammopathy of unclear significance and has declined hematology consultation in past. Negative mammography one year ago. Pt has h/o GERD but not on medications. She also has significant history of depression and her medications were recently changed. Nutrition was consulted and recommended ensure supplements. She also received a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis to screen for new masses and this showed persistent post-surgical seroma, but no other concerning findings. Her albumin was 3.6. She will need a colonoscopy as outpatient. Depression appears to be the most significant cause of her presentation. . # DEPRESSION/ANXIETY: appears significant. Recently changed antidepressant. Also with panic attacks. Continued ativan QHS, prescribed for restless leg. ___ increase PRN but with caution. Pt also has h/o sundowning while hospitalized. Her citalopram dose was increased. She will need outpatient psychotherapy, psyche evaluation. . # H/O COLON CA and CONSTIPATION: due for surveillance colonoscopy. Failure to thrive and weight loss concerning for recurrence. Obtained ___ CT that showed no clear new masses or nodes. She needs to be on an aggressive bowel regimen. Outpatient colonoscopy recommended. . # PERNICIOUS ANEMIA: on monthly B12. Hct ___ high ___ to ___. . # HYPERTENSION: continue lisinopril. . # MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY: unclear significance. Has been stable. Pt has declined work-up in past. . # CONTACT: son ___ ___ daughter-in-law ___ (active in pt's care) ___ (h); ___ (w)
summary: You were admitted with multiple falls. You had a compression fracture of your lumbar spine. You have narrowing of your spine in the lower back region that may be accounting for your pain. You were started on pain control medications. You were seen by physical therapy who recommended rehabilitation. Other x-rays of your bones did not show any fractures. You also have lost a lot of weight. You have a history of cancer. Therefore an abdominal cat scan was done to see if there are any new changes. It showed some post-operative changes and narrowing of your spinal canal. You will need a colonoscopy as an outpatient. Your depression also appears to strongly influence your nutritional state.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ male with PMH notable for CAD s/p recent CABG ___, NPH s/p VP shunt, who presents now with several day history of fatigue and weakness most likely secondary to worsening iron deficiency anemia, exacerbated by bradycardia and known NPH. ACTIVE ISSUES: 1. ANEMIA: Recent outpatient lab work suggestive of iron deficiency anemia, given low iron and low ferritin levels. Recent TSH, B12, folate had all been WNL. Patient's HCT had decreased from 30 to 25 over several days leading up to admission, and patient was transfused with 2u of PRBCs on night of admission. His HCT was noted to increase appropriately from 25.5 to 32.1. His hematocrit remained stable during the remainder of his hospital stay, and the patient remained hemodynamically stable. Stool was guiac negative x2, although there was concern for possible GI bleeding given patient on ASA and plavix. He was continued on oral iron supplementation. Aspirin dose reduced to 81mg daily. To rule out GI etiology of blood loss, outpatient colonoscopy should be scheduled. 2. BRADYCARDIA: Patient's HR noted to be in ___ on presentation; on review of recent clinic visits HR typically in ___. He was placed on telemetry. His metoprolol was held to assess whether this would help improve cardiac autonomic regulation and improve his general fatigue and weakness. His HR remained in the upper ___ throughout. In speaking with his outpatient cardiologist, Dr. ___ was decided that his metoprolol should be held at discharge. 3. GAIT INSTABILITY: He's had multiple previous falls. Per patient's history and recent PCP notes, unsteady gait most likely multifactorial in nature, secondary to fatigue/weakness from anemia, bradycardia, known NPH, and OA/spinal stenosis. CT head showed stable hydrocephalus. ___ was consulted during the course of his hospital stay and fall precautions enacted. He was discharged in time for neurosurgery appointment and scheduled ventriculogram. 4. CAD s/p recent CABG: EKG showed TWI in I, aVL, V2-V6. Cardiac enzymes were negative x2. He was continued on plavix, aspirin, and statin. Beta-blocker was held as discussed above given bradycardia. Cardiac activity monitored via telemetry. 5. NPH s/p VP shunt ___: CT head on admission demonstrated stable hydrocephalus. The patient was monitored for gait instability and urinary incontinence/urgency during the course of his hospital stay. He will follow-up at ___, and has a ventriculogram scheduled for ___. 6. Depression: Thought to also be contributing to his increased fatigue. Mood noted to be dysphoric during stay. Continued bupropion ER 100mg daily. 7. HTN: Continued clonidine patch. Initially held metoprolol given bradycardia in setting of symptomatic anemia. In speaking with his cardiologist, it was recommended that the metoprolol be held. Started patient on losartan 25mg daily on discharge due to elevated BP 140s-160s. INACTIVE ISSUES: 8. BPH: Continued tamsulosin nightly. 9. Glaucoma: Continued brimonidine drops, latanoprost drops, dorzolamide drops. 10. Hypercholesterolemia: Continued rosuvastatin 5mg daily. 11.
