Dementia: Continued donepezil 10mg daily. 12. Osteopenia: Continued calcium, vitamin D. 13. Osteoarthitis/Spinal stenosis: Tylenol prn pain. LABS/STUDIES STILL PENDING AT TIME OF DISCHARGE: none TRANSITIONAL CARE ISSUES: -Patient was a full code during this admission -Will need outpatient colonoscopy, possible EGD -cardiology followup for persistent bradycardia
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with fatigue and weakness, and we discovered you had very low blood counts and a very low heart rate. You received a blood transfusion, and your anemia (low red blood cell count) improved. We also found you have a very low heart rate, which is likely contributing to your feeling tired. We held your medication called metoprolol, which can slow the heart rate. Your cardiologist Dr. ___ was aware of this plan, and she agrees with the decision. We are not sure what caused your anemia, but you will likely need to have a colonoscopy as an outpatient to look for possible bleeding in your colon. Dr. ___ help set up this test. You should also continue to take iron pills. A CT scan of your head did not show any significant change in your hydrocephalus.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old gentlemen with PMH of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, depression, BPH presenting with recurrent urinary retention requiring a Foley and two days of diarrhea. . # Urinary retention. Urology was consulted in the ED and recommended placement of a 2-way 22 ___ Foley catheter. The Foley did not clog up during the admission and was draining well on discharge. He has a history of BPH and also had a UTI on admission. Due to his frequent catheterization, the patient has been having clots that are obstructing Foley drainage. The patient will be discharged with his Foley in place and follow-up with his urologist, Dr. ___, as an outpatient for trial of void. He will be continued on cefpodoxime until his urologist appointment at which time it can be continued or stopped at the outpatient provider's discretion. . # UTI. Patient has had a UTI for approximately 2 months that has not responded to courses of Augmentin and Ciprofloxacin. This may be due to intermittent Foley placement over the past 2 months, colonization, or because of bacterial resistance to antibiotics. He will be treated with cefpodoxime until his outpatient follow-up appointment with urology. . # BPH. Continue home Flomax and Avodart. . # Diarrhea. Given his recent antibiotic use, a stool sample was checked for C. Diff toxin and was negative. The patient was encouraged to keep well hydrated at home. . # Depression. Continue home citalopram. . # Essential tremor. Continue home Sinemet. . # Hyperlipidemia. Continue home niacin. . # Asthma. Continue home Flovent and albuterol PRN. . # Rosacea. Continue home doxycycline. . # CODE: Of note, the patient expressed his wishes to be DNR/DNI.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for urinary retention secondary to a clogged Foley catheter, a urinary tract infection, and two days of diarrhea. Your Foley catheter was replaced with a 2-way 22 ___ catheter per urology recommendations. It has been flushing well and has not clogged during your admission. You will be sent home with this Foley in place and have a trial of void as an outpatient at Dr. ___ ___. Your UTI will be treated with cefpodoxime and your urine culture will be followed by your outpatient urologist. A stool sample was sent for infection and was negative. You were rehydrated with IV fluids. Please hydrate yourself at home by drinking plenty of fluids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of spinal stenosis, temporal arteritis, HTN/HL, depression/anxiety, s/p b/l hip replacement and repair (last ___ years ago), p/w acute on chronic left groin pain limiting her ability to walk likely secondary to worsening spinal stenosis and impingement on L2 nerve root # L2 Radiculopathy- Patient had pain in left groin mainly w/ weight bearing. She had Xrays of her hips which were unchanged from prior. She was without point tenderness, fever, chills, CRP/ESR were normal, WBC was normal, and movement of hips did not elicit pain, so unlikely to be hip pathology or infection. In this patient with known spinal stenosis and MRI with worsenining of crowding and clumping of nerve roots around L2, it is likely that her pain is acute on chronic referred pain from L2 lumbar spinal nerve root compression. She was evaluated by spine and no indication for emergent surgical decompression. Pain may improve with conservative management and steroid injection and if not improved in 4 weeks can follow up with spine clinic (___). The patient required only tylenol for pain medication while inpatient. She was seen by Acute Pain Service and received an epidural steroid injection and will follow up in their clinic. She was seen by ___ and able to ambulate with walker and walk up stairs so was discharged home with ___ services. #Pain control - patient was with minimal to no pain at rest while inpatient. She does have acute on chronic pain from DJD. Patient only required tylenol while inpatient. It was recommended that she use tylenol as much as possible for pain control with limited opioid use. It was recommended that she not take NSAIDs given bleeding risk in an elderly patient on chronic steroids. # Constipation - required senna, miralax and dulcolax supp. # Insomnia - recently started on Mirtazapine 15 mg PO HS with plan to DC benzo by PCP so mirtazapine was continued while inpatient and Clonazepam dose was decreased from home dose of 0.5mg to 0.25mg QHS. This taper should be continued until follow up with PCP ___ ___ and then discontinued. # Depression - continued home Venlafaxine XR 150 mg PO DAILY # TA - baseline L eye blind. R eye with partial loss of vision from macular degeneration. Continued on prednisone 40mg daily with plan to taper per physicians at ___. # HTN - home hydrochlorothiazide most likely has decreased effectiveness given her reduced CrCl of 35. BP was well controlled while inpatient. HCTZ was discontinued on discharge given risk of dehydration and falls with plan for BP follow up with PCP. If antihypertensive is needed, could consider use of chlorthalidone. # Osteoporosis - patient recieved home Alendronate Sodium 70 mg PO QFRI and calcium and vitamin D. **Transitional Issues** - Please discontinue clonazepam on ___. Dose was tapered to 0.25mg QHS while inpatient to be continued on discharge for one week until ___. - HCTZ was discontinued on discharge due to likely decreased effectiveness given reduced CrCl and well controlled BP inpatient. Please recheck BP at follow up - Can consider switching alendronate to alternate bisphosphonate as it is not recommended with CrCl<35 - Pain control - tylenol was used for pain managment. It was recommended that the patient not take NSAIDs and to limit opioid use as much as possible
summary: You were admitted with severe left leg pain. You had an MRI which showed degenerative changes in your spine similar to your prior MRI but with more crowding and narrowing around the L2 nerve. You had Xrays of your hips which were stable from prior Xrays. You were seen by the Spine specialists who felt that there was no need for urgent surgery. While inpatient, you were seen by the Acute Pain Service who performed a steroid injection in your back. You had physicial therapy and it was recommended that you continue physicial therapy at home. While in the hospital, you only required tylenol for pain management. Please continue using tylenol for pain at home. Please avoid NSAIDs such as ibuprofen as they can cause bleeding in your GI tract, especially in combination with your steroids. Please try to avoid using your opioid medications as much as possible as it puts you at risk for falls. While in the hospital, we tapered your clonazepam to 0.25mg at night. You should continue taking this medication at 0.25mg until you see your primary care physician ___ ___ and then stop taking it at that time.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with a complicated past medical history including asthma and OSA who presents with acute onset shortness of breath since approximately 2 am on the morning of admission. She was briefly admitted to the ICU on a BiPap then NRB. She swiftly transitioned to nasal cannula on asthma exacerbation therapy. . ACTIVE ISSUES: #Chronic Asthma with Acute Exacerbation: Given her history of asthma, wheezing on exam, and improvement over several hours with nebs, this was likely another asthma exacerbation. Despite her decreased mobility, we doubted PE, as she had a low D-dimer of 271, and she has no calf tenderness. Given her elevated white count with slight left shift, she could have early pna, but she was afebrile and had no other sx and CXR and her exam was not consistent with infection. Given her risk factors of DM2, HTN, HLD she is also at risk of ACS. She denied any CP upon development of her SOB, her first trop was negative, and her recent cath was unremarkable. The inspiratory nature of her wheezing is also concerning for vocal cord dysfunction. Also, her recent URI is likely a contributing factor to the exacerbation. COPD, Pickwikian, bronchitis, and vocal cord dysfunction are also possible and her acute decompensation is likely multifactorial. She was treated with IV steroids (125 mg solumedrol), albuterol nebs, and a 100% non-rebreather and was brought to the ICU. By the time she reached the ICU, her sats had improved and she was stable on 4L by nasal cannula. She was brought up to the floor where she was continued on Q4 albuterol nebs and 30 mg PO prednisone and her O2 was weaned. She noted significant improvement in her breathing and felt that she was near her baseline by the morning following her admission. She was discharge on albuterol nebs, which she has at baseline, as well as a 3 day prednisone taper and flovent inhaler . INACTIVE ISSUES: #Type 2 DM Uncontrolled with complications- We held her hold home metformin and glipizide and kept her on ISS. . #Benign Hypertension- Home meds were continued: valsartan, furosemide. . #Hyperlipidemia- continued home statin . #GERD- continued omeprazole . #Osteoarthritis- continued home pain treatment regimen with vicodin and motrin . #Panic disorder- continued home quetiapine QHS . #depression- continued mirtazapine and citalopram . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: 1. She will go back to the ___ for continued care. 2. She has a follow-up appointment with a pulmonologist to evaluate her respiratory issues. An ___ appointment was being discussed at discharge and may be arranged pending appointment availability. 3. She has a follow-up appointment with ENT to evaluate for possible vocal cord dysfunction.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had an acute episode of shortness of breath at night that made your oxygen saturation very low. You were given oxygen therapy and IV steroids in the emergency department, which helped your breathing a great deal. It is likely that this episode was the result of an asthma exacerbation and may have been triggered by your recent cold and the congestion and sputum you mentioned. There may also be some involvement from a dysfunction of your vocal cords. You were continued on oxygen by nasal cannula, with a higher rate than at your nursing home and you were given a low dose of oral steroids on your second day in the hospital. You will go back to your nursing home with continued albuterol nebs and oxygen therapy as you were on before admission.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ with a PMHx of afib/flutter (sp PPM implant in ___, generator change in ___, who presented with tenderness and discoloration of pacemaker generator concerning for infection. #Infected Pacemaker: Patient was admitted for pacemaker and leads removal given history of temderness over the pacemaker generator. He went to the OR where the current generator and the 2 current leads as well as a third old lead were removed without complication. A R IJ temporary pacer was placed. He spent 24 hours in the CCU for anesthesia related hypotension but was transferred to the floor afterwards without any further hypotension. He was started on vancomycin per ID recs at this point but quickly transitioned to Nafcillin when cultures from lead and pacer pocket grew out staph epidermis sensitive to Nafcillin. A PICC was placed via ___ in anticipation for ___ weeks of antibiotics. His course of Naficillin will finish ___. He developed a loculated, tender fluid collection in the former pacemaker site, for which he went to the OR to have surgically washed out. The site remained clean and was left to heal by secondary intention. He went for permanent pacemaker placement on ___. The device was placed without complications. #AFib: history of afib with RVR, s/p AV ablation and now pacemaker dependent. Remained ventricularly paced on temporary pacemaker while awaiting permanent ___ placement. Warfarin stopped prior to coming in for procedure. Aftter initial procedure, restarted on warfarin home dose but INR was refractorily subtherapeutic on warfarin due to Nafciliin administration, so was started on rivaroxaban. He was also started on sotalol on ___ for further control of his multiple atrial tachyarrythmias. #DMII: Patient started on home lantus and sliding scale, but due to morning hypoglycemia and evening hyperglycemia, his ___ lantus was reduced, and AM lantus was increased. # Anxiety/Depression: Cont'd Venlafaxine and Lamotrigine. # HLD: Continued simvastatin 10mg prior to DC # GERD: Cont'd omeprazole #HTN: Restarted lisinopril at discharge. Held while hypotensive (after anesthesia). Transitional Issues: -weekly labs (CBC w/ diff, LFTs, Bun, Cr) while on Naficillin -f/u with PCP, ID with OPAT -f/u in EP device clinic in 1 week, with apptmt with Dr. ___ to be scheduled at that point.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had an infected pacemaker. We removed the pacemaker with the leads, inserted a temporary pacemaker, treated you with antibiotics and reinserted a new permanent pacemaker. You will continue to receive one antibiotic, Naficillin, for another 4 weeks, through ___. You will be set up at home in order to do this. Once the antibiotics finish, your PICC line can be removed. You were also started on a medication, sotalol, for your atrial fibrillation. You will continue this medication as an outpatient. your coumadin was switched to a medication called Xarelto, which you will continue taking. This medication does not require regular blood monitoring.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ with a history of HCV cirrhosis and several prior admissions for ascites who presented with abdominal pain and distention consistent with prior episodes of worsening ascites. He underwent bedside paracentesis x2 and ___ guided paracentesis x1, during which over 7 liters of fluid were removed. There was no evidence of bacterial peritonitis. He was discharged in stable condition to a rehabilitation facility for concern about his ability to walk. Active issues: # Abdominal pain and distention due to ascites. Pertioneal fluid analysis showed WBC<250, so there was low concern for bacterial pertionitis. SAAG > 1.1, consistent with ascites secondary to portal hypertnesion. RUQ u/s showed known cirrhosis, no acute hepatic or gall bladde process. He had a bedside paracentesis in the ED and again two days after admsision. Over 7L of ascites was removed during these procedures. # HCV Cirrhosis c/b ascites. Noted to have low albumin (2.7). INR 1.1-1.2. Na 136-139. BPs ___. He was continued on his home diuretic regimen: - Furosemide 60mg daily. We avoided more aggressive diuresis due to concern about his low sodium. - Spironolactone 100mg daily He was also continued on his home medicines. - Rifaximin 550mg bid - Lactulose 10g/15mL TID. Notably, he refused this medicine several times. Inactive issues: # Anxiety/depression. We continued his home medications. His anxiety improved as his abdominal pain was improving. - Clonazepam 2mg po tid - Citalopram 20mg 2TAB daily # Diabetes - Kept on home Glargine and maintained on insulin sliding scale. Sugars were well controlled. Also kept on a diabetic diet. # Diabetic neuropathy - continued home meds - Methadone 40mg qAM, 1500, qHS # HLD - continued home meds. - Simvastatin 40mg qHS Transitional issues: # ___ needs - evaluated by ___ while inpatient. Concern for limited ability to manage daily activities and gait impairment. Discharged to rehabilitation facility for further treatment.
summary: You were sent by your outpatient liver doctor to the ___ ___ with abdominal pain and swelling on ___, similar to symptoms you have had in the past. Ultrasound from the morning of your admission confrimed a large volume of fluid in your abdomen. A sample of this fluid was taken in the emergency department, and there was no evidence of infection in this fluid. You were admitted for pain control and removal of this fluid. In addition to your home dose of methadone, you recevied dilaudid for pain control. We removed 1 liter of fluid on ___, and another 6 liters were removed on ___. You recevied albumin, a blood product, after this fluid was removed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with type 1 diabetes mellitus, asthma, and recurrent pneumonia who was admitted for elective metatarsal resection for osteomyelitis and was found to have community-acquired pneumonia prior to procedure. #Community-acquired pneumonia: She was treated empirically with vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam for both community-acquired pneumonia and osteomyelitis coverage for a total of 6 days, with symptomatic improvement, downtrending WBC count and normalized lactate. #right foot Osteomyelitis: She was treated empirically with vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam for both CAP and osteomyelitis coverage. On ___, she underwent resection of her right ___ metatarsal head without complications. Culture data and pathology are pending at the time of discharge, but preliminary swab from the osteomyelitic site showed MSSA. She was discharged on amoxicillin-clavulanate for both community-acquired pneumonia and osteomyelitis coverage to complete a 10-day course. She will follow up in ___ clinic within a week after discharge. #Diabetes mellitus type 1: At admission, Mrs. ___ also had an episode of hypoglycemia to 27 and her insulin pump was stopped in favor of sliding scale Humalog. ___ endocrinologists were consulted and successfully restarted her insulin pump with appropriate adjustments in the setting of infection and podiatric procedure, and her blood sugars were in the 110s-270s range by day of discharge. CHRONIC ISSUES =========== #Asthma - Continued home inhalers #Hypothyroidism - Continued levothyroxine #HTN - Continued lisinopril #Adrenal insufficiency - Continued fludrocortisone and hydrocortisone #Achalasia/esophageal dysmotility - Continued metoclopramide and ranitidine #Depression - Continued sertraline and trazodone
summary: You were admitted to ___ for elective resection of osteomyelitis of your right foot. ___ developed pneumonia prior to your surgery, and we treated ___ with IV antibiotics for both your pneumonia and your osteomyelitis. Given that blood sugar can be unpredictable during infection and surgery, we monitored your blood sugar closely and adjusted your insulin pump use accordingly with the help of endocrinologists from ___ ___. Your pneumonia symptoms improved, and you were able to undergo the podiatry procedure successfully without complicationsYou were discharged on an oral antibiotic, Augmentin, to complete a 10-day course.

text:
Brief Hospital Course: Given concern for status epilepticus, the patient was admitted to the neurologic ICU for closer monitoring and evaluation. On arrival to the unit, the patient was sleepy and confused, likely a combination of a post-ictal state and sedation from anti-epileptics received in the ED. However, there was no obvious focality noted. There were no further seizures overnight. The following morning, the patient was noted to be awake, but still confused and disoriented. She continued to have difficulty following commands and was pleasantly uninhibited. Her neck was supple. A thorough evaluation revealed no clear source of infectious, toxic, or metabolic disturbances to precipitate the seizure, although a relatively low daily dose of her outpatient keppra was a possibility. Her baseline hematologic abnormalities remained stable. She was afebrile. Keppra was increased to 1250 mg BID. Blood pressure medications (for chronic cirhossis) were held given systloic blood pressures from the ___ to low 100s. However, most of her other home medications were re-started and her diet was resumed given her increased alertness. She was placed on EEG telemetry, which by initial review showed left-sided PLEDs consistent with her known left temporal lesion, but with no seizure activity noted. On ___, the patient was more somnolent during rounds and had a ___ second complex partial seizure with transient loss of responsiveness to questions and right arm shaking. She was bolused with an additional 300 mg of dilantin to bring her level (9's) into therapeutic range.
An epilepsy consult was called and she was noted to have additional periods of eye fluttering and agitation, thought to be possible seizures as well. Pt was noted to have electrographical seizure activity on ___. At the recommendation of epilepsy, keppra was increased to 1500 mg BID, an additional 300 mg dilantin was given, as well as 0.5 mg of ativan for these more frequent episodes. At this point, she was transferred to the floors for further monitoring and evaluation.
During patients neurology floor course she was monitored by EEG through ___. Several of these EEG interpretations were notable for waveforms consistent with mild to moderate encephalopathy. Patients final AED medications at discharge are 1500mg keppra bid, dilantin 100mg TID, Zonegran 100mg nightly, Titrate up to 100mg per week to a max of 300mg total. Once Zonegran is titrated up pt will follow up in epilepsy clinic for dilantin taper. Dilantin is not the ideal AED as pt has liver cirrhosis. Pt is scheduled for follow up with Dr. ___ Dr. ___ on ___.
Problem List:
1. Seizure disorder (see above)
2. Receptive/Expressive Aphasia: Near baseline per son. Has problems with basic comprehension. Pt to follow up with behavioral neurology.
3. ADLs/Cognition/Ambulation: Patient was evaluated by Occupational therapy and physical therapy while in house. They recommended that the patient receive 24 hour surveillance, ADL training, memory training, speech training. They recommend continued OT and ___.
4. ETOH cirrhosis: Pts LFTs at discharge were AST 50, ALT 40, tbili 0.9. When pt was admitted her LFTs were elevated at AST 71, ALT 50, Tbili 1.9. Pt was discharged on home dose of spironolactone of 50mg daily. Last INR 1.4. Cont lactulose.
5. GERD: Switch from prilosec to pantoprazole, as prilosec interacts unfavorably with dilantin.
6. Hx of pancreatitis and pancreatic mass: no active issues. Follow with GI.
7. DM: Pt on ISS while in house. Sent out w/ home dose glimeripide 1mg BID.
8. Depression: Cont mirtazapine and seroquel.
9. HTN: Sent home on spironolactone 50mg.
10. Follow up:
Patient needs to follow up with GI, PCP, ___, behavioral neurology.
summary: you were admitted to the hospital for mental status changes and L sided weakness. You were seen in the ED at ___ where they were concerned that you were having seizure activity. In the ED you received antiepileptic medication. Clinically and electroencephalographically you were found to have seizures. We modified your seizure medications to include keppra 1500mg BID, Dilantin 100mg TID, and zonegran 100mg daily. Starting ___ you should take zonegran 200mg daily, then on ___ you should 300mg of zonegran daily. We also started you on Protonix 40mg twice a day in place of your prilosec for your reflux disease/heart burn. Prilosec interacts with other medications that you are taking. Physical therapy and 0ccupational therapy assessed you during your inpatient stay. It was their recommendation that if you return home that you receive 24 hour supervision, home ___, speech and language therapy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with stage IV SCLC (to the bone marrow) on C1D7 of dose-reduced Carboplatin/Irinotecan who presents to the ED for transfusions and is admitted for hypoxia/tachycardia and radiologic evidence of multifocal PNA. # Stage IV NSCLC with anemia and thrombocytopenia - she was transfused 2 units of pRBCs in the ED, as well as platelets, resulting in an appropriate bump in these parameters - she will follow-up as an outpt with Dr. ___ -- care was coordinated with Dr. ___ while she was inpatient - some consideration should be given to possible pulmonary drug reaction given CT chest findings # Mild hypoxemia, tachycardia and concern for pneumonia vs. pulmonary drug reaction - etiologies of her hypoxemia could include mild emphysema, pneumonia or mild pulmonary drug reaction - her tachycardia could be related to the above, or to her anemia - regardless, these signs improved resolved with transfusion and antibiotics - plan to finish course of antibiotics with four more days of oral levofloxacin and follow-up with Oncologist prior to more chemotherapy - recommend consideration of mild pulmonary drug reaction prior to more chemotherapy -- Dr. ___ that the chemotherapy she received is not commonly known to cause pulmonary reactions -- he did wonder whether her findings could also potentially be lymphangitic spread of tumor - Radiology recommends a CXR PA & Lateral at ___, and future CT scan no more than 3 months from now (if not sooner) to monitor for changes # Miscellanous - was continued on Levothyroxine for hypothyroidism, Sertraline for anxiety/depression, oxycontin bid and oxycodone PRN, and a bowel regimen On the day of discharge she was feeling much better, and was eager to go home. We reviewed her plan of care and the importance of follow-up. She understood and had no additional questions. On the day of discharge, I spent >30min in discharge day services and coordination of care.
summary: You were admitted with a slightly low oxygen level, and tachycardia, with concern for pneumonia. After our evaluation, we're not certain what caused these symptoms but see three possibilities. One, your anemia (low red blood cell count) could have contributed -- after transfusion your counts are now better. Two, you could have a mild pneumonia, so we'll treat you with some antibiotics for that. You also have some mild emphysema from smoking, and that could cause low oxygen levels. Three, the CT scan of your chest suggested that your lungs might have had a small reaction to the chemotherapy -- it's not certain but we recommend you discuss this possibility with your Oncologist before receiving chemotherapy again. It's important you follow-up as described below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ female with history of atrial fibrillation s/p AV junction ablation and BiV pacemaker (___) on eliquis, HFrEF EF 45% ___, LBBB (exercise-related), vascular dementia, generalized anxiety disorder, HTN, and hypothyroidism, recently diagnosed with bilateral lower lobe pneumonia s/p 5 day course of levofloxacin (___), who presented with some mild worsening shortness of breath with hypoxemia initially on 4L O2 on arrival, thought to be secondary to acute on chronic heart failure exacerbation in relation to recent pneumonia. The patient was diuresed with IV lasix 40mg X2 and then 60mg X1 and she was eventually weaned to room air. ACUTE ISSUES ==================== #Dyspnea #Possible Acute on Chronic HFrEF - With history of HFrEF EF 45% on torsemide 30 mg daily at home. She was recently found to have a CAP at her long term care facility and treated with levofloxacin for 5 days last day of treatment was ___. Following this exacerbation she presented to the ED with worsening DOE and new 02 requirement. Based on CXR with bilateral pleural effusions We think that she had an acute on chronic heart failure exacerbation she also had a BNP of 4596. Troponins negative and flat CK-MB, EKG no acute ischemic changes to suggest ischemic etiology. She was diuresed with 40mg IV lasix X2 and an additional dose of 60mg IV lasix X1. We continued her Valsartan 160mg daily and fractionated her metoprolol tartrate 50mg PO QD. On the day of discharge we also increased her torsemide dose to 40mg once daily from 30mg. # RESVOLVED CAP - Chest x-ray obtained in outpatient setting showed bilateral lower lobe pneumonia, s/p 5 day course of levofloxacin (___). Does reside at a facility making her at increased risk for MRSA/MDR GNRs, however on presentation for this hospitalization she was afebrile, without leukocytosis, or cough. We therefore did not start any antibiotics. #Neck Pain - Was endorsing acute on chronic neck pain most pronounced at the back of her head and neck. This was relieved with APAP on the morning of admission, no concerning neurological symptoms or signs, neuro exam non-focal. Thought to be most likely from musculoskeletal pain. CHRONIC ISSUES ==================== #Atrial fibrillation - History of AF previously difficult rate control, did not tolerate digoxin previously. S/p AVJ and BiV PPM ___. We continued her Apixaban 2.5 mg PO BID and fractionated her Metoprolol tartrate 50mg PO QD. #HTN - On home metoprolol XL 200mg daily, valsartan 160mg daily. We continued her home Metoprolol tartrate 50mg PO QD per above and continued her home valsartan 160mg daily. #Vascular Dementia #Anxiety/Depression: We continued her home aripiprazoe 2mg PO QD, home buspirone 10mg BID and home venlafaxine 160mg QAM. #Iron Deficiency Anemia: Continued home ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily #Hypothyroidism: Continued home levothyroxine 75 mcg daily # GERD: Continued home omeprazole 20 mg daily. #Goals of Care - On last hospitalization ___, patient was evaluated by palliative care, was made DNR/Ok to intubate. Had expressed intubation would be reasonable for immediate management of reversible cause, however would not want prolonged intubation or trach. Presented with ___, stating do not hospitalize, however on arrival said was okay with admission. We called the daughter to clarify this and she ___ ___ clarified that she believes that her mother wishes are to be hospitalized if she needs this to be the case and ok for intubation but no chest compressions or resuscitation. We changed the ___ to reflect this. # CODE: DNR, OK to intubate, ok to hospitalize # CONTACT: Name of health care proxy: ___ ___: daughter Phone number: ___ # DISPO: Medicine, pending above
summary: You were brought into the hospital for shortness of breath and a low oxygen level which we think was from your heart failure which caused fluid to build up in your lungs. This could have happened after your pneumonia and you may have needed increased doses of your toremide. We gave you an IV medication called Lasix to help pull the fluid out of your lungs and decrease the work on your heart. We got a CXR which showed that your lungs did have some fluid in them. We also called your daughter and re-discussed your ___ form and clarified that you would like to the return to the hospital if you needed but continued your wishes not to get chest compressions but ok to have an intubation.

text: Brief Hospital Course: CHIEF COMPLAINT: Right arm pain REASON FOR ADMISSION: ___ with recurrent MRSA cellulitis. Risk factors include pre-diabetes mellitus, PCOS, obesity, and family colonization with mrsa. She is afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and the abscess site appears grossly unchanged from yesterday with IV vanc (started ___ at 1g q12h). Her trough ___ was 3.3, however, so she was increased to 1.25g Q8h receiving first dose at midnight ___. ACTIVE ISSUES BY PROBLEM: # MRSA abscess and cellulitis: Her R upper arm abscess was incised and drained with wick packing ___, cultures of which subsequently grew MRSA. She was placed on IV vancomycin initially, then transitioned to PO bactrim on ___ with continued improvement. Her wound was repacked on a daily basis. Her other questionable infectious sites (groin, right nare) were observed closely. Her pain was controlled with ibuprofen and percocet. An infectious disease consult was obtained, who recommended decontamination of family members and house to prevent these recurrent MRSA infections. Please see transition of care issues below for steps on decontamination. Ms ___ will continue ___ DS two tabs BID for 8 more days through ___ and will follow up with her PCP that day to ensure resolution of wound/cellulitis and commence with decontamination for her and her family. # Intertiginous candidiasis: An erythematous rash was noted under both breasts, most consistent with an early candidal intertriginous process. Her intertriginous areas were kept dry with gauze and towels, and miconazole powder was used. The rash improved, and she was not given an rx for miconazole but was encouraged to see her PCP if this infection recurred. # Pre-Diabetes: Patient is obese with history of PCOS and gestational diabetes, as well as a family history of DM. She has pre-diabetes, as her HbA1c this admission was found to be 6.4. Given that several finger sticks were >200, she was placed on insulin sliding scale during hospitalization. She should have f/u with PCP with lifestyle interventions and possible anti-hyperglycemic medications. INACTIVE ISSUES BY PROBLEM: #Depression. Patient was continued on home citalopram 40mg daily #Asthma. Her lungs were clear on exam without wheezing throughout admission and she had subjective SOB or cough. She used her home albuterol and flovent. #Obesity. Undergoing ___ workup for gastric bypass TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -She will require completion of Bactrim course ending ___ -She and her family should undergo individual MRSA decontamination as well as household decontamination, as per infectious disease recommendations. -She has pre-diabetes and will require follow-up with PCP for lifestyle modifications and possible medications. DECONTAMINATION INSTRUCTIONS: Chlorhexadine 2-4% baths (cover whole body except groin/eyes) scrub for ___ minutes, rinse, for ___ day course. ___ cause irritation/drying -OR - ___ to ___ cup bleach ___ full bathtub, soak ___ minutes, rinse, ___. Additionally, should use mupirocin 2% to each nare BID for ___ days. MEDICATION CHANGES: START bactrim 2 pills twice daily until ___ START percocet as needed for severe pain START mupirocin nasal ointment to clear up MRSA colonization ___ the nose. Start this after finishing course of bactrim
summary: You were admitted with a right arm abscess. Your abscess was incised, drained, and packed. You were evaluated by our internal medicine and infectious disease teams and treated with an intravenous antibiotic called vancomycin, with improvement of your infection. You were then switched to an oral antibiotic (Bactrim), which you should continue until ___. It is recommended that you be decontaminated to MRSA, according to infectious disease suggestions. Given that your family has a history of MRSA skin infections, it is also recommended that you decontaminate your house and that individuals are decontaminated according to infectious disease recommendations. These are included ___ your discharge papers and can be addressed by your primary care doctor. While you are colonized, you should not be caring for sick or elderly persons. Your blood sugars were found to be high. Your HbA1c, a marker of your blood sugar, was 6.4. Your finger stick blood sugars were higher than 200 at times, so you were started on insulin injections while ___ the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o male with hypotonic bladder with incomplete emptying and chronic indwelling foley since ___ c/b frequent multidrug resistent UTIs p/w bilateral groin pain right>left and generalized weakness. . ACTIVE ISSUES: # Groin pain: Differential diagnosis included catheter-associated urinary tract infection, bacteruria, and trauma from Foley. Patient presenting with 1 day history of groin pain, which initially resolved after replacing his Foley. Groin pain returned though with lesser intensity. There was no clinical evidence of infection (no fevers, leukocytosis, flank pain; patient was hemodynamically stable). Patient has pyuria and bacteruria related to chronic foley, this is believed to represent bacterial colonization colonizers. Initial urinalysis prior to Foley change was floridly positive and culture grew mixed flora consistent with contamination versus colonization. Repeat urinalysis two days later was improved. Repeat urine culture is pending. Patient remained alert & oriented x 3, and appropriate & conversant. Patient received no antibiotics. It seemed most likely during this admission that presence of blood in urine at presentation and ongoing, intermittent groin pain was secondary to Foley trauma. . # s/p mechanical fall/generalized weakness: Per daughter, patient fell ~5 days prior to admission with head strike. Per patient and family, there were no cardiac symptoms preceding fall, and patient has memory of fall and time directly afterwards. Very likely mechanical fall. Non-contrast head CT was obtained, and showed no acute process. Physical therapy assessed patient, and recommended discharge to rehabilitation. Patient initially refused, and discharge was planned with increased home services including visiting physical therapy and an home attendant in addition to existing services of day care three days a week, and a home attendant with limited hours 7 days a week. Patient was seen by his daughter who felt patient was too weak and far from baseline to go home. Patient was discharged to rehab. . INACTIVE ISSUES: # Coronary artery disease: s/p CABG and stenting x2. Patient was continued on home dose atorvastatin, metroprolol and aspirin. . # Hypertension: Stable. Patient was continued on home metoprolol and isosorbide mononitrate. . # HLN: Stable. Patient was continued on home atorvastatin. . # Diabetes: Stable, controlled with diet. Fasting blood glucoses were monitored daily. . # Depression: Stable. Patient was continued on home dose trazadone and citalopram. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Patient's chronic indwelling Foley catheter needs to be changed every 4 to 6 weeks. Patient was discharged with urology outpatient follow-up. -Final urine cultures still pending.
summary: You were admitted to the ___ with bilateral groin pain. Your urinary catheter had been in place for more than 6 weeks, and was changed in the Emergency Department. After that, you had no more pain. A sample of your urine was taken, which showed evidence of bacteria in your urine. However, you had no signs or symptoms of an infection. We think that these bacteria in your urine are always there at some level, and there is no reason to treat you with antibiotics at this time. We were told by your daughter that you had fallen recently and hit your head. She was concerned about you. We did a scan of your head, and found no evidence of bleeding.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo woman with ___ chronic back pain, depression, presents to the ED with acute back pain and fecal/urinary incontinence. # Back pain: Patient's back pain thought to be acute on chronic back pain secondary to injury possibly causing lumbar disk degeneration and bulging. The exacerbation is likely due to lack of access to pain medication (patient's workman's compensation had stopped). Social work was consulted to help patient attain access to medications. Because of her incontinence, spine and neuro were consulted to r/o cord injury, they cleared her for any risk of acute spinal injury. They did note that the moderate cervical cord compression was stable and probably not contributing to her symptoms. The patient's pain was very persistent and difficult to manage. On Dilaudid ___ PO Q3H, her pain was still an ___ however she was starting to become somewhat sedated and she said she had bad reactions to toradol, motrin and neurontin. Pain management was consulted and they have been helping to modify her pain regimen to improve her symptoms. We believe much of her low tolerance to pain is related to her severely depressed mood and her pain management would significantly improve upon starting an SSRI and inpatient psychiatric observation. Pain management consult will continue to follow the patient. # Depression: Patient with MDD and is currently very depressed. Patient has had a multitude of complaints that would come and go including headache, chest pain, nausea, SOB, leg pain, arm numbness and tingling that were worked up with extensive imaging, EKGs all deemed negative. The patient admitted that many of her complaints including her chest pain could be attributed to her anxiety. These complaints have been thought to be psychosomatic. Psychiatry was consulted to help manage patient's significant depression and anxiety. Patient remained tearful, depressed, and anxious throughout the admission. The psychiatry team had recommended inpatient transfer to psychiatric unit where they would start patient on an SSRI and observe her course. The patient is being transferred to ___ for further evaluation. # UTI: Patient with a UTI likely secondary to her fecal incontinence. Culture grew pansensitive pseudomonas. Patient without white count, fever, or any signs of systemic toxicity. CT urogram negative for evidence of stones or pyelonephritis. This UTI was thought to be an uncomplicated UTI. Patient on 2 day course of levofloxacin, patient still with ?dysuria (hard to gauge as patient has many complaints that are likely psychosomatic) therefore the regimen was changed to start 3 day course of doxycycline to treat the UTI. # Ambulation: Patient was admitted not able to ambulate likely due to pain, which is what probably led to the incontinence. As the patient's pain control improved, patient was able to ambulate with physical therapy for 30 feet on ___. This morning patient was able to get out of bed and sit in the chair. Physical therapy, nursing, and the medicine team believe that much of her inability to walk is more related to feelings of hopelessness and lack of motivation rather than purely pain.
summary: You have been diagnosed with acute on chronic back pain. Your pain probably originated from your injury in ___. Your pain probably increased because of lack of access to medications. You have met with the social workers to help you gain access to medications through your medicare and Mass Health insurance. Because of your incontinence, there was concern that you might have injury to your spine. After extensive MRI of your entire spine, and evaluation by our neurology and spine experts, you were cleared of any acute injury to your spinal cord. You also experienced severe pain during your stay here. We attempted to treat your pain to high dose dilauidid, unfortunately this was unable to completely control your pain. We consulted the pain specialists who prescibred specific medications to help with back spasm and back pain. They recommend seeing you as an outpatient to consider surgical options to treat your chronic back pain. You also developed a urinary tract infection during your admission. This is being treated with a 3 day course of doxycycline antibiotic. You are being transferred to ___ for further psychiatric evaluation. There they will treat your depression and monitor your improvement.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with a history of DJD s/p multiple spinal surgeries, CEA c/b CVA on coumadin with residual weakness, admitted with acute on chronic lumbar spine pain, course complicated by pneumonitis and ___. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # Acute on chronic lumbar back pain- In the setting of acute onset of back pain with "pop" and point midline lumbar region tenderness on exam, her presentation was concerning for disc herniation versus vertebral compression fracture. No evidence of discopathy or acute vertebral compression fracture on CT or MRI spine. She was seen by neurosurgery who felt her pain was muscular in nature. She was started on long-acting Oxycontin and lidocaine patch in addition to her home Dilaudid po. # Sepsis / Aspiration PNA: On ___ she became febrile to 102, with tachycardia, hypoxia, and leukocytosis. Source thought to be secondary to aspiration while sedated in MRI the night prior, however CXR not convincing of discrete infiltrate. She was treated with Vanc/Zosyn for presumed aspiration PNA vs pneumonitis. Given her quick recovery on ___ and resolved WBC, antibiotics were discontinued. Of note, ACS was ruled out with troponins and EKG. # Respiratory Distress / Aspiration PNA: On ___ in setting of position change off the bed pan, she developed acute respiratory distress with hypoxia to ___ requiring ICU transfer. In the MICU, she was placed on bipap to stabilize her CO2 and ph. Labs showed leukocytosis and radiologic infiltrate consistent with PNA. She was treated with Vanc/Zosyn and narrowed to Augmentin to complete a 7 ___ course. She also received Solumedrol IV during this episode and then tapered down to her home prednisone dose. # Bacteruria: UA marginally positive with bacteria. Given suspicion for pulmonary cause, and lack of urinary symptoms, she was not started on antibiotics. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # h/o CVA/ Systemic Anticoagulation- patient on lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin. She was bridging with lovenox to coumadin after recent tenosynovectomy. During this admission her INRs were labile. On discharge she was subtherapeutic, sent home on warfarin and lovenox bridge. Next INR to be checked ___. # MDS / Macrocytic Anemia - diagnosed by 20q deletion, followed by Dr. ___. She was transfused 1 unit for HCT 20 and lethargy on ___. Her Hct improved appropriately. # Pulmonary fibrosis - chronic issue on home O2. She continued home inhalers and prednisone (although tapered up with respiratory distress as described above. # Depression/Mood: stable, continued on escitalopram, buproprion, amitryptyline. #HTN: stable on clonidine, metoprolol, amlodipine. #Diabetes: stable on home insulin regimen. #OSA: on 3L NC at night, does not use CPAP # s/p right ___ dorsal compartment tenosynovectomy- on ___. Follow up as previously scheduled with ortho. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - Home omeprazole was held given ___, can be restarted as ___ more likely prerenal than AIN. - Goal inr ___, will need to be bridged with lovenox - complete course of PO antibiotics for aspiration PNA - non-emergent abdominal US to eval aortic ectasia and non-emergent pelvic US to evaluate uterine lesion.
summary: You were admitted with acute on chronic lower back pain. Both CT and MRI imaging were reassuring. Most likely the pain you are experiencing is muscular in nature and you were treated with muscle relaxants and pain medication. While you were admitted, you had respiratory distress likely triggered by a pneumonia. We transferred you to the Medical ICU for non-invasive ventilation, IV antibiotics and close monitoring. You improved within one ___ and were stable to return to the general medicine floors. You will need to continue oral antibiotic treatments until ___. You also had poorly functioning kidneys because of dehydration at one point. This was resolved with IV fluid hydration. While you were in the hospital, your INR (Coumadin level) was difficult to control. Please make sure you have this checked on ___ or ___. Please continue lovenox until the level is correct.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pleasant ___ with CAD s/p MI, COPD/chronic bronchitis, HTN, PNA with last hospital stay about 1.5 months ago, who presented with confusion, found to have ___. I confirmed the history with his wife this morning. She explained that on the day of admission he worked extensively with ___, outside in the sun for the first time in months. She and her daughter then went out to run some errands, and before they left they told him to stay inside to avoid the warm temperatures, though he was dismissive. They came back after several hours and found him asleep in the sun, shirt off, somewhat sweaty, very warm. He was difficult to arouse, very somnolent. They became concerned about sunstroke and after calling PCP and cardiologist they took him to the ED. In our ED, he was febrile on presentation. He had a fairly complete infectious workup. He was given a dose of vanc/cefepime. He was also given 1L normal saline. Mental status improved quickly to baseline with IVF and a bit of time, but labs showed ___, so he was admitted for further evaluation. ___ night MD held his diuretic but continued all his other home medications (with exception of slightly lower dose of nightly Neurontin). His Cr was improved the morning after admission, and his mental status and functional status continued to be his usual baseline. I had a long discussion with his wife, and we decided to discharge him home but to hold his Torsemide for the next several days. I have given a prescription for laboratory studies to be drawn on ___, forwarded to Dr ___. I spoke to Dr ___ ___ to relate this plan, confirmed that he would tell Dr ___. Wife will call Dr ___ on ___ to get lab results and determine timing of Torsemide resumption. # Acute metabolic encephalopathy: resolved # ___: likely pre-renal due to diuretics, poor ___ exposure # Fever/leukocytosis: leukocytosis resolved; fever likely from sunstroke # ? chronic CHF: pt has mild ___ edema and gives vague hx of possible admission for HF exacerbation. Currently JVP is not elevated, CXR is clear, pt not requiring O2 and ___ is mild. BNP is elevated but ___ is unknown. Treatments as above. # COPD # Recurrent PNA: Followed by a pulmonologist. Has had several pneumonias in the past year, most recently 1.5 months ago. He just finished inhaled tobramycin course on ___. He works closely with ___ for continued respiratory clearance. # HLD: Continued home statin # Anemia: Stable at baseline per our available labs # GERD: Continued home medications # Back pain: Continued home acetaminophen, gabapentin at reduced dose given ___ # CAD: Continued home ASA, bblocker # Depression: Continued home citalopram # Gout: Continued home febuxostat Billing: >30 minutes spent coordinating discharge to home with services resumed
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with somnolence and confusion after having been out in the sun all day yesterday. You had an elevated creatinine level, which is a sign of acute kidney injury or kidney failure. Both your mental status and your kidney function improved with intravenous fluids. An infectious workup showed no signs of infection in either lungs, blood, or urine. Overall, the most likely explanation for your presentation was dehydration and perhaps mild sun-stroke. Your torsemide was held this admission, since it can cause dehydration. You should hold this medication until you speak with Dr ___ until you have a repeat creatinine level/kidney function test.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ was admitted to the ___ on ___ for HFpEF exacerbation as well as hematochezia. ACUTE ISSUES ============= #Acute on chronic HFpEF exacerbation: Peripheral and edema at rehabilitation likely due to inadequate outpatient diureses. CXR at admission was consistent with pulmonary vascular congestion and mild pulmonary edema. Patient was started on Lasix gtt at 20mg/hr with a goal of net negative ___. Strict I/Os were monitored with a Foley catheter. Daily standing weights were taken. Twice-a-day electrolytes were monitored for electrolyte abnormalities and repleted as needed. Lasix was held as needed for symptomatic hypotension on HD1. Hypotension resolved on HD2. Spironolactone 25mg BID was re-started on HD4 after resolution of hypotension. Metolazone 5mg PO daily was added on HD8 to improve diuresis for 1 day. The patient was transitioned to oral torsemide 80mg QD and spirolactone 25mg BID prior to discharge, which she tolerated well without signs of fluid accumulation. #Hematochezia: Most likely secondary to history of hemorrhoids and diverticulosis. Last outpatient colonoscopy in ___. Hb stable during admission at ___ from baseline of ___ in ___. GI was consulted and performed flexible sigmoidoscopy on ___ after resolution of HFpEF exacerbation. BID CBCs were monitored for active bleeding. Patient was given Colace and senna as needed to prevent constipation. Patient was given witch ___ pad for hemorrhoids. #Atrial fibrillation: Patient is s/p multiple cardioversions, AVJ ablation, and BiV pacer (___). Patient's biventricular pacing was adjusted by her home cardiologist (Dr. ___ as an inpatient. Home rivaroxaban was continued. #Complicated, catheter-associated urinary tract infection: Patient developed night sweats/chills on ___ and was found to have a likely urinary tract infection on urinalysis. Indwelling Foley catheter was removed. IV cefepime was started (day 1 = ___ for empiric Pseudomonas coverage. Urine cultures grew pan-sensitive E. coli, so antibiotics were switched to PO Ciprofloxacin 250mg q12hrs prior to discharge, with plans for a 10 day total course (last day ___ for catheter associated UTI. CHRONIC ISSUES =============== #IDDM: Home oral glipizide and sitagliptin were held. Glargine 28U was continued. Insulin sliding scale with TID FSBGs were used. #Chronic back pain: Continued home gabapentin, APAP, and lidocaine patch while inpatient. Her home APAP was uptitrated to 1000mg TID due to poor pain control, resulting in significant improvement in her pain. #GERD: Continued home ranitidine #Anxiety/depression: Continued home sertraline ==================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== NEW MEDICATIONS: [ ] Torsemide 80mg PO DAILY [ ] Add Torsemide 20mg PO QPM PRN: Weight gain > 2 lb, increased edema, or dyspnea [ ] Spironolactone 25mg PO BID FOLLOW-UP ITEMS: [ ] Complete 10-day course of ciprofloxacin for catheter-associated UTI (___) [ ] Please record daily standing weights and provide close outpatient monitoring of PO diuretic regimen. If weight increases > 2 lb, please give additional PRN torsemide dose of 20mg (in addition to standing torsemide 80mg; total of 100mg). [ ] Please call Dr. ___ weights increase > 2lb lb for further recommendations on diuretic regimen. [ ] Please continue 2L daily fluid restriction and 3g sodium restriction. [ ] ___ consider outpatient colonoscopy for further diagnostic work-up if continues to have hematochezia
summary: You came to the hospital because of worsening shortness of breath, leg swelling, and weight gain all related to worsening of your congestive heart failure. You were given IV furosemide (Lasix), spironolactone, and metolazone to help remove extra fluid that caused your shortness of breath, leg swelling, and weight gain. Your pacemaker rate was adjusted by your home cardiologist. You were seen by our inpatient gastroenterology service for bright red blood in your stool. They performed a flexible sigmoidoscopy which showed hemorrhoids, however no evidence of active bleeding. You were diagnosed with a urinary tract infection. You were given IV antibiotics to treat this infection, which were switched to oral antibiotics before you went home. You received a nuclear medicine study ("stress test") to assess the blood flow to your heart. This test was normal.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ============= SUMMARY ============= ___ year old gentleman with history of alcohol abuse, former IV drug abuse, Hep C/EtOH cirrhosis untreated with undetectable viral load (complicated by ascites/grade II esophageal varices) , and depression who was transferred from ___ after experiencing a one day history of diffuse rash starting on his feet that progressed towards his abdomen/groin, associated with chills and mild night sweats, with physical exam consistent with small vessel vasculitis. ============== ACUTE ISSUES ============== # Rash: He was evaluated by Dermatology who performed a biopsy of a lesion on the left thigh. They believed this was secondary to a small vessel vasculitis and prescribed triamcinolone 0.1% ointment. During hospitalization, HIV serology was negative. RPR pending at the time of discharge. HCV viral load was pending. Hepatitis B panel revaled negative HBsAg, HBsAb positive but HBcAb was pending at the time of discharge. C3, C4, and rheumatoid factor were pending at discharge. Cryoglobulins were also pending at discharge. #EtOH/Hepatitis C Cirrhosis: complicated by ascites and esophageal varices (grade II varices seen in ___. Last drink of EtOH 2 weeks prior to presentation. Continued all home medications, thiamine, folate, MVI for this patient. # Depression / anxiety: Continued home desvenlafaxine. # r/o DVT: LENIs were performed that showed no DVT. =================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== -Patient is unclear as to the medications he was on previously. He was unsure if he was on nadolol as an outpatient. He does have a history of grade II varices and was re-started on nadolol 40 mg daily while inpatient. He tolerated this well. -Patient is unsure if he took spironolactone at home. This should be re-evaluated as outpatient to assess need for spironolactone. -Encourage use of lactulose. Patient states he is not taking the lactulose at home. -___ benefit from medication counseling as an outpatient. -Recent alcohol use within 2 weeks of hospitalization. ___ benefit from social work for relapse prevention. -Patinet will be on triamcinolone 0.1% ointment BID. He should only be taking the medication in two week intervals (2 weeks on, followed by 2 weeks off). -Patient was noted to have hematuria during hospitalization. Please obtain repeat UA as outpatient to assess for resolution of the hematuria. -Patient will need left thigh suture removed in two weeks (around ___. He will need to follow up with PCP or ___ to have this removed. -Patient will need PCP, ___ follow up as an outpatient. -Code Status: Full Code (confirmed) -Contact Information: ___ (mother): ___.
summary: You were admitted to ___ due to a rash on your legs. You were seen by the Dermatologists who performed a biopsy of the rash. You also underwent further testing to assess the cause of the rash. To treat the rash, you were started on an ointment called triamcinolone. Please apply the triamcinolone ointment EVERY 12 HOURS to the affected areas. Please only take this medication for two weeks with end date on ___. Please then do not take the triamcinolone for two weeks. The pattern of triamcinolone administration should be to apply to the affected areas for a maximum of 2 weeks on followed by 2 weeks off. PLEASE AVOID THE FACE AND GROIN WHEN ADMINISTERING THIS MEDICAITON AS OVERUSE OF THE MEDICATION CAN LEAD TO SKIN THINNING AND SKIN DAMAGE.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year-old man with a history of chronic abdominal pain, s/p partial hepatic lobectomy due to a right sided hepatic biliary stricture in ___, depression, and hypertension, who presented with acute worsening of his abdominal pain. Upon presentation, the patient was found to have a serum acetaminophen level of 76 and a urine tox positive for cocaine. He was admitted for management of his abdominal pain and potential acetaminophen toxicity from ___. His hospital course, in brief, is as follows. 1. Abdominal pain: He has chronic abdominal pain. Here he had normal lipase and CXR, CT abdomen & pelvis negative for an acute hepatic, pancreatic, or biliary process, normal UA, negative cardiac biomarkers, and unchanged ECG. Prior extensive work up with Dr. ___ no clear etiology. He was managed conservatively with bowel rest, fluids, and small doses of morphine. His pain improved, and he a regular diet prior to discharge. He will continue his home regimen of Ursodiol, Creon, senna, and colace and MS ___, and will follow up with his PCP and with Dr. ___. 2. Transaminitis / Acetaminophen Toxicity: Pt with report of significant tylenol and acetaminophen containing pain med intake for abd pain. ALT 85 on admit rose to 158, AST 174, and LDH at 276. Normal INR, TBili, Albumin. Serum acetaminophen level of 76. Toxicology was consulted in the ED, and the patient received N-acetylcysteine four-bag protocol. His acetaminophen level became undetectable during the treatment, and his LFTs began to normalize. He will follow up with his PCP to ___ his LFTs. 3. Cocaine use: the patient's urine tox screen was positive for cocaine. The patient denied any recent drug use. Social work was consulted and offered support and information. The patient has an uncoming appointment with a psychiatrist. 4. Leukopenia: the patient's WBC dropped to 3.4 during his hospital stay. No clear etiology was discerned. According to the patient, this is a long standing problem, and he will follow up with his PCP for ___ repeat CBC and for further work up. The patient's following medical problems remained stable during his hospital stay. 1. Depression: continued outpatient regimen of Venlafaxine 2. Back pain: continued outpatient regimen of Baclofen 3. Sinusitis: continued outpatient regimen of Flonase
summary: You were admitted to ___ for abdominal pain. A CT scan of your abdomen was normal. Your liver function tests were slightly elevated, and the level of Tylenol, or acetaminophen, in your blood was also high. Your liver function tests are improving, but should be rechecked with your primary care doctor. Your urine showed evidence of cocaine use. Your abdominal pain was treated with small doses of pain medication, fluids, and bowel rest. Your were also treated with medication for Tylenol overdose. You should not take any pain medication that includes Tylenol, or acetaminophen. This includes the Percocet you were taking prior to admission.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH T2DM, dCHF, tracheobronchomalacia, peripheral neuropathy, OSA, and obesity who presents from clinic with RLE redness, cough, and SOB, diagnosed with RLE cellulitis and started on seven day course of oral antibiotics. ACTIVE ISSUES: #) Cellulitis: Likely related to diabetes and peripheral neuropathy. Pt has RLE erythema, swelling and warmth consistent with cellulitis. There is also a similar smaller area on the left medial shin. Given the superficial nature of the infection, was started on coverage for MSSA and MRSA with keflex and bactrim (already received vanc and levo x1 in the ED) for seven days. ___ negative for DVT. Vascular was consulted, who debrided the wound minimally at bedside. She was seen by wound care as well, who made the recommendations listed below. Last day of antibiotic course is ___. #) Cough: 3 days duration, no fever or chills along with URI type symptoms and CXR negative for PNA make URI or viral bronchitis most likely. Received one dose of levaqin in ED. Did not continue antibiotics. She was discharged with albuterol neb and prn cough meds. #) SOB: Pt has dyspnea at baseline which has been thoroughly worked up by outpatient cardiologist and pulmonologist. Found to have dCHF and moderate obstructive defect. Also has diagnosis of tracheobronchomalacia. CXR showed low lung volumes. Appeared euvolemic to dry on exam. Most of her sx have been attributed to obesity. Her lasix dose has been down titrated due to frequent falls and orthostatic symptoms. Not currently on home inhalers. Possibly exacerbated now by her URI. We continued her home dose of lasix and nebs as above. #) Frequent falls: Mentioned in many outpatient notes, thought to be attributed to diabetic neuropathy. Complains of orthostatic sx and appears dry on exam; however, orthostatics were negative. Possibly some autonomic component secondary to her diabetes as well as deconditioning from obesity and inactivity. Uses a walker at home. ___ was consulted, who recommended ___ rehab. She was continued on her home dose of gabapentin for diabetic neuropathy. #) T2DM: On insulin pump and victoza injections at home. Her PCP, ___, is her endocrinologist. While in house, the pt experienced multiple low fingersticks in the ___ which improved with juice and crackers. The ___ team was consulted, who recommended decreasing the basal rate on her insulin pump. At discharge, her rate was 1.0 during the day and 0.8 overnight. CHRONIC ISSUES: #) Diastolic CHF: Followed by cardiology as an outpatient. Found to have dCHF without coronary disease. On spironolactone and lasix at home. Appeared euvolemic to dry on exam. We continued her home aldactone and lasix doses. #) OSA: continued on home CPAP and 2L O2 at night. #) Depression: continued on home clonazepam, bupropion, venlafaxine #) LUTS: on vesicare at home. held in house as nonformulary. can restart at discharge. #) HLD: continued on home omega3 and simvastatin #) Bone health: continued on home vit D and ca Transitional Issues: #WOUND CARE RECOMMENDATIONS: Pressure relief per pressure ulcer guidelines Support surface: Atmos Air Turn and reposition every ___ hours and prn Heels off bed surface at all times Elevate ___ while sitting. Moisturize B/L ___, periwound tissue and feet BID with Aloe Vesta Moisture Barrier Ointment. Commercial wound cleanser or normal saline to cleanse all wounds. Pat the tissue dry with dry gauze. Apply moisture barrier ointment to the periwound tissue with each drg change. Support nutrition and hydration. #ANTIBIOTIC REGIMEN: Keflex and Bactrim x 7 days (end date ___ #DIABETES - INSULIN PUMP: If patient continues to have hypoglycemia, decrease her basal insulin rate by 0.1-0.2
summary: were admitted for a skin infection of your right leg called cellulitisYou were started on antibiotics, and your infection began to improve. The vascular team came by and debrided your woundYou will need to complete a seven day course of antibiotics. ___ also came in with a cough, which was felt to be an upper respiratory tract infection. ___ did not have signs of pneumonia. We have prescribed an inhaler to help with your breathing and cough medicines as needed while the infection resolves. Lastly, you were seen by the diabetes specialists who adjusted your insulin pump basal rate. Please continue this decreased rate and only bolus if your fingersticks are above 150. If ___ continue to have problems, please decrease your basal rate by 0.1-0.2 and contact your endocrinologist Dr. ___ at the number below. He would like to see ___ in clinic on ___ or ___ of next week. Physical therapists have evaluated ___. We feel that with your frequent falls, it would be safest for ___ to attend inpatient physical rehabilitation before going home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: 1. Liver cirrhosis secondary to EtOH use - Has had past complications of hepatorenal syndrome, refractory ascites, and is status post TIPS; has MELD score of 15 on admission. (A) Mental Status Changes: Presented with encephalopathy; pain medications were discontinued. Worsening encephalopathy was attribute to Ms ___ refusal of lactulose at the rehab facility as well as pulling out her feeding tube. Tube was replaced during hospitalization with ___ guidance. Lactulose was restarted with improvement in mental status. Rifaximin was continued. Low dose tramadol was started for pain control which she tolerated well. Morphine IV was used in the short term for pain control prior to mobilizing patient; this was also tolerated well. Higher doses of opiates tend to worsen Ms. ___ confusion, so we were reluctant to increase her pain control further. We would encourage titrating up pain medications as necessary in the rehab facility to encourage mobility with careful monitoring of mental status. . (B) Refractory Ascites - Diuretics were initially held given acute on chronic renal failure; following discontinuation of bactrim (was started for UTI) and nutritional supplementation, renal failure improved to her baseline of 1.2 - 1.4. Diuretics were restarted as per home regimen and her creatinine tolerated this in above range. On day of discharge, we performed a therapeutic paracentesis and we were able to remove a total of 2 liters. . (C) Nutrition - Feeding tube was replaced and tube feeds were continued. Bridle was placed to secure tube. Ensure supplementation was encouraged. . 2. Acute Renal Failure: Thought to be secondary to poor PO intake and reduced tube feed intake, along with bactrim initiated on ___ for E Coli in urine. Admission creatinine was 1.5. Bactrim was held and tube feeds initiated. Diuretics were initially held and renal function recovered to near 1.2. Diuretics were reinitiated Her urinary tract infection was treated with meropenem for a 7 day course, which was completed on ___. . 3. Chronic pain: Pt with difficult to manage pain and pubic ramus fracture. Returns with pain, inability to ambulate and depression. Has mydriasis and AMS, possibly related to anticholinergic toxicity. An ekg was not taken during AMS; during resolved MS, QRS normal. Initially, morphine was used ___ mg q8 hrs prior to mobility, since mobility seemed to precipitate her pain; following resolution of her mental status, she was started on low dose tramadol 25 mg BID. She appeared to tolerate this; we continued her tramadol without seeing any deterioration of her mental status. We recommend using morphine cautiously as necessary prior to mobility as she still appears to continue to be in a lot of pain during movement. . 4. Chronic Diastolic Heart Failure: no evidence of pulm edema or orthopnea during hospitalization. Following return of her creatinine to baseline, we restarted her diuretics and she maintained her creatinine at baseline. . 5. Depression: We continued citalopram 20mg daily. . 6. Dispo: Following improvement in her mental status, resolution of her acute renal failure, re-initiation of her diuretics, and a therapeutic paracentesis, she was discharged on ___, saturating well on room air with creatinine of 1.3. Ambulation limited by pain.
summary: You were admitted from the rehabilitation center because of worsening confusion. Most likely, this happened because you were not taking lactulose as you should. Taking lactulose, although causing the unpleasant side effect of loose stools, is really important to keep your mental status clear. Keeping your mental status clear is an important step forward. You should continue to take lactulose such that you have ___ bowel movements per day. While you were here you also had a urinary tract infection which we treated using antibiotics. Your renal function improved while you were here. Given your recent fall, you've had considerable pain that has been tricky to manage, because pain medications can sometimes make you confused. We have started a low dose pain medication which should help some of your pain. You may need additional pain medication at the rehab to help you through your pain, especially when you are moving around. Nutrition continues to be an important goal for you to meet; we replaced your feeding tube while you probably accidentally pulled out because of your confusion. You should continue to get tube feeds while at the rehab as well as taking regular ensure supplementation with meals. Nutritional supplementation is important to get you strong enough for the future possibility of transplant.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of DMII and obesity who presents with right flank and abdominal pain. # Right flank and abdominal pain: Flank/abdominal pain, tachycardia, leukocytosis, dysuria, pyruia, and urinary frequency are all sugegstive of urinary tract infection, possibly pyelonephritis. Nephrolithiasis was ruled out with CT abdomen/pelvis. Pt. was started on ceftriaxone and then switched to cipro(given hx of pan-sensitive E. coli) on discharge to complete a 10 day course. Given her hematuria, she was also scheduled with urology for cystoscopy. # Multifocal ureteral thickenings: Incidental finding on CT scan. Per radiology read, may be multifocal ureteritis or multifocal urothelial malignancy. Case discussed with urology who recommended outpatient ___ for cystoscopy (given hematuria) and possible direct visualization and biopsy of abnormal regions. Appointment scheduled for ___. # Tachycardia: Resolved with significant amounts of IVF. # IDDM2: Most recent HBA1c 11.2%. Hyperglycemia during this admission likely due to a combination of infection and poorly controlled glucose levels at baseline. Pt. was discharged with PCP ___ for further uptitration of her insulin. # Constipation: Pt. was significantly constipated on admission, which likely contributed to her abdominal pain. She was started on an aggressive bowel regimen with good effect. # Depression: Pt. was continued on home bupropion. # Primary MI prevention: Pt. was continued on home ASA. # Transitional issues - hematuria warrents evaluation by urology with cystoscopy - bilateral multifocal ureteral thickening requires further evaluation by urology - glucose levels very high during this admission; will likely require further uptitration of insulin levels - consider restarting ACEi and statin as outpatient,patient reported she had not been taking these medications
summary: You were admitted due to back and abdominal pain. We were concerned about a urinary tract infection and started you on antibiotics. We also rehydrated you with intravenous fluids. We looked at your urine and there were some red blood cells. A CT scan showed no kidney stones, but did show some thickening of the tubes that run from your kidney to your bladder. We discharged you home to complete a 10 day course of antibiotics (END DAY ___. It is VERY important that you ___ with urology within ___ weeks for evaluation of the blood in your urine and thickened areas on your ureters. PLEASE CALL ___. ___ OFFICE TO SCHEDULE AN APPOINTEMENT. Please also ___ with your primary care physician for further management of your insulin as your glucose levels were high during this admission. You should also discuss restarting lisinopril and simvastatin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMHx of recurrent nephrolithiasis, DM II (previously insulin dependent, now oral agents s/p gastric bypass, c/b neprhopathy and charcot joints), HTN, hypothyroidism, recent admission ___ for klebsiella urosepsis requiring ICU management/intubation for ARDS (s/p nephrostomy; completed 14d course of meropenem on ___, who presents with 3 hours of chills. Found to have UTI. # UTI: chronic nephrolithiasis with documented UTI. Recent hospitalization for klebsiella urosepsis, requiring nephrostomy, complicated by septic shock and ARDS requiring intubation. Patient febrile at home to 100.2. Tm in ED 99.5. WBC 5.9. Patient did not have dysuria (consistent with past UTIs), but given recent history of urosepsis, patient was admitted and treated with IV ceftriaxone. Afebrile through rest of admission and HD stable. Home tamsulosin and allopurinol were continued. Urine cx sensitivities returned Klebsiella Pneumoniae sensitive to Bactrim, so patient was transitioned to Bactrim as outpatient. Given nephrostomy and recent urosepsis admission, will treat as complicated UTI for total of 14 days (2 days inpatient, 12 days outpatient), and will follow up with Urology PRIOR to cessation of this antibiotic course for further plan re: PCNT, stone, etc. (confirmed with pt). Blood culture to be followed-up. # DM c/b Charcot feet: Was previously insulin dependent, but after gastric bypass, DM treated by Metformin. Severe neuropathy associated with DM, resulting in Charcot feet and chronic ___ ulcers. Chronic pain and decreased weight bearing abilities. Followed closely by podiatry. For DM control, metformin held and patient put on insulin sliding scale. For neuropathic pain, patient encouraged to continue weight bearing activity as tolerated; was prescribed lyrica 200mg TID and oxycodone 5mg q4h PRN. # Pressure ulcer from last admission: on gluteal fold. No signs of infection. Ulcers were continued to be covered while in hospital; patient encouraged to frequently get out of bed to chair as tolerated. # Depression: Fluoxetine 40mg daily was continued # HTN: for history of hypertension, but taken off lisinopril during last hospitalization due to septic shock. Was not been restarted on med. TRANSITIONAL CARE ISSUES: ============================= - Continue Bactrim treatment - Follow blood culture - Urology to determine plan to manage R nephrostomy tube and stone - Podiatry to follow-up foot Charcot feet and ___ ulcers
summary: You were admitted here at ___ on ___ for 3-hours of chills and were found to have an UTI. It was good that you came in early, especially in the context of your recent hospitalization for urosepsis that involved ICU stay. Here, you were treated with IV ceftriaxone. On discharge, you no longer have fever or chills, and have not had burning on urination or increase in urinary frequency. Urinary culture revealed a bacteria, Klebsiella Pneumonia, sensitive to Bactrim. You will continue Bactrim after returning home for 12 days. Please call your urologist tomorrow to come up with a plan regarding your known kidney stone and nephrostomy tube.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is an ___ woman with h/o neuroendocine tumor/carcinoid tumor metastatic to the liver presenting from clinic with failure to thrive. Failure to thrive: Per the patient's daughter and outside providers, she has had worsening fatigue and poor appetite of unclear etiology. Diffential diagnosis includes infection, malignancy. VS were all stable. Work-up initiated in clinic with CXR revealing evidence of PNA (no symptoms). She had evidence of ___ and hyponatremia on her labs likely due to poor po intake, and both resolved with IV fluids. UA suggestive of possible UTI, but cultures negative. Blood cultures with no growth. Anemia workup showed iron deficiency. Pt was treated with pneumonia with five day course of levaquin. She had a nutrition consult for poor po intake, and she was given liquid supplements with meals and started on iron and B12 supplementation. ___ evaluated her, and she was able to walk around the nursing unit easily with nursing supervision, discharged to home with ___. She was started on mirtazapine for any possible depression and to increase appetite, however these effects will take ___. While she was here, she also had workup to assess the status of her neuroendocrine tumor. An MRI of the abdomen showed slight interval increase in size of hepatic mass. She was started on octreotide sc TID with no side effects. Due to insurance issues, she could not be discharged with subcutaneous octreotide. Per discussion with her outpatient providers, this was to be arranged through her oncologist's office as an outpatient. Lastly, vitamin levels were drawn to assess for deficiency possibly causing poor functional status. These were pending at time of discharge. Ultimately, the patient appeared to be doing fairly well functionally by discharge with good po intake. Further monitoring should be managed as an outpatient. HTN: Remained normotensive to hypertensive. Home benicar held at admission due to ___. Clonidine continued. Benicar restarted on discharge. Transitional issues: CODE: confirmed full EMERGENCY CONTACT: ___ ___ ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ for malaise and general fatigue/poor appetite. We did several studies for possible infection. Your chest X-ray showed a possible pneumonia, and we treated this with antibioticsYou were also started on dietary supplements and an appetite-stimulating medication at bed time to help ___ feel better. Your oncologist, Dr. ___ starting octreotide while in the hospital to treat any possible effects of your cancer. Your symptoms improved, and we now feel it is safe for ___ to leave the hospital.
