Please continue antibiotics (levofloxacin) for one more day after discharge. Your octreotide unfortunately could not be arranged by the hospital while you were here. Dr. ___ ___ will help ___ arrange for this medication as an outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ right-handed gentleman with PMH of DMII, HTN, HLD, admitted from the ___ resident clinic for workup of what was suspected to be a rapidly progressive dementia. He recently came to the clinic by himself for complainants of memory problems, which he stated started about ___ months ago, and are progressively worsening. This admission, collateral information is available from his wife, and it appears that his problems have been going on for at least a couple of years, although they have certainly become more noticeable recently. Per history, the majority of his problems relate to memory and more complex executive functions (e.g., arranging items in his store), although there may have been some personality change as well (e.g., crying in front of his family). Depression is certainly present, and emotionality in response to some current psychosocial stressors. On mental status exam, he was significantly impaired in attention, executive domains, and memory, although there appears to be some mild aphasia (low-frequency naming impaired, i.e., difficulty with accessing the lexicon) and apraxia as well. The remainder of the neurologic exam is normal without evidence of laterality, motor neuron disease or Parkinsonism. Overall, his presentation is most compatible with a cortical dementia of the Alzheimer's type, of relatively young onset. There is a low suspicion that this is truly a rapidly progressive dementia such as CJD. He was be admitted to the neurology service for accelerated workup. The following were obtained: MRI brain (no acute abnormality, EEG (extended routine - normal study), labs to screen for reversible causes of dementia: CMP (showing mild bilirubin elevation only: TBili 1.9, DBili 0.4), CBC (showing mild microcytic anemia with Hgb 12, Hct 36.8, MCV 65, MCH 21.3, MCHC 32.5, RDW 15.2), normal iron studies & haptoglobin, CRP (0.6), ESR (4), ___ (POSITIVE), RPR (nonreactive), HIV (neg), Lyme (neg), ___ (neg), thyroid function (wnl), anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin (wnl), B12/folate (wnl). The majority of these were pending at the time of discharge. An attempt was made to contact pt and his PCP when ___ returned positive. Home medications were continued, and sertraline was ordered for depression. Outpatient neurolopsychological testing will be arranged (pt was given number to schedule). Pt should follow up in neurology clinic with Drs. ___ or ___
summary: You were admitted for work-up of memory difficulties. These difficulties appear to have been occuring for up to ___ years and not just over the last few months. You had an MRI of your ___, which did not show any abnormalities. We also obtained a number of other labs, which were either normal, or are still pending at the time of discharge. We obtained an EEG, which is still pending. You appear to be depressed, which can contribute to memory difficulties. We are starting treatment for the depression and will see if your memory improves with this. We would also like you to get neuropsychological testing, which is a more detailed evaluation to determine the exact cognitive domains in which you are having difficulty.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old ___ female with a history of AVNRT s/p ablation, HTN, HLD, depression who presented with palpiations and chest heaviness secondary to a pulmonary embolus. ACTIVE PROBLEMS: 1. PULMONARY EMBOLUS: Her palpitations and subxiphoid heaviness were caused by a right lower lobe segmental pulmonary embolus that was likely precipitated by excessive bed rest over the past several weeks. She lacked other PE/DVT risk factors such as trauma, surgery, HRT, smoking, or personal/family history of clotting. She is up to date with age-appropriate cancer screening. There was no sign of right heart strain on EKG. She remained hemodynamically stable. She was started on a heparin drip in the ED and was then placed on subcutaneous lovenox the following morning on the medicine floor. She began coumadin that day and was later discharged with a lovenox bridge, and will continue anticoagulation for ___ months. She received lovenox teaching with the aid of her son who interpreted, and communicated understanding. The need for regular activity was stressed to avoid stasis and further clotting. 2. PALPITATIONS/TACHYCARDIA: the patient has a long-standing history of palpitations which have recently become more frequent. Several episodes of brief tachycardia lasting minutes were captured on telemetry with rates achieving 150s at rest. While an EKG was not obtained at the time, telemetry suggested a possible atrial tachycardia which may explain her symptoms. Her toprol XL was uptitrated to 50mg daily. Electrophysiology follow-up was recommended. 3. ANXIETY/DEPRESSION: She has become increasingly distressed by her palpitations and had essentially restricted herself to bedrest to avoid further episodes. She was continued on venlafaxine and reassured at length that normal activity is safe and essential. After confirming with her PCP, she was given a trial course of ativan to help control worsening anxiety. INACTIVE PROBLEMS: 4. HYPERTENSION: She was modestly hypertensive in house, and was continued on her home antihypertensives. PENDING TESTS AT DISCHARGE: NONE TRANSITIONAL CARE ISSUES: - will need EP follow-up of possible aberrant atrial tachycardia and palpitations - will need monitoring of INR for ___ months of anticoagulation
summary: You were admitted to the hospital due to palpitations. You were found to have a blood clot in your right lung (pulmonary embolus). You were started on blood thinners to treat this clot. You are being started on a pill called Coumadin that you will need to take for at least ___ months. You will need to have bloodwork checked frequently in order to make sure that your Coumadin dose is appropriate. Since the Coumadin takes several days to work, you also need to take Lovenox (another blood thinner given as a subcutaneous shot) for the next several days. Please continue this medication until your primary care doctor tells you to stop. In addition, you were found to have an intermittent fast heart rate and your metoprolol dose was increased.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with history of seizures, HTN, HLD, DM, CAD s/p CABG, presenting with ___ weeks of diarrhea and report of falls at home. # Diarrhea: Resolved. She reported worsening loose stools for ___ weeks. However she had no bowel movements during her hospital stay, with the exception of one very small stool which she said was not saved. She was subsequently restarted on a bowel regimen for ongoing constipation (which has been her baseline since starting the fentanyl patch). Differential diagnosis for her history of diarrhea includes gabapentin drug effect, IBS, viral infection. Unlikely to be new-onset IBD (given questionable history of blood in stools). Colon cancer or divertiulitis unlikely given recent colonoscopy with single sessile 8mm polyp of benign appearance. Narcotic withdrawal unlikely as patient reports stable dose of Fentanyl patch, and no other narcotic use recently. She is s/p appendectomy and cholecystectomy, with no nausea/vomiting to suggest partial bowel obstruction. No recent dietary changes to suggest new presentation of lactose intolerance or Celiac disease. We were unable to obtain a stool sample to pursue continued workup in the inpatient setting. # Back pain: She had back pain on the morning of ___ which woke her from sleep. The pain radiated across her back and left shoulder, and she reported never having this type of pain before. Cardiac workup including EKG and troponin did not reveal evidence of cardiac ischemia. Amylase/lipase and AST/ALT/Tbili were in the normal range. Her back pain was felt to be most likely musculoskeletal in the setting of her recent fall. # Weakness: ESR was mildly elevated but not into the range typically concerning for polymyalgia rheumatica. CK normal. This symptom is most likely related to her known autonomic dysfunction. She was evaluated by Physical Therapy and found to be at high fall risk. Rehabilitation was recommended, to which patient agreed. # UTI: She had marginal Dipstick/UA but did report urinary frequency. She received ciprofloxacin x3 days (first day was ___ for empiric treatment of UTI. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Ankle swelling: Chronic. She had ECHO on ___ with elongated left atrium, normal left ventricular size, LVEF>55%. Abnormal septal motion/position, moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension were noted. Her albumin is normal, total protein very mildly low (6.3). DVT was thought to be unlikely given edema is bilateral. # Postural instability: She has known autonomic dysfunction followed by neurology. She did have history of recent fall, but did not hurt her head. # T2DM: On bedtime 25 glargine and humalog sliding scale at home. Poorly controlled at baseline. She was seen by ___ here, and subsequently we increased her nighttime glargine and sliding scale. # Chronic pain: - Continued Fentanyl Patch 100 mcg/h TD Q72H # Dementia: - Continued Memantine 5 mg PO BID - Continued Donepezil 10 mg PO HS # Depression: - Continued Sertraline 200 mg PO DAILY # Seizure disorder: - Continued Gabapentin 300 mg PO BID # CAD: - Continued Isosorbide Mononitrate (Extended Release) 30 mg PO DAILY - Continued Aspirin, Atorvastatin, Clopidogrel # GERD: - Received Omeprazole 40 mg daily (on esomeprazole at home) # Glaucoma: - Continued Travatan Z (travoprost) 0.004 % ophthalmic QPM to left eye - Continued eye drops TRANSITION ISSUES: - She had no episodes of diarrhea during her admission. If this problem recurs in the outpatient setting she can have outpatient diarrhea workup. - Continue neurology outpatient follow-up for leg weakness. - Metoprolol was held at last admission and continued to be held given history of falls and dizziness. She may need to be restarted on this medication if appropriate in the outpatient setting.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital due to diarrhea. You did not have any diarrhea in the hospital, but you did experience back pain which was new for you. We underwent workup including cardiac enzymes, EKGs and pancreatic/liver enzymes, which did not reveal any evidence that dysfunction of the heart, pancreas or liver was causing your pain. Your pain improved over the course of your admission. You were also found to have a possible urinary tract infection, and you were treated with 3 days of antibiotics. You were also seen by the ___ diabetes specialists who made recommendations regarding your hospital insulin regimen. Because you were found to be unsteady on your feet with a high fall risk, rehabilitation was recommended and you agreed. We hope that their exercises will improve your strength and stability.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o male with history of DM, prior cellulitis and abscess of buttocks who presented with a painful groin rash x 2 days. ACTIVE ISSUES: ============== # Folliculitis, groin: Patient has had a history of prior cellulitis, including recent hospitalization for MSSA abscess and cellulitis. Clinical appearance consistent with bacterial folliculitis. No evidence of fluctuance or abscess amenable to I&D. Pt had already self-drained pustules prior to arrival. Likely chronically colonized and is at high risk for recurrence given underlying poorly controlled diabetes. Initially treated with IV antibiotics in the ED, then transitioned to Bactrim/Keflex with plan for 7 day course (___). Pt was reluctant to switch to oral antibiotics given his previous infections requiring hospitalization and IV antibiotics, however he agreed after extensive counseling. He was also prescribed mupirocin ointment for topical application to the affected area. He was instructed regarding decolonization techniques with chlorhexidine washes prior to discharge. He can use warm compresses for comfort and should avoid shaving in this area. # ___: Patient with creatinine 1.4 upon admission, elevated from baseline of ___. Pt was treated with IVF bolus. Cr downtrended to 1.0 on the day of discharge suggesting possibly pre-renal etiology. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # Left shoulder pain: Pt reported pain has been present for the last month. His exam on admission was consistent with rotator cuff inflammation or impingement. He is also at risk for frozen shoulder given his uncontrolled DM. Lidocaine patch was continued. He will need further work-up as an outpatient. # Diabetes Mellitus Type II: Patient has had long standing diabetes, complicated with peripheral neuropathy. A1c 8.8% in ___. He follows with ___ as an outpatient. He was continued on his home Lantus. Metformin was held and he was put on an insulin sliding scale. # Diabetic neuropathy: Continued home gabapentin, morphine sulfate oral solution. It was unclear why he is taking morphine for this indication. He received IV morphine while in the ED. Should discuss alternative medications for symptom control in the outpatient setting. # Anemia: Chronic anemia dating back to ___ per records. Hgb within baseline range at 12 during this admission. # Hypertension: Continued home olmesartan, nifedipine, clonidine. # Depression: Continued home citalopram. # Anxiety: Continued home lorazepam. # Glaucoma: Contiuned home timolol eyedrops. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== # Complete 7 day course of Bactrim/Keflex (___) # Topical mupirocin ointment to area BID x 7 days # Daily chlorhexidine bath x 7 days # Warm compresses for comfort # Consider outpatient Dermatology appointment if symptoms persist # Further evaluation and management of shoulder pain # CODE: Full # CONTACT: wife ___ (___) ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Citalopram 40 mg PO EVERY EVENING 2. CloniDINE 0.2 mg PO BID 3. Gabapentin 900 mg PO TID 4. MetFORMIN (Glucophage) 1000 mg PO BID 5. NIFEdipine CR 60 mg PO DAILY 6. olmesartan 40 mg Oral every morning 7. Timolol Maleate 0.5% 1 DROP RIGHT EYE BID 8. Lorazepam 1 mg PO Q8H:PRN anxiety 9. Biotene Dry Mouth (lactoperoxi-gluc oxid-pot thio) 1 gum Mucous Membrane q4h:prn dry mouth 10. Glargine 40 Units Bedtime Insulin SC Sliding Scale using REG Insulin 11. Morphine Sulfate (Oral Soln.) 2.5-5 mg PO QHS:PRN pain
summary: You were admitted to ___ because of your skin rash. You were diagnosed with folliculitis (infected hair follicle) and started on antibiotics. There was no evidence of deeper infection that would require drainage. You will need to complete your course of antibiotics at home. You can apply warm compresses to the area for comfort. Avoid shaving in this area. Because this rash has returned several times we recommend that you treat the area with topical therapy to decrease the number of bacteria on your skin. To do this please apply mupirocin ointment to the affected area twice daily for the next week. Please also use daily chlorhexidine washes for the next week.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ year-old woman with a long and complicated history of breast cancer now metastatic to the calvaria s/p local radiation, on letrozole who presented with new word finding difficulties and finding of new L frontal extra-axial lesion with associated L frontal edema. She was evaluated by a multidisciplinary team inpatient consisting of oncology, radiation oncology, and neurosurgery with MRI, CTA, and functional MRI of the brain as well as a re-staging CT torso, with no definitive treatment decisions at discharge. ACUTE ISSUES: #HEADACHE: #METASTATIC BREAST CANCER: #EXTRA-AXIAL LEFT FRONTAL DISEASE WITH ASSOCIATED VASOGENIC EDEMA: Patient presented with new word finding difficulties, noted to have new area of vasogenic edema associated with worsening, compressing extra-axial disease in the left frontal lobe. The patient was given a 10 mg dexamethasone load in the ED and then started on 4 mg Q6H. Her headache and word finding difficulties improved. She also reported transient right upper extremity weakness prior to admission which was no longer present in the hospital. She was evaluated by oncology, rad-onc, and neurosurgery with MRI, CTA, and functional MRI of the brain as well as a re-staging CT torso. CT torso revealed no progression of disease in those areas. fMRI was not read at discharge and was difficult to interpret from neurosurgery's perspective given all of the swelling. There was not a final treatment plan at discharge, though the patient had spoken with Dr. ___ with neurosurgery who had explained to them the benefits and risks of neurosurgery. CHRONIC ISSUES: #DEPRESSION AND ANXIETY: - Continued home citalopram and lorazepam #URINARY INCONTINENCE: - Continued home mirabegron #OSTEOPOROSIS: - Continued home alendronate TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [ ] Follow up headaches [ ] Continue treatment planning/discussion [ ] Taper dexamethasone when felt to be appropriate
summary: You were admitted to ___ because you were having trouble expressing words and right arm weakness. Imaging scans were done of your brain that revealed the known brain lesion with new swelling inside the skull. To help the swelling you were given steroids, and to prevent seizures you were given a medication called Keppra. You were evaluated by a team of different doctors that included ___ medicine doctors, oncologists, radiation oncologists, and neurosurgeons. A functional MRI was done to see which parts of your brain you use most, although it was difficult to read because of all the swelling. You met with the neurosurgeons and discussed surgery options. There is no definitive plan right now at discharge, and the conversation will continue with them and your oncologist Dr. ___ in the coming days regarding what you would like to do. You also had imaging done of your chest, abdomen and pelvis which showed that there was no new disease in those areas.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo male with history of cholangioCa who is admitted with hyponatremia, found to have MDR E.coli UTI and bacteremia. Also developed C. diff diarrhea and generalized weakness/deconditioning. # MDR E Coli UTI w/ bacteremia # Port infection - blood and urine cx from admission + MDR Ecoli. No signs of sepsis. Presumed urinary origin but persistent +Cx (last ___ ab CT w/ and LFTs have remained stable w/o signs cholangitis thus port also concerning for source. port removed on ___. all subsequent cx negative, cath tip cx was negative - started empiric CTX ___ then cefepime ___ - changed to meropenem on ___ after susceptibilities resulted. per ID will transition to ertapenem and (plan for total 4 weeks rx from ___, tentative end date ___. Patient will be followed in ___ clinic, intake has been done. - added on sensitivities to doripenem, ceftolozane/tazobactam, colistin in case has MDR E coli infection in the future - ___ placed ___ after 72 hrs negative cx following port removal # Hyponatremia - Stable. most consistent w/ SIADH also some loss from bile fluid FeNA likely reflecting mixed prerenal cause during this admission in the setting of infection. Na remains stable 127-131 - cont salt tab to 1 g qid - liberalized fluid restrict to 2 L # Pain - on prn oxy at home. patient had increased pain at biliary drain and started on am MS contin however caused sleepiness requiring ritalin. pain needs overall decreased throughout his stay thus at time of discharge on only prn MSIR, pt preferred morphine to his prior oxycodone # Depression - cont on home dose sertraline and xanax # C.diff diarrhea - significant prior antibiotic exposure in past month and his wife was recently treated for C.diff. stool + on ___. - patient will need to continue po vanc for a total of ___fter meropenem course ends # Cholangiocarcinoma - He will f/u with Dr ___ in near future to discuss further treatment. Thus far unable to receive chemotherapy due to infections and poor performance status. #Biliary obstruction - pt noted to have leakage around PTBD site and rise in bili level on ___. drain was exchanged and upsized on ___. leakage now improved and bili returned to normal # Ulcerative colitis w/ ostomy placement - - holding home lomotil w/ C diff infection, cont home omeprazole, and miconazole cream to ostomy site # Hypothyroidism - cont levothyroxine, TSH elevated this admission but free T4 normal in setting of acute illness likely sick euthyroid, home dose continued #Vit D deficiency - vit D sent as alkP noted to be elevated. Vit D level 9. started ergocalciferol ___. Should continue 50000U 1x/week for 3 more weeks then ___ vit D daily # Hyperlipidemia - Continue home atorvastatin. #Generalized weakness - related to deconditioning from multiple hospitalizations since ___, underyling malignancy and poor PO intake. Taking ensure. ___ consulted for ongoing mobilization, rehab placement recommended he will discharge to ___ ___ - ___ #Poor Po intake - pt expressed interest in starting appetite stimulant. started remeron 7.5mg qhs, can increase to 15mg if no excess sedation. Pt should avoid Ativan in conjunction w/ remeron for first few days. if no sedation and still requires Ativan for sleep can resume
summary: You were admitted with low sodium, this improved with salt tabs and restricted fluid intake. Your sodium remains low but has been very stable and is not in the dangerous range. You were also found to have bacterial infection of the urine and blood. This was treated with IV antibiotics, due to delayed clearance of the bacteria and concern that the port may also have gotten contaminated, your port-a-cath was removed on ___. You have not had any + blood cultures since ___ but you will need to complete and extended course of IV antibiotics, the anticipated end date is ___. You also had a diarrheal infection called C.diff. We are treating you with oral antibiotics which will need to be continued through the end of ___. Lastly, your bilirubin levels started to increase but have now returned to normal after exchanging your biliary drain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo female with a history of diverticulitis s/p sigmoid colectomy ___ c/b incisional hernia s/p repair ___, h/o pancreatitis s/p cholecystectomy ___, h/o SBO, ovarian cysts s/p R ovarian cystectomy, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and OSA who presented with acute onset abdominal pain and vomiting. #ABDOMINAL PAIN and VOMITING: She reported sudden onset of vomiting that spontaneously resolved after 3 hours and stabbing R-sided abdominal pain with dull cramping pain across her lower abdomen that lessened in severity not long after the vomiting ended. She did not have diarrhea and was passing gas; last bowel movement was on the morning before she presented. She was afebrile and approximately 12 hours after initial presentation, her abdomen was soft, non-distended, but tender to palpation diffusely with voluntary guarding but no rebound. Her white count was mildly elevated. Lipase and LFTs were normal. She had a CT performed after her severe abdominal pain lessened that showed enteritis limited to the right hemi-abdomen. The time course and progression of the vomiting and pain as well as her extensive abdominal surgical history and prior history of small bowel obstructions were consistent with an SBO. On speaking with radiology, concluded that the findings on CT could represent inflammatory changes after resolved SBO. There was initial concern that the enteritis could be related to lisinopril-induced gut angioedema. However, we felt that SBO was a more plausible alternative so we continued her home lisinopril. She did not require pain medications or anti-emetics for her abdominal pain or nausea. She did take fioricet for a slight migraine that improved. Her abdominal pain significantly improved from greater than ___ to ___ in severity. Her nausea resolved entirely. She tolerated a regular diet and had a bowel movement on the day of discharge. While she was an inpatient, her blood pressures were elevated to 190s systolic, which were brought down with labetalol 100mg PO. It is likely that this was due to pain as they downtrended after her pain resolved. She was maintained on home lisinopril. During her admission, she was given fluids and developed rales at bilateral bases, no elevated JVP or peripheral edema. She was noted to have a II/VI systolic murmur best heard at the LUSB that radiated to the carotids, which could represent aortic stenosis. This could have been pulmonary edema in the setting of high blood pressures, but we recommend clinical follow-up. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # Migraine headaches: Continued home amitriptyline. Received dose of fioricet. # HLD: continued home simvastatin # Depression: continued home fluoxetine TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== -Patient will call to schedule a follow-up appointment with her PCP, ___, in the next ___ weeks. -Please follow up blood pressures, consider up-titration of lisinopril and echo
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had abdominal pain and vomiting. We think that this was caused by a transient bowel obstruction, or a blockage in your small intestine. You had a CT scan that showed some inflammation in the wall of your small bowel, which we think was caused by the obstruction. You received a few doses of antibiotics called ciprofloxacin and metronidazole because we wanted to make sure that there wasn't an infection. We don't think you have an infection because you never had a fever or other symptoms that are associated with infection and you do not need any more antibiotics. Your abdominal pain improved during your stay and your nausea and vomiting went away. You were able to eat a regular diet. You had a bowel movement and were passing gas. This all makes us think that you no longer have an obstruction.

text: Brief Hospital Course: =================== SUMMARY: =================== ___ with a history notable for tracheobronchomalacia and bronchopulmonary dysplasia secondary to premature birth at ___, s/p 4 airway reconstructions and tracheostomy since infancy, history of chronic bronchitis and recurrent tracheitis, as well as chronic supraglottic stenosis and laryngeal stenosis who presented to the ED with recurrent dysphagia. During this hospitalization, she was able to resume eating a regular diet with good pain control. Barium swallow showed the patient to have moderate esophageal dysmotility. =================== ACUTE ISSUES: =================== #Dysphagia #Moderate esophageal dysmotility #Throat pain, odynophagia On admission, the patient reports dysphagia above baseline with a globus sensation, as well as throat pain (without swallowing) and odynophagia, both above baseline. She was admitted in ___ with a similar presentation, at which time evaluation was overall unremarkable and she was recommended a particular protocol (3 swallows with 1 solid food bolus, 2 swallows with 1 liquid food bolus, and occluding trach with each food bolus to improve swallowing pressure and bolus flow). The patient noted relatively weak adherence to this regimen at home. During this admission, IP saw the patient and felt her dysphagia and odynophagia to not be related to the trach. ENT saw the patient, performed bedside endoscopy, and noted only bilateral turbinate hypertrophy and right septal spur as well as supraglottic hyperfunction but no significant explanation for her symptoms. Video swallow showed mild oropharyngeal dysphagia without aspiration, as well as frequent cough after swallowing, though to be due to hypersensitivity. They felt her to be safe to resume s regular diet. Barium swallow showed moderate esophageal dysmotility, which previous barium swallow had not shown. In terms of her diet, she was initially NPO and then advanced, and by discharge was tolerating a normal diet and pills/capsules without significant issues. Pain control was provided with standing Tylenol, NSAIDs, and lozenges with good relief. #Hyperglycemia Fasting blood sugar of 160s-170s on morning labs. Thus, A1c checked and found A1c 7.9 this admission. =================== ACUTE ISSUES: =================== #Tracheobronchomalacia Tracheostomy since birth, followed by IP as an outpatient. Had routine tracheostomy change on ___ and tracheobronchial wash. Per IP this admission, trach appears to be without issue. #Asthma The patient was continued on home home inhalers/nebs. #Depression/anxiety The patient was continue on home fluoxetine. #CODE: presumed full #CONTACT: Name of health care proxy: ___ ___: Mother/HCP Phone number: ___ =================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== []SWALLOWING TECHNIQUES: Please continue to monitor the patient for adherence to her swallowing regimen (3 swallows with 1 solid food bolus, 2 swallows with 1 liquid food bolus, and occluding trach with each food bolus to improve swallowing pressure and bolus flow). []ESOPHAGEAL DYSMOTILITY: The patient would benefit from seeing outpatient gastroenterology for her moderate esophageal dysmotility as it may be contributing to her dysphagia. []OTOLARYNGOLOGY: The patient should follow up with ENT in 4 weeks. []INTERVENTIONAL PULMONOLOGY: The patient should follow up with IP. []NSAIDS: The patient was discharged on ibuprofen. Please consider downtitrating as tolerated. []HYPERGLYCEMIA: The patient was noted have fasting blood sugars of 160s-170s on labs, with A1c 7.9. Consider rechecking and lifestyle modifications and/or pharmacologic intervention.
summary: You were having difficulty swallowing at home and had throat pain, especially with swallowing. You were seen by the Interventional Pulmonologists, who determined that your tracheostomy was in the right place and your swallowing issues were not related to it. You were seen by the Otolaryngologists who performed endoscopy at the bedside. This did not find an explanation for your swallowing issues. You were seen by the Speech Language Pathologists, who helped you work on strategies to improve your swallowing. They felt that you were safe to resume a normal diet. You underwent a video swallow, which did not show any aspiration of food contents into your airways. You underwent a barium swallow, which showed that you had moderate esophageal dysmotility, meaning your esophagus does not move as it normally should. You were provided pain control and given IV fluids until you were able to resume a regular diet.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with a pmh of COPD, depression, anxiety, and back pain, who presents with shortness of breath and back pain. #1. COPD exacerbation: Exam and imaging consistent with COPD exacerbation. She had a dry cough and was not in respiratory distress during her stay. She was treated with Prednisone 40mg daily for 5 days, nebulizers inpatient and switched to inhalers on discharge, as well as 5 days of azithromycin. At discharge her exam and subjective symptoms had improved. She was restarted on her tiotropium and continued on Advair in addition to the above. She was afebrile throughout her stay. Her CXR showed hyperinflation. #2. Pain: Work-up during last admission negative for fracture. Her exam was consistent with spasm. She was treated with Toradol and cyclobenzaprine with good effect. At discharge she was given a soft collar to use while sleeping. She will follow-up with Dr. ___ in 2 days. #3. Anxiety/Depression: On Citalopram 40mg daily and benzodiazepines with history of abuse. Continued citalopram 40mg, and ativan 1mg BID. #4. Tobacco abuse: Still smoking ___ showing some improvement. Discussed quitting with her. Treated with nicotine patch. Pending issues: Blood cultures x2 pending at discharge
summary: You were admitted to ___ for difficulty breathing caused by a COPD exacerbation. You were treated with azithromycin (antibiotic), steroids, nebulizers and your breathing improved. You also had pain from your back/neck. For this you were treated with Toradol a strong anti-inflammatory and a muscle relaxant. Your pain greatly improved. Please discuss physical therapy to help with your pain. Also, if you can tolerate non-steroidal anti-inflammatories such as ibuprofen, these medications will be of benefit for your pain. We are starting iron because of your low blood count and iron deficiency. Please remember to have your colonoscopy in ___. If you are continuing to have pain in your neck, wearing a soft collar while you sleep may help with the pain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with a past medical history of R vertebral artery aneurysm and SAH s/p shunt in ___, surveillance imaging discovered pseudoaneurysm with a dissection s/p pipeline ___ who presented with ___ minutes of right eye vision change associated with headache that spontaneously resolved. She was ruled out for acute stroke. #Migraine #Visual changes: Developed visual change while at home, described as veil descending down her right eye, making he vision "cloudy". Lasted ___ minutes, spontaneously resolved. Associated with right supra-orbital headache with photophonia and phonophobia. Presented to ED where exam was initially notable for decreased visual acuity in the right eye (___) compared to ___ in the left, full visual fields, minimally flat R NLF, and diffusely brisk reflexes with downgoing toes. On repeat examination, neurologic exam was unremarkable and her vision changes resolved. Her right headache persisted for 2 days and resolved with IV fluids. She had some lightheadedness with standing, gait was normal, orthostatics were negative and this resolved with IV fluids, likely dehydration in setting of being NPO for potential angiogram. CTA head, brain MRI were unremarkable. TTE demonstrated PFO, not thought to contribute to her presentation. Cerebral angiogram was deferred. Risk factors notable for: LDL 77, A1C 5.1. The etiology of her vision changes initially concerning for TIA given history of clotting (PE ___ and vertebral artery dissection s/p ___ and shunt placing risk for embolization, however unlikely given reassuring imaging. Her vision changes are most likely a symptom of migraine headaches. Her headaches are chronic, occurring 10 times per month, often unilateral with photophobia, phonophobia, she also has a family history of migraines. We started her on verapamil 120mg XR daily. We also obtained an MRI brain which was normal. There was one small punctate lesion that was not thought to contribute to symptoms. Her home aspirin was decreased from 325 to 81 mg daily, she was initiated on plavix 75 mg daily, and her statin was changed to atorvastatin 20 mg daily (as this medication does not interact with verapamil). She was discharged home with no services. #R vertebral artery aneurysm and SAH s/p ___ and VP shunt (___) #Recurrent aneurysm s/p pipeline placement (___) Seen by neurosurgery as above. VP shunt setting programmable, 1.5 after MRI. Cerebral angiogram deferred given low suspicion for stroke. #Depression: Continued home sertraline, trazodone. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== [] initiated plavix 75 daily, decreased ASA 325 to 81 daily [] initiated verapamil for migraine headaches [] found to have PFO on TTE [] consider discontinuing either trazodone or sertraline, may only need one agent
summary: You came to the hospital because you developed vision changes, lightheadedness, and headaches at home. These symptoms were concerning for a stroke. In the hospital, we performed blood tests and imaging of your brain and determined that you did not have a stroke. We believe your symptoms are related to migraine headaches. We started you on a new medication called "plavix" which is a blood thinner that will help prevent you from getting strokes in the future given your history of brain aneurysm. You will take this with aspirin for the next few months until you see the stroke doctor. We also started you on a new medication called verapamil that you will take every day for migraine prevention. This medication is designed to decrease how many headaches you have per month. Lastly, we changed your cholesterol medicine to atorvastatin 20mg daily because this does not interact with your migraine medicine.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is an ___ year old gentleman with PMH diverticulosis, polycystic kidney disease s/p deceased donor renal transplant in ___ PCKD and recent atypical PTLD (EBV negative clonal b-cell proliferation) s/p rituximab therapy in ___ recently admitted to ___ with E coli O157 colitis, p/w poor PO intake for past two days, chills, weakness, and confusion. # Altered Mental Status with weakness and chills: Had more of an encephalopathic picture at presentation (Neuro saw and did not believe infection was liklely enough to get an LP). He never developed a luekocytosis or fever, and the only remarkable lab abnormality that changed since his previous discharge 10 days prior was a positive UA for possible UTI, although he was without symptoms. Initially he was treated with broad spectrum Abx to cover CNS infections given his immunocompromised state; however, his regimen was narrowed to CTX for a total of 9 days. He devleoped witnessed TIA vs less likely seizure activity on ___ (EEG read pending). Pt does have multiple risk factors for TIA: DM, HTN, High Cholesterol, and CTA showing multiple calcificaitons that could have embolized, although head CTA during the event was negative. Should get ECHO with bubble to r/o cardiac souce. We optimized secondary stroke prevention regimen by keeping blood pressures <140 systolic. Statins are typically added after first CVA/TIA; however, it is a class one contraindication with Cyclosporine for incresed muscle weakness and myalgia. On presentation, the patient said he was "so weak he couldn't get out of bed." This medication interaction could have caused such weakness. Such weakness would also contribute to falls, which the patient has had according to his wife. EEG read is pending. ___ saw and evaluated the patient and recommended home with ___. We started him on Aspirin 81 mg and 75 mg daily of Plavix. Chronic issues: # Polycystic Kidney Disease s/p Renal Transplant in ___ - Continued home immunosuppression with cyclosporine, prednisone, and got daily cyclosporine levels with renal following. # Atypical PTLD: Patient now s/p 4 weeks of Rituxin therapy in ___ of this year. His CBC was without evidence of lymphocytosis, only mild thrombocytopenia. - Has outpatient heme/onc follow up in ___ - He had low Ig and Received IVIG on ___. # Coronary artery disease: Held metoprolol as HR is in ___ and can contribute to weakness. Will hold home atorvastatin as it is a class I contraindication with cyclosporine (myalgias, weakness). - Continue Asa 81 daily. # Hypertension: Continued home lisinopril. # High cholesterol: Hold home statin given contraindication with cyclosporine as above # Diabetes: Hold home metformin and glyburide while inpatient and place on Humalog sliding scale # Spinal stenosis/back pain: Tylenol PRN # Sleep apnea: Has not been using CPAP recently - Provide with a CPAP machine in the hospital. # Urinary retention: Continue home tamsulosin and finasteride. # Depression: Sertraline 75 mg daily, continue Depakote, 500 mg QHS
summary: You came to us with weakness and confusion. We consulted transplant nephrology and neurology to assist us with your care. We looked for a source of infection in your blood, but did not find any. We also recultured your stool but did not find any pathologic/infectious bacteria there either. You did have labwork that showed a possible urinary tract infection, for which you received IV antibiotics. Your confusion and weakness slowly improved over this admission without major interventions. During your stay, you developed a TIA "transient ischemic attach," or a mini-stroke. Our neurology team ruled our seizure-like activity with an EEG and your head MRI and CT scans were both normal. You were started on a new medicine to keep your blood thin to help prevent furture strokes. Lab work showed you had low levels of immunoglobulins and your Oncologist recommended we provide a boost with IV immunoglobulins to help fight any infections you may have. You will be going home with physical therapy and they will work with you to help build strength and balance.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M with stage IIIb NSCLC s/p chemo/XRT, bullous emphysema, a PE in ___ for which he is on Lovenox, a 50 pack-year tobacco habit, exudative non-malignant pleural effusion s/p thoracocentesis ___ and a spontaneous PTX s/p pigtail catheter placement ___ now p/w back pain, tachycardia and hypoxemia, found to have pneumothorax w/ malignant exudative pleural effusion, herniated disc on MRI T spine. Pneumothorax treated with pleurodesis. Course complicated by erythematous rash of unknown etiology, thought to be reaction to dilaudid possibly. # Pneumothorax and Pleural Effusion: Seen on initial CT in ED. IP and Thoracic surgery consulted in the ED, chest tube x2 placed. Pleural fluid findings consistent with malignant exudate; cytology pending. s/p thoracoscopy/pleurodesis ___, with bilateral chest tubes placed. Pain control with morphine PCA, transitioned to PO regimen once chest tube removed. Drained 30cc, then 20cc on day of discharge; instructed to drain one more day and to stop if output<50cc; otherwise, continue draining until 3 days of <50cc. Breathing remained stable with O2 sats 92RA. Ambulated with ___ without significant desats. # Back pain: Pt reports sudden onset worsening pain which started with torsion on his lower back. MRI spine with herniated disc. Description of pain consistent with herniated disc. No neurologic deficits, loss of continence, or other danger signs. Plan to treat conservatively with Tylenol, NSAIDs and muscle relaxants. ___ recommended outpatient ___ prescription provided at discharge. # Rash/erythroderma: Occurring in the context of dilaudid administration in ED. Repeat event on ___ in the OR prior to pleurodesis, unclear precipitant as no medications were administered prior. Erythematous, widely distributed, resolving spontaneously within 24 hours. Likely related to dilaudid, but unclear etiology. Resolved at discharge. # Transaminitis: LFTs elevated from baseline on admission, possibly in the setting of drug effect (got vanc/cefipime/dilaudid in ED, with rash as noted above). Subsequently downtrended. # NSCLC: stage IIIb, s/p chemo and radiation. Last chemotherapy in ___ with carboplatin and premetrexed. Continued home tessalon for symptom control. # Hx of PE: Continued lovenox at therapeutic dose # Depression: Continued home wellbutrin and citalopram. # Hypothyroidism Continued home synthroid. # COPD/OSA: Continued home advair and PRN albuterol. Continued home CPAP. #HLD: Continued home simvastatin. #GERD: Continue home omeprazole. Transitional ============ - Discharged with pleurex in place - pleurex to be drained until output <50cc x3 days; ___ and ___ output were both <50cc, so pt can discontinue daily draining if ___ output is <50cc - f/u with IP ___ - continue outpatient ___ for back pain - FULL CODE
summary: You were admitted for back pain and shortness of breath. You were found to have a herniated disc in your lower back, which was what was causing the back pain, and were given painkillers to treat it; there is no role for surgery at this time. You were also found to have air in your chest cavity, which was drained with a chest tube. You underwent a procedure to affix your lung to the inner wall of your chest to prevent a recurrence of this air buildup. You were discharged with a drain to help continue remove fluids from your lung. You should drain this once more tomorrow morning; if the output is less than 50cc, you do not have to drain it further.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ male with history of depression, otherwise no significant past medical history, initially presenting to ___ with several day history of worsening shortness of breath, found to have submassive extensive bilateral pulmonary emboli with evidence of right heart strain on CT, transferred for ___ evaluation. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # Unprovoked Submassive PE Initially presented with several day history of worsening shortness of breath to ___, ___ showing extensive PE in the right main pulmonary artery extending to all lobes as well as within lobar pulmonary arteries of the left upper and lower lobes. Evidence of flattening of IV septum suggesting right heart strain, also with wedge-shaped infarction in right upper lobe and left lower lobe. TTE and EKG here, however without right heart strain. Trops negative, proBNP 35. Lower extremity U/S with evidence of L gastrocnemius DVT. Vascular medicine consulted, recommending medical management. Has otherwise remained hemodynamically stable during hospital course. Initially hypoxemic on admission, requiring 2L O2, now weaned to RA, with ambulatory O2 sats >92%. Treated with heparin gtt, then transitioned to PO Apixaban on ___. Will take 10mg twice daily for 7 days (___), followed by 5mg twice daily (starting ___. Would also recommend follow up with vascular medicine (Dr. ___ as well as hematology as an outpatient. Screening tests for APLS pending at discharge. #Hypoxemic respiratory failure - Resolved Hypoxemic on admission, requiring 2L O2, now weaned to RA. Ambulatory O2 sats 92-95% RA. Likely ___ PE. CXR otherwise negative. #Moderately dilated aortic root Found incidentally to have mildly dilated aortic root on TTE. Based on ___ ACCF/AHA Thoracic Aortic Guidelines, a follow-up echocardiogram is suggested in 6 months. #Hx of fever of unknown origin Patient expresses history of intermittent fevers in ___. Was seen by rheumatology and ID with extensive workup, ultimately treated with steroids. Believed to be attributed to Familial Mediterranean fever. No fevers recently, but given new diagnosis of unprovoked pulmonary embolism, could re-consider chronic inflammatory process. Will have patient follow up with his PCP as an outpatient for further workup. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #Depression - Continue home Citalopram 20 mg PO/NG daily TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [] Started on PO Apixaban on ___. Will take 10mg twice daily for 7 days (___), followed by 5mg twice daily (starting ___. [] Incidentally noted to have mildly dilated aortic root on TTE. Based on ___ ACCF/AHA Thoracic Aortic Guidelines, a follow-up echocardiogram is suggested in 6 months. [] Please follow up screening tests for APLS (beta 2 glycoprotein, cardiolipin ABs) as results are pending at discharge [] Please ensure follow up with hematology as an outpatient for further hypercoagulability workup. Patient will call to make appointment. [] Follow-up should also be arranged with Dr. ___ in ___ system for vascular medicine in 1 month from discharge [] Please also follow up with PCP to ensure patient is up to date on age-appropriate cancer screenings #CODE: Full Code #CONTACT: Name of health care proxy: ___ ___: wife Cell phone: ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Citalopram 20 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted because you had shortness of breath, and were found to have a pulmonary embolism (i.e. blood clot in your lungs). The clot was relatively extensive on your CT scan, involving your right lung, so you were transferred to ___ for further management. When you were admitted we did blood tests to identify any strain on your heart. These blood tests were negative. We also did an echocardiogram which also was negative for heart strain. Given your clinical improvement and negative testing, our interventionalist team decided to manage your blood clot medically, rather than do a procedure. You were initially treated with IV heparin, then transitioned to Apixaban.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old male w/ hypertension (HTN) and difficult to control GERD (s/p ___ who presented to OSH with chest pain different from his baseline GERD and found to have elevated troponin without EKG changes ruling in for NSTEMI and transfered to ___. . # Non-ST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI): His chest pain syndrome was very different from his baseline GERD with a substernal location, pressure-like pain, and associated diaphoresis and nausea. Eventually, he became chest pain free and without shortness of breath, but took hours to achieve this with morphine and nitro. No heart failure on symptoms or exam. No prior history of coronary disease nor stable anginal syndrome and no prior caths but has smoking history and hypertension. He recieved aspirin 325 mg, clopidogrel loaded 600 mg, heparin gtt, atorvastatin 80 mg daily, metoprolol tartrate 25 mg BID, and his lisinopril was increased to 40 mg daily. He underwent a cath with placement of a bare metal stent to his left circumflex artery. After this, his plavix was changed to prasugrel 10 mg daily because this does not interact with his fluoxetine or omeprazole. His transthoracic ECHO showed no wall motion abnormalities, slight mitral regurgitation. He was discharged on: aspirin 81 mg, prasugrel 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 80 mg daily, metoprolol succinate 50 mg BID, and lisinopril 40 mg daily. . # HTN: His systolic pressure on admisson was 169 and so his lisinopril was increased to 40 mg daily in light of NSTEMI as would prefer to reduce afterload to reduce myocardial oxygen demand. . # Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD): This is chronic and poorly controlled despite past ___. Has been on 40mg omeprazole BID for some time. Because PPI interacts with clopidogrel, placed BMS so that anticoagulation time is minimized. Also, discharged him on prasugrel as ___ antiplatelet agent so that omeprazole can be continued. . # Depression: Symptoms stable. Has been on high dose fluoxetine. Fluoxetine also interacts with plavix so again favored prasugrel in ___ medical management of NSTEMI. . # Siatica: Again long-standing and difficult to control. His PCP has reported that he trusts him to have narcotics on a short-term basis if needed for pain while in house. He is getting set-up with the ___ possible surgical intervention. Was given oxycodone for pain control but this caused nightmares so he asked to not have this continued. Avoided NSAIDS because don't want to irritate known gastritis in the setting of new antiplatelet agents as above. . # CONTACT: ___ (___) / ___ . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Please discontinue prasugrel after a month if concerns for GI bleeding - His AST and ALT were slightly elevated and he was started on a statin. These should be rechecked
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had chest pain which was concerning for a heart attack. Your blood work showed that you had a small heart attack and you underwent a procedure (called cardiac catheterization) where they placed a stent in one of your blood vessels to open it up again. We also started some new medications to control your blood pressure and cholesterol. Finally, there are some new medications to thin your blood so that new blood clots are less likely to form in your heart. However, these medications do increase your risk of bleeding in your stomach slightly so you should monitor yourself for symptoms such as black stools.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female who presented to ___ with acute angle-closure glaucoma. # Acute angle-closure glaucoma: She was initially admitted to ___ after undergoing an elective left knee replacement. Her initial post-operative course was uncomplicated. However, she developed severe L eye pain and decreased vision. There was initially concern for temporal arteritis at ___, so she was started on IV steroids and underwent CT head that was unremarkable. She was then transferred to ___ for further care. At ___ she was seen by ophthalmology who found her to have L-sided acute angle-closure glaucoma. She was admitted for planned procedure. The following day she underwent left laser iridotomy, with excellent result. She then underwent a prophlyactic right laser iridotomy the following day. She had no further eye pain, though her vision was minimally improved in her left eye. She was discharged on eye drops per opthalmology recommendations, with planned follow-up in their clinic in 2 weeks. # Left TKA: Patient underwent uncomplicated L TKA at ___. She was cleared by physical therapy at ___ for discharge to home with home ___. She was given warfarin for DVT prophylaxis at ___ ___, and this was continued at ___. On discharge, per her surgeon's instructions, she will take aspirin 325mg bid with omeprazole for 6 weeks. She was scheduled for follow-up with her surgeon at ___ for 4 weeks from the date of her surgery. # Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine. # HLD: Continued statin # Depression: Continued sertraline
summary: You were transferred from ___ to ___ due to acute angle-closure glaucoma in your left eye. This was treated with a procedure called laser iridotomy. You had the same procedure in your right eye to prevent glaucoma in the future. You will need to take eye drops twice daily until instructed to do otherwise by the ophthalmologist. Make sure to see them in clinic for further instructions. For your knee replacement you will need to take aspirin twice daily for 6 weeks to prevent blood clots. You should take omeprazole daily during that time to prevent stomach ulcers. We have provided you with a prescription for acetaminophen (Tylenol) and oxycodone (a narcotic) to treat your pain. It is often helpful to take pain medications BEFORE going to physical therapy, until your pain is improved. This will help you be more successful with your physical therapy sessions.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ right-handed female with a history of paroxysmal AFib, dyslipidemia, HTN, pulmonary HTN, suspected sarcoidosis, who presented after a brief episode of transient unresponsiveness and facial droop, found to be the result of an acute ischemic stroke. ACUTE ISSUES: # Right middle insular cortex infarct: She presented with the above vague symptoms, and no focal neurologic deficit on exam. On CTA, she was found to have evidence of a cutoff in the right M2 inferior division with corresponding area of infarct on MRI of the right middle insula. Etiology most concerning for cardioembolic event. She was maintained on a heparin drip until she was discharged home with a lovenox bridge to coumadin with goal INR ___. She had a TTE which was unremarkable. Her stroke risk factors were assessed to include HTN, HLD (controlled on statin), afib. # Afib: She was previously not anticoagulated, but given new ischemic stroke she was started on Coumadin with a heparin bridge. Coumadin levels will be followed by her PCP ___ ___ the interim until checking can be taken over by ___ clinic associated with her cardiologist, Dr. ___. She was maintained on her home dose of metoprolol for appropriate rate control. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Hypertension: Her blood pressure was allowed to autoregulate during the first 24hours. After which, both her furosemide and valsartan were reinstated. # Hyperlipidemia: stable, she continued her home atorva 80. # Suspected sarcoidosis: stable, she continued her home colchicine. # Depression: stable, continued home fluoxetine TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: 1) INR will be monitored by Dr. ___ as long as patient has ___. After that time, INR checks will be done at ___ clinic associated with Dr. ___. 2) Both Dr. ___ Dr. ___ telephoned regarding this hospitalization 3) Full Code
summary: You were admitted because of left facial droop and altered mental status. You had an MRI and CT scan of your brain which showed that your symptoms were due to a stroke in the right inner hemisphere of your brain. Fortunately, your neurologic deficits have already resolved and you are back to your baseline functioning. You stroke was likely caused by a clot that developed because of your abnormal heart rhythm, atrial fibrillation. You had an echocardiogram of your heart which was reassuring. To prevent future strokes, we have discussed the options with Dr. ___ ___ have decided on Coumadin. At home you will continue lovenox injections twice a day and Coumadin once a day until your Coumadin levels are therapeutic. At that time, you will discontinue Lovenox and continue only Coumadin. You will have bloodwork to check your Coumadin levels through the ___. These results will be faxed to Dr. ___ office at ___. Dr. ___ is aware you are on Coumadin and will be needing this bloodwork closely monitored. Dr. ___ will call you regarding dose adjustments of Coumadin while you are on this medication. Please make sure that your Coumadin levels are checked at least 2 times weekly to avoid bleeding complications on this medication.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt is a ___ y.o female with h.o fibromyalgia, depression/anxiety, GERD who presents with reports of GERD, dysphagia/odynophagia, and chest cramping/spasm. . #GERD/sternal burning/dysphagia/odynophagia-Pt reports that these symptoms were present prior to last esophageal dilation and had improved, now are worsened. DDX includes gastritis/esophagitis, PUD, esophageal spasm, esophageal stricture, motility/functional disorder. Pt does have a history of GERD and has been taking motrin at home. CXR does not show free air, EGD shows erythema in the stomach, but otherwise no PUD. LFTs/lipase normal. EKG is unchanged from prior and 2 sets of cardiac enzymes were negative. GI recommended changing PPI to 20mg BID, continuing h2 blocker and starting sucralfate. GI also recommending considering motility study and/or video swallowing evaluation. However, in order to obtain an accurate motility study, pt would not be able to take narcotics and benzodiazepines which she is currently taking. Pt did not wish to pursue this study at this time for fear of "withdrawal" from these medications. As her diet was successfully advanced, this further work up (video swallow) was deferred to the outpatient setting. Stools were ordered for guaiac. . #constipation-increased bowel regimen to senna, colace, miralax . #depression/anxiety-continued home meds of fluoxetine, klonapin, doxepin, seroquel, tegretol. Denied worsening of symptoms or SI. . #chronic pain/fibromyalgia/neuropathy-continued home regimen of neurontin, vicodin, held NSAIDS for now given symptoms as above. . #hyponatremia-likely hypovolemic due to poor PO intake as resolved with IVF. But did consider SIADH from psychiatric meds . #anemia-normocytic baseline 35-37. Remained close to baseline. Denied melena/brbpr . #FEN-regular . #ppx-hep SC TID . Transitional care: -consider ordering video swallow study and/or gastric emptying study (pt would have to be off narcotics)
summary: You were admitted with reports of painful and difficulty swallowing, as well as abdominal pain. For this, you underwent an endoscopy that showed some mild irritation (red areas) in your stomach. Biopsies were taken and PENDING at the time of discharge. Given your history of reflux and endoscopy findings it was recommended that you take prilosec 20mg twice a day, pepcid twice a day, and started on a medication called sucralfate to protect your stomach. You were able to eat a regular diet after endoscopy. Your symptoms may be due to a spasm in your esophagus. You may discuss treatment options, if this is the case, with your gastroenterologist. Please discuss the need for a motility study and/or a video swallowing test at your next GI appointment.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of anxiety, depression, IBS presents with nausea and vomiting x 5 days, likely non-organic cause. # Nausea, vomiting, dizziness, paresthesias. Multiple nonspecific complaints of nausea, vomiting, abdominal discomfort, headaches, fatigue, weakness over the past three months. There have been no organic causse or abnormal objective findings on lab studies or physical exam. This is most likely related to anxiety versus depression. Of note, she recently had started amoxicillin for sinusitis seen on MRI. She also had recently started gabapentin and citalopram in the outpatient setting. These three medications were held in case they were contributing to her symptoms. Did not start an alternate antibiotic, as she reports that she had already taken 7 days of antibiotics. Inpatient workup - normal CBC, Chem-10, LFTs, albumin, lactate, EKG, CXR, UA, urine bUCG. Orthostatics negative. Physical exam normal. CK normal. HIV negative. Outpatient workup - B12, TSH, vit D, CRP, IgA, TTG, zinc, RPR, MonoSpot, Lyme serology, MRI negative for multiple sclerosis She was treated with PRN acetaminophen and ondansetron. She was tolerating a regular diet on discharge. She was discharged with PRN acetaminophen and ondasentron and has primary care follow up. # Recent sinusitis. Prescribed amoxicillin by PCP following MRI. Received amoxicillin 500 mg PO Q8H, ___, will discontinue given patient's concern for side effects and she has already received one week course. # External hemorrhoid. Known. Nonthrombosed. Hydrocortisone rectal cream PRN. # Low grade vulvar dysplasia. Followed as outpatient. Uses imiquimod 5% topical 3/WEEK, not on formulary, spoke with pharmacy, will defer substitute since patient was discharged the next day. # CODE: Full, confirmed ___ # CONTACT: ___, husband, ___ ### TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ### 1) PCP followup for further anxiety and depression management. Consider use of another SSRI. Patient apparently developed vomiting to citalopram and gabapentin. 2) Discharged with Tylenol and Zofran PRN for symptom management.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of nausea and vomiting. We reviewed your medical records including previous lab workups and office visits with multiple specialists and with your primary care physician. We checked bloodwork, electrocardiogram, urine, and chest xray, which were all normal. You had a normal physical exam. You were treated with symptomatic medications with Tylenol and Zofran for nausea. We did not find a life-threatening cause for your nausea. You improved and were able to tolerate a regular diet on discharge. The most likely cause of your symptoms is depression and/or anxiety.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ G1P0 at ~7wks GA was admitted given persistant vomiting, very recent hospitalization, and poor oral intake in the setting of a baseline poor nutritional status. A nutrition consult was obtained on HD2, and the patient was observed on a trial of oral intake, supplemented with Ensure, with anti-emetics, placed on IV fluids, and closely monitored for electrolyte abnormalities. She was given zofran, compazine, and phenergan, and her nausea improved somewhat, but she continued to have poor oral intake and thus a PICC line was placed and she was started on total parentaral nutrition on HD4. On HD2, she was noted to have left-sided stiffening. She had received a dose of compazine at 1100 that day, and had received 2 doses of IV phenergan the previous day. Her symptoms resolved with valium and benadryl and the neurology service was consulted. Her presentation was thought most consistent with a dystonic reaction from the anti-emetics, in particular the compazine, and thus this medication was discontinued. Given persistent nausea on just zofran, she was given a trial of rectal phenergan and did not have any additional episodes of dystonia. Her electrolytes were monitored daily and lipids were added to her nutrition on day 3 (HD6) of the TPN. The TPN was cycled on hospital day 8, and she was discharged on HD9 with nursing services and a plan for continued total parentaral nutrition. Her nausea was controlled with zofran, rectal phenergan, and ativan, she had no additional episodes of vomiting, and her electrolytes had been stable for several days. In addition, she was evaluated by social work and psychiatry given her history of anorexia and depression. She denied suicidal ideation or harm to self and was not thought to be actively anorexic or in major depression. Given the stressful nature of the event, the patient desired to start pharmacotherapy and thus was started on a trial of fluoxetine 10mg on HD6. The patient was also given mental health resources by the ___ social worker ___, who gave the patient information to find a therapist through the National Association of Social Workers referral network.
summary: You were hospitalized given your persistent vomiting in pregnancy and poor nutritional status. You were evaluated by the nutrition, and given that you were unable to tolerate oral feeding and remained persistently nauseated, in the setting of poor oral intake for >7 days you were started on parenteral nutrition. This means you were given nutrition through an IV. In order to do this, a central line was placed. This is something that you will go home with, and you will have nursing services in order to help you with getting daily nutrition. You should continue to try to take food orally and use the anti-nausea medications to help with your nausea. You should avoid taking compazine, as you appeared to have a stiffening (dystonic) reaction in the hospital. You should first try zofran for the nausea, and if that doesn't work, then you can try the phenergan suppository.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMHx notable for mild COPD, Crohn's and gastric ulcer presents with ___ weeks of worsening epigastric chest pain with h/o black stools in the setting of pepto bismol use 2wk prior. # Epigastric chest pain: Patient endorses substernal and midepigastric chest pain, felt to be GI etiology given history of burning sensation, inability to PO and ?reguritation vs emesis after eating. Initially, differential included esophageal stricture vs motility issue/spasm vs gastritis vs ulcer. WBC elevated to 14.1. CXR unremarkable. Cardiac etiology is possible, but less likely given lack of history/risk factors, nml ECG x2, enzymes negative. Barium swallow was performed to assess for stricture, showed reflux but no evidence of stricture/narrowing/spasm. Therefore, endoscopy was performed which demonstrated normal mucosa throughout, biopsy results pending at time of discharge. Her pain was controlled with tramadol and oxycodone prn, but she did intermittently require IV morphine x1 doses for severe chest pain. Other studies include: guaiac neg stools, H pylori neg. Home PPI and dicyclomine were continued in house. At time of discharge she was tolerating good POs, with moderately improved pain. # Crohns: Symptoms were felt to be not consistent with Crohns flare, no diarrhea, no ulcerations or abdominal pain. Not on any meds for Crohns at home. #COPD: Per recent Pulm note, mild COPD. Continued Spiriva, Albuterol prn. Patient does continue to smoke, but endorsed wanting to quit and willingness to try nicotine patches upon discharge. # Depression: Controlled currently per patient. Continue home Wellbutrin, Effexor #CODE STATUS: Full confirmed TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - EGD biopsy results pending: GI fellow emailed re: esophagitis with eosinophils - Smoking cessation - F/u with GI - F/u with PCP ___ on ___: Preadmission medications listed are correct and complete. Information was obtained from Patient. 1. BuPROPion 200 mg PO DAILY 2. DiCYCLOmine 20 mg PO QID 3. Omeprazole 20 mg PO DAILY 4. Tiotropium Bromide 1 CAP IH DAILY 5. Venlafaxine XR 37.5 mg PO DAILY 6. Vitamin D ___ UNIT PO DAILY 7. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY 8. Psyllium 1 PKT PO DAILY 9. Fish Oil (Omega 3) 1000 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted because you had severe chest pain and were unable to eat or drink normally. While you were here, we got your pain under control and performed some tests to figure out what was causing your pain. All of the tests of your heart returned normal. In terms of your esophagus and stomach, you first had a swallow study which demonstrated normal esophagus but you had some reflux from your stomach. Then you had an upper endoscopy to look at the inside of your esophagus, which unfortunately didn't reveal the cause of your pain. The inpatient gastroenterologists felt that the best thing to manage your pain will be high dose acid suppression medication.

text: Brief Hospital Course: HOSPITAL COURSE This is a ___ F PMHx prior venous and arterial thrombi of uncertain etiology who presented w six months of painful bilateral breast lesions, acute onset right lower leg pain, found to have tibial infarct, thought to have livedoid vasculopathy secondary to underlying hypercoagulability, INR goal changed to 2.5-3.5 - preferably ___, discharged to rehab ACTIVE ISSUES # Hypercoagulability / Right tibial bone infarct / Thrombotic Vasculopathy - Patient w a history of prior hypercoagulability of uncertain etiology who was admitted with new tibial infarct and skin ulceration. Initially patient anticoagulated on heparin gtt. Underwent evaluation by hematology service and dermatology service who felt presentation was consistent with a thrombobotic vasculopathy, likely ___ antiphospholipid antibody syndrome although underlying malignancy (not yet diagnosed) is another possibility. Patient positive for anticardiolipin IgM and beta 2 glycoprotein 1 IgG. Patient underwent further evaluation of bilateral breast nodules to rule out potential underlying malignant process; patient seen by her outpt breast surgeon Dr. ___ after reviewing a bilateral ultrasound felt that given multifocal nature and appearance on u/s, lesions most c/w thrombotic process. Patient was transitioned to coumadin, goal INR 2.5 - 3.5 (had previously been 2 - 3), with >48hours overlap with heparin bridge. Patient was discharged with scheduled hematology follow-up appointment. She was recommended for follow-up with Dr. ___ in 3 months for repeat ultrasound and MRI of her breasts. Coumadin dosing at discharge was 2.5mg daily. INR was 3.3. ASA and Pletal were continued. INR will need to be checked daily until within 2.5 - 3.5 x 3days in a row. # Pain control - patient with a history of chronic pain, coming in with worseing pain ___ above breast and extremity lesions thought to be thrombotic vasculopathy. Pain was controlled with oxycontin and oxycodone. Patient was continued on home neurontin, nortryptiline, tylenol. Also started on topical diclofenac sodium. INACTIVE ISSUES # COPD: continued albuterol, fluticasone-salmeterol, tiotropium bromide # HLD - Continued atorvastatin # FEN - continued calcium carbonate, vitamin D, iron. # Depression / Anxiety - continued home citalopram, clonazepam TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Full Code - Patient is scheduled for follow-up with outpatient hematologist for additional evaluation and management of underlying hypercoagulability - Patient will need repeat Breast U/S and MRI with ___ Breast Center in 3 months ___ - Patient will need to have stitches removed L leg skin biopsy ___ - Low threshold for additional malignancy work-up; patient should have all age-appropriate cancer screening performed - PICC line will need to be removed prior to discharge from rehab
summary: You were transferred here for further evaluation of the clots you have had and the bumps in your breasts. While you were here, you were seen by the hematologists and the dermatologists who felt that the skin changes you have and the clots you have may be due to a clotting disorder. A breast ultrasound showed clots in your breasts and no evidence of cancer. We kept you on heparin to keep your blood thin and transitioned you to coumadin. It is very important that you continue to take your coumadin to prevent more clots from forming. Your goal INR is now 2.5-3.5. You will need to have stitches removed from you skin biopsy on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman with decompensated alcoholic and HCV cirrhosis who presented with increased falls due to encephalopathy with viral URI as possible trigger, trauma work up negative for acute injury. # Hepatic encephalopathy: Patient presented encephalopathic with increased falls. She reported full medication compliance with ___ BM/day at home. Infectious work up was negative including UA, CXR, and blood cultures, though she did complain of viral URI symptoms (sore throat). CT abdomen showed trace ascites, so when mental status did not initially clear with increased lactulose she was started empirically on SBP treatment with ceftriaxone and albumin. These were discontinued when US of her abdomen with doppler the following day showed no free fluid. US was also negative for portal vein thrombus. Encephalopathy improved with increased lactulose and continued rifaxamin, and patient was able to return home. # Neutropenia: she is baseline pancytopenic, likely came in volume contracted, all counts dropped after albumin. It is possible that she had a mild URI causing drop in counts, encephalopathy and sore throat. She was warned about needing to seek immediate medical care if febrile >100.4F. # Cirrhosis: MELD of 19 on admission. Cirrhosis due to alcohol abuse and hepatitis C. Decompensated by history of encephalopathy and ascites. No history of varices (EGD ___. Is currently initiating transplant work up with outpatient hepatologist Dr. ___. Was seen by transplant social work while here as had outpatient appointment scheduled during hospitalization. Continued spironolactone and furosemide, as well as treatment for encephalopathy as above. CHRONIC ISSUES # Depression/insomnia: Outpatient notes mention that insomnia has been refractory for basic interventions, they were considering outpatient sleep clinic referral. Could be from depression, anxiety disorder, or sleep-wake cycle reversal from liver disease. Continued fluoxetine and minimized low dose ambien use. # Colitis: Focal proctitis seen on colonoscopy and biopsy in ___ without clear etiology, but abdominal pain seems to have responded somewhat to mesalamine, so outpatient hepatologist has kept her on this. Continued here. # Hypertension: Continued Atenolol. No varices on ___ EGD so no need for non-selective beta blocker. # GERD: continued Pantoprazole, zofran for nausea as needed TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Code status: full; Daughter ___ is HCP ___ - Blood cultures pending at discharge - Hepatitis serologies pending to evaluate for immunity to Hep A and B - Consider futher work up for insomnia as would be ideal for patient to be off Ambien to reduce fall risk - Neutropenic at time of discharge with ANC <1000 - Patient was changed from atenolol to propanolol
summary: You were admitted after a fall at home. Imaging of your head, spine, and abdomen did not show any injury. Your lactulose was increased and your mental status improved. It is possible your increased lethargy and confusion ("encephalopathy") were triggered by a mild viral upper respiratory tract infection. Your white blood cell count was low while you were here. White blood cells help us to fight infections. You should avoid coming into contact with anyone who has any symptoms of viral or bacterial infection, and wash your hands frequently. Seek medical attention IMMEDIATELY if you measure a fever at home of greater than 100.4. Your atenolol was changed to propranolol, which is a medication which will help your liver and your heart.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with h/o baseline encephalopathy (AAOx0-1) ___ basal ganglia hemorrhagic stroke, CAD, HTN, hypothyroidism, and depression who presented from nursing home with blocked G-tube (now s/p G tube replacement by ___ found to have worsening bibasilar opacifications concerning for incompletely treated PNA, course c/b hyponatremia. . # BLOCKED G-TUBE: Patient's G-tube was found to be blocked despite multiple attempts to flush with water & and carbonated beverages. He was evaluated for G-tube replacement by the interventional radiology team & consent was obtained from the patient's son. He underwent uncomplicated G-tube replacement on ___ and tube feeds were restarted per nutrition team recs. It was advised that all patient's G tube should be briskly flushed after tube feeds. Crushed medications should be avoided if liquid versions are available. . # BIBASILAR LUNG OPACIFICATIONS: CXR on admission was concerning for worsening B/L bibasilar lung opacifications. Patient was recently treated for CAP in ___ with 10 days of levofloxacin; there was concern that PNA had either been undertreated or that he had aspirated (gross debris present in mouth on admission). He was initially covered broadly for HCAP and aspiration PNA with vanc/cefepime/cipro/flagyl. However, as he remained completely afebrile without leukocytosis or cough throughout hospitalization, and O2 sats were in high ___ throughout, there was ultimately low concern for PNA so antibiotics were DC'ed on ___. He remained afebrile without leukocytosis after stopping antibiotics. Blood and urine cultures have all been negative to date. It was felt that changes on CXR likely represent radiologic sequelae of recent pneumonia compounded with atalectasis and scarring from recurrent aspiration events. . # HYPONATREMIA: Patient was noted to have hyponatremia to 132 on admission, and sodium subsequently trended down to nadir of 127. As patient appeared clinically dry on exam, his hyponatremia was felt to be secondary to hypovolemia in the setting of blocked G-tube and no PO intake. On resumption of tube feeds and flushes, sodium gradually trended back to normal over the course of several days and was 136 on discharge. . # HYPOTHYROIDISM: Continued home levothyroxine. . # DEPRESSION: Continued home citalopram. . # HTN: Continued home metoprolol and amlodipine. . # CHRONIC ENCEPHALOPATHY: Secondary to right basal ganglia bleed. Appeared to be at baseline mental status (AAOx0-1, responds yes/no to some questions, opens eyes to voice) at the time of discharge. . # ANEMIA: Iron deficiency. Hemoglobin at baseline on admission. No active signs/symptoms of bleeding. Home iron supplementation was continued. . =================================== TRANSITION OF CARE: - Please briskly flush G tube after all feeds. - Crushed medications should be avoided if liquid versions are available. - Please remove Foley tonight and check post-void residuals (per phone discussion today).
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because your feeding tube (G-tube) was blocked and therefore not functioning properly. We replaced your G-tube on ___ and it now functions well. Your chest x-ray showed some areas that were concerning for new pneumonia. You were initially treated with IV antibiotics for a couple of days. Since you did not have a fever or any other markers of infection, we ultimately did not feel you had a pneumonia and your antibiotics were stopped. In addition, your sodium level was low due to dehydration from missing your tube feeds: this improved once the G-tube was replaced.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old female w/h/o ITP, MGUS, DM2 and recent h/o pseudomonas/E.coli UTI in ___ who p/w hyperkalemia and acute renal failure # Hyperkalemia: This was from her ARF. She did not have peaked T waves. She was treated with IVFs, Ca Gluconate, kayexalate and insulin/Dextrose until this returned to ___. She came in with a K of 7.2; this trended down to 4.4 by the time of discharge. This improve along with her renal failure (Creatinine of 6 decrease to 2.6 on day of discharge). She was tele monitored. # ARF - baseline Cr appears to be 1.2 -> went up to 2.0 on a clinic visit. Given her decreased PO intake, FeNa (0.33%), it was felt that this was most likely a prerenal condition. Hence, she was aggressively hydrated with IVFs; she also received a blood transfusion (2 units) as well. Furthermore, her urine had moderate Eos -> but no casts per renal.
